Diagnostics.desc: SNMP subsystem counters: traps sent, GET requests serviced, errors.
Diagnostics.example: Traps_Sent: 12  Get_Requests: 4502  Errors: 0

Diagnostics.desc: DNS resolver / authoritative counters — queries, answers, cache hits.
Diagnostics.example: Queries: 12053  Answers: 12053  Cache_Hits: 450

Diagnostics.desc: Per-CPU utilization across all packet-engine cores.
Diagnostics.example: CPU0: 4%  CPU1: 3%  CPU2: 5%  CPU3: 2%

Diagnostics.desc: System memory breakdown — total, used, free, in cache.
Diagnostics.example: Total: 16GB  Used: 4GB  Free: 12GB  Cache: 1GB

Diagnostics.desc: AAA counters — login successes/failures and active sessions.
Diagnostics.example: Login_Success: 4502  Login_Fail: 12  Active_Sessions: 45

Diagnostics.desc: Dump live policy-hit counters from the kernel newnslog ring buffer.
Diagnostics.example: 0  0  14502  pol_hits_RESP_POL_1

Diagnostics.desc: Show current service-state counters (UP/DOWN) from newnslog.
Diagnostics.example: 0  0  1  cac_cur_svc_state_SVC_1_UP

Diagnostics.desc: Disk usage in human-readable units (BSD shell).
Diagnostics.example: Filesystem Size Used Avail Capacity Mounted on /dev/ad0s1a 1.9G 645M 1.1G 36% /

Diagnostics.desc: One-shot top snapshot — load averages, top processes by CPU.
Locate the packet-engine processes (PPE-0, PPE-1, …) and their CPU usage.
Kernel routing table in numeric form (host stack, not the dataplane).
Diagnostics.example: Routing tables Internet: Destination Gateway Flags Netif Expire default 10.1.1.1 UGS 1/1

Diagnostics.desc: Show the last five lines of the main NetScaler audit log.
Diagnostics.example: May 27 15:00:01 <local0.info> 10.1.1.10 PPE-0 : UI_READ : User nsroot

Diagnostics.desc: Surface any failure messages in /var/log/ns.log.
Diagnostics.example: May 27 10:12:05 Login failed for user admin

Diagnostics.desc: Time since boot, current load averages and logged-in user count.
Diagnostics.example: 3:14PM up 10 days, 4:12, 1 user, load averages: 0.02, 0.05, 0.01

Diagnostics.desc: Dump the persisted (saved) ns.conf from /flash/nsconfig — survives reboot.

Start a NetScaler packet trace with a CONNECTION.* filter expression. Every connection that matches the expression is written to /var/nstrace/. Filter expressions support &&, || and grouping with parentheses; the whole filter must be enclosed in double quotes.
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -filter "CONNECTION.DSTIP.EQ(10.248.1.15)"
nstrace: started, output in /var/nstrace/ns_00001.cap

# Expression grammar:
#   CONNECTION.<qualifier>.<method>(<value>)
#   joined by  &&  (AND)  or  ||  (OR)
#   parens for grouping

# Method shortlist by qualifier type:
#   IP/IPv6/INTF/SERVICE_TYPE  : EQ, NE
#   ports / VLAN / CONNID /
#     PPEID / TRAFFIC_DOMAIN_ID: EQ, NE, GT, GE, LT, LE, BETWEEN
#   SVCNAME / LB_VSERVER.NAME /
#     CS_VSERVER.NAME          : EQ, NE, CONTAINS, STARTSWITH, ENDSWITH

What it means:
- The expression is evaluated per-connection. Hits → packets are
  written to the rolling trace files.
- Quote string values: SVCNAME.EQ("svc1") not SVCNAME.EQ(svc1).
- Combine with -size 0 for full packets and -link ENABLED to also
  capture the linked server-side flow when matching the client side
  (or vice-versa).

Diagnostics.desc: Match traffic by client (source) IPv4 address. Methods: EQ, NE.
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -filter "CONNECTION.SRCIP.EQ(10.1.1.1)"
nstrace: started, filter matches client IP 10.1.1.1

What it means:
- SRCIP is the original client IP. On the back-end leg this becomes
  a SNIP (because the ADC source-NATs by default), so use SVCNAME or
  LB_VSERVER.NAME to follow the back-end side.
- Use NE to exclude a noisy client: CONNECTION.SRCIP.NE(127.0.0.1)

Diagnostics.desc: Match traffic by destination IPv4 address. Methods: EQ, NE.
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -filter "CONNECTION.DSTIP.EQ(10.248.1.15)"
nstrace: started, filter matches destination IP 10.248.1.15

What it means:
- DSTIP is the destination as seen by the ADC packet-engine — for
  client → ADC traffic this is a VIP; for ADC → server traffic it is
  the back-end real-server IP.
- Combine with .NE to exclude management subnets, e.g.
    CONNECTION.DSTIP.NE(192.168.0.0/16)  ← (subnet not allowed; use IP only)

Diagnostics.desc: Match traffic where IPv4 address (source OR destination) equals the value. Methods: EQ, NE.
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -filter "CONNECTION.IP.EQ(10.1.1.1)"
nstrace: started, filter matches either side = 10.1.1.1

What it means:
- Equivalent to "(SRCIP.EQ X || DSTIP.EQ X)" but shorter.
- Useful when you don't yet know whether the host you're chasing is
  client or server in the conversation.
- The classic "exclude loopback then match host" idiom:
   "CONNECTION.IP.NE(127.0.0.1) && CONNECTION.IP.EQ(10.102.44.111)"

Diagnostics.desc: Match traffic by client (source) IPv6 address. Methods: EQ, NE.
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -filter "CONNECTION.SRCIPv6.EQ(2001:db8:0:0:1::1)"
nstrace: started, filter matches IPv6 source 2001:db8::1::1

What it means:
- IPv6 form of SRCIP. Address is parsed as a single hex literal —
  do NOT enclose in brackets.
- Methods limited to EQ / NE — no subnet matching.

Diagnostics.desc: Match traffic by destination IPv6 address. Methods: EQ, NE.
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -filter "CONNECTION.DSTIPv6.EQ(2001:db8::10)"
nstrace: started, filter matches IPv6 destination 2001:db8::10

What it means:
- IPv6 form of DSTIP. Same usage rules as DSTIP — typical use is to
  catch traffic destined to a v6 VIP or backend server.

Diagnostics.desc: Match traffic where IPv6 address (source OR destination) equals the value. Methods: EQ, NE.
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -filter "CONNECTION.IPv6.EQ(2001:db8::10)"
nstrace: started, filter matches either side = 2001:db8::10

What it means:
- Combined SRCIPv6 || DSTIPv6 in a single qualifier.

Diagnostics.desc: Match traffic by client (source) TCP/UDP port. Methods: EQ, NE, GT, GE, LT, LE, BETWEEN.
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -filter "CONNECTION.SRCPORT.EQ(443)"
nstrace: started, filter matches source port 443

# BETWEEN form:
> start nstrace -filter "CONNECTION.SRCPORT.BETWEEN(49152,65535)"
nstrace: started, filter matches ephemeral source ports

What it means:
- Use BETWEEN to capture an ephemeral port range — handy when chasing
  client-initiated reconnect storms.
- Most operational filtering uses DSTPORT (server port); SRCPORT is
  rarely useful unless investigating outbound flows.

Diagnostics.desc: Match traffic by destination TCP/UDP port. Methods: EQ, NE, GT, GE, LT, LE, BETWEEN.
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -filter "CONNECTION.DSTPORT.EQ(443)"
nstrace: started, filter matches destination port 443

# OR-pattern for HTTP + HTTPS:
> start nstrace -filter "CONNECTION.DSTPORT.EQ(80) || CONNECTION.DSTPORT.EQ(443)"
nstrace: started, filter matches HTTP or HTTPS

What it means:
- Most useful per-port filter — catches all traffic terminating on
  the named service port (VIP listener or backend server port).
- Combine with -link ENABLED to also capture the corresponding
  back-end leg of each matched flow.

Diagnostics.desc: Match traffic where TCP/UDP port (source OR destination) equals the value. Methods: EQ, NE, GT, GE, LT, LE, BETWEEN.
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -filter "CONNECTION.PORT.EQ(443)"
nstrace: started, filter matches either side port = 443

What it means:
- Equivalent to (SRCPORT.EQ X || DSTPORT.EQ X). Convenient when you
  want any conversation involving the named port, regardless of
  direction.

Diagnostics.desc: Match traffic on a specific VLAN ID. Methods: EQ, NE, GT, GE, LT, LE, BETWEEN.
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -filter "CONNECTION.VLANID.EQ(20)"
nstrace: started, filter matches VLAN 20

What it means:
- Useful on multi-tenant ADCs to isolate one customer's flows by
  their dedicated VLAN.
- VLAN 0 is the native (untagged) VLAN.

Diagnostics.desc: Match traffic by internal PCB (Protocol Control Block) ID. Methods: EQ, NE, GT, GE, LT, LE, BETWEEN.
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -filter "CONNECTION.CONNID.EQ(123456)"
nstrace: started, filter matches PCB 123456

What it means:
- The PCB ID uniquely identifies a single TCP connection on the
  ADC. Pull it from "show ns connectiontable" and feed into the
  filter to capture exactly one conversation end-to-end.

Diagnostics.desc: Match traffic handled by a specific Packet Processing Engine (PE) core. Methods: EQ, NE, GT, GE, LT, LE, BETWEEN.
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -filter "CONNECTION.PPEID.EQ(0)"
nstrace: started, filter matches PE-0 only

What it means:
- Multi-PE ADCs distribute connections across cores. Use PPEID to
  capture only flows handled by one specific PE — useful when one
  PE shows abnormal CPU or drops in "stat ns -detail".
- Get PE count from "show ns -detail" or "shell ps aux | grep ppe".

Diagnostics.desc: Match traffic by NetScaler service name. Methods: EQ, NE, CONTAINS, STARTSWITH, ENDSWITH.
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -filter "CONNECTION.SVCNAME.EQ(\"svc-web1\")"
nstrace: started, filter matches service "svc-web1"

# CONTAINS (fuzzy) form:
> start nstrace -filter "CONNECTION.SVCNAME.CONTAINS(\"web\")"
nstrace: started, filter matches any service whose name contains "web"

What it means:
- SVCNAME refers to the NAME of a configured "service" object, not
  a free-form text. Find names with "show service".
- String values must be in escaped double-quotes inside the outer
  filter quotes: "CONNECTION.SVCNAME.EQ(\"svc1\")".
- Pair with -link ENABLED to also capture the corresponding client
  (VIP-side) leg of every matched server-side flow.

Diagnostics.desc: Match traffic that hits a specific LB virtual server. Methods: EQ, NE, CONTAINS, STARTSWITH, ENDSWITH.
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -filter "CONNECTION.LB_VSERVER.NAME.EQ(\"lb-web-prod\")"
nstrace: started, filter matches LB vserver "lb-web-prod"

# Capture both sides of each matched flow:
> start nstrace -size 0 -filter "CONNECTION.LB_VSERVER.NAME.EQ(\"lb-web-prod\")" -link ENABLED

What it means:
- Filters on the LB vserver name (the front-end VIP container, not
  the back-end services). Find names with "show lb vserver".
- The most common front-end-troubleshooting filter — catches every
  client connection landing on the named VIP.
- "-link ENABLED" follows each client leg to its corresponding
  server leg through the load-balancing decision.

Diagnostics.desc: Match traffic that hits a specific Content Switching virtual server. Methods: EQ, NE, CONTAINS, STARTSWITH, ENDSWITH.
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -filter "CONNECTION.CS_VSERVER.NAME.EQ(\"cs-edge-https\")"
nstrace: started, filter matches CS vserver "cs-edge-https"

What it means:
- For Content-Switching deployments where a CS vserver fronts
  multiple LB vservers. Use CS_VSERVER.NAME to capture before the
  CS-policy decision; use LB_VSERVER.NAME to capture after.

Diagnostics.desc: Match traffic on a specific physical / logical interface. Methods: EQ, NE.
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -filter "CONNECTION.INTF.EQ(\"1/1\")"
nstrace: started, filter matches interface 1/1

What it means:
- Interface ID syntax is "slot/port" (e.g. 1/1, 0/1, LA/1 for LACP).
- Useful when you need to confirm whether traffic is even arriving
  on a specific NIC — combine with "stat interface" packet counters.

Diagnostics.desc: Match traffic by service type (HTTP, SSL, TCP, UDP, FTP, etc.). Methods: EQ, NE.
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -filter "CONNECTION.SERVICE_TYPE.EQ(SSL)"
nstrace: started, filter matches SSL services

# Valid service-type literals:
#  SVC_HTTP | FTP | TCP | UDP | SSL | SSL_BRIDGE | SSL_TCP | NNTP
#  RPCSVR | RPCSVRS | RPCCLNT | SVC_DNS | ADNS | SNMP | RTSP
#  DHCPRA | ANY | MONITOR | MONITOR_UDP | MONITOR_PING | SIP_UDP
#  SVC_MYSQL | SVC_MSSQL | FIX | SSL_FIX | PKTSTEER | SVC_AAA
#  SERVICE_UNKNOWN

What it means:
- Service type is the protocol assigned to each LB/CS vserver and
  service. Filter to a type to catch all conversations of that
  protocol regardless of port (e.g. SSL spans 443, 8443, 9443…).

Diagnostics.desc: Match traffic in a specific Traffic Domain. Methods: EQ, NE, GT, GE, LT, LE, BETWEEN.
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -filter "CONNECTION.TRAFFIC_DOMAIN_ID.EQ(10)"
nstrace: started, filter matches traffic domain 10

What it means:
- Traffic Domains provide L3 segmentation on a single ADC (overlapping
  IP address spaces between domains).
- TD 0 is the default. Use this filter on multi-tenant ADCs that
  separate tenants by Traffic Domain rather than by VLAN.

Diagnostics.desc: Capture full-size packets to/from a host while excluding loopback (which would otherwise fill the trace with health-monitor noise).
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -size 0 -filter "CONNECTION.IP.NE(127.0.0.1) && CONNECTION.IP.EQ(10.102.44.111)"
nstrace: started, full capture for host 10.102.44.111 (loopback excluded)

What it means:
- The 127.0.0.0/8 noise comes from internal monitor probes and
  in-kernel housekeeping — almost always uninteresting and very
  high-volume.
- This idiom keeps the .cap file small and focused on real client
  ↔ server traffic for one host.

Diagnostics.desc: Capture all HTTP and HTTPS traffic terminating on the ADC.
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -size 0 -filter "CONNECTION.DSTPORT.EQ(443) || CONNECTION.DSTPORT.EQ(80)"
nstrace: started, capturing HTTP + HTTPS traffic, full size

What it means:
- Common starting point when "the website is slow" — captures every
  connection landing on standard web ports.
- Pair with -link ENABLED to also pull in the back-end leg of every
  matched front-end flow.

Diagnostics.desc: Capture full back-end traffic for a service AND its corresponding client-side traffic on the same trace.
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -size 0 -filter "CONNECTION.SVCNAME.EQ(\"svc-web1\")" -link ENABLED
nstrace: started, capturing svc-web1 with linked client side

What it means:
- -link ENABLED tells nstrace: "for every matched server-side flow,
  also include the client-side flow that produced it" (and vice
  versa).
- Without -link, you only see one half of every conversation, which
  makes diagnosis nearly impossible for back-end-only filters.
- This is THE canonical filter for diagnosing slow back-end services.

Diagnostics.desc: Capture all client-side traffic to an LB virtual server plus the corresponding back-end (server-side) traffic.
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -size 0 -filter "CONNECTION.LB_VSERVER.NAME.EQ(\"lb-web-prod\")" -link ENABLED
nstrace: started, capturing both legs of every flow on lb-web-prod

What it means:
- Use this when troubleshooting a single VIP end-to-end.
- The capture includes the SSL handshake, HTTP traffic, and the
  selected back-end server's responses.
- Combine with "-capsslkeys ENABLED" to also export SSL session
  keys so Wireshark can decrypt the flows.

Diagnostics.desc: Combine multiple expressions with parentheses to capture either an exact client tuple OR any traffic to a destination port.
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -size 0 -filter "(CONNECTION.SRCIP.EQ(10.1.1.1) && CONNECTION.SRCPORT.EQ(80)) || CONNECTION.DSTPORT.EQ(443)"
nstrace: started, complex OR/AND filter

What it means:
- Group sub-expressions in parens; combine groups with || or &&.
- Demonstrates: "(client side specific) OR (any HTTPS)".
- Operator precedence: && binds tighter than || — always parenthesise
  for clarity.

Diagnostics.desc: Long-running capture with file rotation — 24 files × 3600 sec each = 24 hours of rolling capture history.
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -size 0 -nf 24 -time 3600 -filter "CONNECTION.IP.EQ(10.1.1.1)"
nstrace: started, rotating 24 files of 3600s each

What it means:
- -nf 24    : keep at most 24 trace files in the rotation.
- -time 3600: each file covers 3600 sec (1 hour) of capture.
- Total = 24 hr rolling window. New file replaces the oldest.
- Use when chasing an intermittent issue that happens "sometime
  overnight" — leave running, retrieve files after the event.

Diagnostics.desc: Capture into separate trace files per interface — easier to analyse asymmetric / per-NIC issues. Only valid with default cap format.
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -perNIC ENABLED -filter "CONNECTION.DSTPORT.EQ(443)"
nstrace: started, per-interface trace files

# Resulting files (one per active NIC):
# /var/nstrace/ns_00001_0_1.cap   ← interface 0/1
# /var/nstrace/ns_00001_1_1.cap   ← interface 1/1
# /var/nstrace/ns_00001_LA_1.cap  ← LACP channel LA/1

What it means:
- Default behaviour interleaves all packets into one file regardless
  of incoming interface — annoying when investigating per-NIC drops.
- -perNIC ENABLED produces one file per interface so each .cap can
  be opened separately in Wireshark.

Diagnostics.cmd: start nstrace -size 0 -filter <expression> -link ENABLED -skipLocalSSH ENABLED -capsslkeys ENABLED -capdroppkt ENABLED
Diagnostics.desc: Recommended "kitchen-sink" capture template: full size, link client and server legs, exclude SSH session noise, export SSL keys for Wireshark decryption, and include dropped packets.
Diagnostics.example:
> start nstrace -size 0 \
        -filter "CONNECTION.LB_VSERVER.NAME.EQ(\"lb-web-prod\")" \
        -link ENABLED \
        -skipLocalSSH ENABLED \
        -capsslkeys ENABLED \
        -capdroppkt ENABLED
nstrace: started, full diagnostic capture for lb-web-prod

What it means:
- -size 0           : full packet capture (no truncation).
- -link ENABLED     : also capture the linked back-end / front-end leg.
- -skipLocalSSH ENABLED : exclude your management SSH from the trace
                          (otherwise you capture yourself!).
- -capsslkeys ENABLED   : export the SSL master keys to ns_sslkey.log
                          so Wireshark can decrypt TLS in the trace.
- -capdroppkt ENABLED   : also write packets that the ADC dropped —
                          critical for "which ACL/policy is blocking
                          this traffic?" investigations.
- Combine with -nf / -time for long-running captures.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Summary statistics for every load-balancing vserver: hits, requests/sec, established connections, response-time. First place to look when a VIP "isn't getting traffic".
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat lb vserver

LB Vserver Statistics — All
Name        Hits      Requests  Responses  Cur Cli Conn  Cur Svr Conn
lb-web      102,341   102,341   102,000    8             6
lb-api      45,012    45,012    44,902     4             4
lb-mobile   12,003    12,003    11,998     1             1

What it means:
- Aggregate per-VIP counters in one view. Use to identify which
  VIP is taking the most traffic.
- "Cur Cli/Svr Conn" : current connection counts on each leg.
- Hits = Requests — for HTTP. For TCP they may differ if multiple
  TCP segments make up one logical request.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Full counter set for one LB vserver — Hits, HitsRate, RequestsRate, ResponsesRate, EstablishedConn, BytesReceivedRate, AvgTTFB.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat lb vserver lb-web

LB Vserver Statistics for lb-web
        Hits                  : 102,341
        Requests              : 102,341
        Responses             : 102,000
        Current Cli Conn      : 8
        Total Client Conn     : 50,012
        Current Svr Conn      : 6
        Bytes In              : 18.2 MB
        Bytes Out             : 124.6 MB
        Spillover Hits        : 0
        Bound Service Stats:
          svc_web1: hits 25,420
          svc_web2: hits 25,432
          svc_web3: hits 25,510
          svc_web4: hits 25,979

What it means:
- Per-VIP counters drilling into bound service distribution.
- "Spillover Hits 0" is healthy — non-zero means VIP is overloaded
  and traffic is being deflected to backup vserver.
- Service-hit balance shows whether load is evenly spread (it
  should be roughly equal under ROUNDROBIN; deliberately uneven
  under weighted methods).

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Verbose form: also shows per-service-binding hits, surge-queue depth, spillover counters.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat lb vserver lb-web -detail

LB Vserver Detailed Statistics for lb-web
        Hits                  : 102,341
        Pkts Received         : 1,245,902
        Pkts Sent             : 1,290,000
        Avg Response Time     : 12 ms
        Surge Queue Depth     : 0
        Active Transactions   : 8
        Reset Counter:
          Sent to Client      : 12
          Sent to Server      : 0
        Persistence Counter:
          Cookie Insert       : 102,341
          Cookie Read         : 95,210

What it means:
- Detailed view including packet-level counters and surge queue.
- "Surge Queue Depth" : connections queued waiting for an LB slot.
  Sustained > 0 means the VIP is overloaded.
- "Reset to Client" : how often the ADC TCP-RST'd a client. High
  values indicate timeouts or surge-protection drops.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: One-line-per-vserver summary across the whole appliance (matches the GUI Dashboard view).
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example: Total_Req: 1.5M  Total_Resp: 1.5M  Client_Conn: 450

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Content-switching vserver counters — Hits per CS vserver. The "front door" for many deployments; check this before the LB vservers.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat cs vserver

CS Vserver Statistics — All
Name              Hits        Requests   Cur Cli Conn  Cur Svr Conn
cs-edge-https     1.5M        1.5M       145           120
cs-internal-api   45,012      45,012     8             8

What it means:
- Per-CS-vserver hits. Compare to LB-vserver hits to see how the
  CS routing decisions distribute traffic to back-end LB-vservers.
- A CS vserver with low hits but a high-priority policy that should
  be matching often = the policy expression is wrong.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Single CS vserver detail incl. policy-hit breakdown.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat cs vserver cs-edge-https

CS Vserver Statistics for cs-edge-https
        Hits                  : 1,520,000
        Requests              : 1,520,000
        Responses             : 1,519,500
        Current Client Conn   : 145
        Total Client Conn     : 502,000
        Bound Policy Stats (top):
          cs_pol_mobile  : 450,012
          cs_pol_api     : 120,003
          cs_pol_admin   : 405

What it means:
- Per-CS-vserver counters with policy-hit breakdown.
- Top policies by hits help confirm traffic shape — a policy with
  zero hits over a long uptime is misconfigured.
- Sum of policy hits plus default-LB hits should equal Hits.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Per-CS-policy hit counts. Use this to confirm which policy is steering traffic to which target LB vserver.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat cs policy

CS Policy Statistics — All
Policy Name        Hits        Last Hit Time
cs_pol_mobile      450,012     2026-05-01 11:22:18
cs_pol_api         120,003     2026-05-01 11:22:17
cs_pol_admin       405         2026-05-01 09:14:22
cs_pol_geo_eu      0           never                  ← unused

What it means:
- All CS policies, sorted by hit count.
- "Last Hit Time : never" is a flag: either the policy expression
  matches nothing, or the policy is bound but its priority lets
  another policy win first.
- Use to clean up stale policies after a feature retirement.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Single CS policy hits + bind-point counters.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat cs policy cs_pol_mobile

Policy Name : cs_pol_mobile
        Rule        : HTTP.REQ.HEADER("User-Agent").CONTAINS("Mobile")
        Action      : cs_act_mobile
        Hits        : 450,012
        Undef Hits  : 0
        First Hit   : 2026-04-25 09:00:00
        Last Hit    : 2026-05-01 11:22:18

What it means:
- Per-policy detail. "Undef Hits" : evaluation errors (e.g. header
  not present in a non-HTTP request).
- High Undef Hits hint at a too-broad bind point — the policy is
  evaluating on traffic where the rule grammar can't apply.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: SSL-offload vserver counters — TLS handshakes, alerts, session-reuse rate. Useful when client TLS issues show as "no hits on the LB".
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat ssl vserver vs_https

SSL Vserver Statistics for vs_https
        SSL Sessions            : 8,502
        Total Handshakes        : 145,021
        Handshake Success       : 144,989
        Handshake Failures      : 32
        Session Reuses          : 102,341
        SNI Connections         : 145,012
        Cert Errors:
          Verify Failures       : 12
          Expired Certs         : 0
          Untrusted Certs       : 12

What it means:
- Per-SSL-vserver counters. Failure / total handshake ratio under
  1% is normal.
- "Verify Failures: 12, Untrusted Certs: 12" : 12 clients tried
  with a cert the ADC didn't trust (probably mTLS misconfiguration).
- Session Reuses : how many resumptions skipped a full handshake —
  high ratio = good.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: AAA vservers — auth attempts, success/fail counts.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat aaa vserver

AAA Vserver Statistics — All
Name             Hits      Auth Success  Auth Failure  Active Sessions
auth_vs_main     45,012    44,890        122           45
auth_vs_partner  12,003    11,998        5             12

What it means:
- Per-AAA-vserver: total auth attempts, success/failure split,
  current active sessions.
- Failure ratio jumping over 5% = either an LDAP/RADIUS backend
  problem OR a credential-stuffing attack. Drill into ns.log for
  failure reasons.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Gateway / VPN vserver counters — connected users, ICA proxy state, hits.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat vpn vserver

VPN Vserver Statistics — All
Name             Hits      Active Sessions  Total Sessions  Failed Logins
gw-rwa-corp      45,012    45              50,012           122
gw-rwa-partner   12,003    12              12,003           5

What it means:
- NetScaler Gateway / SSL-VPN per-vserver counters.
- "Active Sessions" : currently logged-in users. Compare with the
  vpn-vserver's MaxLogin value; a sustained ratio near 100% means
  capacity is exhausted.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: GSLB vserver counters — DNS query hits per site/vserver. If a site is starved, check here first.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example: GSLB_VS: Req: 12050, Resp: 12050, AvgRespTime: 15ms

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Single GSLB vserver detail (per-IP hits, MEP status).
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat gslb vserver GSLB_VS

GSLB Vserver Statistics for GSLB_VS
        DNS Requests           : 12,053
        DNS Responses          : 12,053
        Avg Response Time      : 15 ms
        LB Method              : RTT
        Bound GSLB Services:
          GSLB_SVC_Web1 (DC_London)   : Hits 6,201
          GSLB_SVC_Web2 (DC_NewYork)  : Hits 5,852

What it means:
- DNS-level counters for the GSLB vserver. Hits = number of DNS
  queries that returned an answer.
- Service hits show where GSLB sent clients — RTT / static-proximity
  methods may produce uneven distribution by design (most clients
  closer to one DC).

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Cache-redirection vserver counters.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat cr vserver

Cache Redirection Vserver Statistics — All
Name        Hits      Cache Hits  Origin Hits  Bypass Hits
cr-vs-1     50,432    35,012      14,901       519

What it means:
- Per-CR-vserver counters split by destination decision:
  - Cache Hits : sent to cache server (e.g. Squid).
  - Origin Hits : sent to origin (cache miss / non-cacheable).
  - Bypass Hits : skipped CR entirely (matched a bypass policy).
- High Origin/Cache ratio = cache miss rate is bad — review
  cacheability rules or cache server health.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: All backend services — hits, current connections, max-clients headroom.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat service

Service Statistics — All
Name        Hits      Cur Conn  Total Conn  Throughput
svc_web1    25,420    8         12,005      45 Mbps
svc_web2    25,432    8         12,003      44 Mbps
svc_web3    25,510    7         12,002      45 Mbps
svc_web4    25,979    9         12,401      46 Mbps

What it means:
- Per-back-end-service counters. Even distribution = healthy
  load-balancing.
- "Throughput" is per-service bandwidth; sum should approximate
  the LB-vserver's throughput.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Per-service detail. Cross-check vserver hits against backend hits to spot policy-stuck or surge-queue issues.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat service svc_web1

Service Statistics for svc_web1
        State                  : UP
        Hits                   : 25,420
        Current Conn (Cli/Svr) : 8 / 8
        Total Conn             : 12,005
        Bytes In               : 4.5 MB
        Bytes Out              : 31.2 MB
        Avg SRTT (ms)          : 12
        Last Reason for State  : Probe Successful (HTTP 200)

What it means:
- Per-service detail. "Avg SRTT" : smoothed round-trip time to the
  back-end — if it jumps suddenly the back-end has a network or
  CPU issue.
- "Last Reason for State" tells you exactly why the service is
  UP/DOWN — invaluable for health-check tuning.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Service-group level counters; aggregates members.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat servicegroup

Service Group Statistics — All
Name        Members  UP  Hits      Throughput
sg_web      4        4   102,341   180 Mbps
sg_api      3        2   45,012    35 Mbps   ← one member DOWN

What it means:
- Per-service-group view. "Members / UP" = total/healthy ratio.
- A discrepancy (3 members, 2 UP) is a health-check failure on one
  back-end — drill into "stat servicegroup <name>" or
  "show servicegroup <name>" to find which.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Per-service-group counters incl. each member's hits.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat servicegroup sg_web

Service Group Statistics for sg_web
        Members                : 4 (UP: 4, DOWN: 0)
        Hits                   : 102,341
        Total Conn             : 50,012
        Per-member breakdown:
          192.168.1.10:443  Hits 25,420  Cur Conn 8
          192.168.1.11:443  Hits 25,432  Cur Conn 8
          192.168.1.12:443  Hits 25,510  Cur Conn 7
          192.168.1.13:443  Hits 25,979  Cur Conn 9

What it means:
- Drill-down with per-member counters. Helps confirm balanced
  distribution (or deliberately weighted skew).
- A member with disproportionately high hits + connections might
  be the only one not honouring persistence cookies — investigate.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Monitor probe stats — probes sent, failures. Failing monitors → service marked DOWN → vserver shows zero hits.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat lb monitor mon_health

LB Monitor Statistics for mon_health
        Type                   : HTTP
        Probes Sent            : 1,250,000
        Probes Successful      : 1,249,901
        Probes Failed          : 99
        Last Probe State       : Success (HTTP 200)
        Avg Probe Time         : 8 ms

What it means:
- Per-monitor probe counters. Failure ratio < 0.1% normal.
- Avg Probe Time : how long the back-end takes to respond to the
  health check. Sudden increase = back-end performance trouble.
- A growing "Probes Failed" count, even if services stay UP, is
  an early-warning indicator.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Service config + UP/DOWN state + last state-change. The "why is this DOWN" command.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> show service svc_web1

Name             : svc_web1
        IP             : 192.168.1.10
        Port           : 443
        Protocol       : SSL
        State          : UP
        Last State Change : Wed May  1 09:00:00 2026
        Bound Vservers : lb-web, cs-edge-https
        Bound Monitors :
          tcp_default     : UP, last probe 2 sec ago
          mon_health      : UP, last probe 4 sec ago
        Current Conn   : 8 (cli) / 8 (svr)
        Cookie Stats   : insert 12,401  read 11,890

What it means:
- Per-service config + state snapshot. "Last State Change" tells
  you if the service is freshly recovered or has been stable.
- Bound monitors show ALL probes that must pass for UP state.
- "Cookie Stats" : how many persistence cookies this service has
  inserted vs how many returning clients used one.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Service-group config + per-member state.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> show servicegroup sg_web

Name             : sg_web
        Protocol       : SSL
        Maxconn        : 0 (unlimited)
        Bound Members  :
          192.168.1.10:443  State UP   weight 1
          192.168.1.11:443  State UP   weight 1
          192.168.1.12:443  State UP   weight 1
          192.168.1.13:443  State DISABLED  ← admin disabled
        Bound Monitors :
          tcp_default
          mon_health

What it means:
- Per-servicegroup config + member list with per-member state.
- "DISABLED" = administratively out (someone ran "disable" on it).
  "DOWN" = monitor failed.
- Use to confirm a maintenance disable happened on the right
  member before re-enabling.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Rewrite-policy hit counts. Confirms whether a rewrite is firing on the affected vserver.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat rewrite policy

Rewrite Policy Statistics — All
Policy Name           Hits        Undef Hits  Last Hit
pol_xff               1,245,012   0           2026-05-01 11:22:18
pol_strip_server      1,245,000   0           2026-05-01 11:22:18
pol_hsts              1,245,000   0           2026-05-01 11:22:18
pol_old_unused        0           0           never

What it means:
- All rewrite policies and their hit counts.
- Three top policies firing on every request = expected (XFF
  injection, server banner strip, HSTS header on every response).
- Zero-hit policy : either retired traffic pattern, or rule never
  matches (worth reviewing).

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Single rewrite policy detail.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat rewrite policy pol_xff

Policy Name : pol_xff
        Rule         : HTTP.REQ.IS_VALID
        Action       : act_xff
        Hits         : 1,245,012
        Undef Hits   : 0
        First Hit    : 2026-04-15 09:00:00
        Last Hit     : 2026-05-01 11:22:18

What it means:
- Per-policy detail.
- Rule HTTP.REQ.IS_VALID matches every well-formed HTTP request
  (i.e. fires on everything). Hits ~ requests.
- Combine with "stat rewrite action" to confirm action-level
  counters match.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Responder-policy hits — useful when expected traffic is being silently responded-to or dropped.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat responder policy

Responder Policy Statistics — All
Policy Name              Hits     Last Hit
pol_block_admin          1,250    2026-05-01 11:01:14
pol_geo_block_kr         412      2026-05-01 10:14:08
pol_throttle_login       12,403   2026-05-01 11:21:55
pol_decommissioned       0        never

What it means:
- All responder policies. Useful to spot when a block rule starts
  firing more aggressively than usual (sign of a probing campaign).
- pol_throttle_login firing 12k times = legitimate brute-force
  defence working as designed.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Single responder policy detail.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat responder policy pol_throttle_login

Policy Name : pol_throttle_login
        Rule         : sys.check_limit("limit_login")
        Action       : DROP
        Hits         : 12,403
        Undef Hits   : 0
        First Hit    : 2026-04-30 09:00:00
        Last Hit     : 2026-05-01 11:21:55

What it means:
- Per-policy detail. Hits = times the rate-limit was exceeded.
- DROP action silently kills the connection. Compare with RESET
  (TCP RST) or RESPONDWITH (custom error page).

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: AppFW-policy hit counts + violations.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat appfw policy appfw_pol_web

Policy Name : appfw_pol_web
        Rule         : HTTP.REQ.IS_VALID
        Profile      : appfw_pro_web
        Hits         : 1,245,392
        Block Count  : 142
        Top Block Reasons:
          SQLInjection         : 78
          CrossSiteScripting   : 31
          StartURL             : 18

What it means:
- AppFW policy stats with breakdown of WHY requests were blocked.
- Hits = total inspections. Block Count = of those, how many
  triggered an action.
- Sustained high SQLi/XSS = tune profile relaxations OR you have
  legitimate attackers being properly blocked.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: App-QoE policy hits (priority queueing, surge protection).
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat appqoe policy

AppQoE Policy Statistics — All
Policy Name       Hits      Action       Surge Drops
pol_priority_paid  450,012  PRIORITY_HIGH  0
pol_throttle_free  1,200,000 PRIORITY_LOW  240

What it means:
- AppQoE = Application Quality of Experience: prioritises traffic
  classes during overload.
- Surge Drops on the LOW-priority class = ADC was overloaded and
  shed lower-priority traffic to protect the higher-priority class.
  Working as designed.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Shows aggregate ACL hit counters for IPv4 ACLs: packets allowed, packets denied, and total hits since last counter reset — use with "stat ns acl6" for IPv6.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat ns acl

ACL Statistics:
  ACL Hits              : 1,234,567
  ACL Misses            : 45,678
  Packets Denied (ACL)  : 23,456
  Packets Allowed (ACL) : 1,211,111

# Per-ACL stats (use name filter):
> stat ns acl -name BLOCK_EXTERNAL
  Packets Denied : 23,456   Hits: 23,456

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Global IPv6 ACL hit counters.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat ns acl6

ACL Statistics — IPv6 Extended
ACL Name             Action  Hits     Drop Rate (pps)
ACL6_BLOCK_RA        DENY    421      0.0
ACL6_ALLOW_LOCAL     ALLOW   45,012   2

What it means:
- IPv6 extended ACL counters — counterpart to "stat ns acl".
- IPv6 ACLs are independent of IPv4; you need a v4 + v6 ACL set
  for dual-stack environments.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Simple-ACL counters (line-rate ACLs).
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat ns simpleacl

Simple ACL Statistics
Source IP        Hits     TTL
10.99.0.5        45       3540s   ← will expire automatically
192.168.99.10    0        7200s

What it means:
- Simple ACLs = fast-path drop rules used for emergency mitigation.
- They auto-expire after their TTL — useful for short-term blocks
  without permanent config changes.
- Hits = how many packets each rule has dropped during its lifetime.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Live real-time view of every vserver's hits-per-second from the NetScaler kernel counter ring. Run from BSD shell (after "shell" command).
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
# shell nsconmsg -K /var/nslog/newnslog -d current -g vserver_hits_rate

Reltime: 0:0:1:0
Index Counter Group / Name                  Value    Rate
    1 vserver_hits_rate (lb-web)            102341   452
    2 vserver_hits_rate (lb-api)            45012    198
    3 vserver_hits_rate (cs-edge-https)     1520000  6700

What it means:
- newnslog binary counter dump filtered to per-vserver hit RATES.
- "Rate" column is per-second. Top vservers by rate help identify
  which VIP is the dominant traffic shaper.
- Use during live troubleshooting — much lower overhead than
  running "stat lb vserver" repeatedly.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Live request-rate view across all vservers.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
# shell nsconmsg -K /var/nslog/newnslog -d current -g requests_rate

Reltime: 0:0:1:0
Index Counter Group / Name              Value      Rate
    1 requests_rate (HTTP)              4,502,000  4502
    2 requests_rate (SSL)               2,101,000  2101

What it means:
- Aggregate per-protocol request rate.
- "Rate" is requests per second, smoothed.
- Sudden divergence between HTTP and SSL request rates may indicate
  a TLS-stripping bug or one feature being overloaded.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Continuously watch vserver_hits counters (refreshes every 7 sec, the default newnslog interval).
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
# shell nsconmsg -K /var/nslog/newnslog -d statswt0 -g vsvr_hits | tail -10

Time Sample Counter Group / Name      Value
2026-05-01 11:00:00  vsvr_hits (lb-web)  102000
2026-05-01 11:01:00  vsvr_hits (lb-web)  102341
2026-05-01 11:02:00  vsvr_hits (lb-web)  102801
2026-05-01 11:03:00  vsvr_hits (lb-web)  103401

What it means:
- "statswt0" is the historical (time-series) ring of newnslog.
  Each row is a 1-minute sample.
- Use to graph trends over the last few hours / days.
- Combine with "tail -100" or pipe to a CSV for analysis in Excel.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Aggregate vserver hits from the historical newnslog ring buffer — no live capture needed.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
# shell nsconmsg -K /var/nslog/newnslog -s ConLb=2 -d oldconmsg

Sample older counter dump (uses ConLb=2 wider columns):

Index Counter                          Value         Rate
    1 lb_tot_pri_responses_overall     1,245,402     4502
    2 lb_tot_avg_resp_time_ms          12            0
    3 lb_tot_pri_clt_responses         1,245,402     4502

What it means:
- "-s ConLb=2" widens the column display so very long counter
  values aren't truncated.
- "-d oldconmsg" : legacy historical mode (kept for backward
  compatibility scripts).

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Show timestamped vserver counters with explicit sample count — use to correlate hit drops with timestamps in /var/log/ns.log.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
# shell nsconmsg -K /var/nslog/newnslog -d setime -s totalcount=2000 -g vserver

Showing last 2000 vserver-counter samples:
2026-05-01 11:22:18 vserver_hits (lb-web) 102341
2026-05-01 11:22:18 vserver_hits (lb-api) 45012
... (1998 more rows)

What it means:
- "-d setime" prints a fixed number of recent samples (totalcount).
- "-g vserver" filters to vserver counters only.
- Useful for exporting a fixed-size dataset to a forensic file
  during an incident.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Replay total vserver hits from a saved newnslog file (post-incident analysis).
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
# shell nsconmsg -K /var/nslog/newnslog -d past -s ConLb=2 -g vsvr_tot_hits

Past vserver total-hits across all retained samples:
2026-04-30 09:00:00  vsvr_tot_hits (lb-web)  98000
2026-04-30 11:00:00  vsvr_tot_hits (lb-web)  99401
2026-05-01 09:00:00  vsvr_tot_hits (lb-web)  100012

What it means:
- "-d past" : full historical retention from this newnslog file.
- newnslog rotates after a configurable size — older data is in
  /var/nslog/newnslog.0, .1, .2, etc.
- For longer history, point "-K" at a specific older file.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: List every connection landing on a specific VIP — proves traffic is reaching the appliance even when "stat lb vserver" hits look low.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> show ns connectiontable -filter "Vsvr == 10.0.0.10:443"

Source IP:Port            Dest IP:Port            State            Idle
203.0.113.45:54321        10.0.0.10:443           ESTABLISHED      0:00:01
198.51.100.20:62103       10.0.0.10:443           ESTABLISHED      0:00:00
198.51.100.30:42118       10.0.0.10:443           TIME_WAIT        0:00:09

Total connections to 10.0.0.10:443 : 142

What it means:
- Filters live conntable to only flows targeting the named VIP.
- Useful when one specific VIP is suspected of misbehaving — see
  exactly which clients are connected and in what state.
- "TIME_WAIT" entries are normal transient state after close.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Same as above but filtered by vserver name.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> show ns connectiontable -filter "VsvrName == web-vs"

Source IP:Port            Dest IP:Port            State            Idle
203.0.113.45:54321        10.0.0.10:443           ESTABLISHED      0:00:01
... (142 rows)

What it means:
- Same as above but filters by vserver NAME instead of IP — more
  robust if the VIP IP might change or if the vserver is on a
  range / wildcard.
- Filter expressions support && and ||:
    -filter "VsvrName == web-vs && SrcIP == 203.0.113.45"

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Quick BSD-shell variant when you only need a count of in-flight connections for a VIP.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> show ns conntable | grep 10.0.0.10

203.0.113.45:54321  10.0.0.10:443  ESTABLISHED  TCP  0:00:01
198.51.100.20:62103 10.0.0.10:443  ESTABLISHED  TCP  0:00:00
198.51.100.30:42118 10.0.0.10:443  TIME_WAIT    TCP  0:00:09

What it means:
- Quick-and-dirty filtering of conntable via grep — useful in
  scripted contexts where the structured -filter syntax is
  unavailable or harder to compose.
- Less efficient for large tables; prefer "-filter" form for
  routine use.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Aggregate connection-table counters: total flows, surge-queue, half-open.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stat ns connectiontable

Connection Table Statistics
        Current Total Conn         : 8,502
        Total Lifetime Conn        : 145,021
        State Distribution:
          ESTABLISHED              : 7,432
          TIME_WAIT                : 921
          CLOSE_WAIT               : 142
          SYN_SENT                 : 7
        Conn Memory Used           : 14.2 MB / 1.0 GB

What it means:
- Aggregate conntable stats. ESTABLISHED is the dominant state.
- "CLOSE_WAIT" rising = back-end app is closing connections but
  not the local socket — application bug.
- "Conn Memory Used" : guard against running out; busy ADCs
  may need conn-table tuning ("set ns config -maxConn").

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Capture only traffic destined to a single VIP (VSVRIP filter is the cleanest way to isolate one vserver). -size 0 = full packets, -mode TXB = decrypt with private key when available.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> start nstrace -size 0 -filter "VSVRIP == 10.0.0.10 && VSVRPORT == 443" -mode TXB
nstrace started successfully

What it means:
- -size 0 : full packet capture (no truncation). Default 164 bytes.
- -filter : only matching connections are written to the trace file.
- -mode TXB : capture transmitted-buffered packets in addition to RX.
- Files land in /var/nstrace/ as ns_<id>_NN.cap, rotated every 1h
  by default (see -nf and -time flags).

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Stop the running capture; produces a .cap in /var/nstrace/.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> stop nstrace

nstrace: stopped, captured 128394 packets, 0 dropped
Trace file: /var/nstrace/ns_00001.cap

What it means:
- Stops the running capture and seals the .cap files so they can be
  copied off-box (scp /var/nstrace/*.cap user@host:/path/).
- "0 dropped" means no kernel buffer overruns — capture is complete.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Status of any running nstrace capture.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> show nstrace
        State: Running
        Packets captured: 128394
        Packets dropped: 0
        Current file: /var/nstrace/ns_00001.cap
        Filter: CONNECTION.DSTIP.EQ(10.248.1.15)
        Mode: NEW_RX TXB
        Size: 0 (full packet)
        File rotation: 24 files × 3600 sec

What it means:
- Use to monitor an ongoing capture without stopping it.
- "Packets dropped > 0" means the capture buffer is overrunning —
  shrink -size, narrow the -filter, or capture for less time.
- Same command also displays the configured filter so you can confirm
  exactly what is being matched.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Watch ns.log for syslog events tied to a specific VIP (state changes, monitor flaps).
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
# shell tail -f /var/log/ns.log | grep 10.0.0.10

May  1 11:22:01 NS-ADC PPE-0 : default LB SVC_AVAIL Service svc_web1 (10.0.0.10:443) UP
May  1 11:22:08 NS-ADC PPE-0 : default LB SVC_DOWN Service svc_web2 (10.0.0.10:443) DOWN

What it means:
- Live ns.log tail filtered to a single VIP IP — see only events
  related to that vserver.
- Combine multiple greps:
    tail -f ns.log | grep 10.0.0.10 | grep DOWN
- Press Ctrl+C to stop the tail.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Tail the binary newnslog ring (output is binary; pipe through nsconmsg to render).
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
# shell tail -f /var/log/newnslog 2>&1 | strings | head -10

This binary file is best read via nsconmsg.

# Better:
# shell nsconmsg -K /var/log/newnslog -d current -g <pattern>

What it means:
- /var/log/newnslog is BINARY — using "tail -f" prints unreadable
  output. Use "nsconmsg" to decode it instead.
- For live tailing of decoded counters:
    shell nsconmsg -K /var/log/newnslog -d current -g vserver_hits_rate -s reltime=1
  (refreshes every 1 sec).

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Reset ALL appliance counters (vserver hits, services, policies, SSL, etc.). Use to baseline before/after a change.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> clear ns stats
Are you sure you want to clear all statistical counters? [Y/N] Y
Done

> stat ns | head -3
        HTTP Requests/sec     : 0    ← reset
        Throughput (Mbps)     : 0    ← reset

What it means:
- Resets ALL counters (vservers, services, monitors, AppFW, etc.)
  to zero.
- Useful as a clean baseline before reproducing an issue.
- Does NOT affect connection table or running config — only
  statistics.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Clear stats for a single LB vserver only.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> clear lb vserver lb-web
Done

> stat lb vserver lb-web | head -3
        Hits      : 0     ← reset
        Requests  : 0     ← reset

What it means:
- Resets stats for a specific LB vserver only — surgical compared
  to "clear ns stats".
- Useful when diagnosing one VIP's behaviour without disturbing
  baseline counters elsewhere.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Clear stats for a single CS vserver only.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> clear cs vserver cs-edge-https
Done

> stat cs vserver cs-edge-https | head -3
        Hits      : 0     ← reset

What it means:
- Resets the per-CS-vserver hit counters and policy-hit
  breakdowns. Underlying LB-vserver counters untouched.
- Pairs naturally with "clear cs policy" if you want to reset
  per-policy hits at the same time.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Drop all entries from the in-memory connection table — disruptive; resolves stuck flows.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> clear ns connectiontable
Are you sure you want to clear all active connections? [Y/N] Y
Done. 8,502 connections cleared.

What it means:
- DESTRUCTIVE: closes EVERY active TCP/UDP flow on the dataplane.
- Used during emergency conditions where the conntable is
  corrupted or memory-exhausted.
- Production users will see RST on every existing connection;
  re-establish takes a few seconds.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Vserver state (UP / DOWN / OUT OF SERVICE) + bound services and current effective state. A DOWN vserver records zero hits.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> show lb vserver lb-web

Name             : lb-web
        State            : UP
        Effective State  : UP
        IP : 10.0.0.10:443 (SSL)
        Persistence      : COOKIEINSERT
        LB Method        : LEASTCONNECTION
        Bound Services   : 4 (UP: 4)

What it means:
- Per-VIP config + state.
- "Effective State" : computed from individual service health.
  UP requires at least one service UP.
- For deeper service-level detail, follow up with
  "show service <svc>" on each bound service.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Confirm the VIP is configured and ARP-eligible. A typo or missing -arp ENABLED stops all hits cold.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> show ns ip 10.0.0.10

IP                  Type   Mode    State      VLAN  Options
10.0.0.10           VIP    Active  Enabled    100   ARP=ENABLED, MGMTACCESS=DISABLED

What it means:
- Looks up a single IP in the ADC's IP table.
- Type VIP : front-end Virtual IP for an LB/CS/GSLB vserver.
- ARP ENABLED : ADC responds to ARP requests for this IP. Disable
  on float-VIPs in HA-active-active scenarios where another node
  owns the responder role.
- MGMTACCESS DISABLED : VIP doesn't accept management traffic.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Confirm the VIP has an ARP entry — if upstream switch hasn't learned it, traffic never arrives.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> show ns arp 10.0.0.1

IP            HWaddr               IF      VLAN  Type     Age
10.0.0.1      00:1a:1e:11:22:33    1/1     1     DYNAMIC  60s

What it means:
- ARP table entry for the gateway / next-hop of the named VIP.
- Useful when troubleshooting "VIP not reachable" — confirms
  L2 resolution to the upstream router is happening.
- Type DYNAMIC : auto-resolved. STATIC : manually pinned.
- Age : seconds since the entry was last refreshed.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Confirm reverse path back to the client; missing static route = SYN/ACKs blackhole and hits stay low.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> show route 10.0.0.10

Network/Mask        Gateway     Vlan  State  Source   Cost
10.0.0.0/24         (direct)    100   UP     CONNECT  0

What it means:
- Routing decision the ADC would use for the named destination.
- "(direct)" : the destination is on a directly-attached subnet
  (no gateway hop needed).
- For routes via a gateway, the column shows the next-hop IP.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Pull the full config block for the vserver — bindings, policies, persistence, listen policy. Quick sanity check.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> show ns runningconfig | grep -i lb-web

add lb vserver lb-web SSL 10.0.0.10 443
set lb vserver lb-web -lbMethod LEASTCONNECTION -persistenceType COOKIEINSERT
bind lb vserver lb-web svc_web1
bind lb vserver lb-web svc_web2
bind ssl vserver lb-web -certkeyName wildcard.global.com

What it means:
- Filtered runningConfig — only lines mentioning the named vserver.
- Quick way to capture every config line involved in one VIP for
  documentation / change-control purposes.
- Use case-insensitive (-i) so partial matches across naming
  conventions still hit.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: List listen-policies. A misfiring listen-policy on a wildcard vserver siphons hits away from the expected one.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> show ns listenpolicy

Listen Policy : default
        Policy expression  : (none — accept all by default)
        Bound vservers     : (all)

# With explicit policies:
Listen Policy : restrict_internal
        Policy expression  : CLIENT.IP.SRC.IN_SUBNET(10.0.0.0/8)
        Bound vservers     : lb-internal-api

What it means:
- Listen policies decide WHICH vserver responds to a given inbound
  connection. By default, the IP+port match wins.
- Use to enforce CIDR-based access (only internal subnets reach
  this VIP) without ACLs.
- Bound vservers ignore connections that don't match the listen
  expression.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: AppFlow / IPFIX export config. If counters look right but reporting/MAS shows zero, the export side may be broken.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
> show appflow

AppFlow Statistics:
        Total flows exported   : 14,520,341
        Active collectors      : 1
          Collector_1 (10.1.1.50:4739): connected, last sent 2 sec ago
        Active actions         : 1
          AF_ACT_1 (Sample 100%, page tracking)
        Active policies        : 1
          AF_POL_GLOBAL (rule TRUE, hits 14,520,341)

What it means:
- AppFlow exports IPFIX records to external collectors (Splunk,
  Citrix ADM, Plixer Scrutinizer, etc.).
- "ClientSample 100" : every flow exported. Lower percentages
  reduce overhead on the ADC + collector.
- Useful for application-performance monitoring and security
  forensics with full flow + metadata fidelity.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: NITRO REST stat endpoint — JSON response with all counters, suitable for Prometheus/Grafana exporters.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
$ curl -k -u nsroot:secret https://10.10.10.10/nitro/v1/stat/lbvserver/lb-web | jq

{
  "errorcode": 0,
  "message": "Done",
  "lbvserver": [{
    "name"       : "lb-web",
    "totalrequests": "102341",
    "curclntconnections": "8",
    "totalpktssent"     : "1290000"
  }]
}

What it means:
- NITRO is the ADC's REST/JSON management API. Per-vserver stats
  via /stat/lbvserver/<name>.
- "-k" : skip TLS verification (NSIP self-signed). For production,
  add proper trust anchors.
- Output is JSON; pipe through "jq" or jq-equivalent for readable
  formatting.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: NITRO bulk stat for every LB vserver in one call.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
$ curl -k -u nsroot:secret https://10.10.10.10/nitro/v1/stat/lbvserver | jq '.lbvserver[] | .name + " " + .totalrequests'

"lb-web 102341"
"lb-api 45012"
"lb-mobile 12003"
"lb-default 0"

What it means:
- Without a name suffix, returns ALL LB vservers — useful for
  bulk monitoring scripts.
- Pair with jq to extract specific fields: name + hits count
  becomes a clean per-vserver summary.
- Same shape works for other endpoints: /stat/csvserver,
  /stat/service, /stat/servicegroup.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: NITRO stat endpoint for a CS vserver.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
$ curl -k -u nsroot:secret https://10.10.10.10/nitro/v1/stat/csvserver/cs-edge-https | jq

{
  "errorcode": 0,
  "csvserver": [{
    "name"               : "cs-edge-https",
    "totalrequests"      : "1520000",
    "totalresponses"     : "1519500",
    "curclntconnections" : "145"
  }]
}

What it means:
- Same shape as the LB-vserver endpoint but for Content-Switching
  vservers.
- Use for monitoring scripts that want CS-vserver-level metrics
  rather than the underlying LB-vserver split.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: NITRO call returning per-bound-service hit counters alongside the vserver totals.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example:
$ curl -k -u nsroot:secret "https://10.10.10.10/nitro/v1/stat/lbvserver/lb-web?statbindings=yes" | jq

{
  "errorcode": 0,
  "lbvserver": [{
    "name": "lb-web",
    "totalrequests": "102341",
    "lbvserver_service_binding": [
      { "servicename": "svc_web1", "totalrequests": "25420" },
      { "servicename": "svc_web2", "totalrequests": "25432" },
      { "servicename": "svc_web3", "totalrequests": "25510" },
      { "servicename": "svc_web4", "totalrequests": "25979" }
    ]
  }]
}

What it means:
- "?statbindings=yes" includes per-bound-service stats inside the
  vserver response.
- Single API call returns the full picture: VIP-level totals plus
  the load distribution across back-end services.
- Use for dashboards that want to show hit-distribution heatmaps
  per VIP.

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Show real-time per-service statistics (requests, throughput, response time, errors) for a specific load-balancing service.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example: Req: 45021  Resp: 45020  Throughput: 12Mbps

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Show real-time per-vserver statistics (hits, current/max clients, throughput, surge queue) for a specific LB vserver.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example: Vserver: LB_Prod, Req: 5042, Resp: 5042, PktsIn: 120k, PktsOut: 115k

VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.desc: Show real-time per-vserver statistics for a specific Content Switching vserver, including request distribution across target LB vservers.
VIP Hit Counter Troubleshooting.example: Hits: 10540, Requests: 10540, Responses: 10490

System & Status.desc: Dumps the firewall's internal running-state database (cfg, session, interface, HA and dataplane state trees) in a human-readable pretty-printed format — the standard data-collection command PAN TAC asks for alongside a tech-support file when the GUI/CLI config view disagrees with actual dataplane behaviour

cfg.general.hostname: PA-VM

Routing.desc: Performs a Forwarding Information Base (FIB) lookup for a specific destination IP — shows the actual next-hop the dataplane would use
Routing.example:
> show routing fib lookup ip 8.8.8.8

FIB lookup results for ip 8.8.8.8

Dest: 8.8.8.8
Longest matching route:
  0.0.0.0/0 via 10.0.0.1 egress eth1/1 (default-route)
  VR: default

# Confirms the exact forwarding path for a destination IP at the dataplane level.
# Useful for validating routing after a change or debugging traffic drops.
# Distinguish from: show routing route (control plane RIB) — this shows the FIB.

Routing.desc: Display the OSPF link-state database listing all LSA types, ages, and advertising routers
Routing.example:
> show routing protocol ospf database

OSPF database, Area 0.0.0.0
  Type      LS ID         Adv Router    Seq #      Age    Checksum
  router    1.1.1.1       1.1.1.1       0x80000004  412    0x003c4a
  router    2.2.2.2       2.2.2.2       0x80000003  396    0x0027a1
  router    3.3.3.3       3.3.3.3       0x80000005  228    0x005f12
  network   10.0.12.2     2.2.2.2       0x80000002  396    0x00a4b3
  summary   192.168.10.0  2.2.2.2       0x80000001  396    0x00d182

What it means:
- Type "router"  = Router LSA (each router's own links)
- Type "network" = Network LSA (DR-generated for broadcast segments)
- Type "summary" = Summary LSA (ABR inter-area routes)
- Age increments each second; LSAs are refreshed at 1800 s and expire at 3600 s.

Remote Router-ID:     203.0.113.1

Negotiated Capabilities:
  Route Refresh:        yes
  4-byte AS:            yes
  Graceful Restart:     yes  (restart time 120 s)
  Add-Path (Receive):   no
  Add-Path (Send):      no

Prefixes:
  Received:  4200
  Accepted:  4200
  Sent:      12

VPN.desc: Tunnel state summary (up/down, peer, local/remote proxy-IDs)
VPN.example:
Name             Type      Status     Local IP     Remote IP
AWS-VPN          ipsec     up         10.0.1.1     52.10.20.30
Azure-VPN        ipsec     up         10.0.1.1     13.94.55.66
HQ-Branch        ipsec     down       10.0.1.1     203.0.113.55

VPN.desc: Clear IKE phase-1 SAs for a named gateway, forcing full IKE re-negotiation including phase-2; causes a brief VPN outage
VPN.example:
admin@PA-FW-01> show vpn ike-sa gateway AWS-GW
Gateway   Peer-Address     State   Lifetime  Algorithm
AWS-GW    198.51.100.1     active  26432s    AES-256/SHA-256/DH14

admin@PA-FW-01> clear vpn ike-sa gateway AWS-GW
Cleared IKE SA for gateway AWS-GW.

# Phase-1 re-negotiates automatically (~5–15s).
# Traffic drops during re-negotiation — warn users if tunnelling critical services.
# Monitor recovery:
#   show vpn ike-sa gateway AWS-GW  (wait for State: active)

VPN.desc: Clear (delete) all IPsec phase-2 SAs for a named tunnel, forcing an immediate IKE phase-2 re-negotiation
VPN.example:
# Check current SA before clearing:
admin@PA-FW-01> show vpn ipsec-sa tunnel tunnel.1
Gateway  Tunnel        LocalNet         PeerNet          Enc  Hash  SPI-Inbound
AWS-GW   tunnel.1      10.0.0.0/24      172.16.0.0/16    AES  SHA2  0x1a2b3c4d

# Force re-key:
admin@PA-FW-01> clear vpn ipsec-sa tunnel tunnel.1
Cleared IPSec SA for tunnel tunnel.1.

# Phase-2 re-negotiates automatically within a few seconds.
# Use when: encryption counters mismatch, decryption errors, or after changing phase-2 crypto profile.
# To also re-negotiate phase-1: clear vpn ike-sa gateway <gw>

VPN.desc: IKE gateways with peer-address, version, status
VPN.example:
Name        Peer-IP         Version   State    Last error

High Availability.desc: Configures HA in active/passive mode
High Availability.example:
# (configure) set deviceconfig high-availability group 7 mode active-passive
[edit]

High Availability.desc: Configures HA in active/active mode
High Availability.example:
# (configure) set deviceconfig high-availability group 7 mode active-active device-id 0
[edit]

High Availability.desc: Sets the HA peer device IP address
High Availability.example:
# (configure) set deviceconfig high-availability group 7 peer-ip 10.0.0.2
[edit]

High Availability.desc: Sets this device as preferred primary in HA election
High Availability.example:
# (configure) set deviceconfig high-availability group 7 election-option device-priority 90
[edit]

High Availability.desc: Forces device into suspended HA state — use for planned maintenance
High Availability.example: Suspended HA. This device will not take over if peer fails until resumed.

High Availability.desc: Returns a suspended device to functional HA state
High Availability.example: HA state set to functional. Device will participate in HA election.

High Availability.desc: Forces immediate running config sync to the HA peer
High Availability.example: Sync to remote initiated. Job ID: 215. Use 'show jobs id 215' to monitor.

High Availability.desc: Shows the HA failover history including total flap count, time of last flap, and per-link (HA1/HA2) transition counts — useful for diagnosing intermittent HA instability.

> show high-availability flap-statistics

HA1            : 1
HA2            : 2

# Frequent flaps may indicate:
# - Marginal HA1 heartbeat link (check with: show high-availability link-monitoring)
# - Preemption misconfiguration
# - Physical link instability on HA ports

Certificate Management.cmd: request certificate generate signed-by <ca-name> certificate-name <name> name <cn> signed-by <ca>
Certificate Management.desc: Generates a new certificate signed by an internal CA
Certificate Management.example: Generated certificate Web-Server signed by Root-CA. Use 'show certificate Web-Server' to view.

Certificate Management.cmd: request certificate import format <pem|pkcs12> certificate-name <name> file <path>
Certificate Management.desc: Imports an external certificate from PEM or PKCS12 format
Certificate Management.example: Imported certificate Web-Server from file web-server.p12. Use 'show certificate Web-Server' to view.

Certificate Management.desc: Adds a CA certificate to a certificate profile for client cert verification
Certificate Management.example:
# (configure) set shared certificate-profile GP-Profile CA Root-CA
[edit]

Certificate Management.desc: Displays cached certificate revocation status from OCSP/CRL lookups
Certificate Management.example:
Total cached SSL forward-proxy certificates: 1842
Oldest entry: 2026/05/01 14:22:01
Cache utilisation: 38%
Revoked certificate Web-Server. CRL will be updated on next publish.

Certificate Management.cmd: request certificate generate signed-by external certificate-name <name> name <cn> filename <csr-file>
Certificate Management.desc: Generates a Certificate Signing Request (CSR) for signing by a public/external CA — writes the PEM-encoded CSR to <csr-file> on the firewall instead of self-signing with an internal CA.
Certificate Management.example:
> request certificate generate signed-by external certificate-name web-server name www.example.com filename web-server.csr
Success: CSR generated and stored as web-server.csr

# Export the CSR to a workstation for submission to your CA:
> scp export certificate name web-server.csr to admin@10.0.0.5:/tmp/

# After the CA signs it, import the certificate:
> request certificate import format pem certificate-name web-server file <path-to-signed-cert>

Monitoring & Diagnostics.desc: Clears all previously configured packet diagnostic filters and counters on the dataplane — always run this before setting a new filter to avoid capturing stale session data.

# 1. debug dataplane packet-diag clear
# 2. debug dataplane packet-diag set filter match source 10.1.1.1 destination 8.8.8.8

# 4. debug dataplane packet-diag start

        Name        : admin

# (configure) set user-id-agent AD-Agent-01 host 10.0.0.50 port 5007
[edit]

User-ID.desc: Resets the User-ID subsystem and clears all mappings — use with caution
User-ID.example: User-ID daemon reset. All cached mappings dropped. Mappings will repopulate from agents.

User-ID.desc: Lists all members of an AD/LDAP group as known to User-ID
User-ID.example:
Group: cn=Domain Users,cn=Users,dc=acme,dc=local
Member count: 1842
Members:
  ACME\jbloggs
  ACME\asmith
  ...

User-ID.desc: Manually removes a user's IP-to-username mapping from the table
User-ID.example: User ACME\jbloggs logged out from vsys1. IP-User mapping removed.

User-ID.desc: Every known username → IP mapping
User-ID.example:
Vsys    User                Group memberships  Mappings
vsys1   ACME\jbloggs        12                 1 (10.0.2.55)
vsys1   ACME\asmith         12                 1 (10.0.2.100)

User-ID.desc: Who is this IP mapped to right now
User-ID.example:
IP             : 10.0.2.55
User           : ACME\jbloggs
Source         : AD
Idle timeout   : 1582 sec remaining

User-ID.desc: Filter the IP-to-user mapping table by mapping source (which User-ID mechanism learned it).
User-ID.example:
admin@PA> show user ip-user-mapping all type GP

IP              Vsys   From   User                IdleTimeout(s)  MaxTimeout(s)
10.0.2.100      vsys1  GP     ACME\asmith         3204            10800

# <type> values: UIA (Windows User-ID agent), UID (PAN-OS integrated agent),
# GP (GlobalProtect), XMLAPI, CP (Authentication/Captive Portal), SSO,
# SYSLOG, TS (Terminal Server agent), RADIUS, EDIR, NTLM, UNKNOWN.

User-ID.cmd: show user ip-user-mapping all option count
User-ID.desc: Return just the total number of active IP-to-user mappings (no table) — quick capacity/health check against the platform limit.
User-ID.example:
admin@PA> show user ip-user-mapping all option count

Total: 1452 in-use mappings

User-ID.desc: Show the IP address(es) a specific user is currently mapped to.
User-ID.example:
admin@PA> show user ip-user-mapping user acme\jbloggs

IP              Vsys   From   User                IdleTimeout(s)  MaxTimeout(s)
10.0.2.55       vsys1  AD     ACME\jbloggs        1582            10800

User-ID.desc: All current mappings within a given NetBIOS / AD domain.
User-ID.example:
admin@PA> show user ip-user-mapping domain ACME

IP              Vsys   From   User                IdleTimeout(s)  MaxTimeout(s)
10.0.2.55       vsys1  AD     ACME\jbloggs        1582            10800
10.0.2.100      vsys1  GP     ACME\asmith         3204            10800

User-ID.desc: Same IP-to-user table but read from the management-plane User-ID process (idmgr). Compare with the dataplane copy (show user ip-user-mapping all) to spot MP-to-DP sync gaps.
User-ID.example:
admin@PA> show user ip-user-mapping-mp all

IP              Vsys   From                User                       IdleTimeout(s)  MaxTimeout(s)
10.0.2.55       vsys1  AD                  ACME\jbloggs               1582            10800
10.0.2.100      vsys1  GP                  ACME\asmith                3204            10800

# The MP copy is the master that idmgr distributes to the dataplane;
# policy enforcement uses the DP copy. The two should match.

User-ID.desc: Management-plane lookup for a single IP. Handy when the dataplane shows no mapping but the MP does — points to a MP-to-DP distribution problem rather than a learning problem.
User-ID.example:
admin@PA> show user ip-user-mapping-mp ip 10.0.2.55

IP             : 10.0.2.55
User           : ACME\jbloggs
From           : AD
Idle timeout   : 1582 sec remaining

User-ID.desc: Management-plane mapping table filtered by source type.
User-ID.example:
admin@PA> show user ip-user-mapping-mp all type XMLAPI

IP              Vsys   From    User                IdleTimeout(s)  MaxTimeout(s)
10.0.2.77       vsys1  XMLAPI  ACME\svc-api        600             3600

User-ID.cmd: show user ip-user-mapping-mp all option count
User-ID.desc: Total mapping count on the management plane — compare with the dataplane count to confirm the two planes are in sync.
User-ID.example:
admin@PA> show user ip-user-mapping-mp all option count

Total: 1452 in-use mappings

User-ID.desc: IP + source-port-range to user mappings from the Terminal Server (TS) Agent. Used for multi-user hosts (Citrix / RDS) where many users share one IP and are told apart by source port.
User-ID.example:
admin@PA> show user ip-port-user-mapping all

IP              Vsys   User                  Port Start  Port End
10.4.5.6        vsys1  ACME\tsuser01         20000       20199
10.4.5.6        vsys1  ACME\tsuser02         20200       20399
10.4.5.6        vsys1  ACME\tsuser03         20400       20599

# Each user on the shared TS host gets a unique source-port block; the
# firewall maps a session's source port to the correct user.

User-ID.desc: All port-range-to-user allocations for one Terminal Server IP.
User-ID.example:
admin@PA> show user ip-port-user-mapping ip 10.4.5.6

IP              Vsys   User                  Port Start  Port End
10.4.5.6        vsys1  ACME\tsuser01         20000       20199
10.4.5.6        vsys1  ACME\tsuser02         20200       20399

User-ID.cmd: show user ip-port-user-mapping all option count
User-ID.desc: Total number of active IP-port-user (Terminal Server) mappings.
User-ID.example:
admin@PA> show user ip-port-user-mapping all option count

Total: 240 in-use mappings

User-ID.desc: Management-plane copy of the Terminal Server IP-port-user table. Compare against the dataplane to confirm TS-agent mappings have been pushed to the DP.
User-ID.example:
admin@PA> show user ip-port-user-mapping-mp all

IP              Vsys   User                  Port Start  Port End
10.4.5.6        vsys1  ACME\tsuser01         20000       20199
10.4.5.6        vsys1  ACME\tsuser02         20200       20399

User-ID.desc: Management-plane port-range-to-user allocations for one Terminal Server IP.
User-ID.example:
admin@PA> show user ip-port-user-mapping-mp ip 10.4.5.6

IP              Vsys   User                  Port Start  Port End
10.4.5.6        vsys1  ACME\tsuser01         20000       20199

Sessions & Flows.desc: Shows the dataplane session-rate meter — new sessions/sec, active session count, and per-vsys packet/byte counters used to gauge whether the firewall is approaching its session-setup rate limit
Sessions & Flows.example:
> show session meter

vsys1:
Number of sessions          : 18422
Packets per second           : 96341
New connection establish rate: 2140 cps
KBps                         : 486213

Total (all vsys):
Sessions       : 18422 / 262144 max
Session setup rate : 2140 cps  (platform max ~40000 cps on this model)

What it means:
- "cps" (connections per second) climbing toward the platform max
  during a spike often shows up as new-connection timeouts before
  CPU or session-table exhaustion does — check this first when
  users report "sometimes it's slow to connect."
- Compare against "show session info" for the raw active/max session
  counts without the per-second rate.

Sessions & Flows.desc: Clear all sessions for a given source IP
Terminate a single active session by its session ID; traffic for that flow is dropped immediately
Sessions & Flows.example:
# Find the session ID first:
admin@PA-FW-01> show session id 1024
Session          1024
        c2s flow: source 10.1.1.100 [vsys1], sport 55234
                  dst 8.8.8.8, dport 443, proto 6
                  state ACTIVE, type FLOW, flags 0x88000

# Clear it:
admin@PA-FW-01> clear session id 1024
Session 1024 cleared.

# The client will experience a TCP RST/timeout and must re-establish.
# To clear all sessions from a specific source IP:
#   clear session all filter source 10.1.1.100

Packet Diagnostics.cmd: debug dataplane packet-diag set filter match source <ip> destination <ip>
Packet Diagnostics.example: Packet filter: src=10.0.2.55 dst=8.8.8.8 added. Run 'debug dataplane packet-diag set filter on' to activate.

Packet Diagnostics.cmd: debug dataplane packet-diag set filter on
Packet Diagnostics.example: Packet filter is on. All active filters will match.

Packet Diagnostics.cmd: debug dataplane packet-diag set capture stage receive file rx.pcap
Packet Diagnostics.example: Capture stage 'receive' configured to write to /var/tmp/rx.pcap.

Packet Diagnostics.cmd: debug dataplane packet-diag set capture stage drop file drop.pcap
Packet Diagnostics.example: Capture stage 'drop' configured to write to /var/tmp/drop.pcap.

Packet Diagnostics.cmd: debug dataplane packet-diag set capture on
Packet Diagnostics.desc: Start a packet capture on the PAN-OS dataplane; PCAPs are written to /var/tmp/ and retrieved with tftp/scp or the GUI
Packet Diagnostics.example:
# First set a filter (scope the capture to avoid capturing everything):
admin@PA-FW-01> debug dataplane packet-diag set filter match source 10.1.1.100 destination 8.8.8.8

# Start capture:
admin@PA-FW-01> debug dataplane packet-diag set capture on
Packet capture is on. PCAPs are being written to /var/tmp/.

# Reproduce the issue, then stop capture:
admin@PA-FW-01> debug dataplane packet-diag set capture off

# Retrieve the file:
#   scp admin@<fw-ip>:/var/tmp/pdpcap* ./
# Or via GUI: Monitor > Packet Capture > Export

Packet Diagnostics.cmd: debug dataplane packet-diag set capture off
Packet Diagnostics.desc: Stop the active dataplane packet capture; files remain in /var/tmp/ and can be retrieved via SCP or the GUI
Packet Diagnostics.example:
admin@PA-FW-01> debug dataplane packet-diag set capture off
Packet capture is off. Use 'view-pcap filter-pcap <file>' or scp out.

# View files on the firewall:
admin@PA-FW-01> show capture-files
/var/tmp/pdpcap_0_0_0.pcap  (1.4 MB)
/var/tmp/pdpcap_0_0_1.pcap  (2.1 MB)

# SCP to your workstation:
#   scp admin@10.0.0.1:/var/tmp/pdpcap_0_0_0.pcap ./capture.pcap

# View inline (human-readable summary):
#   view-pcap filter-pcap /var/tmp/pdpcap_0_0_0.pcap

Reading from /var/tmp/rx.pcap, 412 packets:
1  09:30:11.123  10.0.2.55 -> 8.8.8.8  ICMP echo request id=1024 seq=1
2  09:30:11.128  8.8.8.8 -> 10.0.2.55  ICMP echo reply   id=1024 seq=1

Packet Diagnostics.cmd: scp export filter-pcap from rx.pcap to <user>@<host>:/tmp/
Packet Diagnostics.desc: Pull captured pcap off the firewall
Packet Diagnostics.example: scp: copying /var/tmp/rx.pcap to admin@10.0.0.50:/tmp/rx.pcap ... done (412 KB).

Packet Diagnostics.desc: Display the currently configured packet-diag filter criteria and capture stage settings so you can confirm the filter is active before starting a capture
Packet Diagnostics.example:
Packet filter:
    src-ip    : 10.1.1.100
    dst-ip    : 10.2.2.200
    src-port  : any
    dst-port  : any
    proto     : any
    non-ip    : no
  Stages captured:
    receive   : enabled  → file: /var/log/pan/rx.pcap
    drop      : enabled  → file: /var/log/pan/drop.pcap
    transmit  : disabled
