SSL Decryption.desc: Display the currently active SSL/TLS decryption rules from the running policy
SSL Decryption.example:
> show running decryption-policy

Rule 1: "Decrypt-Outbound-SSL"
  From: Trust         To: Untrust
  Source: any         Destination: any
  Service: ssl        Category: any
  Action: decrypt     Type: ssl-forward-proxy
  Certificate Profile: corp-forward-trust
  Profile: default-decryption

Rule 2: "No-Decrypt-Finance"
  From: Trust         To: Untrust
  Source: any         Destination: finance-category
  Action: no-decrypt
  Description: Exempt banking sites from decryption

What it means:
- Rules are evaluated top-down; first match wins.
- "no-decrypt" rules must be above broader decrypt rules for exemptions to apply.

SSL Decryption.cmd: test decryption-policy-match from <src-zone> to <dst-zone> source <src-ip> destination <dst-ip> destination-port <port>
SSL Decryption.desc: Test which SSL/TLS decryption rule applies to a specific traffic flow
SSL Decryption.example:
> test decryption-policy-match from Trust to Untrust source 10.0.1.50 destination 1.2.3.4 destination-port 443

Rule matched: "Decrypt-Outbound-SSL"
  Action:              decrypt
  Type:                ssl-forward-proxy
  Certificate Profile: corp-forward-trust
  Decryption Profile:  default-decryption

# Use this before committing a new decryption policy to confirm that
# traffic is hitting the intended rule (especially exemption rules).

DNS Security.desc: Lists every FQDN address object the firewall has resolved through DNS proxy, along with the resolved IP addresses, record type, remaining TTL, and hit count — the PAN-OS 9.1+ replacement for the deprecated "request system fqdn show", and the fastest way to confirm an FQDN object actually resolved before blaming a security rule.
DNS Security.example:
admin@PA-VM> show dns-proxy fqdn all

Domain               IP/Name          Type  Class  TTL   Hits
------               -------          ----  -----  ---   ----
updates.example.com  203.0.113.10     A     IN     118   42
updates.example.com  203.0.113.11     A     IN     118   17
api.example.com      Not Used

# "Not Used" means the FQDN object isn't referenced by any active
# security rule, so the firewall never queries DNS for it — add it to a
# rule and wait for the next refresh cycle (default every 30 min) to
# populate a real IP.

Key Generation.desc: Generate an ECC private key using the prime256v1 curve
Key Generation.example: (Elliptic curve P-256 private key written to ec.key — no output on success)

Key Generation.desc: Generate an ECC private key using the secp384r1 (P-384) curve
Key Generation.example:
$ openssl ecparam -name secp384r1 -genkey -noout -out ec.key
$ ls -lh ec.key
-rw------- 1 user user 306 Jan  1 12:00 ec.key
# No stdout on success — key is written directly to ec.key.
# P-384 provides ~192-bit security, preferred for government/NIST compliance.

Key Generation.desc: Generate an Ed25519 private key — modern, fast signing algorithm
Key Generation.example: (Ed25519 private key written to ed25519.key)

Key Generation.example: mK8xPqR3sT7uV2wX1yZ0aB5cD9eF4gH6iJ8kL2mN0pQ7rS3tU1v

Key Generation.desc: Extract the public key from any private key (RSA, EC, or Ed25519)
Key Generation.example: (Public key extracted from private.key and written to public.key)

Key Generation.desc: Generate 3072-bit RSA key via genpkey (modern API)
Key Generation.example:
$ openssl genpkey -algorithm RSA -pkeyopt rsa_keygen_bits:3072 -out rsa3072.key
.........................+++
.......+++
$ ls -lh rsa3072.key
-rw------- 1 user user 1.7K Jan  1 12:00 rsa3072.key
# 3072-bit RSA provides ~128-bit equivalent security (NIST minimum for 2030+).

Key Generation.desc: Generate password-protected 4096-bit RSA key
Key Generation.example:
$ openssl genpkey -algorithm RSA -pkeyopt rsa_keygen_bits:4096 -aes-256-cbc -out rsa4096.key
Enter PEM pass phrase:
Verifying - Enter PEM pass phrase:
.....................................................................+++
# Key is encrypted with AES-256-CBC using the supplied passphrase.
# Strip passphrase later: openssl rsa -in rsa4096.key -out rsa4096-nopass.key

Key Generation.desc: Generates a P-384 (secp384r1) EC private key offering 192-bit security — required by NSA Suite B and preferred for long-lived TLS certificates and signing keys.
Key Generation.example:
$ openssl genpkey -algorithm EC -pkeyopt ec_paramgen_curve:P-384 -out ec384.key
$ openssl pkey -in ec384.key -text -noout | head -3
Private-Key: (384 bit)
ASN1 OID: secp384r1
NIST CURVE: P-384

Key Generation.desc: Generates a P-521 (secp521r1) EC private key at the highest NIST security level (260-bit) — suitable for root CA keys where maximum longevity is required.
Key Generation.example:
$ openssl genpkey -algorithm EC -pkeyopt ec_paramgen_curve:P-521 -out ec521.key
$ openssl pkey -in ec521.key -text -noout | head -3
Private-Key: (521 bit)
ASN1 OID: secp521r1
NIST CURVE: P-521

Key Generation.desc: X25519 key for modern ECDH
Key Generation.example:
$ openssl genpkey -algorithm X25519 -out x25519.key
$ openssl pkey -in x25519.key -text -noout
X25519 Private-Key:
priv:
    XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX
    XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX
# X25519 (Curve25519 ECDH) is used for key exchange in TLS 1.3.

Key Generation.desc: X448 key (higher security level than X25519)
Key Generation.example:
$ openssl genpkey -algorithm X448 -out x448.key
# X448 (Curve448 ECDH) provides ~224-bit security — stronger than X25519.
# Supported in TLS 1.3 but less widely deployed than X25519.

Key Generation.desc: Generates an Ed448 (Edwards-curve) signing key — a modern alternative to RSA/ECDSA offering strong security with small key sizes, supported in TLS 1.3 certificates.
Key Generation.example:
$ openssl genpkey -algorithm ed448 -out ed448.key
$ openssl pkey -in ed448.key -text -noout
ED448 Private-Key:
priv:
    6c:2a:f8:e9:...(56 bytes)...
pub:
    dc:b0:1e:4f:...(57 bytes)...
# Ed448 keys are 57 bytes; no separate curve parameter needed

Key Generation.desc: List every named EC curve supported by the build
Key Generation.example:
secp112r1 : SECG/WTLS curve over a 112 bit prime field
...
prime256v1: X9.62/SECG curve over a 256 bit prime field
secp384r1 : NIST/SECG curve over a 384 bit prime field
secp521r1 : NIST/SECG curve over a 521 bit prime field
...(40+ curves listed)

Key Generation.desc: Save EC parameters to a file
Key Generation.example:
$ openssl ecparam -name prime256v1 -out ecparams.pem
$ cat ecparams.pem
-----BEGIN EC PARAMETERS-----
BggqhkjOPQMBBw==
-----END EC PARAMETERS-----
# Saves the P-256 curve parameters to a standalone PEM file.
# Used with: openssl ecparam -in ecparams.pem -genkey -noout -out ec.key

Key Generation.desc: Write 256 random bytes to a binary file
Key Generation.example: (256 bytes of cryptographically random data written to random.bin)

Key Generation.desc: Print 16 random bytes hex (32 chars) — token / IV material
Key Generation.example:
$ openssl rand -hex 16
a3f8c21d9b04e765c8f2d91a4b30e587
# Generates 16 cryptographically random bytes and prints 32 hex characters.
# Useful for generating IVs, nonces, API keys, or session tokens.
# For base64 output: openssl rand -base64 32

Key Inspection & Manipulation.desc: Generates a 2048-bit RSA private key in PEM format — the minimum recommended size; prefer 4096-bit for CA keys or genpkey with EC/Ed25519 for new deployments.
Key Inspection & Manipulation.example:
$ openssl genrsa -out server.key 2048
Generating RSA private key, 2048 bit long modulus (2 primes)
..............+++++
.......+++++
e is 65537 (0x010001)

$ ls -lh server.key
-rw------- 1 user user 1.7K Jun 28 10:00 server.key
# Protect with a passphrase: openssl genrsa -aes256 -out server.key 2048

$ openssl genpkey -algorithm RSA -out server.key
..................................................................+++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++*
# Generates a default 2048-bit RSA key using the modern genpkey API.
# Specify size: openssl genpkey -algorithm RSA -pkeyopt rsa_keygen_bits:4096 -out server.key

Key Inspection & Manipulation.desc: Generates a P-256 (prime256v1/secp256r1) EC private key — the most widely deployed TLS curve, offering 128-bit security with broad hardware accelerator support.
Key Inspection & Manipulation.example:
$ openssl genpkey -algorithm EC -pkeyopt ec_paramgen_curve:P-256 -out server.key
$ openssl pkey -in server.key -text -noout | head -3
Private-Key: (256 bit)
ASN1 OID: prime256v1
NIST CURVE: P-256

$ openssl rsa -in server.key -check
RSA key ok
# Verifies the mathematical consistency of an RSA private key.
# "RSA key ok" = valid; error messages indicate a corrupted or malformed key.
# For EC keys: openssl ec -in ec.key -check

Key Inspection & Manipulation.desc: Extracts the public key component from an RSA private key file and writes it in PEM format — needed when sharing the public key for encryption or verification without exposing the private key.
Key Inspection & Manipulation.example:
$ openssl rsa -in server.key -pubout -out server-public.key
writing RSA key

$ cat server-public.key
-----BEGIN PUBLIC KEY-----
MIIBIjANBgkqhkiG9w0BAQEFAAOCAQ8AMIIBCgKCAQEA2a8...
-----END PUBLIC KEY-----

$ openssl rsa -pubin -in server-public.key -text -noout | grep "Public-Key"
Public-Key: (2048 bit)

$ openssl pkey -in server.key -pubout
-----BEGIN PUBLIC KEY-----
MIIBIjANBgkqhkiG9w0BAQEFAAOCAQ8AMIIBCgKCAQEA...
-----END PUBLIC KEY-----
# Prints the public key to stdout — works for RSA, EC, Ed25519, X25519.
# The universal replacement for openssl rsa -pubout and openssl ec -pubout.

Key Inspection & Manipulation.desc: Displays human-readable details of a private key (RSA, EC, or Ed25519/Ed448) including key size, curve OID, and raw key material — works for all modern key types via the unified pkey interface.
Key Inspection & Manipulation.example:
$ openssl pkey -in server.key -text -noout
Private-Key: (2048 bit, 2 primes)
modulus:
    00:c4:2b:8f:9e:...
publicExponent: 65537 (0x10001)
privateExponent:
    1d:7a:...
prime1: ...
prime2: ...
# For EC keys: shows curve OID and private/public point coordinates

Key Inspection & Manipulation.desc: Print full RSA key structure (n, e, d, p, q, primes, CRT coeffs)
Key Inspection & Manipulation.example:
Private-Key: (2048 bit, 2 primes)
modulus:
    00:d4:8a:f2:b1:...
publicExponent: 65537 (0x10001)
privateExponent:
    4e:...
prime1:
    00:...
prime2:
    00:...

Key Inspection & Manipulation.desc: Public modulus of an RSA key — use to cross-check against cert/CSR
Key Inspection & Manipulation.example:
$ openssl rsa -in private.key -noout -modulus
Modulus=D48AF2B1C3E4F5A6789B0C1D2E3F4A5B6C7D8E9F0A1B2C3D4E5F6A7B8C9D0E1F2A3B4C5D6E7F8A9B0C1D2E3F24A8C

# Common use: verify that the private key matches a certificate
$ openssl x509 -noout -modulus -in server.crt | md5sum
$ openssl rsa  -noout -modulus -in private.key | md5sum
# If both MD5 hashes are identical, the key and certificate are a matching pair.

# Also verify a CSR matches:
$ openssl req  -noout -modulus -in server.csr | md5sum

Key Inspection & Manipulation.desc: Print EC key parameters and curve
Key Inspection & Manipulation.example:
Private-Key: (256 bit)
priv:
    87:a4:f3:...
pub:
    04:b8:...
ASN1 OID: prime256v1
NIST CURVE: P-256

Key Inspection & Manipulation.desc: Print the public key for any key type (RSA / EC / Ed25519)

Key Inspection & Manipulation.desc: Convert legacy PEM to unencrypted PKCS#8
Convert + encrypt to PKCS#8 with AES-256-CBC
Key Inspection & Manipulation.example:
$ openssl pkcs8 -topk8 -v2 aes-256-cbc -in server.key -out server-pkcs8-enc.key
Enter Encryption Password:
Verifying - Enter Encryption Password:
$ head -1 server-pkcs8-enc.key
-----BEGIN ENCRYPTED PRIVATE KEY-----
# Converts a traditional RSA key to PKCS#8 format encrypted with AES-256-CBC.
# PKCS#8 encrypted keys are required by Java KeyStore and many frameworks.

Key Inspection & Manipulation.desc: Decrypt a PKCS#8 key back to PEM
Key Inspection & Manipulation.example:
$ openssl pkcs8 -in server-pkcs8-enc.key -out server-plain.key
Enter Password:
$ head -1 server-plain.key
-----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----
# Decrypts a PKCS#8 encrypted private key to an unencrypted PKCS#8 key.
# Remove the password protection for use by services that cannot prompt.

CSR & Certificate Signing.desc: Generate a CSR with all subject fields specified inline
CSR & Certificate Signing.example:
$ openssl req -new -key server.key -out server.csr -subj "/C=GB/ST=London/O=Acme Ltd/CN=example.com"
$ openssl req -in server.csr -noout -subject
subject=C=GB, ST=London, O=Acme Ltd, CN=example.com
# Creates a CSR with all subject fields on the command line.
# Submit server.csr to your CA; they sign it and return the certificate.

CSR & Certificate Signing.desc: Create a self-signed root CA certificate valid for 10 years
CSR & Certificate Signing.example: (Self-signed CA certificate valid for 10 years written to ca.crt)

CSR & Certificate Signing.cmd: openssl x509 -req -in request.csr -CA ca.crt -CAkey ca.key -CAcreateserial -out signed.crt -days 365 -sha256
CSR & Certificate Signing.desc: Sign a CSR with a CA certificate to produce a signed certificate
CSR & Certificate Signing.example:
Signature ok
subject=C = GB, ST = London, O = Acme Corp, CN = example.com
Getting CA Private Key

CSR & Certificate Signing.desc: Generate a self-signed certificate and key pair in one step
CSR & Certificate Signing.example:
Generating a RSA private key
..........+++++
writing to self.key
(Self-signed certificate written to self.crt)

CSR & Certificate Signing.cmd: openssl x509 -req -in req.csr -CA ca.crt -CAkey ca.key -extfile san.ext -out cert.crt -days 365 -sha256
CSR & Certificate Signing.desc: Sign a CSR and include Subject Alternative Names from an extension file
CSR & Certificate Signing.example:
$ openssl x509 -req -in server.csr -CA ca.crt -CAkey ca.key     -extfile san.ext -CAcreateserial -out server.crt -days 365 -sha256
Signature ok
subject=C=GB, ST=London, O=Acme Ltd, CN=example.com
Getting CA Private Key
# san.ext content example:
#   subjectAltName = DNS:example.com, DNS:www.example.com, IP:10.0.0.1
# -CAcreateserial auto-creates ca.srl to track certificate serial numbers.

CSR & Certificate Signing.desc: Generate a CSR with SANs defined in a custom config file
CSR & Certificate Signing.example:
# openssl-san.cnf:
# [req]
# distinguished_name = req_distinguished_name
# req_extensions = v3_req
# [req_distinguished_name]
# [v3_req]
# subjectAltName = @alt_names
# [alt_names]
# DNS.1 = example.com
# DNS.2 = www.example.com
$ openssl req -new -key server.key -out server.csr -config openssl-san.cnf
# Embeds SANs in the CSR — some CAs will honour them; others use their own policy.

CSR & Certificate Signing.desc: Generate CSR with SAN on the command line (no config file)
CSR & Certificate Signing.example: (CSR with SAN written to req.csr — verify with openssl req -in req.csr -text -noout)

CSR & Certificate Signing.desc: Verify the self-signature on a CSR to confirm the private key matches and the CSR is not corrupted
CSR & Certificate Signing.example:
$ openssl req -verify -in server.csr -noout
verify OK

# "verify OK" means the CSR signature is valid — the embedded public key
# matches the private key used to sign the request.

# Failure output:
$ openssl req -verify -in bad.csr -noout
verification failure
34359836488:error:0906D06C:PEM routines:...
# Indicates the CSR was corrupted or the wrong private key was used.

# To also inspect the CSR contents:
#   openssl req -text -noout -in server.csr

CSR & Certificate Signing.desc: CSR content + self-signature verification in one command
CSR & Certificate Signing.example:
verify OK
Certificate Request:
    Data:
        Version: 1 (0x0)
        Subject: C=GB, ST=London, O=Acme Corp, CN=example.com
    Requested Extensions:
            X509v3 Subject Alternative Name:
                DNS:example.com, DNS:*.example.com

CSR & Certificate Signing.cmd: openssl ca -config openssl-ca.cnf -extensions v3_intermediate_ca -days 3650 -in intermediate.csr -out intermediate.crt
CSR & Certificate Signing.desc: Sign an intermediate CA cert with your root
CSR & Certificate Signing.example:
$ openssl ca -config openssl-ca.cnf -extensions v3_intermediate_ca     -days 3650 -notext -md sha256 -in intermediate.csr -out intermediate.crt
Using configuration from openssl-ca.cnf
Check that the request matches the signature
Signature ok
Certificate Details: ...
Sign the certificate? [y/n]: y
1 out of 1 certificate requests certified, commit? [y/n]: y
# Signs an intermediate CA CSR using a root CA and a config with CA extensions.

CSR & Certificate Signing.cmd: openssl ca -config openssl-ca.cnf -extensions server_cert -days 365 -in req.csr -out server.crt
CSR & Certificate Signing.desc: Sign a server cert with full CA workflow (index.txt + serial)
CSR & Certificate Signing.example:
$ openssl ca -config openssl-ca.cnf -extensions server_cert     -days 365 -notext -md sha256 -in server.csr -out server.crt
Using configuration from openssl-ca.cnf
Signature ok
Certificate is to be certified until Jan  1 00:00:00 2027 GMT (365 days)
Sign the certificate? [y/n]: y
# Signs a server CSR using a CA managed via the openssl ca mini-CA infrastructure.
# Tracks issued certificates in the ca database (index.txt, serial file).

Using configuration from openssl-ca.cnf
Revoking Certificate 01.
Data Base Updated

CSR & Certificate Signing.desc: Generate a self-signed certificate and private key in a single command (-nodes = no passphrase)
CSR & Certificate Signing.example:
# Generates key.pem and cert.pem in the current directory
openssl req -x509 -newkey rsa:4096 -keyout key.pem -out cert.pem -sha256 -days 365 -nodes \
  -subj "/C=GB/ST=London/O=MyOrg/CN=internal.example.com"

Generating a RSA private key
...............................................................................................++++
........++++
writing new private key to 'key.pem'

# Verify the result:
openssl x509 -in cert.pem -noout -subject -dates
subject=C = GB, ST = London, O = MyOrg, CN = internal.example.com
notBefore=Jun 15 09:00:00 2026 GMT
notAfter=Jun 15 09:00:00 2027 GMT

# Add SANs (required by modern browsers): append -addext "subjectAltName=DNS:internal.example.com"

Certificate Inspection.desc: Print the serial number of an X.509 certificate in hexadecimal; used for CRL lookups and certificate tracking
Certificate Inspection.example:
$ openssl x509 -in server.crt -noout -serial
serial=1A2B3C4D5E6F7A8B

# The serial is assigned by the CA and is unique within that CA.
# Used to check revocation status:
#   openssl ocsp -issuer ca.crt -cert server.crt -url http://ocsp.ca.example.com

# Convert hex serial to decimal (for GUI CAs that display decimal):
#   printf "%d\n" 0x1A2B3C4D5E6F7A8B

# Extract serial along with validity dates:
#   openssl x509 -in server.crt -noout -serial -dates

Certificate Inspection.desc: Display only the Subject Alternative Names extension

    DNS:example.com, DNS:*.example.com, IP Address:93.184.216.34

Certificate Inspection.desc: Check the mathematical consistency of an RSA private key; confirms the key is not corrupted
Certificate Inspection.example:
$ openssl rsa -in server.key -check
RSA key ok

# A healthy key returns "RSA key ok" and exits 0.

# Failed check output:
$ openssl rsa -in corrupt.key -check
unable to load Private Key
4294955904:error:0906D06C:PEM routines:PEM_read_bio:no start line

# To also display key details (modulus, exponent):
#   openssl rsa -in server.key -text -noout
# To verify the key matches a certificate (moduli must match):
#   openssl rsa  -noout -modulus -in server.key | md5sum
#   openssl x509 -noout -modulus -in server.crt | md5sum

Certificate Inspection.desc: Print all subjects in a PEM certificate chain file

$ openssl crl2pkcs7 -nocrl -certfile chain.pem | openssl pkcs7 -print_certs -noout
subject=CN=example.com
issuer=CN=R3, O=Let's Encrypt, C=US

issuer=CN=ISRG Root X1, O=Internet Security Research Group, C=US
# Lists all certificates in a PEM bundle with their subject and issuer.
# Useful for verifying a full certificate chain is present.

Certificate Inspection.desc: Displays certificate details
Certificate Inspection.example:
$ openssl x509 -in example.crt -text -noout
Certificate:
    Data:
        Version: 3 (0x2)
        Subject: CN=example.com
        X509v3 Subject Alternative Name:
            DNS:example.com, DNS:www.example.com
        Validity
            Not After : Apr  1 00:00:00 2026 GMT
# Generic form using a variable — substitute <cert> with the actual filename.

Certificate Inspection.desc: Shows certificate issuer
Certificate Inspection.example:
$ openssl x509 -in server.crt -issuer -noout
issuer=C=US, O=Let's Encrypt, CN=R3
# Prints only the Issuer distinguished name of the certificate.
# The issuer must match the subject of the next certificate up the chain.

Certificate Inspection.desc: Shows certificate subject
Certificate Inspection.example:
$ openssl x509 -in server.crt -subject -noout
subject=CN=example.com
# Prints only the Subject distinguished name.
# For multi-SAN certificates, the CN is often just the primary domain name.

Certificate Inspection.desc: Shows certificate validity dates
Certificate Inspection.example:
$ openssl x509 -in server.crt -dates -noout
notBefore=Jan  1 00:00:00 2026 GMT
notAfter=Apr  1 00:00:00 2026 GMT
# Prints the validity window (Not Before and Not After dates).
# Quick check for certificate expiry without parsing the full -text output.

Certificate Inspection.desc: Shows certificate fingerprint
Certificate Inspection.example:
$ openssl x509 -in server.crt -fingerprint -noout
SHA1 Fingerprint=A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6:...
# Prints the SHA-1 fingerprint of the DER-encoded certificate.
# For SHA-256: openssl x509 -in server.crt -fingerprint -sha256 -noout
# Compare against the fingerprint shown in a browser certificate viewer.

Certificate Inspection.desc: Verifies certificate chain
Certificate Inspection.example:
$ openssl verify -CAfile /etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt server.crt
server.crt: OK
# Verifies the certificate chain against the CA bundle.
# "OK" = certificate is valid, not expired, and chain is trusted.
# Failure message includes the reason: e.g. "certificate has expired" (code 10).

Certificate Inspection.desc: Extracts keys and certs from PFX
Certificate Inspection.example:
$ openssl pkcs12 -in bundle.pfx -nodes -out bundle.pem
Enter Import Password:
MAC verified OK
$ grep "BEGIN" bundle.pem
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
-----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----
# Extracts certificate(s) and private key from a PKCS#12 file to a PEM bundle.
# -nodes = no passphrase on the extracted private key.

Certificate Inspection.desc: Bundles a private key, certificate, and optional CA chain into a password-protected PKCS#12 (.pfx/.p12) file — the format Windows, IIS, and Java keystores import directly.
Certificate Inspection.example:
$ openssl pkcs12 -export -out bundle.pfx -inkey server.key -in server.crt -certfile chain.crt
Enter Export Password:
Verifying - Enter Export Password:
$ ls -lh bundle.pfx
-rw------- 1 user user 4.1K Jan  1 12:00 bundle.pfx
# Packages a certificate, private key, and optional chain into a PKCS#12 file.
# Import into Windows: double-click bundle.pfx → Certificate Import Wizard.

Certificate Inspection.desc: Generates a Certificate Signing Request (CSR) using an existing private key, prompting for Subject Distinguished Name fields — submit the CSR to a CA to obtain a signed certificate.
Certificate Inspection.example:
$ openssl req -new -key server.key -out server.csr
You are about to be asked to enter information that will be incorporated into your certificate request.
Country Name (2 letter code) [AU]: US
State or Province Name: California
Locality Name: San Francisco
Organization Name: Example Corp
Organizational Unit Name: IT
Common Name: www.example.com
Email Address: admin@example.com

$ openssl req -in server.csr -noout -subject
subject=C=US, ST=California, O=Example Corp, CN=www.example.com

Certificate Inspection.desc: Generates key + CSR
Certificate Inspection.example:
$ openssl req -newkey rsa:2048 -nodes -keyout server.key -out server.csr -subj "/CN=example.com"
Generating a RSA private key
......+++++
e is 65537 (0x010001)
# Generates a new 2048-bit RSA key AND a CSR in one step.
# -nodes = no passphrase on the generated private key.

Certificate Inspection.desc: Purposes a cert is valid for (server, client, code-sign…)
Certificate Inspection.example:
$ openssl x509 -in server.crt -noout -purpose
Certificate purposes:
SSL client : No
SSL client CA : No
SSL server : Yes
SSL server CA : No
Netscape SSL server : Yes
S/MIME signing : No
S/MIME encryption : No
CRL signing : No
Any Purpose : Yes
OCSP helper : No
Time Stamp signing : No
# Shows the certificate's intended purposes based on Key Usage and EKU extensions.

Certificate Inspection.desc: Just the EKU extension values
Certificate Inspection.example:
$ openssl x509 -in server.crt -noout -ext extendedKeyUsage
X509v3 Extended Key Usage:
    TLS Web Server Authentication, TLS Web Client Authentication
# Shows the Extended Key Usage (EKU) extension — what the cert is authorised for.
# Server certs: id-kp-serverAuth (1.3.6.1.5.5.7.3.1)
# Client certs: id-kp-clientAuth (1.3.6.1.5.5.7.3.2)

Certificate Inspection.desc: Just the keyUsage extension
Certificate Inspection.example:
$ openssl x509 -in server.crt -noout -ext keyUsage
X509v3 Key Usage: critical
    Digital Signature, Key Encipherment
# 'critical' means a CA or application MUST understand and enforce this extension.
# Common values: digitalSignature (TLS auth), keyEncipherment (RSA key exchange),
# keyCertSign (CA certs), cRLSign (CRL signing), dataEncipherment (rare).

Certificate Inspection.desc: AIA extension — OCSP URL and CA issuer URL
Certificate Inspection.example:
$ openssl x509 -in server.crt -noout -ext authorityInfoAccess
Authority Information Access:
    OCSP - URI:http://ocsp.example.com
    CA Issuers - URI:http://crt.example.com/intermediate.crt
# OCSP URI: where to check revocation status in real time.
# CA Issuers URI: where to download the issuing CA cert (for chain building).
# Used by TLS clients to build and validate the full certificate chain.

Certificate Inspection.desc: CRL Distribution Points
Certificate Inspection.example:
$ openssl x509 -in server.crt -noout -ext crlDistributionPoints
X509v3 CRL Distribution Points:
    Full Name:
      URI:http://crl.example.com/intermediate.crl
# CRL = Certificate Revocation List. Clients download this periodically
# to check if the cert has been revoked.
# OCSP (see authorityInfoAccess) is the online alternative to CRL download.

Certificate Inspection.desc: Hash of the issuer name (used by OpenSSL CAfile symlinks)
Certificate Inspection.example:
$ openssl x509 -in ca.crt -noout -issuer_hash
4a6481c9
# Returns the hash of the issuer DN field.
# OpenSSL trust stores use symlinks named <hash>.0, <hash>.1, etc.
# to map issuer hashes to certificate files — used by c_rehash.
# Run c_rehash /etc/ssl/certs/ after adding a new CA cert.

Certificate Inspection.desc: Hash of the subject name (c_rehash symlink target)
Certificate Inspection.example:
$ openssl x509 -in ca.crt -noout -subject_hash
4a6481c9
# Returns the hash of the subject DN.
# Used by c_rehash to create the symlink <hash>.0 → ca.crt in the trust store.
# The issuer_hash of a leaf cert should match the subject_hash of its issuing CA.
# Verify: openssl verify -CAfile chain.pem leaf.crt

Certificate Inspection.desc: Exit non-zero if cert expires within 7 days (scriptable)
Certificate Inspection.example:
Certificate will not expire
(Exit code 0 = valid for 7 days; 1 = expires within 7 days)

Certificate Inspection.desc: NotBefore + NotAfter in one command
Certificate Inspection.example:
$ openssl x509 -in server.crt -noout -startdate -enddate
notBefore=Jan  1 00:00:00 2025 GMT
notAfter=Jan  1 00:00:00 2026 GMT
# Quick check for cert validity window without parsing full -text output.
# Script-friendly: check expiry in days:
#   openssl x509 -in cert.crt -noout -checkend 2592000 && echo 'OK' || echo 'EXPIRES < 30d'

Certificate Inspection.desc: Full cert minus noisy pubkey + signature hex
Certificate Inspection.example:
$ openssl x509 -in server.crt -noout -text -certopt no_pubkey,no_sigdump
Certificate:
    Data:
        Version: 3 (0x2)
        Serial Number: 04:a3:...:7f
        Signature Algorithm: sha256WithRSAEncryption
        Issuer: C=US, O=Example CA, CN=Example Intermediate CA
        Validity
            Not Before: Jan  1 00:00:00 2025 GMT
            Not After : Jan  1 00:00:00 2026 GMT
        Subject: CN=*.example.com
        X509v3 extensions:
            X509v3 Subject Alternative Name:
                DNS:*.example.com, DNS:example.com
            X509v3 Key Usage: critical
                Digital Signature, Key Encipherment
# Omits the lengthy Public Key and Signature hex blocks for cleaner output.

Certificate Inspection.desc: Checks the extended key usage and basic constraints to list what purposes the certificate is valid for (SSL client, SSL server, S/MIME, etc.) — quickly verifies certificate is appropriate for intended use
Certificate Inspection.example:
$ openssl x509 -in server.pem -noout -purpose
Certificate purposes:
SSL client : No
SSL client CA : No
SSL server : Yes
SSL server CA : No
Netscape SSL server : Yes
S/MIME signing : No
S/MIME encryption : No
CRL signing : No
Any Purpose : Yes
OCSP helper : No
Time Stamp signing : No

Certificate Inspection.desc: Verify a certificate against a local CA chain file
Certificate Inspection.example:
$ openssl verify -CAfile ca-chain.crt server.crt
server.crt: OK

# If the chain is incomplete or the cert is revoked / expired:
$ openssl verify -CAfile ca-chain.crt expired.crt
error 10 at 0 depth lookup: certificate has expired

# Depth 0 = the leaf cert; depth 1 = intermediate CA; depth 2 = root CA.
# Use -partial_chain to allow verification against an intermediate CA
# without requiring the root in the CAfile.
# Combine with -crl_check -CRLfile crl.pem to verify against a local CRL.

Certificate Inspection.desc: Prints the SHA-256 fingerprint (hash) of a certificate's DER encoding — used to verify a certificate matches what a browser/client reports, to pin a cert, or to confirm two files contain the exact same certificate without a byte-for-byte diff
Certificate Inspection.example:
$ openssl x509 -noout -fingerprint -sha256 -in cert.pem
sha256 Fingerprint=A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6:07:18:29:3A:4B:5C:6D:7E:8F:90:A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6:07:18:29:3A:4B:5C:6D:7E:8F:90

# Compare against what a live server presents:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -servername example.com </dev/null 2>/dev/null | \
  openssl x509 -noout -fingerprint -sha256
sha256 Fingerprint=A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6:07:18:29:3A:4B:5C:6D:7E:8F:90:A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6:07:18:29:3A:4B:5C:6D:7E:8F:90
# Matching fingerprints confirm it is the same certificate.

Certificate Inspection.desc: Prints the issuer name hash, issuer key hash, and serial number that an OCSP responder uses to identify this certificate — useful for cross-checking OCSP requests/responses by hand
Certificate Inspection.example:
$ openssl x509 -in cert.crt -noout -ocspid
Issuer Name Hash: 4A5C3B8F9E1D2A6C7B8E9F0A1B2C3D4E5F6A7B8C
Issuer Key Hash:  9F8E7D6C5B4A39281706F5E4D3C2B1A0918273645
Serial Number:    0A1B2C3D4E5F6A7B8C9D0E1F2A3B4C5D6E7F8A9B

What it means:
- These three values are exactly what OCSP request/response CertID fields
  encode (RFC 6960) — compare them against a captured OCSP request to
  confirm the responder is being asked about the right certificate.
- Handy when an OCSP responder returns "unknown" and you need to verify
  the client hashed the correct issuer before blaming the CA.

Verification & Matching.desc: Verify a certificate is properly signed by the specified CA
Verification & Matching.example:
$ openssl verify -CAfile ca.crt server.crt
server.crt: OK
# Verifies server.crt against the CA certificate in ca.crt.
# "OK" means the signature and chain are valid; error messages include the reason.
# For a chain: use -CAfile with the root and intermediate CAs concatenated.

Verification & Matching.desc: Verify a certificate using a full chain with intermediate CA
Verification & Matching.example:
$ openssl verify -CAfile root-ca.crt -untrusted intermediate.crt server.crt
server.crt: OK
# Verifies the full chain: server.crt signed by intermediate.crt signed by root-ca.crt.
# -untrusted provides intermediate CAs that are not directly trusted anchors.
# Multiple intermediates: -untrusted int1.crt -untrusted int2.crt

Verification & Matching.desc: Get MD5 hash of certificate modulus for key/cert matching
Verification & Matching.example:
$ openssl x509 -noout -modulus -in server.crt | openssl md5
(stdin)= a1b2c3d4e5f6a1b2c3d4e5f6a1b2c3d4
$ openssl rsa -noout -modulus -in server.key | openssl md5
(stdin)= a1b2c3d4e5f6a1b2c3d4e5f6a1b2c3d4
# If both MD5 hashes match, the certificate and private key are a pair.
# For EC keys: openssl ec -noout -pubout -in ec.key | openssl md5

Verification & Matching.desc: Get MD5 hash of private key modulus — compare with cert to confirm match
Verification & Matching.example:
$ openssl rsa -noout -modulus -in server.key | openssl md5
(stdin)= a1b2c3d4e5f6a1b2c3d4e5f6a1b2c3d4
$ openssl x509 -noout -modulus -in server.crt | openssl md5
(stdin)= a1b2c3d4e5f6a1b2c3d4e5f6a1b2c3d4
# Matching MD5 hashes confirm the certificate and private key are a pair.
# For EC keys use: openssl ec -noout -pubout -in ec.key | openssl md5

Verification & Matching.desc: Get MD5 hash of CSR modulus — compare with key and cert
Verification & Matching.example:
$ openssl req -noout -modulus -in server.csr | openssl md5
(stdin)= a1b2c3d4e5f6a1b2c3d4e5f6a1b2c3d4
$ openssl rsa -noout -modulus -in server.key | openssl md5
(stdin)= a1b2c3d4e5f6a1b2c3d4e5f6a1b2c3d4
# Confirms the CSR was generated from the correct private key before submission.

Verification & Matching.desc: Verify a Diffie-Hellman parameters file is valid
Verification & Matching.example:
DH parameters appear to be ok.
(No output on failure — exit code non-zero)

Verification & Matching.desc: Verify leaf against root, using intermediates, with verbose output
Verification & Matching.example:
$ openssl verify -CAfile root-ca.crt -untrusted intermediate.crt -verbose server.crt
server.crt: /CN=example.com
error 0 at 0 depth lookup: ok
server.crt: OK
# -verbose shows each certificate in the chain being validated.
# -untrusted provides intermediate CAs that are not direct trust anchors.

Verification & Matching.desc: Verify against the system-trust directory (c_rehash style)
Verification & Matching.example:
$ openssl verify -CApath /etc/ssl/certs server.crt
server.crt: OK
# Uses the system CA certificate directory (hashed) instead of a single file.
# -CApath requires certificates named by their subject hash (c_rehash /etc/ssl/certs).

Verification & Matching.desc: Let OpenSSL stop at an intermediate rather than chaining to a root
Verification & Matching.example:
$ openssl verify -partial_chain -CAfile intermediate.crt server.crt
server.crt: OK
# -partial_chain allows verification to succeed against an intermediate CA
# without requiring the full chain back to a root CA.
# Useful when you only have the intermediate and want to verify leaf cert.

Verification & Matching.desc: Print the full chain as OpenSSL built it
Verification & Matching.example:
$ openssl verify -show_chain -CAfile ca.crt server.crt
server.crt: OK
Chain:
depth=0: CN=example.com (untrusted)
depth=1: C=US, O=Let's Encrypt, CN=R3 (untrusted)
depth=2: C=US, O=Internet Security Research Group, O=ISRG Root X1 (trusted)
# -show_chain displays the full certificate chain used during verification.
# "trusted" marks the root CA from the -CAfile; others are "untrusted" intermediates.

Verification & Matching.desc: Hash of the public key — compare against cert and CSR
Verification & Matching.example:
$ openssl pkey -in server.key -pubout | openssl sha256
(stdin)= 3c4f9a1b2d5e6f7890abc1d2e3f4a5b6c7d8e9f0a1b2c3d4e5f6789012345678
$ openssl x509 -in server.crt -pubkey -noout | openssl sha256
(stdin)= 3c4f9a1b2d5e6f7890abc1d2e3f4a5b6c7d8e9f0a1b2c3d4e5f6789012345678
# SHA-256 hash comparison of public keys — confirms cert and key are paired.
# Works for RSA, EC, Ed25519 — a universal key-cert matching method.

Verification & Matching.desc: Hash of the cert's public key — pin / match against key/CSR
Verification & Matching.example:
$ openssl x509 -in server.crt -pubkey -noout | openssl sha256
(stdin)= 3c4f9a1b2d5e6f7890abc1d2e3f4a5b6c7d8e9f0a1b2c3d4e5f6789012345678
$ openssl pkey -in server.key -pubout | openssl sha256
(stdin)= 3c4f9a1b2d5e6f7890abc1d2e3f4a5b6c7d8e9f0a1b2c3d4e5f6789012345678
# Extracts public key from cert and hashes it — compare with the key file hash.

Verification & Matching.desc: Hash of the CSR's public key — compare against private key
Verification & Matching.example:
$ openssl req -in server.csr -pubkey -noout | openssl sha256
(stdin)= 3c4f9a1b2d5e6f7890abc1d2e3f4a5b6c7d8e9f0a1b2c3d4e5f6789012345678
$ openssl pkey -in server.key -pubout | openssl sha256
(stdin)= 3c4f9a1b2d5e6f7890abc1d2e3f4a5b6c7d8e9f0a1b2c3d4e5f6789012345678
# Three-way check: if CSR, key, and cert all produce the same hash, they match.

$ openssl x509 -in server.crt -noout -fingerprint -sha1
SHA1 Fingerprint=A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6:07:08:09:0A:0B:0C:0D:0E:0F:10:11:12:13:14
# SHA-1 fingerprint — matches what browsers display in the certificate details.
# For SHA-256: openssl x509 -in server.crt -noout -fingerprint -sha256

Format Conversion.cmd: openssl x509 -in cert.der -inform DER -out cert.pem -outform PEM
Format Conversion.desc: Convert a DER-encoded certificate to PEM format
Format Conversion.example:
$ openssl x509 -in server.der -inform DER -out server.pem -outform PEM
$ head -1 server.pem
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
# Converts a DER-encoded (binary) certificate to PEM (Base64 + header/footer).
# PEM is the most common format for Linux/Apache/Nginx; DER is used by Java and Windows.

Format Conversion.desc: Convert a PEM certificate to DER format
Format Conversion.example:
$ openssl x509 -in server.pem -outform DER -out server.der
$ file server.der
server.der: data
# Converts PEM to DER binary format.
# DER files do not have the -----BEGIN/END----- headers.

Format Conversion.cmd: openssl pkcs12 -export -out bundle.pfx -inkey private.key -in cert.crt -certfile ca.crt
Format Conversion.desc: Bundle certificate, private key, and CA chain into a PKCS12 (.pfx) file
Format Conversion.example:
Enter Export Password:
Verifying - Enter Export Password:
(PFX bundle written to bundle.pfx)

Format Conversion.desc: Extract certificate and private key from a PKCS12 file to PEM
Format Conversion.example:
$ openssl pkcs12 -in bundle.pfx -out bundle.pem -nodes
Enter Import Password:
MAC verified OK
$ grep "BEGIN" bundle.pem
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
-----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----
# -nodes = no passphrase on the extracted private key.
# The output PEM contains all certs in the chain plus the private key.

Format Conversion.desc: Extract only the certificate(s) from a PKCS12 file
Format Conversion.example:
$ openssl pkcs12 -in bundle.pfx -nokeys -out certs-only.pem
Enter Import Password:
MAC verified OK
$ grep "BEGIN" certs-only.pem
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
# Extracts only the certificate(s) — private key is not written to the output file.

Format Conversion.desc: Extract only the private key from a PKCS12 file
Format Conversion.example:
$ openssl pkcs12 -in bundle.pfx -nocerts -out private.key -nodes
Enter Import Password:
MAC verified OK
$ grep "BEGIN" private.key
-----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----
# Extracts only the private key — no certificate data in the output.

Format Conversion.desc: Remove the passphrase from an encrypted RSA private key
Format Conversion.example:
$ openssl rsa -in encrypted.key -out decrypted.key
Enter pass phrase for encrypted.key:
writing RSA key
# Removes the passphrase from an encrypted RSA private key.
# Output is a plaintext PEM RSA key — secure the file with filesystem permissions.

Format Conversion.desc: Add 3DES passphrase encryption to an RSA private key
Format Conversion.example:
$ openssl rsa -in server.key -des3 -out server-encrypted.key
writing RSA key
Enter PEM pass phrase:
Verifying - Enter PEM pass phrase:
$ head -2 server-encrypted.key
-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----
Proc-Type: 4,ENCRYPTED
# Adds DES3 passphrase encryption to an existing RSA private key.

Format Conversion.desc: Convert a PKCS#7 (.p7b) bundle to PEM format
Format Conversion.example:
$ openssl pkcs7 -in bundle.p7b -inform DER -print_certs -out chain.pem
$ grep "BEGIN" chain.pem
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
# Converts a PKCS#7 / CMS .p7b bundle (common in Windows) to PEM format.
# If the .p7b is PEM-encoded, omit -inform DER.

Format Conversion.desc: Convert a traditional RSA key to PKCS#8 unencrypted format
Format Conversion.example:
$ openssl pkcs8 -topk8 -in server.key -out server-pkcs8.key -nocrypt
$ head -1 server-pkcs8.key
-----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----
# Converts a traditional RSA key (PKCS#1) to PKCS#8 unencrypted format.
# PKCS#8 is required by Java KeyStore, Node.js, and many modern frameworks.
# For an encrypted output: omit -nocrypt and supply a passphrase when prompted.

Format Conversion.cmd: openssl pkcs12 -export -out bundle.pfx -inkey private.key -in cert.crt -certfile chain.crt -name "my-cert"
Format Conversion.desc: Build PFX with a friendly-name alias
Format Conversion.example:
$ openssl pkcs12 -export -out bundle.pfx     -inkey server.key -in server.crt     -certfile intermediate.crt -name "example.com"
Enter Export Password:
Verifying - Enter Export Password:
$ ls -lh bundle.pfx
-rw------- 1 user user 4.1K Jan  1 12:00 bundle.pfx
# Creates a PKCS#12 (.pfx/.p12) bundle with the cert, key, and chain.
# -name sets the "friendly name" visible in Windows Certificate Manager.

Format Conversion.desc: PFX encrypted with legacy RC2/3DES algorithms (for old Java/Windows)
Format Conversion.example:
$ openssl pkcs12 -export -out legacy.pfx -inkey private.key -in cert.crt -legacy
Enter Export Password: ****
Verifying - Enter Export Password: ****
# The -legacy flag uses RC2/3DES encryption compatible with older Java keystores
# (JKS) and Windows Server 2003/2008 that don't support AES-256 PFX.
# Import to Java: keytool -importkeystore -srckeystore legacy.pfx -srcstoretype PKCS12

Format Conversion.desc: Extract only the client-cert leaf from a PFX
Format Conversion.example:
$ openssl pkcs12 -in bundle.pfx -clcerts -nokeys -out client-only.crt
Enter Import Password: ****
$ cat client-only.crt
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
MIIDXTCCAkWg...
-----END CERTIFICATE-----
# -clcerts = client (leaf) certificates only
# -nokeys  = exclude the private key
# Useful when you need only the public cert to share with a server admin.

Format Conversion.desc: Extract only the CA chain (no leaf, no key)
Format Conversion.example:
$ openssl pkcs12 -in bundle.pfx -cacerts -nokeys -out ca-chain.crt
Enter Import Password: ****
$ openssl x509 -in ca-chain.crt -noout -subject
subject=C=US, O=Example CA, CN=Example Intermediate CA
# -cacerts = CA certificates only (intermediate + root), NOT the leaf
# -nokeys  = exclude private key
# Useful for extracting the trust chain separately from the leaf cert.

Format Conversion.desc: PKCS#7 bundle → PEM chain
Format Conversion.example:
$ openssl pkcs7 -print_certs -in chain.p7b -out chain.pem
$ cat chain.pem
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
MIIDXTCCAk...  (leaf)
-----END CERTIFICATE-----
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
MIIDYTCAk...   (intermediate CA)
-----END CERTIFICATE-----
# PKCS#7 (.p7b/.p7c) is common in Windows environments.
# -print_certs extracts all certs in the bundle as PEM-concatenated output.

Format Conversion.desc: Build a PKCS#7 cert bundle for Windows/Java import
Format Conversion.example:
$ openssl crl2pkcs7 -nocrl -certfile leaf.crt -certfile chain.pem -out bundle.p7b
$ openssl pkcs7 -in bundle.p7b -print_certs -noout
subject=CN=*.example.com
issuer=CN=Example Intermediate CA
subject=CN=Example Intermediate CA
issuer=CN=Example Root CA
# -nocrl skips the CRL (we only want a cert bundle).
# The output .p7b can be imported into IIS, Windows certificate store,
# or Java keystores that accept PKCS#7 format.

Format Conversion.desc: Convert a private key to DER
Format Conversion.example:
$ openssl pkey -in private.key -outform DER -out private.der
$ xxd private.der | head -2
00000000: 3082 04a3 0201 0002 8201 0100 bc72 ...   (DER binary)
# DER (Distinguished Encoding Rules) is the binary form of PEM.
# Required by some Java applications and hardware security modules (HSM).
# PEM is base64(DER) wrapped in -----BEGIN/END----- headers.

Format Conversion.desc: Convert a DER key to PEM
Format Conversion.example:
$ openssl pkey -in private.der -inform DER -out private.pem
$ head -1 private.pem
-----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----
# Converts a DER binary key back to PEM text format.
# Most Linux/Unix tools (nginx, Apache, curl) expect PEM-encoded keys.
# Add -aes256 to encrypt the output PEM with a passphrase:
#   openssl pkey -in private.der -inform DER -out private.pem -aes256

Format Conversion.desc: Build a concatenated PEM chain (leaf → root)
Format Conversion.example:
$ cat leaf.crt intermediate.crt root.crt > fullchain.pem
$ openssl verify -CAfile root.crt fullchain.pem
fullchain.pem: OK
# Order matters: leaf first, then each intermediate, then root last.
# nginx ssl_certificate expects this order.
# Apache SSLCertificateFile (leaf) + SSLCertificateChainFile (chain) are separate.
# Verify the chain: openssl verify -CAfile root.crt -untrusted intermediate.crt leaf.crt

Format Conversion.cmd: openssl pkcs12 -export -out bundle.pfx -inkey private.key -in cert.crt -certfile chain.crt
Format Conversion.desc: Bundle a private key, certificate, and CA chain into a PKCS#12 (.pfx) file for Windows/IIS import
Format Conversion.example:
# Prompts for an export password to protect the PFX
openssl pkcs12 -export -out bundle.pfx -inkey private.key -in cert.crt -certfile chain.crt
Enter Export Password:
Verifying - Enter Export Password:

# Verify the resulting PFX:
openssl pkcs12 -info -in bundle.pfx -noout
MAC: sha256, Iteration 2048
MAC length: 32, salt length: 20
PKCS7 Encrypted data: ...
    Certificate bag
    Certificate bag  (intermediate)
    PKCS8 Shrouded Keybag

# Import on Windows: double-click bundle.pfx and follow the certificate import wizard.

TLS Client Testing.desc: Connect to a TLS endpoint and display the full certificate chain and handshake
TLS Client Testing.example:
CONNECTED(00000003)
depth=2 C=US, O=DigiCert Inc, CN=DigiCert Global Root CA
depth=1 C=US, O=DigiCert Inc, CN=DigiCert TLS RSA SHA256 2020 CA1
depth=0 CN=example.com
---
Certificate chain
 0 s:CN=example.com
   i:C=US, O=DigiCert Inc, CN=DigiCert TLS RSA SHA256 2020 CA1
---
SSL-Session:
    Protocol  : TLSv1.3
    Cipher    : TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
    Session-ID: A3F2...
    Master-Key: (empty for TLS 1.3)
---
read R BLOCK
closed

TLS Client Testing.desc: Connect with SNI (Server Name Indication) — required for virtual hosting
TLS Client Testing.example:
# Without -servername the server may return its default (catch-all) cert.
# With SNI, it returns the cert for the virtual host.
CONNECTED(00000003)
...
Subject Name: CN=hostname.example.com
SAN: hostname.example.com
...
SSL-Session:
    Protocol  : TLSv1.3
    Cipher    : TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384

TLS Client Testing.desc: Connect and display every certificate in the chain
TLS Client Testing.example:
CONNECTED(00000003)
---
Certificate chain
 0 s:CN=example.com
   i:C=US, O=Let's Encrypt, CN=R3
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
MIIE...(leaf cert PEM)
-----END CERTIFICATE-----
 1 s:C=US, O=Let's Encrypt, CN=R3
   i:C=US, O=Internet Security Research Group, CN=ISRG Root X1
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
MIIF...(intermediate PEM)
-----END CERTIFICATE-----
---
# Paste each PEM block into a .crt to inspect or import individually.

TLS Client Testing.desc: Connect and validate the certificate against a specific CA bundle
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -CAfile /etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt < /dev/null
CONNECTED(00000003)
depth=2 CN=ISRG Root X1
depth=1 CN=R3
depth=0 CN=example.com
verify return:1
...
SSL handshake has read 4738 bytes and written 413 bytes
---
New, TLSv1.3, Cipher is TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
    Verify return code: 0 (ok)
---

TLS Client Testing.desc: Force TLS 1.2 only for the connection test
TLS Client Testing.example:
# If the server supports TLS 1.2 you see:
CONNECTED(00000003)
...
SSL-Session:
    Protocol  : TLSv1.2
    Cipher    : ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384

# If TLS 1.2 is disabled server-side:
CONNECTED(00000003)
write:errno=104
# or: alert handshake failure

TLS Client Testing.desc: Force TLS 1.3 only for the connection test
TLS Client Testing.example:
# Server accepts TLS 1.3:
CONNECTED(00000003)
SSL-Session:
    Protocol  : TLSv1.3
    Cipher    : TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384

# Server does not support TLS 1.3 (legacy server):
CONNECTED(00000003)
SSL-Session:
    Protocol  : TLSv1.2   ← negotiated down
# Or: handshake failure if server is strict

TLS Client Testing.desc: Test connectivity using a specific cipher suite
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -cipher ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384 < /dev/null 2>&1 | grep -E "Cipher|error"
New, TLSv1.2, Cipher is ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384
# Tests whether the server accepts a specific TLS 1.2 cipher suite.
# If the cipher is not supported: no peer certificate available / handshake failure.

TLS Client Testing.desc: Request OCSP stapling status information during the TLS handshake
TLS Client Testing.example:
CONNECTED(00000003)
...
OCSP response:
======================================
OCSP Response Data:
    OCSP Response Status: successful (0x0)
    Response Type: Basic OCSP Response
    Cert Status: Good
    This Update: Jun 14 08:00:00 2026 GMT
    Next Update: Jun 21 08:00:00 2026 GMT
======================================
# If no OCSP staple is returned: OCSP response: no response sent

TLS Client Testing.desc: Check certificate expiry dates directly from a live TLS endpoint
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -dates
notBefore=Jan  1 00:00:00 2026 GMT
notAfter=Apr  1 00:00:00 2026 GMT
# Fetches the live leaf certificate and prints its validity dates.
# Quick expiry check for any HTTPS endpoint without importing the certificate.

TLS Client Testing.desc: Test TLS on SMTP using STARTTLS negotiation
TLS Client Testing.example:
220 mail.example.com ESMTP
EHLO openssl.client
250-mail.example.com
250 STARTTLS
STARTTLS
220 Ready to start TLS
CONNECTED(00000003)
...
SSL-Session:
    Protocol  : TLSv1.2
    Cipher    : ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384
# Also works with: -starttls ftp | imap | ldap | pop3 | xmpp

TLS Client Testing.desc: Test that only TLS 1.2+ is accepted by the server
TLS Client Testing.example:
# If server correctly rejects old protocols:
CONNECTED(00000003)
SSL-Session:
    Protocol  : TLSv1.2   (or TLSv1.3)

# Verifying TLS 1.0/1.1 are truly disabled:
openssl s_client -connect hostname:443 -tls1_1
  → handshake failure  ✓ good
openssl s_client -connect hostname:443 -tls1
  → handshake failure  ✓ good

TLS Client Testing.desc: Start a simple TLS test server on port 4433 for client testing
TLS Client Testing.example:
# Server side — starts listening:
Using default temp DH parameters
Ciphers supported in s_server binary
...
SECURE Renegotiation IS supported

# Client connects:  openssl s_client -connect 127.0.0.1:4433
# Server logs:
Client IP: 127.0.0.1
SSL_accept:before SSL initialization
SSL_accept:TLSv1.3 read client hello
SSL_accept:TLSv1.3 write server hello
SSL_accept:SSLv3/TLS write certificate
SSL_accept:TLSv1.3 write finished
ACCEPT

TLS Client Testing.desc: Benchmark RSA 2048-bit sign and verify operations per second
TLS Client Testing.example:
Doing 2048 bits private rsa's for 10s: 18722 2048 bits private RSA's in 10.00s
Doing 2048 bits public rsa's for 10s: 605743 2048 bits public RSA's in 10.01s
             sign    verify    sign/s verify/s
rsa 2048 bits 0.000534s 0.000017s   1872.2  60513.8
# sign/s is the critical metric for TLS handshake capacity under load

TLS Client Testing.desc: Benchmark ECDH key exchange performance on P-256 curve
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl speed ecdhp256
Doing ECDH ops on P-256: 4500 in 3.00s = 1499.98 ECDH/s
# Benchmarks ECDH key exchange operations per second on P-256 (prime256v1).
# Higher is better; modern CPUs with AVX2 exceed 5000 ops/s.
# Compare: openssl speed ecdhp384 ecdhp521 ecdhx25519
# X25519 typically outperforms P-256 on software-only implementations.

TLS Client Testing.desc: Tests SSL/TLS connection
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 < /dev/null
CONNECTED(00000003)
depth=2 C=US, O=Internet Security Research Group, CN=ISRG Root X1
depth=1 C=US, O=Let's Encrypt, CN=R3
depth=0 CN=example.com
verify return:1
---
New, TLSv1.3, Cipher is TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
Server public key is 2048 bit
    Verify return code: 0 (ok)
---

TLS Client Testing.desc: Tests SSL/TLS with SNI
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect 10.0.0.10:443 -servername example.com < /dev/null 2>&1 | grep "subject="
subject=CN=example.com
# -servername sends the TLS SNI extension — essential for virtual-hosted HTTPS.
# Without SNI the server may return the default (wrong) certificate.

TLS Client Testing.desc: Opens a TLS 1.2-only connection to the specified host and port, refusing TLS 1.3 negotiation — use to verify that TLS 1.2 is still supported or to test legacy client compatibility.
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -tls1_2 < /dev/null 2>&1 | grep -E "Protocol|Cipher|Verify"
    Protocol  : TLSv1.2
    Cipher    : ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384
    Verify return code: 0 (ok)

# If TLS 1.2 is disabled on the server:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -tls1_2 < /dev/null 2>&1 | grep -i "alert\|error"
140...error:no protocols available

TLS Client Testing.desc: Opens a TLS 1.3-only connection to the target — use to confirm TLS 1.3 is available and to inspect the negotiated cipher suite and certificate chain.
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -tls1_3 < /dev/null 2>&1 | grep -E "Protocol|Cipher|Verify"
    Protocol  : TLSv1.3
    Cipher    : TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
    Verify return code: 0 (ok)

# Check supported TLS 1.3 groups:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -tls1_3 2>&1 | grep "Supported groups"
Supported groups: x25519:secp256r1:secp384r1

TLS Client Testing.desc: Shows full certificate chain
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -showcerts < /dev/null 2>&1 | grep "BEGIN"
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----     (leaf: CN=example.com)
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----     (intermediate: CN=R3)
# -showcerts dumps all certificates in the chain, not just the leaf.
# Pipe to a file to save the chain: openssl s_client ... -showcerts < /dev/null > chain.pem

TLS Client Testing.desc: Requests OCSP stapling
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -status < /dev/null 2>&1 | grep -A6 "OCSP response"
OCSP response:
======================================
OCSP Response Data:
    OCSP Response Status: successful (0x0)
    Cert Status: Good
    This Update: Jan  1 12:00:00 2026 GMT
# -status requests OCSP stapling from the server.  "Cert Status: Good" = not revoked.

TLS Client Testing.desc: Tests specific cipher
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -cipher ECDHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256 < /dev/null 2>&1 | grep "Cipher"
New, TLSv1.2, Cipher is ECDHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256
# Tests whether the server accepts a specific TLS 1.2 cipher suite.
# If rejected: "no peer certificate available" or "no ciphers available".

TLS Client Testing.desc: Tests specific EC curve
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -curves X25519 < /dev/null 2>&1 | grep "Cipher|Curve"
New, TLSv1.3, Cipher is TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
Server Temp Key: X25519, 253 bits
# -curves restricts the ECDH curves offered in the ClientHello.
# Common values: X25519, P-256, P-384, X448

TLS Client Testing.desc: Measures handshake performance
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_time -connect example.com:443 -time 10
No session reuse - Doing SSLv3/TLS in  1 connection - 10.00s
148 connections in 10s; 14.80 connections/user/sec, bytes read 28656
148 connections in  10.00 real seconds, 19 bytes read per connection
# Measures how many full TLS handshakes complete in the time window.
# Add -reuse to test session-resumption performance:
#   openssl s_time -connect example.com:443 -time 10 -reuse

TLS Client Testing.desc: Lists supported ciphers
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl ciphers -v | head -10
TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384     TLSv1.3  Kx=any   Au=any   Enc=AESGCM(256)  Mac=AEAD
TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256 TLSv1.3  Kx=any   Au=any   Enc=CHACHA20(256) Mac=AEAD
TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256     TLSv1.3  Kx=any   Au=any   Enc=AESGCM(128)  Mac=AEAD
ECDHE-ECDSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384 TLSv1.2 Kx=ECDH  Au=ECDSA Enc=AESGCM(256) Mac=AEAD
ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384   TLSv1.2 Kx=ECDH  Au=RSA   Enc=AESGCM(256) Mac=AEAD
# Columns: cipher name | min TLS version | key exchange | auth | encryption | MAC
# Filter: openssl ciphers -v 'HIGH:!aNULL:!MD5' to see a security policy.

TLS Client Testing.desc: Negotiate ALPN — check whether HTTP/2 is offered
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -servername example.com -alpn "h2,http/1.1" < /dev/null 2>&1 | grep "ALPN"
ALPN protocol: h2
# Negotiates HTTP/2 via the ALPN TLS extension.
# "h2" in the response confirms the server supports HTTP/2 (RFC 7540).
# "http/1.1" = server only supports HTTP/1.1.

TLS Client Testing.desc: Force session resumption to test session-ticket / ID reuse
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -servername example.com -reconnect < /dev/null 2>&1 | grep "Reused|New"
New,    TLSv1.3, Cipher is TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
Reused, TLSv1.3, Cipher is TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
Reused, TLSv1.3, Cipher is TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
# Makes 5 connections; "Reused" confirms TLS session resumption (PSK/ticket) works.
# Session resumption reduces handshake RTT and server CPU load.

TLS Client Testing.desc: Save the TLS session to disk
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -servername example.com -sess_out /tmp/session.pem < /dev/null
New, TLSv1.3, Cipher is TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
$ head -1 /tmp/session.pem
-----BEGIN SSL SESSION PARAMETERS-----
# Saves the TLS session ticket for manual resumption testing.
# Resume: openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -sess_in /tmp/session.pem

TLS Client Testing.desc: Resume using a previously-saved session
TLS Client Testing.example:
# First, save a session:
#   openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -servername example.com \
#     -sess_out sess.pem </dev/null
# Then resume:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -servername example.com \
    -sess_in sess.pem </dev/null 2>&1 | grep 'Reuse\|Session-ID'
Reused, TLSv1.3, Cipher is TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
# 'Reused' confirms the server accepted the session ticket.
# TLS 1.3 uses PSK tickets, not session IDs.

TLS Client Testing.desc: Fail the connection on any verify error (don't mask in output)
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect expired.badssl.com:443 -servername expired.badssl.com \
    -verify_return_error </dev/null
verify error:num=10:certificate has expired
CONNECT:error in s_client
# Without -verify_return_error, s_client shows verify errors but still connects.
# With this flag, any verify failure aborts the connection with a non-zero exit code.
# Useful in scripts: if openssl s_client ... -verify_return_error; then echo OK; fi

TLS Client Testing.desc: Validate using a custom trust bundle
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect internal.corp:443 -servername internal.corp \
    -CAfile /etc/ssl/certs/corp-root-ca.crt </dev/null 2>&1 | grep 'Verify return'
Verify return code: 0 (ok)
# Uses the specified CA bundle instead of the system trust store.
# Essential for internal PKI where the CA is not publicly trusted.
# Add -verify_return_error to fail the connection on any issue.

TLS Client Testing.cmd: openssl s_client -connect host:443 -servername host 2>/dev/null </dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -text
TLS Client Testing.desc: Full text of whatever cert the server presents
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -servername example.com \
    2>/dev/null </dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -text
Certificate:
    Data:
        Version: 3 (0x2)
        Serial Number: 04:a3:...
        Signature Algorithm: sha256WithRSAEncryption
        Issuer: C=US, O=DigiCert Inc, CN=DigiCert TLS RSA SHA256 2020 CA1
        Validity
            Not Before: Jan  1 00:00:00 2025 GMT
            Not After : Jan  1 00:00:00 2026 GMT
        Subject: CN=*.example.com
# Pipes just the server cert to x509 for full text decode without the TLS handshake noise.

TLS Client Testing.cmd: openssl s_client -connect host:443 -servername host 2>/dev/null </dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -subject -issuer -dates -ext subjectAltName
TLS Client Testing.desc: Common one-liner: subject/issuer/dates/SAN of the presented cert
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -servername example.com \
    2>/dev/null </dev/null \
  | openssl x509 -noout -subject -issuer -dates -ext subjectAltName
subject=CN=*.example.com
issuer=C=US, O=DigiCert Inc, CN=DigiCert TLS RSA SHA256 2020 CA1
notBefore=Jan  1 00:00:00 2025 GMT
notAfter =Jan  1 00:00:00 2026 GMT
X509v3 Subject Alternative Name:
    DNS:*.example.com, DNS:example.com

TLS Client Testing.desc: Offer a specific list of ECDH groups (TLS 1.3)
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -servername example.com \
    -groups X25519:P-256:P-384 </dev/null 2>&1 | grep 'Server Temp Key'
Server Temp Key: X25519, 253 bits
# Advertises X25519, P-256, P-384 in that preference order in the TLS ClientHello.
# 'Server Temp Key: X25519' confirms the server selected X25519 for ECDH.
# Use to verify that the server supports modern forward-secrecy key exchange.

TLS Client Testing.desc: Offer a specific list of signature algorithms
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -servername example.com \
    -sigalgs 'RSA+SHA256:ECDSA+SHA256' </dev/null 2>&1 | grep 'Cipher\|Protocol'
Protocol  : TLSv1.3
Cipher    : TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
# Restricts the signature_algorithms extension to RSA-SHA256 and ECDSA-SHA256.
# Useful for testing whether a server can negotiate with limited sig-alg support
# (e.g. validating compatibility with older clients).

TLS Client Testing.desc: Force a specific TLS 1.3 ciphersuite
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -servername example.com \
    -tls1_3 -ciphersuites TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 </dev/null 2>&1 | grep Cipher
Cipher    : TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256
# Forces TLS 1.3 and restricts to a single ciphersuite.
# If the server doesn't support TLS 1.3 or that ciphersuite, the handshake fails:
#   140...:error:...no protocols available
# Use to test ciphersuite negotiation or validate server TLS 1.3 support.

TLS Client Testing.desc: STARTTLS on IMAP (143)
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect mail.example.com:143 -starttls imap
* OK [CAPABILITY IMAP4rev1 STARTTLS] Dovecot ready.
a001 STARTTLS
* OK Begin TLS negotiation
[TLS handshake proceeds]
[Verify cert...]
Cipher    : ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384
# Connects to IMAP (143) and issues STARTTLS before negotiating TLS.
# Useful to verify that a mail server's STARTTLS/IMAP is working correctly.

TLS Client Testing.desc: STARTTLS on POP3 (110)
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect mail.example.com:110 -starttls pop3
+OK Dovecot POP3 ready.
STLS
+OK Begin TLS negotiation now.
[TLS handshake proceeds]
# Connects to POP3 port 110 and issues the STLS command to upgrade to TLS.
# Verifies the mail server's STARTTLS implementation for POP3 clients.

TLS Client Testing.desc: STARTTLS on FTP
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect ftp.example.com:21 -starttls ftp
220 ProFTPD Server ready.
AUTH TLS
234 AUTH TLS successful
[TLS handshake proceeds]
Cipher    : ECDHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256
# Connects to FTP control port 21 and sends AUTH TLS (FTPS explicit mode).
# Validates that the FTP server supports FTPS/TLS encryption.

TLS Client Testing.desc: STARTTLS on LDAP (389 → 636 TLS-upgrade)
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect dc.example.com:389 -starttls ldap
[LDAP StartTLS Extended Operation...]
[TLS handshake proceeds]
Cipher    : ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384
Verify return code: 0 (ok)
# Connects to LDAP port 389 and sends a StartTLS extended operation.
# Validates LDAP-over-TLS (not to be confused with LDAPS on port 636).
# For LDAPS (always-TLS): openssl s_client -connect dc.example.com:636

TLS Client Testing.desc: STARTTLS on PostgreSQL
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect db.example.com:5432 -starttls postgres
[PostgreSQL SSL request sent]
[TLS handshake proceeds]
Cipher    : ECDHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256
Verify return code: 0 (ok)
# Sends the PostgreSQL SSL negotiation byte (0x0000008004d2162f) before TLS.
# Validates that ssl=on is configured in postgresql.conf and pg_hba.conf
# requires hostssl connections for the target database.

TLS Client Testing.desc: STARTTLS on MySQL/MariaDB
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect db.example.com:3306 -starttls mysql
[MySQL Greeting packet received]
[MySQL SSL_REQUEST sent]
[TLS handshake proceeds]
Cipher    : ECDHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256
# Performs the MySQL capability exchange then sends an SSL_REQUEST packet
# before negotiating TLS — required by MySQL's STARTTLS-like protocol.
# Validates require_secure_transport=ON in MySQL/MariaDB server configuration.

TLS Client Testing.cmd: openssl s_client -connect host:5671 -servername host -tls1_3 -cert client.crt -key client.key
TLS Client Testing.desc: Present a client cert during a TLS handshake (mTLS)
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect rabbitmq.example.com:5671 -servername rabbitmq.example.com \
    -tls1_3 -cert client.crt -key client.key </dev/null 2>&1 | grep -E 'Verify|Cipher|Protocol'
Protocol  : TLSv1.3
Cipher    : TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
Verify return code: 0 (ok)
# Presents a client certificate during mTLS (mutual TLS) handshake.
# The server verifies the client cert against its CA trust store.
# Common for service-to-service auth in AMQP (5671), Kafka (9093), etcd, etc.

TLS Client Testing.desc: Extract just cipher/protocol/session-ID fields
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -servername example.com \
    -prexit 2>&1 </dev/null | grep -E '(Session-ID|Cipher|Protocol)'
Protocol  : TLSv1.3
Cipher    : TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
Session-ID: D3F09A...
# -prexit forces session info to print at exit (needed with </dev/null EOF).
# Extracts the three most useful fields in one line for scripting or audit.

TLS Client Testing.cmd: openssl s_client -connect host:443 -servername host -status 2>/dev/null </dev/null | grep -A5 'OCSP response'
TLS Client Testing.desc: Check whether the server sends an OCSP stapled response in the TLS handshake
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -servername example.com \
    -status 2>/dev/null </dev/null | grep -A5 'OCSP response'
OCSP response:
======================================
OCSP Response Data:
    OCSP Response Status: successful (0x0)
    Response Type: Basic OCSP Response
    This Update: Jun 20 08:00:00 2026 GMT
    Next Update: Jun 27 08:00:00 2026 GMT
    Cert Status: good
# If no OCSP response is stapled: 'OCSP response: no response sent'
# Stapling avoids the client having to query the OCSP responder directly.

TLS Client Testing.desc: Connect using TLS 1.3 only and request OCSP staple from the server
TLS Client Testing.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -tls1_3 -status -servername example.com
CONNECTED(00000003)
OCSP response:
======================================
OCSP Response Status: successful (0x0)
Response Type: Basic OCSP Response
Cert Status: Good
This Update: Jun 19 00:00:00 2026 GMT
Next Update: Jun 26 00:00:00 2026 GMT
======================================
depth=2 C=US, O=ISRG, CN=ISRG Root X1
depth=1 C=US, O=Let's Encrypt, CN=R3
depth=0 CN=example.com
...
SSL-Session:
    Protocol  : TLSv1.3
    Cipher    : TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
    Session-ID: A3B2...

# -tls1_3  → forces TLS 1.3 only; server falls back to alert if not supported
# -status  → sends a Certificate Status Request extension; server returns OCSP staple
# A "Cert Status: Good" OCSP staple proves the cert is not revoked, eliminating
# a separate OCSP round-trip for the client.

TLS Server Testing.desc: Tiny TLS test server that serves a status page on /
TLS Server Testing.example:
$ openssl s_server -accept 4433 -cert server.crt -key server.key -www
Using default temp DH parameters
ACCEPT
# Starts a test HTTPS server on port 4433.
# Test with: curl -k https://localhost:4433
# The -www flag returns an HTML page with TLS session details.

TLS Server Testing.desc: Require client cert (mTLS) on the test server
TLS Server Testing.example:
$ openssl s_server -accept 4433 -cert server.crt -key server.key -CAfile ca.crt -verify 1
Using default temp DH parameters
ACCEPT
# -verify 1 requests a client certificate (but does not require it).
# Use -Verify 1 (capital V) to make client authentication mandatory.

TLS Server Testing.desc: Force TLS 1.3 only on the test server
TLS Server Testing.example:
$ openssl s_server -accept 4433 -cert server.crt -key server.key -tls1_3
Using default temp DH parameters
ACCEPT
# Restricts the test server to TLS 1.3 only.
# Useful for verifying client TLS 1.3 support in isolation.

TLS Server Testing.desc: Advertise ALPN for testing client HTTP/2 negotiation
TLS Server Testing.example:
$ openssl s_server -accept 4433 -cert server.crt -key server.key -alpn "h2,http/1.1"
Using default temp DH parameters
ACCEPT
# Advertises HTTP/2 and HTTP/1.1 via ALPN extension.
# Test: openssl s_client -connect localhost:4433 -alpn h2,http/1.1

TLS Server Testing.desc: Benchmark: count fresh TLS handshakes in 30 seconds
TLS Server Testing.example:
$ openssl s_time -connect example.com:443 -new -time 30
Collecting connection statistics for 30 seconds
142 connections in 30.00s; 4.73 connections/user sec, bytes read 524288
142 connections in 30s; 4.73 connections/user sec
# Tests TLS handshake throughput using new sessions only.
# Useful for benchmarking server TLS performance or testing rate limits.

$ openssl s_time -connect example.com:443 -reuse -time 30
Collecting connection statistics for 30 seconds
2048 connections in 30.00s; 68.27 connections/user sec
# Tests session resumption throughput — resumed connections should be much faster.
# Compare with -new to measure the session resumption speedup ratio.

Cipher & Protocol Listing.desc: All ciphers this OpenSSL build supports with no anon/null ciphers
Cipher & Protocol Listing.example:
$ openssl ciphers -v "ALL:!aNULL:!eNULL" | head -5
TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384    TLSv1.3 Kx=any  Au=any  Enc=AESGCM(256) Mac=AEAD
TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256 TLSv1.3 Kx=any  Au=any  Enc=CHACHA20/POLY1305(256) Mac=AEAD
TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256    TLSv1.3 Kx=any  Au=any  Enc=AESGCM(128) Mac=AEAD
ECDHE-ECDSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384 TLSv1.2 Kx=ECDH Au=ECDSA Enc=AESGCM(256) Mac=AEAD
ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384   TLSv1.2 Kx=ECDH Au=RSA   Enc=AESGCM(256) Mac=AEAD
# Shows all cipher suites excluding anonymous and null-encryption variants.

Cipher & Protocol Listing.desc: Default cipher list (post-TLS-1.3: includes AEAD suites)
Cipher & Protocol Listing.example:
$ openssl ciphers -v "DEFAULT" | wc -l
77
$ openssl ciphers -v "DEFAULT" | head -3
TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384    TLSv1.3 Kx=any  Au=any  Enc=AESGCM(256) Mac=AEAD
TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256 TLSv1.3 Kx=any  Au=any  Enc=CHACHA20/POLY1305(256)
ECDHE-ECDSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384 TLSv1.2 Kx=ECDH ...
# Lists all cipher suites in the DEFAULT OpenSSL cipher list for this build.

$ openssl ciphers -v -tls1_2 | grep "TLSv1.2" | wc -l
49
# Lists cipher suites available for TLS 1.2 negotiation.
# Useful for confirming which TLS 1.2 ciphers your OpenSSL build supports.

Cipher & Protocol Listing.desc: Only TLS 1.3 ciphersuites ("-s" = supported by current settings)
Cipher & Protocol Listing.example:
$ openssl ciphers -v -s -tls1_3 2>/dev/null
TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384    TLSv1.3 Kx=any  Au=any  Enc=AESGCM(256) Mac=AEAD
TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256 TLSv1.3 Kx=any  Au=any  Enc=CHACHA20/POLY1305 Mac=AEAD
TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256    TLSv1.3 Kx=any  Au=any  Enc=AESGCM(128) Mac=AEAD
# -s = only show ciphers supported by the current SSL library build.
# TLS 1.3 cipher suites cannot be configured; all three are always enabled.

Cipher & Protocol Listing.desc: Include IANA/RFC cipher names next to OpenSSL names
Cipher & Protocol Listing.example:
$ openssl ciphers -stdname -v "DEFAULT" | head -5
TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384                 TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384          TLSv1.3 Kx=any  Au=any  Enc=AESGCM(256)
TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256           TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256    TLSv1.3 ...
ECDHE-ECDSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384         TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 TLSv1.2 ...
# -stdname shows both the OpenSSL name and the official IANA/RFC cipher name.
# Useful for cross-referencing cipher names between OpenSSL and IANA registries.

Cipher & Protocol Listing.desc: Cipher list with hex IDs — useful when correlating with packet captures
Cipher & Protocol Listing.example:
$ openssl ciphers -V "DEFAULT" | head -5
          0x13,0x02 - TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384      TLSv1.3 Kx=any  Au=any  Enc=AESGCM(256) Mac=AEAD
          0x13,0x03 - TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256 TLSv1.3 ...
          0xC0,0x2C - ECDHE-ECDSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384 TLSv1.2 ...
# -V (uppercase) shows the hex cipher ID codes alongside cipher names.
# Useful for matching cipher IDs seen in Wireshark TLS handshake captures.

Cipher & Protocol Listing.desc: First page of supported EC curves
Cipher & Protocol Listing.example:
$ openssl ecparam -list_curves | head -10
  secp112r1 : SECG/WTLS curve over a 112 bit prime field
  secp112r2 : SECG curve over a 112 bit prime field
  secp128r1 : SECG curve over a 128 bit prime field
  prime192v1: NIST/X9.62/SECG curve over a 192 bit prime field
  secp224k1 : SECG curve over a 224 bit prime field
  prime256v1: X9.62/SECG curve over a 256 bit prime field  (= P-256)
  secp384r1 : NIST/SECG curve over a 384 bit prime field   (= P-384)
  secp521r1 : NIST/SECG curve over a 521 bit prime field   (= P-521)
# Lists all elliptic curves available for ECDSA key generation and ECDH.

$ openssl list -providers
Providers:
  default
    name: OpenSSL Default Provider
    version: 3.0.11
    status: active
# Lists loaded OpenSSL 3.x providers.
# Add -verbose for build/directory information about each provider.

Cipher & Protocol Listing.example:

Cipher & Protocol Listing.desc: Full build info: version, compile flags, platform, openssldir
Cipher & Protocol Listing.example:
$ openssl version -a
OpenSSL 3.0.11 19 Sep 2023 (Library: OpenSSL 3.0.11 19 Sep 2023)
built on: Mon Sep 18 17:39:55 2023 UTC
platform: linux-x86_64
options:  bn(64,64)
compiler: gcc ...
OPENSSLDIR: "/usr/lib/ssl"
ENGINESDIR: "/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/engines-3"
