Seeding source: os-specific
CPUINFO: OPENSSL_ia32cap=...
# -a shows all version information including build options and directories.

TLS Handshake Debugging.desc: Print each TLS protocol message direction + type as it flies by
TLS Handshake Debugging.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -servername example.com -msg < /dev/null 2>&1 | head -20
>>> TLS 1.3, Handshake [length 0122], ClientHello
    01 00 01 1e 03 03 ...
<<< TLS 1.3, Handshake [length 0082], ServerHello
    02 00 00 7e 03 03 ...
# -msg prints raw TLS record contents (hex + type annotation).
# Shows ClientHello, ServerHello, Certificate, and Finished messages.

TLS Handshake Debugging.desc: Hex-dump every wire byte — very noisy but shows true framing
TLS Handshake Debugging.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -servername example.com -debug < /dev/null 2>&1 | head -30
write to 0x... [0x...] (316 bytes => 316 (0x13C))
0000 - 16 03 01 01 37 01 00 01  33 03 03 ...
# -debug prints all SSL/TLS read/write buffers in hex.
# More verbose than -msg — shows network-level byte streams.
# Useful when diagnosing protocol-level handshake failures.

TLS Handshake Debugging.desc: Full structured TLS message trace (extensions, certs, key_shares)
TLS Handshake Debugging.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -servername example.com -trace < /dev/null 2>&1 | head -20
Sent Record
  Header:
    Version = TLS 1.0 (0x301)
    Content Type = Handshake (22)
    Length = 317
  ClientHello, Length=313
    client_version=0x303 (TLS 1.2)
    random (len=32): ...
# -trace gives the most detailed view: structured field-by-field handshake decode.
# Requires OpenSSL built with enable-ssl-trace (default in most distro packages).

TLS Handshake Debugging.desc: Print the TLS state machine transitions
TLS Handshake Debugging.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -servername example.com -state < /dev/null 2>&1 | grep "SSL_connect"
SSL_connect:before SSL initialization
SSL_connect:SSLv3/TLS write client hello
SSL_connect:SSLv3/TLS read server hello
SSL_connect:TLSv1.3 read encrypted extensions
SSL_connect:SSLv3/TLS read server certificate
SSL_connect:SSLv3/TLS read server done
SSL_connect:SSLv3/TLS write finished
SSL_connect:SSLv3/TLS read finished
# -state prints each state transition during the TLS handshake.
# Useful for identifying exactly where a handshake fails.

TLS Handshake Debugging.desc: Ask the server for an OCSP stapled response
TLS Handshake Debugging.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -servername example.com -status < /dev/null 2>&1 | grep -A5 "OCSP"
OCSP response:
    OCSP Response Status: successful (0x0)
    Cert Status: Good
    This Update: Jan  1 12:00:00 2026 GMT
# -status requests OCSP stapling; the server sends the stapled OCSP response.
# If "No OCSP response received" — the server does not support OCSP stapling.

TLS Handshake Debugging.desc: Print every TLS extension the server sends back
TLS Handshake Debugging.example:
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -servername example.com -tlsextdebug < /dev/null 2>&1 | grep "TLS server extension"
TLS server extension "renegotiation info" (id=65281), len=1
TLS server extension "server name" (id=0), len=0
TLS server extension "EC point formats" (id=11), len=4
TLS server extension "session ticket" (id=35), len=0
TLS server extension "supported versions" (id=43), len=2
TLS server extension "key share" (id=51), len=36
# -tlsextdebug shows all TLS extensions in the ServerHello.

TLS Handshake Debugging.desc: Write a Wireshark-readable pre-master-secret file for decryption
TLS Handshake Debugging.example:
$ SSLKEYLOGFILE=/tmp/ssl-keys.log openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -servername example.com < /dev/null
$ head -2 /tmp/ssl-keys.log
CLIENT_HANDSHAKE_TRAFFIC_SECRET 1234abcd... 5678ef01...
SERVER_HANDSHAKE_TRAFFIC_SECRET 1234abcd... 9abc0123...
# Writes TLS 1.3 session keys in NSS key log format.
# Import into Wireshark: Edit → Preferences → Protocols → TLS → (Pre)-Master-Secret log

TLS Handshake Debugging.desc: Translate an OpenSSL error code (e.g. 0A000086) to a human string
TLS Handshake Debugging.example:
$ openssl errstr 0x0A000086
error:0A000086:SSL routines::certificate verify failed
$ openssl errstr 0x0A000418
error:0A000418:SSL routines::tlsv1 alert unknown ca
# Translates an OpenSSL hex error code to a human-readable error string.
# Find error codes in output like: 140234...error:0A000086:SSL routines...

OCSP & CRL.desc: Check a certificate's revocation status using OCSP
OCSP & CRL.example:
$ openssl ocsp -issuer intermediate.crt -cert server.crt -url http://r3.o.lencr.org -resp_text 2>/dev/null | grep -E "Cert Status|This Update"
Cert Status: good
This Update: Jan  1 12:00:00 2026 GMT
# Queries the OCSP responder URL to check if the certificate is revoked.
# Get the OCSP URL from: openssl x509 -in cert.crt -noout -ocsp_uri

OCSP & CRL.desc: Display the contents of a DER-encoded CRL file
OCSP & CRL.example:
$ openssl crl -in crl.der -inform DER -noout -text
Certificate Revocation List (CRL):
        Version 2 (0x1)
    Signature Algorithm: sha256WithRSAEncryption
        Issuer: CN=Example CA
        Last Update: Jan  1 12:00:00 2026 GMT
        Next Update: Jan 15 12:00:00 2026 GMT
    Revoked Certificates:
        Serial Number: 04C1D2E3F4
            Revocation Date: Dec 15 10:00:00 2025 GMT
# Decodes a DER-encoded CRL and lists revoked certificate serial numbers.

OCSP & CRL.desc: Display the contents of a PEM-encoded CRL file
OCSP & CRL.example:
$ openssl crl -in crl.pem -noout -text
Certificate Revocation List (CRL):
    Issuer: C=US, O=Let's Encrypt, CN=R3
    Last Update: Jan  1 12:00:00 2026 GMT
    Next Update: Jan  8 12:00:00 2026 GMT
    X509v3 CRL Number: 123456
    Revoked Certificates:
        Serial Number: 04...  Revocation Date: Dec 20 09:00:00 2025 GMT
# Decodes a PEM-encoded CRL — same output, no -inform flag needed.

OCSP & CRL.cmd: openssl ocsp -index index.txt -CA ca.crt -rsigner ca.crt -rkey ca.key -port 8080
OCSP & CRL.desc: Start a simple local OCSP responder for testing revocation
OCSP & CRL.example:
$ openssl ocsp -index index.txt -CA ca.crt -rsigner ca.crt -rkey ca.key -port 8080
Waiting for OCSP client connections...
# Starts a simple OCSP responder using the CA's index.txt revocation database.
# index.txt is the OpenSSL CA database (managed by openssl ca command).
# Test: openssl ocsp -issuer ca.crt -cert server.crt -url http://localhost:8080

OCSP & CRL.desc: OCSP query without verifying the OCSP responder's cert (debug only)
OCSP & CRL.example:
$ openssl ocsp -issuer intermediate.crt -cert server.crt -text -url http://r3.o.lencr.org -noverify 2>/dev/null | grep -E "Cert Status|This Update"
Cert Status: good
This Update: Jan  1 12:00:00 2026 GMT
# -text shows the full decoded OCSP response.
# -noverify skips OCSP response signature verification (useful for testing).

OCSP & CRL.cmd: openssl ocsp -issuer ca.crt -cert cert.crt -url http://ocsp.ca.com -header Host=ocsp.ca.com
OCSP & CRL.desc: OCSP with explicit Host header (some responders need it)
OCSP & CRL.example:
$ openssl ocsp -issuer intermediate.crt -cert server.crt     -url http://10.0.0.1 -header Host=ocsp.example.com 2>/dev/null | grep "Cert Status"
Cert Status: good
# -header Host= sends an HTTP Host header — required when querying by IP address
# or when the OCSP responder uses virtual hosting.

$ openssl ocsp -issuer intermediate.crt -cert server.crt -respin ocsp-response.der -text 2>/dev/null | grep -E "Cert Status|This Update|Next Update"
Cert Status: good
This Update: Jan  1 12:00:00 2026 GMT
Next Update: Jan  8 12:00:00 2026 GMT
# -respin reads a pre-fetched OCSP response binary rather than querying the network.
# Useful for verifying a stapled OCSP response offline.

OCSP & CRL.desc: Quick CRL info (who issued, lastUpdate, nextUpdate)
OCSP & CRL.example:
$ openssl crl -in crl.pem -noout -issuer -lastupdate -nextupdate
issuer=C=US, O=Let's Encrypt, CN=R3
lastUpdate=Jan  1 12:00:00 2026 GMT
nextUpdate=Jan  8 12:00:00 2026 GMT
# Quick CRL metadata check — shows who signed it and when it expires.
# Download fresh CRL: curl -O $(openssl x509 -in cert.crt -noout -ext crlDistributionPoints | grep URI | awk '{print $2}')

$ openssl crl -in crl.pem -noout -CAfile ca.crt
verify OK
# Verifies the CRL's digital signature against the CA certificate.
# "verify OK" = the CRL was signed by the expected CA.
# Error: "signature failure" = CRL may be forged or from a different CA.

OCSP & CRL.desc: List revoked serial numbers in a CRL
OCSP & CRL.example:
$ openssl crl -in crl.pem -noout -text | grep -A1 "Serial Number"
        Serial Number: 04C1D2E3F4A5B6C7
            Revocation Date: Dec 15 10:00:00 2025 GMT
        Serial Number: 04D5E6F7A8B9C0D1
            Revocation Date: Dec 20 09:00:00 2025 GMT
# Lists all revoked certificate serial numbers and their revocation dates.
# Compare against your cert's serial: openssl x509 -in cert.crt -noout -serial

Hashing & HMAC.example:
$ openssl dgst -sha256 /etc/hosts
SHA256(/etc/hosts)= 9f86d081884c7d659a2feaa0c55ad015a3bf4f1b2b0b822cd15d6c15b0f00a08
# Computes the SHA-256 digest of a file and prints in "name(file)= hash" format.
# Compare: sha256sum /etc/hosts — produces the same hash value.

$ openssl dgst -sha1 firmware.bin
SHA1(firmware.bin)= da39a3ee5e6b4b0d3255bfef95601890afd80709
# SHA-1 is cryptographically broken — use only for non-security checksums.
# Many firmware vendors still publish SHA-1 hashes for file integrity checks.

$ openssl dgst -md5 archive.tar.gz
MD5(archive.tar.gz)= d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e
# MD5 is cryptographically broken — do NOT use for security purposes.
# Acceptable only for non-security integrity checks (download verification).

$ openssl dgst -sha512 backup.tar.gz
SHA512(backup.tar.gz)= cf83e1357eefb8bdf1542850d66d8007d620e4050b5715dc83f4a921d36ce9ce47d0d13c5d85f2b0ff8318d2877eec2f63b931bd47417a81a538327af927da3e
# SHA-512 provides 256-bit collision resistance — suitable for high-security contexts.

$ openssl dgst -r -sha256 /etc/hostname
9f86d081884c7d659a2feaa0c55ad015a3bf4f1b2b0b822cd15d6c15b0f00a08 */etc/hostname
# -r prints the hash in sha256sum-compatible format (hash then filename).
# Useful for piping into sha256sum --check style scripts.

Hashing & HMAC.desc: Compute the SHA-256 digest of a file
Hashing & HMAC.example:
$ openssl dgst -sha256 report.pdf
SHA256(report.pdf)= 2cf24dba5fb0a30e26e83b2ac5b9e29e1b161e5c1fa7425e73043362938b9824
# Equivalent to sha256sum report.pdf — both use the same SHA-256 implementation.

Hashing & HMAC.desc: Compute the SHA-512 digest of a file
Hashing & HMAC.example:
$ openssl dgst -sha512 report.pdf
SHA512(report.pdf)= cf83e1357eefb8bdf1542850d66d8007...
# Equivalent to sha512sum report.pdf.

Hashing & HMAC.desc: Compute the MD5 digest of a file (legacy use only — not for security)
Hashing & HMAC.example:
$ openssl dgst -md5 legacy-archive.tar.gz
MD5(legacy-archive.tar.gz)= d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e
# Only use MD5 for compatibility with legacy systems — not for security.

$ openssl dgst -sha256 -c report.pdf
SHA256(report.pdf)= 2c:f2:4d:ba:5f:b0:a3:0e:...
# -c prints the hash with colon-separated bytes — useful for visual comparison.

$ openssl dgst -sha3-256 sensitive.dat
SHA3-256(sensitive.dat)= a7ffc6f8bf1ed76651c14756a061d662f580ff4de43b49fa82d80a4b80f8434
# SHA3-256 uses the Keccak sponge construction — distinct from SHA-2.
# Available in OpenSSL 1.1.1+ and OpenSSL 3.x.

$ openssl dgst -sha3-512 document.txt
SHA3-512(document.txt)= a69f73cca23a9ac5c8b567dc185a756e97c982164fe25859e0d1dcc1475c80a615b2123af1f5f94c11e3e9402c3ac558f500199d95b6d3e301758586281dcd26
# SHA3-512 uses the Keccak sponge construction — separate design from SHA-2.
# Available in OpenSSL 1.1.1+ and OpenSSL 3.x.

$ openssl dgst -blake2s256 document.txt
BLAKE2s256(document.txt)= 9aec6806794561107e594b1f6a8a6b0c92a0cba9acf5e5e93cca06f781813b0
# BLAKE2s is designed for 32-bit platforms; BLAKE2b for 64-bit.
# Often faster than SHA-256 in software; used in WireGuard and many modern protocols.

$ openssl dgst -sha256 -hmac "mysecretkey" message.txt
HMAC-SHA256(message.txt)= 3ecb5de3e83bcb8c9f4a0ac4e24a69a5b5f6c9d0e1f2a3b4c5d6e7f80910a1b2
# Computes HMAC-SHA256 using the string "mysecretkey" as the key.
# HMAC provides integrity + authentication (requires knowledge of the shared key).
# For a hex key: openssl dgst -sha256 -mac HMAC -macopt hexkey:0011223344 file.txt

Hashing & HMAC.cmd: openssl mac -macopt hexkey:00112233 -macopt digest:SHA256 HMAC < file.txt
Hashing & HMAC.example:
$ echo -n "message" | openssl mac -macopt hexkey:0011223344556677 -macopt digest:SHA256 HMAC
3D8A7B2F9C1E4D6A5B0F8E2C7A3D1B9E4F6C0A8D5E2B7F9C1A4D3B6E8F0A2C5
# OpenSSL 3.x mac subcommand — uses EVP MAC API with a hex-encoded key.
# Supports: HMAC, CMAC, GMAC, POLY1305, SIPHASH.

Hashing & HMAC.desc: Binary SHA-256 re-encoded as base64 (e.g. for SRI integrity attributes)
Hashing & HMAC.example:
$ openssl dgst -sha256 -binary /etc/hostname | openssl base64
n4bQgYhMfWWaL+qgxVrQFaO/TxsrC4Is0V1sFbDwCgg=
# Produces the SHA-256 digest as Base64 — useful for HTTP "Digest" headers
# and for comparing with base64-encoded hashes from APIs or configuration files.

Symmetric Encryption & Encoding.desc: Base64-encode a binary file to a text file
Symmetric Encryption & Encoding.example:
$ openssl base64 -in binary.dat -out binary.b64
$ cat binary.b64
SGVsbG8gV29ybGQh
# Encodes a binary file to Base64 text with 64-character line wrapping.
# One-liner: openssl base64 -in file | tr -d '
'  (no line breaks)

Symmetric Encryption & Encoding.desc: Base64-decode a text file back to binary
Symmetric Encryption & Encoding.example:
$ openssl base64 -d -in binary.b64 -out binary.dat
$ file binary.dat
binary.dat: data
# Decodes Base64 text back to binary.
# Equivalent to: base64 -d file.b64 > file.bin

Symmetric Encryption & Encoding.desc: Encrypt a file using AES-256-CBC with a password
Symmetric Encryption & Encoding.example:
$ openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -salt -pbkdf2 -in plaintext.txt -out encrypted.enc
enter aes-256-cbc encryption password:
Verifying - enter aes-256-cbc encryption password:
$ ls -lh encrypted.enc
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user 48 Jan  1 12:00 encrypted.enc
# -salt adds random salt; -pbkdf2 uses PBKDF2 for key derivation (OpenSSL 1.1.1+).
# Decrypt: openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -d -pbkdf2 -in encrypted.enc -out decrypted.txt

Symmetric Encryption & Encoding.desc: Decrypt an AES-256-CBC encrypted file using a password
Symmetric Encryption & Encoding.example:
$ openssl enc -d -aes-256-cbc -pbkdf2 -in encrypted.enc -out decrypted.txt
enter aes-256-cbc decryption password:
$ cat decrypted.txt
This is the original plaintext.
# -d flag decrypts; must match the same cipher, -pbkdf2, and password used to encrypt.
# OpenSSL 3.x: -iter <N> can specify the PBKDF2 iteration count explicitly.

$ openssl base64 -in binary.dat -out binary.b64
$ cat binary.b64
SGVsbG8gV29ybGQh
# Encodes a binary file to Base64 text with 64-character line wrapping.
# One-liner without line breaks: openssl base64 -in file | tr -d '
'

Symmetric Encryption & Encoding.desc: Decodes a Base64-encoded text file back into its original binary form — the inverse of "openssl base64 -in <file> -out <out>.b64"; functionally equivalent to the standalone "base64 -d" utility but useful when scripting against OpenSSL directly
Symmetric Encryption & Encoding.example:
$ openssl base64 -d -in binary.b64 -out binary.dat
$ file binary.dat
binary.dat: data

# Round-trip check — encode then decode should reproduce the original file:
$ openssl base64 -in original.bin -out original.b64
$ openssl base64 -d -in original.b64 -out roundtrip.bin
$ diff original.bin roundtrip.bin && echo "identical"
identical

# Equivalent to: base64 -d binary.b64 > binary.dat

$ openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -salt -pbkdf2 -in plaintext.txt -out encrypted.enc
enter aes-256-cbc encryption password:
Verifying - enter aes-256-cbc encryption password:
# -salt adds a random 8-byte salt prefix to the ciphertext (important for PBKDF2).
# Always include -pbkdf2 to use PBKDF2 key derivation (OpenSSL 1.1.1+).

$ openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -d -pbkdf2 -in encrypted.enc -out decrypted.txt
enter aes-256-cbc decryption password:
# Decrypts an AES-256-CBC encrypted file.  Must use the same parameters as encryption.
# If encrypted without -pbkdf2, omit -pbkdf2 on decryption (though deprecated).

$ openssl rand -hex 32
a3f8c21d9b04e765c8f2d91a4b30e5879c2a1d3b8f0e4c7d6a5b2e1f9c0d8a7b
# Generates 32 cryptographically random bytes printed as 64 hex characters.
# Useful for AES-256 keys, HMAC secrets, or unique identifiers.

$ openssl enc -aes-256-gcm -pbkdf2 -in plaintext.txt -out encrypted.bin
enter aes-256-gcm encryption password:
Verifying - enter aes-256-gcm encryption password:
# AES-256-GCM provides authenticated encryption — integrity built in.
# Decrypt: openssl enc -d -aes-256-gcm -pbkdf2 -in encrypted.bin -out decrypted.txt

$ openssl enc -d -aes-256-gcm -pbkdf2 -in encrypted.bin -out decrypted.txt
enter aes-256-gcm decryption password:
# Decrypts an AES-256-GCM encrypted file (authenticated decryption).
# Tampered ciphertext will fail with an authentication error.

$ openssl enc -aes-256-ctr -pbkdf2 -in plaintext.txt -out encrypted.bin
enter aes-256-ctr encryption password:
# AES-CTR mode: stream cipher mode — no padding, efficient for large files.
# CTR mode does not include authentication — pair with an HMAC for integrity.

$ openssl enc -chacha20 -pbkdf2 -in plaintext.txt -out encrypted.bin
enter chacha20 encryption password:
# ChaCha20 stream cipher — fast on CPUs without AES-NI hardware acceleration.
# For authenticated encryption, use chacha20-poly1305 instead (AEAD).

Symmetric Encryption & Encoding.desc: Explicit KDF parameters — portable between boxes
Symmetric Encryption & Encoding.example:
$ openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -pbkdf2 -md sha256 -iter 100000 \
    -in plain.txt -out enc.bin
enter AES-256-CBC encryption password: ****
Verifying - enter AES-256-CBC encryption password: ****
# -pbkdf2: uses PBKDF2 key derivation (stronger than legacy EVP_BytesToKey)
# -md sha256 -iter 100000: SHA-256 HMAC, 100k iterations (OWASP minimum)
# Decrypt: openssl enc -d -aes-256-cbc -pbkdf2 -md sha256 -iter 100000 \
#            -in enc.bin -out plain.txt

$ openssl rand -base64 32 | tr -d '=' | head -c 32
Z3kQP8mRvXnYt2jCwLfHoUgsBd9AeI4N
# Generates 32 bytes of random data, base64-encodes it, strips padding '=',
# then trims to 32 printable characters — safe for tokens, API keys, passwords.
# Adjust 'head -c N' for desired token length.
# For hex: openssl rand -hex 32  (64 hex chars = 32 bytes)

$ openssl rand -engine rdrand -hex 32
engine "rdrand" set.
a3f819d2c4b07e5f...(64 hex chars)...
# Forces use of Intel RDRAND hardware RNG instead of the software PRNG.
# -engine rdrand requires the rdrand engine to be available (OpenSSL 1.1.x).
# OpenSSL 3.x uses the FIPS provider or ossl_legacy.so engine instead.
# Verify RDRAND support: openssl engine -t rdrand

Digital Signatures.desc: Digitally sign a file using an RSA private key
Digital Signatures.example:
$ openssl dgst -sha256 -sign server.key -out signature.bin document.txt
$ ls -lh signature.bin
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user 256 Jan  1 12:00 signature.bin
# Creates a detached RSA PKCS#1 v1.5 signature over the SHA-256 hash of file.txt.
# Verify: openssl dgst -sha256 -verify public.key -signature signature.bin document.txt

Digital Signatures.desc: Verify a digital signature against a file using the public key
Digital Signatures.example:
$ openssl dgst -sha256 -verify public.key -signature signature.bin document.txt
Verified OK
# "Verified OK" = the signature is valid for this file and public key.
# "Verification Failure" = file was tampered with or wrong key was used.

Digital Signatures.cmd: openssl pkeyutl -sign -inkey private.key -in hash.bin -out sig.bin -pkeyopt digest:sha256
Digital Signatures.example:
$ openssl dgst -sha256 -binary document.txt > hash.bin
$ openssl pkeyutl -sign -inkey server.key -in hash.bin -out sig.bin -pkeyopt digest:sha256
$ ls -lh sig.bin
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user 256 Jan  1 12:00 sig.bin
# Signs a pre-computed hash using RSA PKCS#1 v1.5 with SHA-256.
# pkeyutl works with RSA, EC (ECDSA), and Ed25519 keys.

$ openssl pkeyutl -verify -pubin -inkey public.key -in hash.bin -sigfile sig.bin
Signature Verified Successfully
# Verifies a signature created with openssl pkeyutl -sign.
# -pubin = input is a public key file (SubjectPublicKeyInfo format).

Digital Signatures.cmd: openssl pkeyutl -sign -inkey rsa.key -in hash.bin -out sig.bin -pkeyopt rsa_padding_mode:pss -pkeyopt rsa_pss_saltlen:digest
Digital Signatures.desc: RSA-PSS signature (required for TLS 1.3 with RSA)
Digital Signatures.example:
$ openssl pkeyutl -sign -inkey server.key -in hash.bin -out sig.bin     -pkeyopt rsa_padding_mode:pss -pkeyopt rsa_pss_saltlen:digest
$ ls -lh sig.bin
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user 256 Jan  1 12:00 sig.bin
# RSA-PSS signature — probabilistic padding, required by TLS 1.3 and X.509 v3.
# -pkeyopt rsa_pss_saltlen:digest = salt length equals the hash output length.

Digital Signatures.cmd: openssl cms -sign -in file.txt -signer cert.crt -inkey private.key -out signed.p7s -outform DER
Digital Signatures.example:
$ openssl cms -sign -in document.txt -signer server.crt -inkey server.key     -out signed.p7s -outform DER
$ ls -lh signed.p7s
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user 3.4K Jan  1 12:00 signed.p7s
# Creates a detached CMS/S/MIME signature in DER format.
# Verify: openssl cms -verify -in signed.p7s -inform DER -content document.txt -CAfile ca.crt

Digital Signatures.cmd: openssl cms -verify -in signed.p7s -inform DER -content file.txt -CAfile ca.crt
Digital Signatures.example:
$ openssl cms -verify -in signed.p7s -inform DER -content document.txt -CAfile ca.crt
Verification successful
# "Verification successful" = signature is valid and signer cert is trusted.
# The -content flag provides the original data for detached signatures.

Performance & Benchmarks.example:
$ openssl speed 2>/dev/null | tail -10
Doing md5 for 3s on 16 size blocks: 13824000 md5s in 3.00s
Doing sha256 for 3s on 16 size blocks: 10234000 sha256s in 3.00s
Doing aes-256-cbc for 3s on 16 size blocks: 19284000 aes-256-cbcs in 3.00s
type             16 bytes     64 bytes    256 bytes   1024 bytes   8192 bytes
md5             73760.00k   226432.00k   554240.00k   827392.00k   985088.00k
sha256          54581.33k   171264.00k   406357.33k   613376.00k   711680.00k
aes-256 cbc    102848.00k   303616.00k   631296.00k   815104.00k   880640.00k
# Runs OpenSSL's built-in benchmark across all available algorithms.

Performance & Benchmarks.desc: Benchmark AES-256-GCM via EVP (uses AES-NI if available)
Performance & Benchmarks.example:
$ openssl speed -evp aes-256-gcm 2>/dev/null | tail -3
Doing aes-256-gcm for 3s on 16 size blocks: 7421560 aes-256-gcms in 3.00s
type             16 bytes     64 bytes    256 bytes   1024 bytes   8192 bytes  16384 bytes
aes-256-gcm     39581.65k   129493.33k   404821.33k  1111040.00k  1638400.00k  1720320.00k
# -evp benchmarks using the high-level EVP API (uses AES-NI hardware if available).
# Compare hardware-accelerated vs software: run with and without OPENSSL_ia32cap=~0x200000200000000.

$ openssl speed -evp sha256 2>/dev/null | tail -3
Doing sha256 for 3s on 16 size blocks: 18230000 sha256s in 3.00s
type             16 bytes     64 bytes    256 bytes   1024 bytes   8192 bytes
sha256          97226.67k   282026.67k   637269.33k  1045504.00k  1228800.00k
# Benchmarks SHA-256 via EVP API.  On modern CPUs with SHA-NI, throughput is
# significantly higher than on older CPUs without the instruction set extension.

$ openssl speed rsa4096 2>/dev/null | tail -3
Doing 4096 bit private rsa's for 10s: 312 4096 bit private RSA's in 10.00s
Doing 4096 bit public rsa's for 10s: 18432 4096 bit public RSA's in 10.00s
                  sign    verify    sign/s verify/s
rsa 4096 bits  0.032051s 0.000543s     31.2   1843.2
# 4096-bit RSA is roughly 8x slower than 2048-bit for signing operations.
# sign/s = server-side TLS handshake rate; verify/s = client side (much cheaper).

Performance & Benchmarks.desc: Benchmark P-384 sign + ECDH op rates
Performance & Benchmarks.example:
$ openssl speed ecdsap384 ecdhp384 2>/dev/null | grep -E "sign|ecdh"
                        sign    verify    sign/s verify/s
 384 bit ecdsa (P-384) 0.0000842s 0.0001741s  11876.0  5742.5
                              op      op/s
 384 bit ecdh (P-384)  0.0000892s  11211.2
# P-384 provides ~192-bit security — required for Suite B / CNSS Policy 15.
# P-384 is ~2x slower than P-256 for both signing and key agreement.

Performance & Benchmarks.desc: Benchmark with 8 async jobs (multi-engine / QAT / KTLS)
Performance & Benchmarks.example:
$ openssl speed -async_jobs 8 rsa2048 2>/dev/null | tail -3
Doing 2048 bit private rsa's for 10s (8 async jobs): 3072 2048 bit private RSA's in 10.00s
                  sign    verify    sign/s verify/s
rsa 2048 bits  0.003255s 0.000091s    307.2  13107.2
# -async_jobs enables asynchronous mode for engines that support it (e.g. QAT).
# On standard CPUs without async hardware, throughput may be similar to synchronous.

$ openssl speed -multi 4 -evp aes-256-gcm 2>/dev/null | tail -3
type             16 bytes     64 bytes    256 bytes   1024 bytes   8192 bytes  16384 bytes
aes-256-gcm    298240.00k  1048576.00k  3166208.00k  8601600.00k 12353536.00k 12845056.00k
# -multi 4 runs 4 parallel benchmark threads — shows aggregate throughput.
# Replace 4 with $(nproc) to benchmark using all available CPU cores.

Providers & FIPS (OpenSSL 3+).desc: Providers loaded with their version and parameters
Providers & FIPS (OpenSSL 3+).example:
$ openssl list -providers -verbose
Providers:
  default
    name: OpenSSL Default Provider
    version: 3.0.11
    status: active
  legacy
    name: OpenSSL Legacy Provider
    version: 3.0.11
    status: active (if loaded)
# Lists loaded OpenSSL 3.x providers and their status.
# Providers replace legacy engine support in OpenSSL 3.x.

Providers & FIPS (OpenSSL 3+).desc: Cipher algorithms exposed by a specific provider
Providers & FIPS (OpenSSL 3+).example:
$ openssl list -cipher-algorithms -provider legacy | grep -E "^DES|^RC4|^BF"
BF-CBC
DES-CBC
DES-EDE3-CBC
RC4
# The legacy provider enables deprecated algorithms (DES, RC4, Blowfish, MD2).
# Load at runtime: openssl enc -provider legacy -des-cbc -in file.txt ...
# Do NOT use these for security purposes — enabled only for compatibility.

Providers & FIPS (OpenSSL 3+).desc: Run a command under an alternate openssl.cnf (e.g. FIPS config)
Providers & FIPS (OpenSSL 3+).example:
$ OPENSSL_CONF=/etc/ssl/fipsmodule.cnf openssl list -providers
Providers:
  fips
    name: OpenSSL FIPS Provider
    version: 3.0.9
    status: active
  base
    name: OpenSSL Base Provider
    version: 3.0.9
    status: active
# Loads the FIPS provider via a custom openssl.cnf pointing to fipsmodule.cnf.
# When active, only FIPS 140-3 approved algorithms are available.

$ openssl fipsinstall -out /etc/ssl/fipsmodule.cnf -module /usr/lib64/ossl-modules/fips.so
INSTALL PASSED
# Validates and installs the FIPS provider module.
# Generates fipsmodule.cnf with the HMAC integrity check value for the FIPS .so.
# Must be re-run after OS/package updates that replace the FIPS module binary.

$ openssl fipsinstall -in /etc/ssl/fipsmodule.cnf -verify
VERIFY PASSED
# Verifies that the FIPS module's HMAC matches the value in fipsmodule.cnf.
# "VERIFY PASSED" = the FIPS .so has not been tampered with since installation.
# Run after any system update that might have touched the FIPS library.

$ openssl list -kdf-algorithms | head -8
HKDF
PBKDF2
SCRYPT
SSHKDF
TLS1-PRF
SSKDF
X942KDF-ASN1
ARGON2D
# Lists available Key Derivation Function (KDF) algorithms.
# PBKDF2 and HKDF are most common; ARGON2D requires the legacy or argon2 provider.

Providers & FIPS (OpenSSL 3+).cmd: openssl kdf -keylen 32 -kdfopt digest:SHA256 -kdfopt key:password -kdfopt salt:salt -kdfopt iter:100000 PBKDF2
Providers & FIPS (OpenSSL 3+).desc: Run PBKDF2 from the command line (OpenSSL 3+)
Providers & FIPS (OpenSSL 3+).example:
$ openssl kdf -keylen 32 -kdfopt digest:SHA256 -kdfopt key:password     -kdfopt salt:saltsalt -kdfopt iter:100000 PBKDF2 | xxd | head -2
00000000: 3c4f 9a1b 2d5e 6f78 90ab c1d2 e3f4 a5b6  <O..-^ox........
00000010: c7d8 e9f0 a1b2 c3d4 e5f6 0102 0304 0506  ................
# Derives a 32-byte key using PBKDF2-SHA256 with 100,000 iterations.
# OpenSSL 3.x kdf subcommand replaces manual EVP_KDF API usage for testing.

Providers & FIPS (OpenSSL 3+).desc: Generic OSSL_STORE loader (OpenSSL 3+) — inspects any URI-addressable store (file:, engine:, pkcs11:) without needing a type-specific subcommand
Providers & FIPS (OpenSSL 3+).example:
$ openssl storeutl -noout -text file:cert.pem
Certificate
subject=CN = www.example.com, O = Example Corp
issuer=CN = DigiCert TLS RSA SHA256 2020 CA1
Not Before: Jan 15 00:00:00 2026 GMT
Not After : Jan 15 23:59:59 2027 GMT
# storeutl unifies access to certs/keys/CRLs across file:, pkcs11: and other
# OSSL_STORE-registered schemes — useful for inspecting HSM-backed keys.

Show & Status.cmd: show ap dot11 24ghz summary (with channel/power asterisk legend)
Show & Status.desc: Per-radio summary. Asterisks (*) on channel or power indicate manually assigned (not RRM-controlled). TX power format: <current_level>/<max_level> (<dBm>); level 1 = max, level 8 = min.
Show & Status.example:
* global assignment
AP Name       Mac Address     Slot  Admin State  Oper State  Width  Txpwr          Channel    Mode
AP-OFFICE-01  70bd.9685.9c80  1     Enabled      Up          40     3/8 (14 dBm)   (149,153)  REAP

Show & Status.desc: Lists configured media-stream (multicast-direct) groups with their multicast address range and QoS/admission settings, used to convert best-effort multicast video/voice into per-client unicast-like delivery
Show & Status.example:
WLC# show wireless media-stream group summary

Number of Groups: 2

Group Name          Start IP         End IP           Qos
------------------  ---------------  ---------------  ---
video-stream-hd     239.1.1.1        239.1.1.10        Platinum
voice-multicast      239.2.2.1        239.2.2.5         Platinum

What it means:
- Media-stream groups let the WLC admit specific multicast ranges onto
  wireless with per-client stream limits, instead of blasting multicast
  at the lowest mandatory rate to every client.
- If a group shows here but streams still look choppy, check
  "show wireless media-stream client" for per-client admission failures.

AP Management.desc: Rename an AP. Useful after replacing hardware — keeps the policy/site/RF-tag bindings.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-TEMP-01 name AP-OFFICE-01
WLC-01# show ap summary | include AP-OFFICE-01
AP-OFFICE-01  3   CW9166I-ROW  70bd.9685.9c80  cc6e.2aae.dc20  10.16.2.11  Registered

AP Management.desc: Set the AP location string (max 256 chars). Visible in WLC, Cisco DNAC, and Prime Infrastructure for map placement.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 location "London HQ – 3rd Floor – East Wing"
WLC-01# show ap name AP-OFFICE-01 config general | include Location
  AP Location                            : London HQ – 3rd Floor – East Wing

AP Management.desc: Set primary controller for an AP. AP joins this controller first on reboot.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 controller primary WLC-01 10.10.0.10
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 controller secondary WLC-02 10.10.0.11
WLC-01# show ap name AP-OFFICE-01 config general | include Controller
  Primary Cisco Controller Name        : WLC-01
  Primary Cisco Controller IP Address  : 10.10.0.10
  Secondary Cisco Controller Name      : WLC-02
  Secondary Cisco Controller IP Address: 10.10.0.11

AP Management.desc: Reboot an AP. Tears down the CAPWAP tunnel; clients on this AP will deauth and roam to neighbours.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 reset
WLC-01# show ap summary | include AP-OFFICE-01
AP-OFFICE-01  3   CW9166I-ROW  70bd.9685.9c80  cc6e.2aae.dc20  10.16.2.11  Joining
# After ~30 seconds:
AP-OFFICE-01  3   CW9166I-ROW  70bd.9685.9c80  cc6e.2aae.dc20  10.16.2.11  Registered

AP Management.desc: Admin-disable an AP. Both radios off; CAPWAP stays up. Use for maintenance.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 shutdown
WLC-01# show ap dot11 5ghz summary | include AP-OFFICE-01
AP-OFFICE-01  70bd.9685.9c80  1   Disabled  Down  40   -  -  REAP

AP Management.desc: Re-enable a previously shutdown AP. Radios come back up in ~5 seconds; clients re-associate.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 no shutdown
WLC-01# show ap dot11 5ghz summary | include AP-OFFICE-01
AP-OFFICE-01  70bd.9685.9c80  1  Enabled  Up  40  3/8 (14 dBm)  (149,153)  REAP

AP Management.desc: Pin 2.4 GHz channel manually. Asterisk in `show ap dot11 24ghz summary` indicates manual assignment.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 dot11 24ghz channel 6
WLC-01# show ap dot11 24ghz summary | include AP-OFFICE-01
AP-OFFICE-01  70bd.9685.9c80  0  Enabled  Up  20  3/8 (14 dBm)  6*  REAP
# Revert to RRM control:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 dot11 24ghz channel global

AP Management.desc: Pin 5 GHz channel manually. Use only when RRM picks a suboptimal channel (e.g. due to recurring DFS hits or known interferer).
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 dot11 5ghz channel 36
WLC-01# show ap dot11 5ghz summary | include AP-OFFICE-01
AP-OFFICE-01  70bd.9685.9c80  1  Enabled  Up  40  3/8 (14 dBm)  (36,40)*  REAP
# Asterisk (36,40)* = manually pinned; not under RRM/DCA control.
# Revert with: ap name AP-OFFICE-01 dot11 5ghz channel global

AP Management.desc: Manual TX power level 1-8 (1 = max, 8 = min). `global` reverts to RRM/RF-profile control.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 dot11 24ghz txpower 3
WLC-01# show ap dot11 24ghz summary | include AP-OFFICE-01
AP-OFFICE-01  70bd.9685.9c80  0  Enabled  Up  20  3*/8 (14 dBm)  6  REAP
# (3*) = manually pinned power; not under RRM/TPC control.

AP Management.desc: Manual 5 GHz TX power level 1-8 (1 = max, 8 = min). `global` reverts to RRM/RF-profile.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 dot11 5ghz txpower 3
WLC-01# show ap dot11 5ghz summary | include AP-OFFICE-01
AP-OFFICE-01  70bd.9685.9c80  1  Enabled  Up  40  3*/8 (14 dBm)  (149,153)  REAP
# Revert: ap name AP-OFFICE-01 dot11 5ghz txpower global

AP Management.desc: Disable 2.4 GHz radio on a single AP. Useful for testing or band-shaping.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 dot11 24ghz shutdown
WLC-01# show ap dot11 24ghz summary | include AP-OFFICE-01
AP-OFFICE-01  70bd.9685.9c80  0  Disabled  Down  -  -  -  REAP
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 no dot11 24ghz shutdown   ; re-enable

AP Management.desc: Disable the 5 GHz radio on a single AP. Other radios (2.4 GHz, 6 GHz) remain up.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 dot11 5ghz shutdown
WLC-01# show ap dot11 5ghz summary | include AP-OFFICE-01
AP-OFFICE-01  70bd.9685.9c80  1  Disabled  Down  -  -  -  REAP
# Re-enable:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 no dot11 5ghz shutdown

AP Management.desc: Set AP to Local mode (central switching). Standard for site-wide deployments where the WLC is on the same campus and clients tunnel back to the WLC.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 mode local
Changing the AP's mode will cause the AP to reboot. Continue? [y/n]: y
WLC-01# show ap name AP-OFFICE-01 config general | include Mode
  AP Mode                                : Local
  Operation State                         : Joining
# After ~30 sec reboot completes:
WLC-01# show ap name AP-OFFICE-01 config general | include Mode
  AP Mode                                : Local
  Operation State                         : Registered

AP Management.desc: Set AP to FlexConnect mode — local switching at the branch. Required for remote sites with high WAN latency.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 mode flexconnect
AP will reboot. Continue? [y/n]: y
# After reboot, confirm Flex profile is bound via site tag:
WLC-01# show ap name AP-OFFICE-01 config general | include Mode
  AP Mode                            : FlexConnect

AP Management.desc: Monitor mode — no client service, only RRM/WIPS scanning. Used as a dedicated scanner AP for high-fidelity RF visibility.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 mode monitor
Changing the AP's mode will cause the AP to reboot. Continue? [y/n]: y
WLC-01# show ap name AP-OFFICE-01 config general | include Mode
  AP Mode                                : Monitor
# Monitor APs scan ALL channels but serve NO clients — useful at
# 1:10 ratio with serving APs for full rogue/interferer coverage.

AP Management.desc: Per-AP site-tag override (otherwise inherited from default).
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 tag site SITE-FL1
WLC-01# show ap tag summary | include AP-OFFICE-01
AP-OFFICE-01  cc6e.2aae.dc20  SITE-FL1  POLICY-FL1  RF-DEFAULT  No  Static

AP Management.desc: Per-AP policy-tag override (otherwise inherited from default-policy-tag).
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 tag policy POLICY-FL1
WLC-01# show ap tag summary | include AP-OFFICE-01
AP-OFFICE-01  cc6e.2aae.dc20  SITE-FL1  POLICY-FL1  RF-DEFAULT  No  Static

AP Management.desc: Assigns an RF tag to a specific AP
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 tag rf HIGH-DENSITY
WLC-01# show ap tag summary | include AP-OFFICE-01
AP-OFFICE-01  cc6e.2aae.dc20  SITE-FL1  POLICY-FL1  HIGH-DENSITY  No  Static

AP Management.desc: Turn off the AP status LED. Useful in conference rooms or where the LED is distracting.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 led disable
WLC-01# show ap name AP-OFFICE-01 config general | include LED
  AP LED State                        : Disabled

AP Management.desc: AP join priority when the AP load is at limit. 1 = highest, 4 = lowest. Lower-priority APs are kicked off first when the WLC hits its AP cap.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# ap name AP-OFFICE-01 priority 1
WLC-01# show ap name AP-OFFICE-01 config general | include Priority
  AP Priority                          : 1

AP Management.desc: Single-AP summary view. Faster than scrolling `show ap summary` on a large WLC.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# show ap name AP-OFFICE-01 summary
AP Name           Slots AP Model       Ethernet MAC    Radio MAC     IP Address    State
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
AP-OFFICE-01      3     CW9166I-ROW    70bd.9685.9c80  cc6e.2aae.dc20 10.16.2.11   Registered

AP Management.desc: Same comprehensive AP config view (alias of `show ap config general <ap>`).

  IP Default Gateway                   : 10.16.2.1

AP Management.desc: Shows memory utilisation statistics for the specified AP including total RAM, used memory, and free memory — sustained high memory use may indicate a software issue or excessive client load.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# show ap name AP-OFFICE-01 stats memory

  Swap Used      :   0 MB
  Buffers        :  24 MB
  Cached         :  88 MB
# Memory consistently >85% warrants investigation; restart radio or upgrade AP firmware

AP Management.desc: AP temperature reading. Sustained high values approaching the threshold = airflow/cooling issue.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# show ap name AP-OFFICE-01 stats temperature
Temperature Statistics for AP-OFFICE-01
  Current Temperature:   42 C
  Max Threshold:         85 C
  Min Threshold:        -10 C
  Status:               Normal

AP Management.desc: AP power state — actual power being drawn vs negotiated PoE class.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# show ap name AP-OFFICE-01 stats power
Power Statistics for AP-OFFICE-01

  PoE Class:            Class 4 (802.3at PoE+, 30W)
  Power Drawn:          28.5 W
  Status:               Normal (full radio chain)
# If Class 3 (15.4W) is granted on a Wi-Fi 6E AP, the AP runs in reduced-power mode.

AP Management.desc: PoE negotiation history and current class.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# show ap name AP-OFFICE-01 stats poe
PoE Stats for AP-OFFICE-01

  Requested PoE Class    : Class 6 (802.3bt PoE++ Type 3)

  Requested Power        : 60W
  Last Negotiation Time  : 14:30:01 Apr 21 2026
  Negotiation Result     : Reduced (switch granted less than requested)

WLC-01# show ap name AP-OFFICE-01 stats reboot-history

14:23:45 Feb 5 2026    Power cycle

  No crash files present.

# crashinfo_20260417-141233.txt (1198765 bytes)

cc6e.2aae.dc30  AP-OFFICE-02        10.16.2.12         03/15/26 14:30:05
...

AP Management.desc: Detailed per-AP join statistics including failure reasons by phase.
AP Management.example:
WLC-01# show ap join stats detailed AP-OFFICE-01
AP Discovery Phase Statistics:
  Total Discovery Requests Received: 2
  Discovery Requests Replied To    : 2

  Total Config Requests Sent   : 13
