Ref 16. Process Control & Job Management.example:
$ jobs
[1]-  Running    tar -czf backup.tar.gz /var/www &
[2]+  Stopped    vim config.txt

$ bg %1
[1]+ tar -czf backup.tar.gz /var/www &
# resumes job 1 in background

$ fg %1
tar -czf backup.tar.gz /var/www
# brings job 1 to foreground

$ nice -n 10 make -j4
# runs make with lower priority (niceness 10)

$ sudo nice -n -5 rsync -avz /src/ /dest/
# runs rsync at higher priority (negative nice, requires root)

$ renice -5 -p 4821
4821 (process ID) old priority 0, new priority -5
$ ps -o pid,ni,comm -p 4821
  PID  NI COMMAND
 4821  -5 myapp

$ nohup ./long-script.sh > /tmp/out.log 2>&1 &
[1] 12345
# process continues after logout; output to /tmp/out.log

$ long-running-script.sh &
[1] 5102
$ disown
$ jobs
(no output — job detached from shell)
$ ps aux | grep long-running-script
user  5102  0.5  0.1  12345  2048 ?  S  10:15  0:03 bash long-running-script.sh

$ rsync -avz /data/ remote:/backup/ &
[1] 6234
$ # Shell returns immediately; rsync runs in background
[1]+  Done  rsync -avz /data/ remote:/backup/

Ref 17. Advanced Networking.cmd: ip link add vlan10 link eth0 type vlan id 10
Ref 17. Advanced Networking.desc: Create an 802.1Q tagged VLAN sub-interface (VLAN 10) on top of eth0
Ref 17. Advanced Networking.example:
# Create the VLAN interface
ip link add vlan10 link eth0 type vlan id 10

# Assign an IP address
ip addr add 192.168.10.1/24 dev vlan10

# Bring it up
ip link set vlan10 up

# Verify
ip -d link show vlan10
3: vlan10@eth0: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 1500 qdisc noqueue state UP
    link/ether aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
    vlan protocol 802.1Q id 10 <REORDER_HDR>

# Remove: ip link del vlan10

# destination mirrors source exactly; extra files in dest deleted

$ rsync -aAXv --exclude={"/dev/*","/proc/*","/sys/*","/tmp/*"} / /backup/
# full system backup preserving ACLs (A) and extended attrs (X)

$ dd if=/dev/sda of=/backup/sda.img bs=4M status=progress
5242880000 bytes (5.2 GB, 4.9 GiB) copied, 120.3 s, 43.6 MB/s
# raw disk image; requires device unmounted or read-only

$ dd if=/dev/zero of=/swapfile bs=1M count=2048 status=progress
2147483648 bytes (2.1 GB) copied, 4.5 s, 477 MB/s
$ chmod 600 /swapfile && mkswap /swapfile && swapon /swapfile

$ ddrescue -d /dev/sda /backup/sda.img /backup/sda.log
GNU ddrescue 1.25
rescued: 499.8 GB, error size: 0 B, current rate: 120 MB/s
# ddrescue recovers failing drives; continues around bad sectors

$ btrfs subvolume snapshot /data /snap/data-2026-06-21
Create a snapshot of '/data' in '/snap/data-2026-06-21'

$ export RESTIC_PASSWORD=changeme
$ restic init --repo /backup/restic-repo

# use after OS reinstall to remove stale host key

$ ssh-keyscan -H 192.168.1.5 >> ~/.ssh/known_hosts

# adds host key to known_hosts without manual verification prompt

$ ssh -L 8080:internal-web:80 -N -f admin@bastion.corp.com
# forwards localhost:8080 → internal-web:80 via bastion; runs in background

$ ssh -R 2222:localhost:22 admin@remote-host
# exposes local SSH port 22 as port 2222 on remote-host

$ ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no -o UserKnownHostsFile=/dev/null admin@10.0.0.5

$ ssh -o ConnectTimeout=5 admin@10.0.0.100

# fails fast after 5s instead of waiting OS default (~75s)

# sends keepalive every 30s; disconnects after 3 missed responses

Ref 30. Bash Scripting Essentials.example:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -u
# Treat unset variables as errors:
# $ MYVAR=""
# $ echo $TYPO   → error: TYPO: unbound variable

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -x
# Print each command before executing:
# + echo hello
# hello
# + rm /tmp/test.txt

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -o pipefail
# Pipe fails if ANY command in the pipe fails:
# grep "error" app.log | sort | uniq  → exits non-zero if grep finds nothing

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
# Combined: exit on error (-e), undefined vars (-u), pipefail
# Standard safe header for production scripts

#!/usr/bin/env bash
IFS=$'\n\t'
# sets IFS to newline+tab (prevents word-splitting on spaces)
# safer than default IFS=' \t\n' when processing filenames

#!/usr/bin/env bash
echo "Deployment complete"
echo "Server: $HOSTNAME"
echo "Time: $(date)"
# echo to stdout; use printf for reliable formatting

#!/usr/bin/env bash
name="Alice"
printf "Hello, %s!\n" "$name"
# printf is more reliable than echo for special chars

#!/usr/bin/env bash
read -r filename
echo "Processing: $filename"
# -r: raw mode; prevents backslash interpretation

#!/usr/bin/env bash
if check_service nginx; then
    exit 0  # success
else
    exit 1  # failure
fi

#!/usr/bin/env bash
if [[ ! -f "$CONFIG" ]]; then
    echo "ERROR: config file not found: $CONFIG" >&2
    exit 1
fi

Display Filters — Basic.desc: Match packets to or from a single host
Display Filters — Basic.example:
# Apply in the Wireshark filter bar or with tshark -Y
# Matches any frame where 10.0.0.1 appears as source OR destination.
Frame 42: 74 bytes  10.0.0.1 → 10.0.0.50  TCP 80 → 54321 [SYN,ACK]
Frame 43: 60 bytes  10.0.0.50 → 10.0.0.1  TCP 54321 → 80  [ACK]
# Use "Follow TCP Stream" on any matching frame to reconstruct the session.

Display Filters — Basic.desc: Packets sourced from a given IP
Display Filters — Basic.example:
# Shows only frames originating from 10.0.0.1 — one direction only.
Frame 42:  10.0.0.1 → 10.0.0.50  TCP [SYN]
Frame 44:  10.0.0.1 → 10.0.0.50  HTTP GET /index.html
# Pair with ip.dst == 10.0.0.1 to see the reply stream separately.

Display Filters — Basic.desc: Packets destined to a given IP
Display Filters — Basic.example:
# Shows only frames arriving at 10.0.0.1.
Frame 43:  10.0.0.50 → 10.0.0.1  TCP [SYN,ACK]
Frame 47:  10.0.0.50 → 10.0.0.1  HTTP 200 OK (text/html)
# Useful for counting inbound traffic volume or spotting unexpected senders.

Display Filters — Basic.desc: CIDR — whole subnet, source or destination
Display Filters — Basic.example:
# Matches any packet where source OR destination falls in the /24 subnet.
# Wireshark expands the CIDR internally; works with /8, /16, /24, /32.
Frame 5:  10.0.0.1  → 10.0.0.50   ARP  Who has 10.0.0.50?
Frame 6:  10.0.0.50 → 10.0.0.1    ARP  10.0.0.50 is at 00:0c:29:aa:bb:cc
Frame 7:  10.0.0.1  → 10.0.0.50   TCP [SYN]

Display Filters — Basic.desc: Exclude anything involving a specific host
Display Filters — Basic.example:
# Hides all traffic involving 10.0.0.1 — useful when that host is noisy
# and you want to focus on other conversation.
# Equivalent: !(ip.addr == 10.0.0.1)
Frame 55:  10.0.0.2 → 8.8.8.8  DNS Standard query A example.com
Frame 56:  8.8.8.8  → 10.0.0.2 DNS Standard query response A 93.184.216.34
# All 10.0.0.1 frames are suppressed from view; the capture is unchanged.

# Both addresses must appear in each frame — effectively a conversation filter.
# Identical to using the "Follow Stream" right-click on a matching packet.
Frame 10: 10.0.0.1 → 10.0.0.2  TCP [SYN]
Frame 11: 10.0.0.2 → 10.0.0.1  TCP [SYN,ACK]
Frame 12: 10.0.0.1 → 10.0.0.2  TCP [ACK]
# Only these two hosts are shown; any third-party traffic is hidden.

# IPv6 equivalent of ip.addr — matches source or destination.
# Use ipv6.src and ipv6.dst for directional filtering.
Frame 20: 2001:db8::1 → 2001:db8::2  ICMPv6 Echo Request
Frame 21: 2001:db8::2 → 2001:db8::1  ICMPv6 Echo Reply
# Combine with tcp.port or udp.port to narrow by service.

# Matches frames where the MAC appears in either src or dst Ethernet header.
# Useful when the MAC is known but IP has changed (DHCP, failover).
Frame 3: aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff → ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff  ARP Who has 192.168.1.1?
Frame 4: 00:50:56:8f:00:01 → aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff  ARP 192.168.1.1 is at 00:50:56:8f:00:01
# Use eth.src or eth.dst to restrict to one direction.

# Captures all Ethernet-level broadcasts — ARP requests, some DHCP, NetBIOS.
Frame 1:  00:11:22:aa:bb:cc → ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff  ARP Who has 10.0.0.1?
Frame 2:  00:de:ad:be:ef:01 → ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff  DHCP Discover
Frame 3:  00:50:79:66:68:09 → ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff  NetBIOS Name Service
# High broadcast rate (>100/s on a VLAN) suggests a broadcast storm or misconfiguration.

Display Filters — Basic.desc: Frames tagged with a specific VLAN
Display Filters — Basic.example:
# Matches 802.1Q-tagged frames with VLAN ID 20.
# Note: Wireshark must see the raw tagged frames (e.g. from a SPAN/mirror port);
# access-port captures have the tag stripped by the switch.
Frame 5:  10.20.0.1 → 10.20.0.50  TCP [SYN]  (VLAN 20)
Frame 6:  10.20.0.50 → 10.20.0.1  TCP [SYN,ACK] (VLAN 20)
# Combine: vlan.id == 20 and tcp.port == 443 to narrow to HTTPS on VLAN 20.

Display Filters — Basic.desc: Jumbo frames or fragmented IP above 1500
Display Filters — Basic.example:
# Matches frames larger than 1500 bytes (standard Ethernet MTU).
# Indicates jumbo frames (if switch/NIC supports them) or oversized frames.
# Frame 12:  10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.10  TCP [PSH,ACK] Len=4096  (jumbo path)
# If DF=1 frames > 1500 are seen dropping — suspect PMTUD blackhole.

# Narrows the display to a specific 5-minute window.
# Correlate packet captures with a known incident time window.
# Adjust timestamps to UTC or local time matching your capture clock.
# Wireshark clock format: View → Time Display Format → Date and Time of Day

Display Filters — Basic.desc: Jump to a specific frame by number
Display Filters — Basic.example:
# Jumps display to frame 1234 in the packet list.
# Equivalent to Ctrl+G (Go to Packet) → enter 1234.
# Useful when referencing a specific frame from a prior analysis session.

Display Filters — Basic.desc: Packets near TTL exhaustion — loops, traceroute replies
Display Filters — Basic.example:
# Matches packets with TTL nearly expired (fewer than 5 hops remaining).
# Indicates routing loops or unexpectedly long paths.
# Frame 8:  src=10.0.0.1 dst=10.0.0.2  TTL=2  ICMP Time Exceeded
# To exclude traceroute noise: ip.ttl < 5 and not icmp.type == 11

Display Filters — Basic.desc: DF-bit packets near MTU — classic PMTUD hunt
Display Filters — Basic.example:
# Matches IP packets with Don't Fragment (DF) set that approach MTU size.
# Key indicator for Path MTU Discovery (PMTUD) blackhole issues.
# If DF=1 packets > 1400 bytes are being dropped, look for:
#   icmp.type == 3 and icmp.code == 4  (Fragmentation Needed replies)
# Absence of those ICMP messages confirms a PMTUD blackhole.

Display Filters — Basic.desc: All QUIC (HTTP/3 transport) packets — UDP 443 with QUIC magic bytes
Display Filters — Basic.example:
# Matches all QUIC datagrams.
# QUIC runs over UDP (typically port 443) and carries HTTP/3.
# Frame 1:  10.0.0.5:54321 → 10.0.0.10:443  QUIC Initial  DCID=...
# Frame 2:  10.0.0.10:443  → 10.0.0.5:54321  QUIC Handshake
# If you see no QUIC frames on port 443, the server may have disabled HTTP/3.
# Combine: quic and ip.addr == 10.0.0.10  → QUIC to a specific server.

Display Filters — Basic.desc: Packets using Multipath TCP (MPTCP) extensions in the TCP options
Display Filters — Basic.example:
# Matches TCP frames that carry Multipath TCP (RFC 8684) options.
# MPTCP allows a single connection to span multiple network paths.
# Frame 1:  10.0.0.5:54321 → 10.0.0.10:80  [SYN]  Options: MPTCP MP_CAPABLE
# Frame 2:  10.0.0.10:80   → 10.0.0.5:54321 [SYN,ACK] Options: MPTCP MP_CAPABLE
# MPTCP is used by iOS for Siri and increasingly in Linux kernel 5.6+.

Display Filters — TCP/UDP.example:
# Matches TCP frames where source OR destination port is 443 (HTTPS).
# Shows the full bidirectional TLS session.
# Frame 1: 10.0.0.5:54321 → 10.0.0.10:443  [SYN]
# Frame 2: 10.0.0.10:443  → 10.0.0.5:54321  [SYN,ACK]
# Pair with the tls filter to inspect handshake details.

# Matches TCP frames where the source port is 22 (SSH server replies).
# Shows only traffic leaving an SSH server — the response direction.
# Frame 5:  10.0.0.1:22 → 10.0.0.5:60234  [PSH,ACK] Len=52
# For the full session use: tcp.port == 22

# Matches TCP frames destined for port 80 — client-to-server HTTP.
# Frame 1:  10.0.0.5:55123 → 10.0.0.10:80  [SYN]
# Frame 3:  10.0.0.5:55123 → 10.0.0.10:80  GET /index.html HTTP/1.1
# Pair with tcp.srcport == 80 to see server responses separately.

# Matches pure SYN packets — new TCP connection attempts (3-way handshake step 1).
# Frame 1:  10.0.0.5:54321 → 10.0.0.10:80  [SYN] Seq=0 Win=65535
# High rate from one IP = port scan or SYN flood.
# Pair with Statistics → Conversations to count connections per source.

# Matches SYN-ACK packets — server accepting a connection (step 2).
# Frame 2:  10.0.0.10:80 → 10.0.0.5:54321  [SYN,ACK] Seq=0 Ack=1
# SYN seen but no SYN-ACK = port is closed or firewalled.

# Matches all TCP RST packets — abrupt connection termination.
# RST from server = port closed or session rejected by application.
# RST from client = application aborted the connection.
# Frame 4:  10.0.0.10:80 → 10.0.0.5:54321  [RST,ACK] Seq=1 Ack=1
# High RST rate often means firewall blocking or a crashed service.

# Matches FIN packets — graceful TCP connection teardown.
# Normal flow: client FIN → server FIN,ACK → client ACK
# Frame 20: 10.0.0.5:54321 → 10.0.0.10:80  [FIN,ACK] Seq=423 Ack=1890
# Absence of FIN (only RST) indicates ungraceful disconnection.

# Matches frames Wireshark has identified as TCP retransmissions.
# [TCP Retransmission] appears in the Info column for these frames.
# Frame 15: 10.0.0.5:54321 → 10.0.0.10:80  [TCP Retransmission] Seq=1001
# High retransmission rate → packet loss, congestion, or MTU mismatch.

# Matches fast retransmissions — triggered by 3 duplicate ACKs.
# Indicates packet loss detected before RTO timer expires.
# Frame 18: 10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.10  [TCP Fast Retransmission] Seq=2001
# Fast retransmission is less severe than RTO expiry but still means loss.

# Matches duplicate ACK frames — receiver signalling a missing segment.
# Three duplicate ACKs trigger fast retransmission at the sender.
# Frame 16: 10.0.0.10 → 10.0.0.5  [TCP Dup ACK 14#1] Ack=2001 Win=65535
# Frame 17: 10.0.0.10 → 10.0.0.5  [TCP Dup ACK 14#2] Ack=2001 Win=65535
# Consistent dup ACKs point to segment loss between sender and receiver.

# Matches frames Wireshark flagged as arriving out of sequence.
# [TCP Out-Of-Order] in the Info column — receiver must re-assemble.
# Frame 22: 10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.10  [TCP Out-Of-Order] Seq=3001 Len=1460
# Caused by asymmetric routing, NIC offloading, or real reorder in transit.
# Combine with tcp.stream eq <n> to isolate a single flow.

Display Filters — TCP/UDP.desc: Segments never seen — capture gaps or real loss
Display Filters — TCP/UDP.example:
# Matches frames where Wireshark infers a gap in the sequence space.
# [TCP Previous segment not captured] appears in Info.
# Frame 25: 10.0.0.10 → 10.0.0.5  [TCP Dup ACK 20#1] Ack=2001
# Often caused by an asymmetric capture path (you see the ACK but not the data).
# True loss triggers retransmission; check for tcp.analysis.retransmission nearby.

# Matches TCP Zero Window announcements — receiver buffer completely full.
# [TCP ZeroWindow] in the Info column. Data transfer stalls.
# Frame 30: 10.0.0.10 → 10.0.0.5  [TCP ZeroWindow] Win=0
# Frame 31: 10.0.0.5  → 10.0.0.10 [TCP ZeroWindowProbe]
# Frame 32: 10.0.0.10 → 10.0.0.5  [TCP ZeroWindowProbeAck]
# Indicates slow application consumer or memory pressure on the receiver.

# Matches TCP Window Update segments — receiver re-opening a previously
# shrunk or zeroed window after freeing buffer space.
# [TCP Window Update] in the Info column.
# Frame 33: 10.0.0.10 → 10.0.0.5  [TCP Window Update] Win=65535
# A Window Update after Zero Window means the receiver is ready to accept data again.

Display Filters — TCP/UDP.desc: All Wireshark TCP analysis tags in one filter
Display Filters — TCP/UDP.example:
# Matches any TCP frame that Wireshark has flagged with an analysis note.
# Includes retransmissions, out-of-order, zero-window, fast-retransmit, dup-ACK, etc.
# Equivalent to filtering every tcp.analysis.* field simultaneously.
# Frame 15: [TCP Retransmission]
# Frame 22: [TCP Out-Of-Order]
# Frame 30: [TCP ZeroWindow]
# Use this as a broad 'health check' filter, then narrow to specific flag types.

# Matches TCP frames where the raw window size field is zero.
# Indicates the receiver's buffer is full — sender must pause data transfer.
# Frame 30: 10.0.0.10 → 10.0.0.5  [TCP ZeroWindow]  Win=0
# After the receiver drains its buffer it sends a Window Update (Win > 0).
# Sustained zero windows point to slow application consumption or memory pressure.
# Pair with tcp.analysis.zero_window for the Wireshark-annotated view.

Display Filters — TCP/UDP.desc: TCP segments that actually carry payload
Display Filters — TCP/UDP.example:
# Matches TCP segments that carry payload (filters out pure ACKs).
# Reduces noise when you only want to see data-bearing frames.
# Frame 10: 10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.10  [PSH,ACK] Len=512
# Useful for payload analysis; combine with ip.addr to scope to one host.

Display Filters — TCP/UDP.desc: First TCP stream — right-click → Follow Stream to set this
Display Filters — TCP/UDP.example:
# Matches all frames belonging to TCP stream index 0.
# Wireshark assigns a sequential stream index (eq 0, eq 1, eq 2 …) to each
# unique TCP connection in the capture.
# Right-click → Follow → TCP Stream auto-applies this filter.
# Change the number to isolate a different connection:
#   tcp.stream eq 5  → the 6th TCP connection in the capture

# Matches DNS traffic on UDP port 53 (queries and responses).
# Frame 1:  10.0.0.5:52341 → 8.8.8.8:53    DNS query A www.example.com
# Frame 2:  8.8.8.8:53    → 10.0.0.5:52341  DNS response A 93.184.216.34
# For DNS over TCP (large responses, AXFR zone transfers): tcp.port == 53
# Combine: udp.port == 53 and dns.flags.rcode != 0  → failed DNS queries

Display Filters — TCP/UDP.desc: Large UDP datagrams — e.g. DNS EDNS0 responses
Display Filters — TCP/UDP.example:
# Matches UDP datagrams whose total length exceeds 512 bytes.
# DNS traditionally caps UDP at 512 bytes (EDNS0 raises this).
# Frame 8:  8.8.8.8 → 10.0.0.5  DNS response Len=1452 (EDNS0)
# Large UDP from unknown sources can indicate amplification-attack traffic.

# Matches TCP SYN frames advertising an MSS below the standard 1460 bytes.
# Standard MSS = 1500 (MTU) - 20 (IP) - 20 (TCP) = 1460 bytes.
# Lower values indicate:
#   - PPPoE path: MSS = 1452 (1500 - 8 PPPoE overhead)
#   - VPN tunnel: MSS reduced to fit tunnel encapsulation
#   - Explicit MSS clamping by a firewall/NAT device
# Frame 1:  SYN  Options: MSS=1452  → likely PPPoE or VPN client

Display Filters — HTTP/HTTPS.example:
# Matches all HTTP/1.x frames (requests and responses on any port).
# Does not show HTTPS (TLS-encrypted) — use tls for encrypted traffic.
# Frame 3:  10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.10  GET /index.html HTTP/1.1
# Frame 4:  10.0.0.10 → 10.0.0.5  HTTP/1.1 200 OK  (text/html, 4.2 KB)

# Matches only HTTP request frames (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, etc.).
# Filters out responses — shows only what the client is requesting.
# Frame 3:  GET /api/v1/users HTTP/1.1  Host: 10.0.0.10
# Frame 7:  POST /login HTTP/1.1  Host: 10.0.0.10  Content-Length: 32
# Useful for auditing all endpoints accessed during a test session.

# Matches only HTTP response frames from the server.
# Frame 4:  HTTP/1.1 200 OK   Content-Type: application/json
# Frame 8:  HTTP/1.1 401 Unauthorized  WWW-Authenticate: Bearer
# Pair with http.response.code to filter specific status codes.

# Matches HTTP POST requests — form submissions, API writes, file uploads.
# Frame 7:  POST /login HTTP/1.1  Host: 10.0.0.10
#   Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
#   Content-Length: 32   username=admin&password=secret
# Right-click → Follow → HTTP Stream to see the full request + response body.

# Matches HTTP GET requests — page loads, API reads, resource fetches.
# Frame 3:  GET /index.html HTTP/1.1   Host: example.com
# Frame 5:  GET /api/v1/status HTTP/1.1  Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGc...

# Matches any HTTP request whose URI path contains the string "login".
# Frame 7:  POST /login HTTP/1.1
# Frame 12: GET /user/login?redirect=/dashboard HTTP/1.1
# Useful for tracking authentication flows during security testing.

Display Filters — HTTP/HTTPS.desc: Requests for a specific Host header
Display Filters — HTTP/HTTPS.example:
# Matches HTTP requests targeting a specific virtual host.
# Frame 3:  GET /index.html HTTP/1.1  Host: example.com
# Useful when a server hosts multiple virtual hosts on the same IP.

# Matches HTTP requests made by curl (or tools advertising curl as UA).
# Frame 3:  GET /api/data HTTP/1.1  User-Agent: curl/8.5.0
# Useful for identifying scripted or automated HTTP traffic.

# Matches HTTP 500 Internal Server Error responses.
# Frame 8:  HTTP/1.1 500 Internal Server Error  Content-Length: 1234
# Follow the stream to see the error body and the matching request.
# Use during load testing to quickly find server-side failures.

# Matches all HTTP 4xx (client errors) and 5xx (server errors).
# Frame 8:  HTTP/1.1 401 Unauthorized
# Frame 12: HTTP/1.1 403 Forbidden
# Frame 15: HTTP/1.1 500 Internal Server Error
# Good starting filter when debugging broken API calls or auth failures.

# Matches HTTP 301 (Moved Permanently) and 302 (Found/Temporary) redirects.
# Frame 6:  10.0.0.10 → 10.0.0.5  HTTP/1.1 301 Moved Permanently
#            Location: https://example.com/
# Useful for tracing redirect chains or detecting redirect loops.

Display Filters — HTTP/HTTPS.desc: Cookie header with a session cookie
Display Filters — HTTP/HTTPS.example:
# Matches HTTP requests that carry a Cookie header containing 'sessionid'.
# Frame 12: 10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.10  GET /dashboard HTTP/1.1
#   Cookie: sessionid=abc123xyz; csrftoken=def456
# Useful for session hijacking analysis or verifying Secure/HttpOnly flags.

# Matches any frame that has an HTTP Authorization header.
# Frame 3:  10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.10  GET /api/data HTTP/1.1
#   Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiJ9...
# Frame 7:  10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.10  GET /admin HTTP/1.1
#   Authorization: Basic dXNlcjpwYXNzd29yZA==   (base64 user:password)
# Unencrypted Basic auth over HTTP exposes credentials in the clear.

# Matches frames with a Content-Type header that includes 'json'.
# Frame 15: 10.0.0.10 → 10.0.0.5  HTTP/1.1 200 OK
#   Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8
# Combine with http.request.method == "POST" to see all JSON API payloads.

# Matches all HTTP/2 frames (binary framing layer over TLS or cleartext).
# Requires decryption if running over TLS — set SSLKEYLOGFILE first.
# Frame 5:  10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.10  HTTP2 SETTINGS frame
# Frame 6:  10.0.0.10 → 10.0.0.5  HTTP2 SETTINGS ACK
# Frame 7:  10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.10  HTTP2 HEADERS (GET /api/v1/data)
# HTTP/2 multiplexes multiple streams over one TCP connection.

# Matches HTTP/2 HEADERS frames (type 1) — equivalent to HTTP/1.1 request/response headers.
# Frame 7:  10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.10  HTTP2 HEADERS
#   :method: GET
#   :path: /api/v1/users
#   :scheme: https
#   :authority: api.example.com
# HTTP/2 frame types: 0=DATA, 1=HEADERS, 4=SETTINGS, 6=PING, 7=GOAWAY, 8=WINDOW_UPDATE

# Matches all QUIC (Quick UDP Internet Connections) packets — HTTP/3 transport.
# QUIC runs over UDP (typically port 443) with TLS 1.3 embedded.
# Frame 1:  10.0.0.5:54321 → 10.0.0.10:443  QUIC Initial  DCID=0x1234...
# Frame 2:  10.0.0.10:443  → 10.0.0.5:54321  QUIC Handshake  SCID=0xABCD...
# Frame 3:  10.0.0.5:54321 → 10.0.0.10:443  QUIC 1-RTT (application data)
# Requires Wireshark 3.3+ for full QUIC dissection.

Display Filters — HTTP/HTTPS.desc: Matches QUIC long-header Initial packets on QUIC v1 traffic — the packet-type enum reorders on QUIC v2, where Initial is value 1 instead (Retry becomes 0).
Display Filters — HTTP/HTTPS.example:
# QUIC v1: 0=Initial, 1=0-RTT, 2=Handshake, 3=Retry — this filter targets the handshake-start packet.
No.   Time      Source        Destination     Protocol  Info
 14   0.001203  10.0.0.5      93.184.216.34   QUIC      Initial, DCID=... SCID=... Token Length: 0
 15   0.001987  10.0.0.5      93.184.216.34   QUIC      Initial, DCID=... SCID=... PKN: 1
# On QUIC v2 captures use quic.long.packet_type == 1 for the equivalent Initial-packet match.

Display Filters — TLS/SSL.example:
# Matches all TLS/SSL record layer frames (handshake, data, alerts).
# Frame 5:  10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.10  TLSv1.3 Handshake Protocol: Client Hello
# Frame 6:  10.0.0.10 → 10.0.0.5  TLSv1.3 Handshake Protocol: Server Hello
# Application data records appear as [encrypted] after the handshake.

# Matches TLS handshake messages only (not application data or alerts).
# Frame 5:  TLSv1.3  Handshake Protocol: Client Hello
# Frame 6:  TLSv1.3  Handshake Protocol: Server Hello
# Frame 7:  TLSv1.3  Handshake Protocol: Certificate
# Frame 8:  TLSv1.3  Handshake Protocol: Finished

Display Filters — TLS/SSL.desc: ClientHello — inspect SNI, cipher list, versions
Display Filters — TLS/SSL.example:
# Matches TLS ClientHello messages (type 1) — client initiates TLS.
# Frame 5:  TLSv1.3 Client Hello
#   SNI: example.com
#   Supported versions: TLS 1.3, TLS 1.2
#   Cipher suites offered: 17
# Useful for inventorying TLS versions and cipher suites in use.

# Matches TLS ServerHello messages (type 2) — server selects parameters.
# Frame 6:  TLSv1.3 Server Hello
#   Version: TLS 1.3
#   Cipher Suite: TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
#   Session ID: (empty — TLS 1.3 uses PSK/ticket resumption)

Display Filters — TLS/SSL.desc: Certificate message — the server's cert chain
Display Filters — TLS/SSL.example:
# Matches Certificate messages (type 11) — server's certificate chain.
# Frame 7:  TLSv1.3  Certificate
#   Certificate 1: CN=example.com   Issuer: CN=R3
#   Certificate 2: CN=R3            Issuer: CN=ISRG Root X1
# Expand the certificate tree to inspect expiry dates and SANs.

# Matches ClientHello frames with SNI set to "example.com".
# SNI is sent in the ClientHello extension before encryption.
# Frame 5:  Client Hello — server_name: example.com
# Useful for filtering TLS sessions to a specific virtual host.

Display Filters — TLS/SSL.desc: Matches TLS 1.0 record-layer frames (legacy_version 0x0301) — flags a deprecated protocol version (RFC 8996) that shouldn't appear on a modern network.
Display Filters — TLS/SSL.example:
# Matches TLS 1.0 record-layer frames (version 0x0301).
# TLS 1.0 is deprecated (RFC 8996) — should not appear in modern traffic.
# Frame 5:  TLSv1.0 Record Layer  — legacy client detected.
# Pair with ip.src to identify which clients are still using TLS 1.0.

# Matches TLS 1.2 record-layer frames (version 0x0303).
# TLS 1.2 is still widely used but being phased out in favour of 1.3.
# Note: TLS 1.3 also uses 0x0303 in the record layer for backward compatibility.
# Frame 5:  TLSv1.2 Record Layer: Handshake Protocol: Client Hello

Display Filters — TLS/SSL.desc: Matches TLS record-layer frames whose declared version is TLS 1.3 (0x0304) — note that TLS 1.3 ClientHello messages still advertise a legacy_version of 0x0303 for middlebox compatibility, so also check the "Supported Versions" extension when confirming a handshake actually negotiated 1.3
Display Filters — TLS/SSL.example:
# Matches TLS 1.3 record-layer frames (version 0x0304).
# TLS 1.3 provides forward secrecy and a shorter 1-RTT handshake.
Frame 5:  TLSv1.3 Record Layer: Handshake Protocol: Client Hello
  Supported Versions extension: TLS 1.3 (0x0304), TLS 1.2 (0x0303)

# To find the negotiated version regardless of record-layer wrapper version,
# filter on the extension instead:
tls.handshake.extensions.supported_version == 0x0304

Display Filters — TLS/SSL.desc: TLS alert records — version/cert/other handshake failures
Display Filters — TLS/SSL.example:
# Matches TLS Alert messages — handshake failures or protocol errors.
# Frame 9:  TLSv1.3 Alert  Level: Fatal  Description: Certificate Unknown (46)
# Frame 10: TLSv1.3 Alert  Level: Fatal  Description: Handshake Failure (40)
# Alert level 2 = Fatal (connection terminated); level 1 = Warning.

# Matches TLS Fatal alerts (level 2) — connection will be torn down.
# Frame 18: 10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.10  TLSv1.3 Alert (Level: Fatal, Description: Certificate Unknown)
# Common fatal alert descriptions:
#   42 = bad_certificate       48 = unknown_ca
#   70 = protocol_version      40 = handshake_failure
# A fatal alert always precedes an RST or FIN.

Display Filters — TLS/SSL.desc: Any handshake that negotiates a cipher
Display Filters — TLS/SSL.example:
# Matches any TLS handshake frame that contains a CipherSuite field.
# Appears in both ClientHello (offered suites) and ServerHello (chosen suite).
# Frame 2:  ClientHello  — cipher_suites: TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384, TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 ...
# Frame 3:  ServerHello  — cipher_suite:  TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
# Use tls.handshake.ciphersuite == 0x1302 to match a specific suite (e.g. TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384).

Display Filters — DNS.example:
# Matches all DNS traffic (queries and responses, UDP/TCP port 53).
# Frame 1:  10.0.0.5 → 8.8.8.8   DNS  Standard query  A  example.com
# Frame 2:  8.8.8.8 → 10.0.0.5   DNS  Standard query response  A  93.184.216.34
# Use Statistics → DNS for a breakdown of query types and RCODE distribution.

# Matches DNS query frames only (QR flag = 0).
# Frame 1:  10.0.0.5 → 8.8.8.8  DNS Query  A  example.com
# Useful for counting outbound queries or spotting unusual lookups.
# Pair with dns.qry.name to find lookups for specific domains.

# Matches DNS response frames only (QR flag = 1).
# Frame 2:  8.8.8.8 → 10.0.0.5  DNS Response  A  93.184.216.34  TTL 3600
# Pair with dns.flags.rcode != 0 to find failed responses only.

# Matches DNS queries where the QNAME contains "example.com".
# Catches all subdomains: api.example.com, mail.example.com, etc.
# Frame 1:  Query: api.example.com   Type: A
# Frame 3:  Query: mail.example.com  Type: MX

# Matches DNS A record queries (type 1 — IPv4 address lookups).
# Frame 1:  DNS Query  A  example.com
# A records are the most common query type — hostname to IPv4 mapping.
# See also: dns.qry.type == 28 for AAAA (IPv6) lookups.

# Matches DNS AAAA record queries (type 28 — IPv6 address lookups).
# Frame 1:  DNS Query  AAAA  example.com
# Modern resolvers query both A and AAAA simultaneously (Happy Eyeballs).
# Absence of AAAA queries may indicate IPv6 is disabled on the client.

# Matches DNS MX record queries (type 15 — mail exchanger lookups).
# Frame 1:  DNS Query  MX  example.com
# Frame 2:  DNS Response  MX 10  mail.example.com
# High MX query rate from one host can indicate email address enumeration.

# Matches DNS SRV record queries (type 33 — service locator).
# Used by SIP, XMPP, Kerberos, and LDAP for service discovery.
# Frame 1:  DNS Query  SRV  _sip._tcp.example.com
# Frame 2:  DNS Response  SRV  10 20 5060 sipserver.example.com

Display Filters — DNS.desc: DNS responses with an error (NXDOMAIN, SERVFAIL…)
Display Filters — DNS.example:
# Matches DNS responses with a non-zero return code (error responses).
# RCODE values: 1=FormErr, 2=ServFail, 3=NXDomain, 5=Refused
# Frame 2:  DNS Response  NXDOMAIN  nonexistent.example.com
# Frame 4:  DNS Response  SERVFAIL  broken.example.com
# High NXDOMAIN rate may indicate DGA malware or misconfigured hosts.

# Matches DNS NXDOMAIN responses (domain does not exist).
# Frame 2:  DNS Response  No such name  A  typo.examle.com
# Sudden spike in NXDOMAIN responses can indicate Domain Generation Algorithm
# (DGA) malware generating random domain names for C2 beaconing.

# Matches DNS SERVFAIL (rcode 2) — server could not complete the query.
# Frame 5:  8.8.8.8 → 10.0.0.5  DNS Standard query response SERVFAIL
# Common causes: authoritative server unreachable, DNSSEC validation failure,
# resolver misconfiguration, or upstream recursion failure.
# Combine with dns.qry.name to see which domains are failing.

Display Filters — DNS.desc: DNS responses that cross the 512-byte UDP boundary
Display Filters — DNS.example:
# Matches DNS responses larger than 512 bytes (the classic UDP limit).
# Modern resolvers use EDNS0 to allow up to 4096 bytes over UDP.
# Frame 8:  8.8.8.8 → 10.0.0.5  DNS response Len=1380 A www.example.com
# If the resolver doesn't support EDNS0 and gets a response > 512 bytes,
# it falls back to TCP — watch for a matching tcp.port == 53 stream.

Display Filters — DNS.desc: Truncated responses — client will retry over TCP
Display Filters — DNS.example:
# Matches DNS responses with the TC (Truncated) bit set.
# Frame 9:  8.8.8.8 → 10.0.0.5  DNS response (truncated)
# The response was too large for the negotiated UDP payload size.
# The resolver should retry over TCP (port 53/tcp).
# If no TCP retry follows, the answer is silently incomplete.

Display Filters — DHCP.example:
# Matches all DHCP frames (Discover, Offer, Request, ACK, NAK) on UDP 67/68.
# Frame 1:  0.0.0.0 → 255.255.255.255  DHCP Discover  Xid=0x3c4b2a1e
# Frame 2:  10.0.0.1 → 10.0.0.50       DHCP Offer     IP: 10.0.0.50
# Frame 3:  0.0.0.0 → 255.255.255.255  DHCP Request   requesting 10.0.0.50
# Frame 4:  10.0.0.1 → 10.0.0.50       DHCP ACK       lease: 86400s

# Matches DHCP Discover packets (option 53 value = 1).
# Sent by client at boot as a broadcast to locate DHCP servers.
# Frame 1:  0.0.0.0 → 255.255.255.255  DHCP Discover  chaddr: aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff
# Multiple Discovers without an Offer = no DHCP server reachable on this VLAN.

# Matches DHCP Offer packets (option 53 value = 2).
# Server response to Discover — offering an IP address to the client.
# Frame 2:  10.0.0.1 → 255.255.255.255  DHCP Offer  IP: 10.0.0.100  lease: 86400
# Multiple Offers from different source IPs = rogue DHCP server on network.

# Matches DHCP Request packets (option 53 value = 3).
# Client broadcasts acceptance of a specific server's Offer.
# Frame 3:  0.0.0.0 → 255.255.255.255  DHCP Request  requesting 10.0.0.100
# Also sent unicast during lease renewal (50% of lease time).

# Matches DHCP ACK packets (option 53 value = 5) — lease confirmed.
# Frame 4:  10.0.0.1 → 10.0.0.100  DHCP ACK  lease: 86400s  router: 10.0.0.1
# Client configures the IP, mask, gateway, and DNS options from the ACK.

# Matches DHCP NAK packets (option 53 value = 6) — lease rejected.
# Server rejects a Request (e.g. client moved to a different subnet).
# Frame 4:  10.0.0.1 → 255.255.255.255  DHCP NAK
# Client must restart the DORA process from Discover.

# Matches DHCP Release packets (option 53 value = 7).
# Client gracefully releases its lease when disconnecting.
# Frame 10: 10.0.0.100 → 10.0.0.1  DHCP Release  releasing 10.0.0.100

Display Filters — DHCP.desc: Everything from a specific client MAC
Display Filters — DHCP.example:
# Matches DHCP packets from a specific client MAC address.
# Replace aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff with the client's hardware address.
# Frame 1:  DHCP Discover  chaddr: aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff
# Frame 3:  DHCP Request   chaddr: aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff
# Useful for isolating a single client's DORA conversation.

# Matches DHCPv6 frames (Solicit, Advertise, Request, Reply) on UDP 546/547.
# Frame 1:  fe80::1 → ff02::1:2  DHCPv6 Solicit   DUID: xx:xx:xx:xx
# Frame 2:  fe80::1 → fe80::2    DHCPv6 Advertise  IA_NA: 2001:db8::100
# Used for stateful IPv6 address assignment (vs SLAAC which uses RA only).

Display Filters — DHCP.desc: DHCP option payloads — use for option 82 checks
Display Filters — DHCP.example:
# In older Wireshark builds (<3.0) DHCP frames are decoded under the bootp dissector.
# bootp.option.value matches any DHCP option value field.
# Upgrade to Wireshark 3.0+ where DHCP has its own dedicated dissector
# and dhcp.option.dhcp and related fields are available.

Display Filters — ARP / ICMP.example:
# Matches all ARP frames (Requests and Replies).
# Frame 1:  ARP  Who has 10.0.0.1? Tell 10.0.0.5
# Frame 2:  ARP  10.0.0.1 is at 00:50:56:aa:bb:cc
# High ARP rate on a VLAN can indicate an ARP storm or active scanning tool.

# Matches ARP Request frames (opcode 1 = "Who has?").
# Frame 1:  ARP Request  Who has 10.0.0.1? Tell 10.0.0.5
# Rapid ARP requests targeting many different IPs = network discovery / scan.

# Matches ARP Reply frames (opcode 2 = "Is at").
# Frame 2:  ARP Reply  10.0.0.1 is at 00:50:56:aa:bb:cc
# Unsolicited ARP replies (Gratuitous ARP) are used when a device changes IP
# or a cluster IP fails over — normal in HA environments.

# Wireshark expert flag — set when the same IP is claimed by two MAC addresses.
# Frame 3:  [ARP] Duplicate IP address detected for 10.0.0.1
#   First seen from: 00:50:56:aa:bb:cc
#   Now seen from:   00:11:22:33:44:55
# Indicates duplicate IP configuration or ARP poisoning (MITM attack).

Display Filters — ARP / ICMP.desc: ARP sourced from a specific IP
Display Filters — ARP / ICMP.example:
# Matches ARP frames where the sender protocol address is 10.0.0.1.
# Captures all ARP requests and replies originating from that IP.
# Useful for confirming a host's ARP behaviour or detecting IP spoofing.

# Matches all ICMP frames (echo, unreachable, time exceeded, redirect…).
# Frame 1:  ICMP Echo (ping) request  id=0x0001 seq=1  ttl=64
# Frame 2:  ICMP Echo (ping) reply    id=0x0001 seq=1  ttl=64
# Common types: 0=Echo Reply, 3=Unreachable, 8=Echo Request, 11=TTL Exceeded

# Matches ICMP Echo Reply messages (type 0) — ping responses.
# Frame 2:  ICMP Echo reply  id=0x0001 seq=1  ttl=64
# If Echo Requests (type 8) are seen but no type 0: host is down or firewalled.

# Matches ICMP Echo Request messages (type 8) — ping probes.
# Frame 1:  ICMP Echo request  id=0x0001 seq=1  ttl=64
# Many Echo Requests to different hosts in rapid succession = ping sweep.

# Matches ICMP Destination Unreachable messages (type 3).
# Sub-codes: 0=Net, 1=Host, 2=Protocol, 3=Port, 4=Fragmentation Needed
# Frame 3:  ICMP Destination unreachable  (Port unreachable)
# Code 4 (Fragmentation Needed) = PMTUD signal — look for DF-bit packets.

# Matches ICMP Destination Unreachable / Fragmentation Needed messages.
# This is the PMTUD signal: a router can't forward the packet because
# it exceeds the next-hop MTU and the DF bit is set.
# Frame 15: router → 10.0.0.5  ICMP Destination Unreachable (Fragmentation Needed)
#   Next-Hop MTU: 1452
# The sender should reduce its MSS to fit. Absence of these messages
# when DF+large packets are dropped = PMTUD Blackhole.

# Matches ICMP Time Exceeded messages (type 11) — TTL expired in transit.
# Each hop in a traceroute sends a Time Exceeded reply back to the source.
# Frame 5:  ICMP Time-to-live exceeded (Time to live exceeded in transit)
#   Source: 10.10.1.1  (intermediate router hop)
# Code 0 = TTL exceeded; Code 1 = Fragment reassembly time exceeded.

Display Filters — ARP / ICMP.desc: All ICMPv6 traffic (NDP, RA, RS, PMTUD)
Display Filters — ARP / ICMP.example:
# Matches all ICMPv6 traffic — NDP, RA, RS, Echo, PMTUD signals, etc.
# Frame 1:  fe80::1 → ff02::1   ICMPv6 Router Advertisement
# Frame 2:  fe80::2 → fe80::1   ICMPv6 Neighbor Solicitation
# Frame 3:  fe80::1 → fe80::2   ICMPv6 Neighbor Advertisement
# Use icmpv6.type to narrow: 133=RS, 134=RA, 135=NS, 136=NA, 128=Echo Request

# Matches ICMPv6 Neighbor Solicitation (NS) — the IPv6 equivalent of ARP Request.
# Frame 5:  fe80::aabb:ccff:fedd:eeff → ff02::1:ff00:1  ICMPv6 Neighbor Solicitation
#   Target: 2001:db8::1
# NS is sent to the solicited-node multicast address of the target.
# If no Neighbor Advertisement (type 136) follows, the target is unreachable.

# Matches ICMPv6 Neighbor Advertisement (NA) — the IPv6 ARP Reply equivalent.
# Sent in response to a Neighbor Solicitation or gratuitously after address assignment.
# Frame 6:  fe80::1 → fe80::2   ICMPv6 Neighbor Advertisement
#   Target: 2001:db8::1  Override=1  Solicited=1
#   Target Link-Layer Address: 00:11:22:33:44:55
# If no NA follows an NS, the target IPv6 address is unreachable.

# Matches ICMPv6 Router Advertisement (RA) — sent by routers to advertise
# the link prefix, gateway, MTU, and SLAAC/DHCPv6 flags.
# Frame 1:  fe80::1 → ff02::1  ICMPv6 Router Advertisement
#   Prefix: 2001:db8::/64  A-flag=1 (SLAAC)  L-flag=1
#   Router Lifetime: 1800s
#   MTU: 1500
# Hosts use RA to auto-configure IPv6 addresses (SLAAC) and set their default gateway.

Display Filters — VoIP / SIP / RTP.example:
# Matches all SIP signalling frames (UDP/TCP 5060, TLS 5061).
# Frame 1:  10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.20  INVITE sip:bob@example.com SIP/2.0
# Frame 2:  10.0.0.20 → 10.0.0.5  SIP/2.0 100 Trying
# Frame 3:  10.0.0.20 → 10.0.0.5  SIP/2.0 200 OK
# Use Telephony → VoIP Calls for a graphical call flow diagram.

# Matches SIP INVITE messages — initiating a voice/video call.
# Frame 1:  INVITE sip:bob@example.com SIP/2.0
#   Call-ID: 1234@10.0.0.5  From: Alice  To: Bob
#   Content-Type: application/sdp   (SDP offer inside the body)
# Each INVITE starts a new call leg; re-INVITE renegotiates media parameters.

# Matches SIP BYE messages — terminating an active call.
# Frame 40: BYE sip:bob@example.com SIP/2.0  Call-ID: 1234@10.0.0.5
# If BYE is absent at call end — the call was dropped rather than hung up.
# Look for SIP 4xx/5xx errors that may have caused an unexpected teardown.

# Matches SIP error responses (4xx client, 5xx server, 6xx global failure).
# Frame 5:  SIP/2.0 404 Not Found       (called party doesn't exist)
# Frame 8:  SIP/2.0 403 Forbidden       (authentication failure)
# Frame 12: SIP/2.0 503 Service Unavailable  (server overload / registrar down)

Display Filters — VoIP / SIP / RTP.desc: Every SIP message in a specific call
Display Filters — VoIP / SIP / RTP.example:
# Matches all SIP messages belonging to a specific call leg.
# Replace <id> with the actual Call-ID value from the SIP headers.
# Frame 1:  INVITE  Call-ID: abc123@10.0.0.5
# Frame 5:  200 OK  Call-ID: abc123@10.0.0.5
# Frame 40: BYE     Call-ID: abc123@10.0.0.5
# Shows the complete signalling dialogue for one call from start to finish.

# Matches RTP media stream frames (voice/video payload packets).
# Frame 10: 10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.20  RTP  PT=PCMU (0)  Seq=1  TS=160
# RTP uses dynamic UDP ports negotiated in the SDP body of the INVITE.
# Use Telephony → RTP → Show All Streams for jitter and packet loss stats.

# Matches RTP frames carrying G.711 μ-law (PCMU) codec — payload type 0.
# Standard PSTN-quality voice codec, 64 kbps, common in enterprise VoIP.
# Frame 10: RTP  PCMU  Seq=1  SSRC=0x12345678  Timestamp=160
# Use Telephony → RTP → Play Streams to listen to the captured audio.

# Matches RTP frames carrying G.711 a-law (PCMA) codec — payload type 8.
# Used predominantly in European and international telephony networks.
# Frame 10: RTP  PCMA  Seq=1  SSRC=0xABCDEF01  Timestamp=160

Display Filters — VoIP / SIP / RTP.desc: Marker-bit RTP packets (talk-spurt start, DTMF events)
Display Filters — VoIP / SIP / RTP.example:
# Matches RTP frames with the Marker bit set.
# In voice codecs: marks the first packet after a silence period ends.
# In video codecs: typically marks the last RTP packet of a video frame.
# Frame 35: RTP  PT=PCMU  Marker=1  — start of a new voice talkspurt.

# Matches RTCP control packets — quality reports for RTP media streams.
# RTCP carries statistics: packet loss, jitter, and round-trip delay.
# Frame 20: RTCP Sender Report  SSRC=0x12345678
#   Packet count: 500  Octet count: 80000  NTP TS: 2026-01-01 12:00:00 UTC
# Expand Receiver Report Blocks to see per-stream jitter and loss percentage.

Display Filters — VoIP / SIP / RTP.desc: Session Description Protocol bodies inside SIP
Display Filters — VoIP / SIP / RTP.example:
# Matches frames containing Session Description Protocol (SDP) payloads.
# SDP bodies travel inside SIP INVITE and 200 OK responses.
# Frame 5:  10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.10  SIP/SDP  INVITE sip:bob@example.com
#   Content-Type: application/sdp
#   v=0
#   o=alice 2890844526 2890844526 IN IP4 10.0.0.5
#   m=audio 49170 RTP/AVP 0
# The 'm=' line reveals the RTP port for the media stream.

Display Filters — AD / Kerberos / SMB /.example:
# Matches all Kerberos protocol frames (typically UDP/TCP port 88).
# Frame 1:  10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.1  KRB5  AS-REQ  (user@DOMAIN.COM)
# Frame 2:  10.0.0.1 → 10.0.0.5  KRB5  AS-REP  (TGT issued)
# Frame 3:  10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.1  KRB5  TGS-REQ (requesting service ticket)
# Use Wireshark → Statistics → Credentials to extract Kerberos usernames.

# Matches Kerberos AS-REQ messages (type 10) — login authentication requests.
# Client sends AS-REQ to the KDC to obtain a Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT).
# Frame 1:  KRB5 AS-REQ  cname: alice@DOMAIN.COM  sname: krbtgt/DOMAIN.COM
# High AS-REQ rate without AS-REP responses may indicate Kerberoasting attack.

# Matches Kerberos TGS-REQ messages (type 12) — service ticket requests.
# Client presents its TGT to the KDC to request a ticket for a service.
# Frame 3:  KRB5 TGS-REQ  sname: host/fileserver.domain.com  realm: DOMAIN.COM
# Multiple TGS-REQ for different services in short succession = normal operation.

# Matches any Kerberos error response (KRB-ERROR frames).
# Frames contain a numeric error code and descriptive text.
# Frame 5:  KRB5 KRB-ERROR  error-code: eRR-CLIENT-REVOKED (18)
# All non-zero error codes indicate authentication or authorisation failures.

# Matches Kerberos error 24: KDC_ERR_PREAUTH_FAILED — wrong password.
# The pre-authentication data supplied was invalid (incorrect credentials).
# Frame 5:  KRB5 KRB-ERROR  eRR-PREAUTH-FAILED  (alice@DOMAIN.COM)
# Multiple KDC_ERR_PREAUTH_FAILED from one IP = brute-force or password spray.

# Matches Kerberos error 37: KRB_AP_ERR_SKEW — clock skew too large.
# Kerberos requires all clocks to be within 5 minutes of each other.
# Frame 5:  KRB5 KRB-ERROR  eRR-SKEW  — time difference > 300 seconds
# Fix: ensure all domain members sync to the PDC emulator via NTP.

# Matches LDAP frames on TCP 389 (cleartext) or TCP 636 (LDAPS).
# Frame 1:  10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.1  LDAP  bindRequest  (simple auth)
# Frame 2:  10.0.0.1 → 10.0.0.5  LDAP  bindResponse  result: success
# Simple binds on port 389 expose credentials in BASE64 — visible as cleartext.

# Matches LDAP searchRequest messages (messageType 3).
# Frame 3:  LDAP searchRequest  base: DC=domain,DC=com
#   filter: (sAMAccountName=alice)  scope: wholeSubtree
# Excessive LDAP searches from one host = Active Directory enumeration.

# Matches SMBv1 frames (TCP 445 or 139).
# Frame 1:  SMB  Negotiate Protocol Request
# Frame 2:  SMB  Negotiate Protocol Response
# SMBv1 is deprecated and exploitable (EternalBlue/MS17-010).
# Use smb2 filter for modern Windows file sharing.

# Matches SMBv2/3 frames (TCP 445) — modern Windows file sharing.
# Frame 1:  SMB2  Negotiate Protocol Request   Dialects: [3.1.1, 3.0.2, 2.1]
# Frame 2:  SMB2  Negotiate Protocol Response  Dialect: 3.1.1
# Frame 3:  SMB2  Session Setup Request        (Kerberos authentication)
# SMB 3.x supports encryption and multichannel — preferred over SMBv1/v2.

# Matches SMB2 CREATE requests — opening a file, pipe, or directory.
# SMB2 command code 5 = CREATE (equivalent to SMB1 Open/NtCreate).
# Frame 20: 10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.10  SMB2 Create Request File: \\share\file.docx
# Combine with smb2.filename to search for a specific file name:
#   smb2.cmd == 5 and smb2.filename contains "password"

# Matches SMB2 CLOSE requests — releasing an open file handle.
# Frame 25: 10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.10  SMB2 Close Request  FileId: 0x0001...
# Useful for tracking file lifecycle: CREATE (5) → READ/WRITE → CLOSE (6).
# High CLOSE rate without corresponding READs may indicate directory enumeration.

Display Filters — AD / Kerberos / SMB /.desc: SMB2 responses with an NT_STATUS error
Display Filters — AD / Kerberos / SMB /.example:
# Matches SMB2 responses carrying a non-zero (error) NT_STATUS code.
# Frame 22: 10.0.0.10 → 10.0.0.5  SMB2 Create Response  NT_Status: STATUS_ACCESS_DENIED (0xC0000022)
# Common error codes:
#   0xC0000022 = STATUS_ACCESS_DENIED
#   0xC0000034 = STATUS_OBJECT_NAME_NOT_FOUND
#   0xC000006D = STATUS_LOGON_FAILURE
# High error rate on a DC port suggests brute-force or misconfigured permissions.

# Matches DCE/RPC protocol frames used by Windows RPC services.
# Many Windows protocols run over DCE/RPC: WMI, DCOM, SAMR, LSARPC, DRSUAPI.
# Frame 10: 10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.10  DCERPC Bind  Interface: DRSUAPI (AD replication)
# Frame 11: 10.0.0.10 → 10.0.0.5  DCERPC Bind_ack
# Frame 12: 10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.10  DCERPC Request: DsGetNcChanges
# High DCERPC error rate can indicate DCSync attacks or replication issues.

Display Filters — Routing / Switching Co.example:
# Matches all OSPF frames (IP protocol 89).
# Frame 1:  10.0.0.1 → 224.0.0.5  OSPF  Hello Packet  (area 0.0.0.0)
# Frame 5:  10.0.0.1 → 10.0.0.2   OSPF  DB Description (exstart state)
# Use to troubleshoot neighbour formation, LSA flooding, or area mismatches.

# Matches OSPF Hello packets (message type 1).
# Hellos establish and maintain adjacencies between OSPF neighbours.
# Frame 1:  OSPF Hello  Router-ID: 1.1.1.1  Area: 0.0.0.0  Neighbours: [2.2.2.2]
# Missing Hellos within the Dead Interval causes the neighbour to go Down.
# Common mismatch causes: Hello/Dead interval, area ID, MTU, authentication.

# Matches EIGRP frames (IP protocol 88, multicast 224.0.0.10).
# Frame 1:  10.0.0.1 → 224.0.0.10  EIGRP Hello  (AS 100)
# Frame 3:  10.0.0.1 → 10.0.0.2    EIGRP Update (routes advertised)
# EIGRP is Cisco proprietary — only seen in Cisco IOS/IOS-XE environments.

# Matches BGP frames (TCP port 179).
# Frame 1:  10.0.0.1 → 10.0.0.2  BGP OPEN  (AS 65001, hold time 90s)
# Frame 2:  10.0.0.2 → 10.0.0.1  BGP OPEN  (AS 65002, hold time 90s)
# Frame 3:  10.0.0.1 → 10.0.0.2  BGP KEEPALIVE
# Session resets appear as BGP NOTIFICATION messages (filter: bgp.type == 3).

# Matches BGP OPEN messages (type 1) — session establishment.
# Frame 1:  BGP OPEN  My AS: 65001  Hold Time: 90  BGP ID: 10.0.0.1
#   Capabilities: 4-byte AS, route-refresh, multiprotocol (IPv4 unicast)

# Matches BGP UPDATE messages (type 2) — route advertisements and withdrawals.
# Frame 5:  BGP UPDATE  Withdrawn: 192.168.0.0/24  NLRI: 10.10.0.0/16
# Each UPDATE can carry multiple prefixes added or withdrawn simultaneously.

# Matches BGP NOTIFICATION messages (type 3) — error conditions.
# Frame 8:  BGP NOTIFICATION  Error: Hold Timer Expired (code 4)
# Frame 9:  BGP NOTIFICATION  Error: UPDATE Message Error, bad NEXT_HOP (code 3/8)
# A NOTIFICATION immediately terminates the BGP session.

# Matches STP/RSTP/MSTP Bridge PDUs (BPDUs) — multicast to 01:80:c2:00:00:00.
# Frame 1:  STP  Configuration BPDU  Root: 4096:00:50:56:aa:bb:cc  Cost: 0
# TCN (Topology Change Notification) BPDUs indicate a topology change event.
# High BPDU rate or flapping port IDs can cause MAC table instability.

# Matches LLDP frames (EtherType 0x88cc, multicast to 01:80:c2:00:00:0e).
# Frame 1:  00:50:56:aa:bb:cc → 01:80:c2:00:00:0e  LLDP
#   Chassis ID: 00:50:56:aa:bb:cc  Port ID: GigabitEthernet0/1
#   System Name: switch01.example.com  TTL: 120s
# Useful for discovering adjacent device identity and port mappings.

# Matches CDP frames (Cisco proprietary SNAP 0x2000, to 01:00:0c:cc:cc:cc).
# Frame 1:  00:50:56:aa:bb:cc → 01:00:0c:cc:cc:cc  CDP
#   Device ID: router01.example.com  Port: GigabitEthernet0/0
#   Platform: Cisco ISR4451  Version: Cisco IOS XE 17.9.4a
# CDP is Cisco-only — use lldp filter for multi-vendor environments.

# Matches all VRRP (Virtual Router Redundancy Protocol) packets.
# VRRP advertisements are sent to 224.0.0.18, IP protocol 112.
# Frame 1:  10.0.0.1 → 224.0.0.18  VRRPv2 Advertisement  VirtualRtrID=1  Priority=100
# Frame 2:  10.0.0.2 → 224.0.0.18  VRRPv2 Advertisement  VirtualRtrID=1  Priority=90
# A gap in advertisements (>3× interval) causes a state transition from Backup to Master.

# Matches Cisco HSRP (Hot Standby Router Protocol) multicast packets.
# HSRP v1 uses 224.0.0.2 UDP/1985; HSRPv2 uses 224.0.0.102 UDP/1985.
# Frame 3:  10.0.0.254 → 224.0.0.2  HSRP  Hello  State=Active  Priority=110  Group=1
# Frame 4:  10.0.0.253 → 224.0.0.2  HSRP  Hello  State=Standby Priority=100  Group=1
# If Active stops sending Hellos, Standby holds-down then transitions to Active.

# Matches BFD (Bidirectional Forwarding Detection) control packets.
# BFD uses UDP ports 3784 (single-hop) or 4784 (multi-hop).
# Frame 1:  10.0.0.1 → 10.0.0.2  BFD Control  State=Up  Your Discrim=12345  Interval=300ms
# BFD detects forwarding-plane failures much faster than routing-protocol timers.
# A State=Down transition in both peers confirms the link or path has failed.

Display Filters — 802.11 / Wireless.example:
# Matches all 802.11 (Wi-Fi) frames — requires monitor mode capture.
# Frame 1:  aa:bb:cc:11:22:33 → ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff  802.11 Beacon  SSID: CorpWiFi
# Enable monitor mode on Linux: sudo iw dev wlan0 set type monitor
# On macOS: Wireless Diagnostics → Window → Sniffer

# Matches 802.11 Management frames (frame type field = 0).
# Includes: Beacon, Probe Request/Response, Auth, Assoc, Deauth, Disassoc.
# Frame 1:  Beacon         SSID: CorpWiFi  Channel: 6
# Frame 5:  Probe Request  SSID: * (wildcard broadcast probe from client)
# Frame 8:  Auth Request   to AP 00:11:22:33:44:55

# Matches 802.11 Beacon frames (subtype 8) — APs advertise ~10 per second.
# Frame 1:  Beacon  SSID: CorpWiFi  BSSID: 00:11:22:33:44:55  Channel: 6
#   RSN: WPA2-CCMP  Supported rates: 6,9,12,18,24,36,48,54 Mbps
# Multiple BSSIDs sharing the same SSID = same ESS (roaming network).

# Matches 802.11 Probe Request frames (subtype 4).
# Clients send probe requests to find specific SSIDs or discover all APs.
# Frame 5:  Probe Request  SA: aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff  SSID: * (wildcard)
# The client's Preferred Network List (PNL) is exposed by directed probes.

# Matches 802.11 Probe Response frames (subtype 5).
# APs reply to directed probe requests with their capabilities.
# Frame 7:  Probe Response  BSSID: 00:11:22:33:44:55  SSID: CorpWiFi
#   Capabilities: ESS, Privacy  RSN: WPA2-CCMP  Channel: 6

# Matches 802.11 Authentication frames (subtype 11).
# Frame 8:  Authentication  Open System  Seq: 1  Status: Successful
# Frame 9:  Authentication  Open System  Seq: 2  Status: Successful
# WPA2/3: Authentication is Open System; the 4-way EAPOL handshake follows.

# Matches 802.11 Association Request frames (subtype 0).
# Client requests to join an AP after successful authentication.
# Frame 10: Association Request  SSID: CorpWiFi  Capabilities: ESS
#   Supported rates: 1,2,5.5,11,6,9,12,18,24,36,48,54 Mbps
#   RSN: WPA2 (CCMP pairwise and group cipher)

# Matches 802.11 Deauthentication frames (subtype 12).
# Frame 15: Deauthentication  BSSID: 00:11:22:33:44:55  Reason: 2
# Reason 2 = Previous authentication no longer valid.
# Flood of deauth frames targeting a client = deauthentication (DoS) attack.
# WPA3 / 802.11w Protected Management Frames (PMF) can mitigate deauth attacks.

# Matches 802.11 Disassociation frames (subtype 10).
# Frame 20: Disassociation  Reason: 8 (STA leaving the BSS)
# Disassoc preserves authentication state; Deauth (0x0c) removes both.

# Matches 802.11 Control frames (frame type 1).
# Includes: RTS, CTS, ACK, PS-Poll, Block ACK Request/Response.
# Frame 2:  Acknowledgement    RA: aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff
# Frame 3:  Clear-To-Send      RA: aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff  Duration: 160μs
# Control frames coordinate medium access and are not normally analysed.

# Matches 802.11 Data frames (FC Type=2).
# Type 0 = Management, Type 1 = Control, Type 2 = Data.
# Frame 10: STA(aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff) → AP(00:11:22:33:44:55)  Data  Seq=1024
# Filter data frames only to focus on actual payload traffic
# rather than management overhead (Beacons, Probes, Auth, Assoc).

# Matches 802.11 frames that carry the literal SSID string 'CorpWiFi'.
# Appears in Beacon, Probe Request, and Probe Response frames.
# Frame 1:  AP(00:11:22:33:44:55) → ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff  Beacon  SSID=CorpWiFi  Ch=6
# Frame 5:  STA → ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff  Probe Request  SSID=CorpWiFi
# Requires monitor-mode capture to see 802.11 management frames.

Display Filters — 802.11 / Wireless.desc: Everything involving a specific wireless MAC
Display Filters — 802.11 / Wireless.example:
# Matches all 802.11 frames involving a specific wireless MAC address
# (source, destination, BSSID, or transmitter).
# Frame 3:  aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff → 00:11:22:33:44:55  Data  Seq=512
# Frame 4:  00:11:22:33:44:55 → aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff  Data  ACK
# Use wlan.sa or wlan.da to restrict to source or destination respectively.

# Matches EAPOL (EAP over LAN) frames used in 802.1X/WPA handshakes.
# Frame 12: STA → AP  EAPOL Start
# Frame 13: AP  → STA EAP-Request  Identity
# Frame 14: STA → AP  EAP-Response Identity: user@domain.com
# Frame 15: AP  → STA EAP-Request  PEAP (TLS Start)
# The 4-way WPA2 handshake also uses EAPOL Key frames after 802.1X auth.

Display Filters — 802.11 / Wireless.desc: Frames captured on a specific channel
Display Filters — 802.11 / Wireless.example:
# Matches frames captured on 802.11 radio channel 36 (5 GHz, 5180 MHz).
# Requires a monitor-mode capture with radiotap or prism headers.
# Frame 1:  [Channel: 36  Signal: -55 dBm]  Beacon  SSID=CorpWiFi
# Channel 36 is a non-overlapping 5 GHz channel (UNII-1 band).
# Common 5 GHz non-overlapping 20 MHz channels: 36, 40, 44, 48, 149, 153, 157, 161

Display Filters — 802.11 / Wireless.desc: Frames received below -75 dBm — weak-signal hunting
Display Filters — 802.11 / Wireless.example:
# Matches frames received with a signal strength below -75 dBm (weak signal).
# Radiotap headers carry per-frame RF metadata from the capture adapter.
# Frame 12: [Signal: -78 dBm]  Data  STA → AP  [TCP Retransmission]
# Signal thresholds (approximate):
#   > -65 dBm : Excellent (streaming video OK)
#   -65 to -75: Good (VoIP / browsing OK)
#   < -75 dBm : Poor (packet loss, retransmissions expected)
#   < -85 dBm : Unusable

Display Filters — VPN / IPsec / GRE.example:
# Matches ISAKMP / IKEv1 / IKEv2 frames (UDP port 500; NAT-T on UDP 4500).
# Frame 1:  10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.10  ISAKMP  IKEv2 IKE_SA_INIT Request
# Frame 2:  10.0.0.10 → 10.0.0.5  ISAKMP  IKEv2 IKE_SA_INIT Response
# IKEv2: SA_INIT (2 msgs) + IKE_AUTH (2 msgs) = full tunnel setup in 4 messages.

# Matches ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) frames — IPsec encrypted data.
# ESP payload is encrypted; Wireshark shows SPI and sequence number only.
# Frame 5:  10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.10  ESP  SPI=0x12345678  Seq=42
# To decrypt: Edit → Preferences → Protocols → ESP → provide session keys.

# Matches AH (Authentication Header) frames — IPsec integrity without encryption.
# AH provides data integrity and origin authentication but no confidentiality.
# Frame 5:  10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.10  AH  SPI=0xABCDEF01  Seq=10
# AH is rarely deployed; ESP with NULL encryption covers the same use case.

# Matches GRE (Generic Routing Encapsulation) frames (IP protocol 47).
# Frame 1:  10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.10  GRE  Protocol: IPv4 (0x0800)
# GRE is used for DMVPN, mGRE, or GRE-over-IPsec configurations.
# Inner payload (IPv4/IPv6/MPLS) is visible if not additionally encrypted.

# Matches frames with IP protocol number 47 (GRE).
# Equivalent to the gre display filter; useful in compound expressions:
#   ip.src == 10.0.0.5 and ip.proto == 47
# Can detect unexpected GRE tunnels from unauthorised hosts.

# Matches frames with IP protocol 50 (ESP — IPsec data traffic).
# Frame 5:  10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.10  Protocol: ESP  (encrypted)
# No ESP seen after IKE negotiation = Phase 2 / CHILD_SA failure.
# Check: mismatched transform sets, ACLs blocking protocol 50.

# Matches frames with IP protocol 51 (AH — Authentication Header).
# Frame 5:  10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.10  Protocol: AH
# If AH is seen, traffic is integrity-protected but NOT encrypted.
# Rarely used in practice — ESP with authentication covers the same need.

# Matches L2TP (Layer 2 Tunneling Protocol) control frames (UDP 1701).
# Frame 1:  10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.10  L2TP  Start-Control-Connection-Request
# L2TP/IPsec VPNs combine L2TP tunnel with IPsec encryption.
# Control messages: SCCRQ, SCCRP, SCCCN, ICRQ, ICRP, ICCN, CDN, StopCCN.

# Matches PPPoE Session frames (EtherType 0x8864) — DSL broadband.
# Frame 1:  aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff → 00:11:22:33:44:55  PPPoE Session  PPP LCP
# PPPoE Discovery phase uses EtherType 0x8863 — filter with: pppoed
# Frame 5:  PPPoE Session  PPP IPv4  10.0.0.5 → 8.8.8.8  DNS Query

# Matches WireGuard VPN frames (UDP, default port 51820).
# Frame 1:  10.0.0.5:51820 → 10.0.0.10:51820  WireGuard  Handshake Initiation
# Frame 2:  10.0.0.10:51820 → 10.0.0.5:51820  WireGuard  Handshake Response
# After handshake: Transport Data frames carry encrypted packets (opaque payload).

Display Filters — Operators & Syntax.example:
# Uses the "in" set operator — matches TCP port 80, 443, or 8080.
# Equivalent to: tcp.port == 80 or tcp.port == 443 or tcp.port == 8080
# More readable for multi-value sets; works for most numeric fields.
# ip.addr in {10.0.0.1 10.0.0.2 10.0.0.3} — filter multiple hosts at once.

Display Filters — Operators & Syntax.cmd: !(arp or stp or cdp or lldp)
Display Filters — Operators & Syntax.example:
# Excludes noisy background layer-2 traffic to clean up the display.
# Hides ARP broadcasts, STP BPDUs, CDP and LLDP neighbour announcements.
# Useful as a first filter when opening a capture — remove protocol noise first.
# Equivalent: not arp and not stp and not cdp and not lldp

Display Filters — Operators & Syntax.desc: Boolean composition — subnet minus a port
Display Filters — Operators & Syntax.example:
# Matches all traffic within the 10.0.0.0/8 RFC 1918 range, excluding SSH.
# CIDR notation is natively supported in Wireshark display filters.
# Useful for isolating intranet traffic while hiding management SSH sessions.

Display Filters — Operators & Syntax.desc: Regex content search across the whole frame
Display Filters — Operators & Syntax.example:
# Uses the "matches" operator (Perl-compatible regex) to search raw frame bytes.
# Matches any frame whose payload contains the literal string "User-Agent: curl".
# More powerful than "contains" — supports full PCRE regex patterns.
# frame matches "(?i)password=" — case-insensitive search for credential fields.

# The ~ operator is shorthand for "matches" (regex match against field content).
# Matches HTTP frames where User-Agent contains "Mozilla" as a regex pattern.
# Case-sensitive by default; use ~ "(?i)mozilla" for case-insensitive matching.

# Matches TCP frames whose payload contains the hex bytes 50:4f:53:54 ("POST").
# Useful for finding POST requests in TCP streams without HTTP dissection.
# Bytes can be expressed as ASCII strings: tcp.payload contains "POST"
# Handy for finding cleartext data in non-standard port traffic.

Display Filters — Operators & Syntax.desc: Filter by protocol name in the dissector chain
Display Filters — Operators & Syntax.example:
# Matches frames where the dissected protocol stack includes "gtp".
# frame.protocols is a colon-separated string of all detected protocols.
# Example stack: "eth:ethertype:ip:udp:gtp:ip:tcp:http"
# Useful for filtering mobile/carrier traffic without knowing GTP port numbers.

Capture Filters.desc: Captures packets to or from a specific IP
Capture Filters.example:
# Replace <ip> with the target, e.g.: host 192.168.1.50
# Captures all traffic to OR from that host (source or destination).
# Apply in Wireshark: Capture → Options → Capture filter field
# tshark: tshark -i eth0 -f "host 192.168.1.50"

Capture Filters.desc: Captures packets from a specific source IP
Capture Filters.example:
# Captures only packets originating FROM the specified IP.
# e.g. src host 10.10.10.1  — traffic leaving a specific server.
# tshark: tshark -i eth0 -f "src host 10.10.10.1"

Capture Filters.desc: Captures packets to a specific destination IP
Capture Filters.example:
# Captures only packets destined TO the specified IP.
# e.g. dst host 10.10.10.5  — traffic arriving at a specific host.
# Combine: src host A and dst host B for unidirectional session capture.

# Captures packets within an entire subnet.
# e.g. net 10.10.10.0/24  or  net 10.10.10.0 mask 255.255.255.0
# Useful for capturing all traffic within a VLAN or network segment.
# tshark: tshark -i eth0 -f "net 192.168.0.0/16"

Capture Filters.desc: Captures packets on a specific port
Capture Filters.example:
# Captures TCP or UDP packets on the specified port (source or destination).
# e.g. port 443  — captures all HTTPS traffic (both directions).
# Shorthand for: tcp port <port> or udp port <port> combined.
# tshark: tshark -i eth0 -f "port 443"

Capture Filters.desc: Captures TCP packets on a port
Capture Filters.example:
# Captures only TCP packets on the specified port.
# e.g. tcp port 443  — HTTPS only, ignoring UDP/443 (QUIC).
# More precise than port <port> when protocol specificity is needed.
# tshark: tshark -i eth0 -f "tcp port 22"

Capture Filters.desc: Captures UDP packets on a port
Capture Filters.example:
# Captures only UDP packets on the specified port.
# e.g. udp port 53   — DNS queries and responses.
# e.g. udp port 5060 — SIP signalling traffic.
# tshark: tshark -i eth0 -f "udp port 161"

# Captures all ICMP packets (echo, unreachable, time exceeded, redirect…).
# Shows ping requests/replies, traceroute TTL-expired messages, MTU errors.
# Combine: icmp and host 10.0.0.1  — pings to/from a specific host.
# tshark: tshark -i eth0 -f "icmp" -c 20

# Captures ICMPv6 packets (ping6, NDP, MLD, Router Advertisements).
# Essential for troubleshooting IPv6 neighbour discovery and RA issues.
# tshark: tshark -i eth0 -f "icmp6"  — watch ND/RA activity in real time.

# Captures ARP requests and replies (EtherType 0x0806).
# Use to detect ARP storms, duplicate IPs, or ARP poisoning attempts.
# ARP request: Who has 10.0.0.1? Tell 10.0.0.5
# ARP reply:   10.0.0.1 is at 00:50:56:aa:bb:cc
# tshark: tshark -i eth0 -f "arp" -T fields -e arp.src.proto_ipv4 -e arp.dst.proto_ipv4

Capture Filters.desc: Captures packets to/from a MAC address
Capture Filters.example:
# BPF: capture frames to or from a specific MAC address.
# Example: ether host aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff
# Captures all L2 frames where AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF is src OR dst.
# Useful when tracking a device by MAC before IP is known (DHCP phase).

Capture Filters.desc: Captures packets from a MAC address
Capture Filters.example:
# BPF: capture frames FROM a specific MAC (source only).
# Example: ether src aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff
# Only frames originating from this MAC are captured.
# Combine with port filters: ether src aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff and port 443

Capture Filters.desc: Captures packets to a MAC address
Capture Filters.example:
# BPF: capture frames TO a specific MAC (destination only).
# Example: ether dst aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff
# Only frames destined for this MAC are captured.
# Useful for capturing traffic sent to a router/gateway MAC.

# BPF: captures any TCP frame with the SYN bit set (new connections).
# Matches SYN and SYN-ACK frames.
# To capture pure SYN only: tcp[tcpflags] & (tcp-syn|tcp-ack) == tcp-syn
# High SYN rate from a single host indicates a port scan or SYN flood.

# BPF: captures TCP frames with the FIN bit set (graceful close).
# Both the initiating FIN and the FIN-ACK reply match.
# Pair with tcp[tcpflags] & tcp-rst != 0 to capture all teardowns:
#   tcp[tcpflags] & (tcp-fin|tcp-rst) != 0

# BPF: captures TCP frames with the RST bit set (abrupt close / port closed).
# RST from server = port closed or application reject.
# RST from client = application aborted the session.
# High RST rate can indicate a firewall, a crashed service, or a scan.

# BPF: captures all IPv6 traffic.
# Ethertype 0x86DD in the Ethernet header.
# Combines with other filters: ip6 and tcp
# Useful when filtering out IPv4 background noise on dual-stack hosts.

# BPF: captures IP packets with protocol field 6 (TCP).
# Equivalent to the 'tcp' shorthand in modern libpcap.
# Example: ip proto 6 and dst host 10.0.0.1
# Also: ip6 proto 6  for TCP inside IPv6 packets.

# BPF: captures IP packets with protocol field 17 (UDP).
# Equivalent to the 'udp' shorthand.
# Example: ip proto 17 and port 53  → DNS over UDP
# Also: ip6 proto 17  for UDP inside IPv6.

# BPF: captures IP packets with protocol field 1 (ICMP).
# Equivalent to the 'icmp' shorthand.
# Example: ip proto 1 and src host 10.0.0.1  → ICMP from one host
# For ICMPv6 use: ip6 proto 58

Capture Filters.desc: Captures packets larger than a size
Capture Filters.example:
# BPF: captures frames longer than <bytes> bytes (inclusive of all headers).
# Example: greater 1500  → jumbo frames or fragmented IP
# Example: greater 1400 and ip[6:2] & 0x4000 != 0  → DF-bit packets near MTU
# Useful for detecting MTU mismatches or jumbo-frame paths.

Capture Filters.desc: Captures packets smaller than a size
Capture Filters.example:
# BPF: captures frames shorter than <bytes> bytes.
# Example: less 100  → tiny frames (ACKs, control packets, scans)
# Tiny frames at high rate may indicate TCP ACK storms, SYN floods,
# or network scanning tools sending minimal probe packets.

# BPF: excludes all traffic on a specific port (src or dst).
# Example: not port 22  → capture everything except SSH
# Useful for reducing noise from management traffic during troubleshooting.
# Combine: host 10.0.0.1 and not port 22 and not port 161

# BPF: excludes all traffic to or from a specific host.
# Example: not host 10.0.0.254  → hide default-gateway traffic
# Useful when one host (e.g. a monitoring server) dominates the capture.
# Combine: not host 10.0.0.254 and not broadcast

# BPF: captures HTTP and HTTPS traffic in a single filter.
# Matches TCP or UDP on port 80 or 443, either direction.
# Example: host 10.0.0.5 and (port 80 or port 443)
# For TLS capture, combine with an SSLKEYLOGFILE for decryption in Wireshark.

# BPF: captures all IPv4 multicast packets (dest 224.0.0.0/4).
# Includes routing protocols (OSPF 224.0.0.5, EIGRP 224.0.0.10),
# PIM (224.0.0.13), VRRP (224.0.0.18), mDNS (224.0.0.251).
# Combine: ip multicast and ip proto 103  → PIM only

# BPF: captures IPv4 broadcast packets (dst 255.255.255.255).
# Includes DHCP Discover/Request, NetBIOS Name Service broadcasts.
# High broadcast rate (>100 pps) may indicate a broadcast storm.
# Note: directed broadcasts (x.x.x.255) may need a separate filter.

# BPF: captures all 802.1Q VLAN-tagged frames (Ethertype 0x8100).
# Requires a monitor/SPAN port that delivers raw 802.1Q tags.
# Combine: vlan and host 10.20.0.5  → VLAN-tagged traffic to/from a host.
# vlan 20  → only frames tagged with VLAN ID 20.

# BPF: captures PPPoE Session frames (Ethertype 0x8864).
# Used on DSL/broadband links where PPPoE encapsulates IP.
# Example output frame: PPPoESessionData → PPP → IP → TCP
# Combine: pppoes and tcp port 80  → HTTP inside a PPPoE session.

# BPF: captures traffic on any privileged (well-known) port 1–1024.
# Matches TCP or UDP frames where src or dst port is in that range.
# Example: portrange 1-1024 and host 10.0.0.5
# Useful for auditing which services a host is connecting to.

# BPF: captures all TCP traffic on port 22 (SSH), either direction.
# Frames show encrypted payload — use for session counts, not content.
# High number of SYN-only frames to port 22 = brute-force or scanner.
# Combine: tcp port 22 and tcp[tcpflags] & tcp-syn != 0  → new SSH attempts.

# BPF: captures all TCP traffic on port 3389 (RDP), either direction.
# Matches RDP client-to-server and server-to-client frames.
# RDP traffic is encrypted; useful for session-timing or connection-count analysis.
# Combine: tcp port 3389 and net 10.0.0.0/24  → RDP within subnet.

# BPF: captures DNS queries and responses over UDP port 53.
# Frame 1: 10.0.0.5:52341 → 8.8.8.8:53  DNS query A www.example.com
# Frame 2: 8.8.8.8:53 → 10.0.0.5:52341  DNS response 93.184.216.34
# For DNS over TCP (large responses, zone transfers): tcp port 53

# BPF: captures NTP traffic on UDP port 123.
# NTP uses a client/server and symmetric peer model.
# Frame 1: 10.0.0.5:56789 → 216.239.35.0:123  NTPv4 Client
# Frame 2: 216.239.35.0:123 → 10.0.0.5:56789  NTPv4 Server  Stratum=1
# Large NTP traffic from many sources to a single server may indicate NTP amplification.

# BPF: captures IKE (Internet Key Exchange) for IPsec, UDP port 500.
# IKE Phase 1 (Main Mode or Aggressive Mode) runs on port 500.
# Frame 1: 10.0.0.1:500 → 10.0.0.2:500  IKEv2 IKE_SA_INIT Request
# Frame 2: 10.0.0.2:500 → 10.0.0.1:500  IKEv2 IKE_SA_INIT Response
# Once NAT is detected, traffic moves to UDP 4500 (NAT-T).

# BPF: captures NAT-Traversal IPsec (IKE + ESP encapsulated in UDP 4500).
# When one or both peers are behind NAT, IKE moves from 500 to 4500.
# Frame 3: 10.0.0.1:4500 → 10.0.0.2:4500  IKEv2 IKE_AUTH Request
# ESP packets are also wrapped in UDP 4500 for NAT traversal:
# Frame 10: 10.0.0.1:4500 → 10.0.0.2:4500  ESP (encrypted payload)

# BPF: captures ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) packets, IP protocol 50.
# ESP carries encrypted IPsec payload — content is not visible.
# Frame 5: 10.0.0.1 → 10.0.0.2  ESP (SPI=0x12345678)  Seq=1
# Combine with ip host 10.0.0.1  → ESP from a specific peer.
# If behind NAT, ESP is encapsulated in UDP 4500 (use udp port 4500 filter instead).

# BPF: captures AH (Authentication Header) packets, IP protocol 51.
# AH provides integrity and authentication but NOT encryption.
# Frame 6: 10.0.0.1 → 10.0.0.2  AH (SPI=0xABCDEF01)  Next-Header: TCP
# AH is incompatible with NAT (it authenticates the IP header).
# Rarely seen in modern deployments — ESP (proto 50) is used instead.

# BPF: captures ICMPv6 packets (protocol 58 inside IPv6).
# Includes NDP (NS/NA/RS/RA), Echo (ping6), and PMTUD (Packet Too Big).
# Frame 1: fe80::1 → ff02::1   ICMPv6 Router Advertisement
# Frame 2: fe80::2 → fe80::1   ICMPv6 Neighbor Solicitation (type 135)
# Equivalent Wireshark display filter: icmpv6

# BPF: captures IPv6 multicast packets (dst ff00::/8).
# Includes all-nodes (ff02::1), all-routers (ff02::2), solicited-node (ff02::1:ff.../104).
# Frame 1: fe80::1 → ff02::1     ICMPv6 Router Advertisement
# Frame 2: fe80::2 → ff02::1:ff00:1  ICMPv6 Neighbor Solicitation
# Frame 3: :: → ff02::1:2        DHCPv6 Solicit

# BPF: captures IPv6 all-nodes multicast (link-local scope ff02::1).
# All IPv6 interfaces join this group — equivalent to IPv4 broadcast for some purposes.
# Frame 1: fe80::1 → ff02::1  ICMPv6 Router Advertisement (RA)
# Frame 2: fe80::1 → ff02::1  ICMPv6 Redirect
# RAs sent to ff02::1 provide prefix, gateway, and SLAAC configuration to all hosts.

# BPF: captures IPv6 all-routers multicast (ff02::2).
# Only routers and DHCPv6 relay agents listen on this address.
# Frame 1: fe80::2 → ff02::2  ICMPv6 Router Solicitation (RS)
# RS is sent by hosts at boot to request an immediate RA from routers.
# If no RA reply follows the RS, the host won't get SLAAC addressing.

# BPF: captures mDNS traffic over IPv6 (ff02::fb, UDP port 5353).
# mDNS enables zero-configuration service discovery on link-local scope.
# Frame 1: fe80::aabb → ff02::fb  MDNS PTR _http._tcp.local
# Frame 2: fe80::ccdd → ff02::fb  MDNS PTR answer MyDevice._http._tcp.local
# Combine: ip6 dst ff02::fb or (udp port 5353 and ip dst 224.0.0.251)  → all mDNS

# BPF: captures LLMNR (Link-Local Multicast Name Resolution) over IPv6 (ff02::1:3).
# LLMNR is used by Windows for local name resolution when DNS fails.
# Frame 1: fe80::1 → ff02::1:3  LLMNR Query  _WPAD.local
# Frame 2: fe80::2 → ff02::1:3  LLMNR Query  FILESERVER.local
# For IPv4 LLMNR: udp and dst host 224.0.0.252 and port 5355

Capture Filters.desc: TLS traffic to/from a specific host
Capture Filters.example:
# BPF: captures HTTPS traffic specifically to or from 10.0.0.1.
# More precise than a bare port filter — scoped to one host.
# Frame 1: 10.0.0.5:54321 → 10.0.0.1:443  [SYN]
# Frame 2: 10.0.0.1:443  → 10.0.0.5:54321  [SYN,ACK]
# In Wireshark, decrypt with SSLKEYLOGFILE to see the TLS application data.

# BPF: captures all subnet traffic except SSH, reducing management noise.
# Frames in the 10.0.0.0/24 subnet on any port other than 22 are captured.
# Useful for troubleshooting application issues on a subnet
# while ignoring background SSH session polling.
# Extend: net 10.0.0.0/24 and not port 22 and not port 161  (also hide SNMP)

Capture Filters.desc: All frames involving a specific MAC
Capture Filters.example:
# BPF: all Ethernet frames involving a specific MAC address (src or dst).
# Functionally identical to 'ether host' but with the literal MAC inline.
# Example: ether host 00:50:56:8f:aa:bb  → all frames involving a VMware NIC
# Useful when you know a MAC from 'arp -a' output but not the IP yet.

# BPF: captures Ethernet frames tagged with VLAN ID 20 (802.1Q).
# Requires a raw SPAN/mirror port delivering tagged frames.
# Frame 1: src → dst  [802.1Q VLAN=20] IP TCP
# Combine with IP filters inside the VLAN:
#   vlan 20 and host 10.20.0.5  → VLAN-20 traffic for one host

# BPF: captures ICMP Echo Reply packets (type 0) only.
# Matches the response direction of a ping.
# Frame 2: 10.0.0.2 → 10.0.0.1  ICMP Echo Reply  id=0x1234 seq=1 TTL=64
# Pair with icmp[icmptype] == icmp-echo to see requests:
#   icmp[icmptype] == icmp-echo or icmp[icmptype] == icmp-echoreply

Capture Filters.desc: Any SYN or FIN in the TCP flags
Capture Filters.example:
# BPF: captures any TCP frame with SYN or FIN flags set.
# Matches connection open (SYN, SYN-ACK) and graceful close (FIN, FIN-ACK).
# Useful for recording only the handshake and teardown lifecycle
# without capturing the full data payload — very lightweight capture.
# Add tcp-rst for abrupt closes: tcp[tcpflags] & (tcp-syn|tcp-fin|tcp-rst) != 0

# BPF (libpcap 1.9+): excludes SSH (22) and Telnet (23) from capture.
# Reduces management-protocol noise during application troubleshooting.
# Older libpcap equivalent: not port 22 and not port 23
# Extend: port not in (22,23,161,162)  — also hide SNMP

# BPF: captures GRE (Generic Routing Encapsulation) packets, IP protocol 47.
# GRE tunnels carry routed protocols or MPLS inside IP.
# Frame 1: 10.0.0.1 → 10.0.0.2  GRE  Encapsulated: IP 192.168.1.0 → 192.168.2.0
# Wireshark dissects the inner IP automatically.
# Also used for PPTP (GRE with enhanced-GRE, proto 47).

# BPF: captures frames with total length > 1500 bytes.
# Standard Ethernet MTU is 1500 bytes; larger frames require jumbo support.
# Frame 5: src → dst  [Jumbo]  Len=4096  IP TCP
# If you see drops with DF=1 on > 1500 byte frames, suspect MTU mismatch.
# Combine: greater 1500 and ip[6] & 0x40 != 0  → DF-bit jumbo frames

# BPF: captures frames with total length < 100 bytes.
# Tiny frames include pure ACKs (~54 bytes), ICMP unreachable, RSTs.
# High tiny-frame rate from a scanner or DoS tool is a red flag.
# Frame 3: src → dst  Len=54  TCP [ACK]  (pure acknowledgement, no data)

# BPF: captures all Ethernet broadcast (ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff) AND multicast frames.
# Broadcast: ARP, DHCP Discover, NetBIOS.
# Multicast: routing protocols, mDNS, SSDP, LLMNR, PIM.
# Frame 1: → ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff  ARP Who has 10.0.0.1?
# Frame 2: → 01:00:5e:00:00:05  OSPF Hello  224.0.0.5
# High L2 broadcast rate (>500 pps) may indicate a broadcast storm.

Capture Filters.cmd: tshark -i eth0 -f "tcp port 443" -Y "tls.handshake.type==1" -T fields -e ip.src -e tls.handshake.extensions_server_name
Capture Filters.desc: Capture TLS ClientHellos on eth0 and print only the source IP and SNI — fast way to see which hostnames clients on a link are trying to reach without saving a full pcap.
Capture Filters.example:
192.168.1.42  api.example.com
192.168.1.42  fonts.googleapis.com
192.168.1.99  registry.example.org
192.168.1.42  cdn.example.com

Capture Filters.desc: Capture TLS traffic on port 443 but exclude a specific host — useful to remove monitoring/scanner noise
Capture Filters.example:
# Start capture in Wireshark: Capture → Options → enter filter in Capture filter box
# Or via tshark:
#   tshark -i eth0 -f 'tcp port 443 and not host 10.0.0.1' -w /tmp/tls.pcap

# BPF syntax notes:
#   and / or / not   → boolean operators
#   host <ip>        → matches src OR dst
#   src host <ip>    → source only
#   dst host <ip>    → destination only
#   port <n>         → src OR dst port
#   tcp / udp        → protocol qualifier

# Common extensions of this pattern:
#   'tcp port 443 and (host 10.0.0.5 or host 10.0.0.6)' → two specific hosts
