OOBE & Setup.desc: Open a Command Prompt during Windows 11 OOBE — required before running any setup-time bypass commands. Press the key combo on any OOBE screen.
OOBE & Setup.example: (Opens a Command Prompt during Windows Setup/OOBE — before any user account is created)

OOBE & Setup.desc: Bypass the Microsoft account requirement on Windows 11 OOBE — launches the legacy local-account creation flow. Open Command Prompt with Shift+F10 first, then run this. Works on current Windows 11 24H2/25H2 builds.
OOBE & Setup.example: (Launches the local account creation flow in Windows 11 OOBE — bypasses Microsoft account requirement)

OOBE & Setup.desc: Legacy sign-in bypass — disables the network/Microsoft-account requirement so OOBE can complete offline. Run from Shift+F10 Command Prompt; system will reboot. NOTE: does not always work — Microsoft removed/blocked the script in some Windows 11 24H2 builds. If it fails or has no effect, use start ms-cxh:localonly instead.
OOBE & Setup.example: (Registers a registry key that forces OOBE to restart without a network requirement; PC reboots to apply)

OOBE & Setup.desc: Drop the network during OOBE so Windows falls back to local-account flow. Run from Shift+F10 Command Prompt.
OOBE & Setup.example:
Windows IP Configuration
Successfully released the IP address on adapter Ethernet.

OOBE & Setup.desc: Kill the OOBE network screen — forces Windows to skip ahead. Use only if start ms-cxh:localonly is unavailable on your build.
OOBE & Setup.example:
SUCCESS: The process "oobenetworkconnectionflow.exe" with PID 4840 has been terminated.
(Kills the network-required page in Windows 11 OOBE)

OOBE & Setup.desc: Edit registry during OOBE — useful for setting HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\OOBE\BypassNRO=1 then reboot to skip network screen.
OOBE & Setup.example: (Opens Registry Editor — used during OOBE to navigate to HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\OOBE and set BypassNRO = 1)

OOBE & Setup.desc: Reboot from the OOBE Command Prompt after applying registry tweaks.
OOBE & Setup.example:
C:\> shutdown /r /t 0
(Immediate reboot — no delay, no warning to logged-in users)

# Common variations:
  shutdown /s /t 0       — shutdown (power off) immediately
  shutdown /r /t 60      — reboot in 60 seconds with a warning
  shutdown /a            — abort a pending shutdown
  shutdown /r /t 0 /f    — force close apps and reboot immediately
  Restart-Computer       — PowerShell equivalent

EC2.desc: Returns full metadata for all EC2 instances in the region: state, type, AMI, VPC, subnet, security groups, tags, and network interfaces. Use --filters and --query to narrow output.
EC2.example:
$ aws ec2 describe-instances --query "Reservations[].Instances[].[InstanceId,State.Name,InstanceType,PrivateIpAddress,Tags[?Key==`Name`].Value|[0]]" --output table
--------------------------------------------------------------
|                     DescribeInstances                      |
+---------------------+-------+----------+---------------+---+
| i-0a1b2c3d4e5f67890 | running | t3.medium | 10.0.1.42  | WebServer-01 |
| i-0b2c3d4e5f678901 | stopped | t3.small  | 10.0.1.55  | BackupAgent  |

EC2.desc: Lists all EBS volumes in the region with their ID, size, state, type, IOPS, availability zone, and attachment information. Filter by --filters Name=status,Values=available to find unattached volumes.
EC2.example:
$ aws ec2 describe-volumes --query "Volumes[].[VolumeId,Size,State,VolumeType,AvailabilityZone,Attachments[0].InstanceId]" --output table
---------------------------------------------------------------
| vol-0abc1234def56789 | 100 | in-use    | gp3 | us-east-1a | i-0a1b2c3d |
| vol-0def5678abc12345 |  20 | available | gp3 | us-east-1b | None       |
# Unattached volumes incur cost — review and delete if unused

EC2.desc: Returns all security groups with their inbound and outbound rules, protocol, port ranges, and CIDR sources. Use --group-ids to query a specific group.
Lists all VPCs in the region with their ID, CIDR block, state, whether it is the default VPC, DHCP options, and tags.
EC2.example:
$ aws ec2 describe-vpcs --query "Vpcs[].[VpcId,CidrBlock,IsDefault,State,Tags[?Key==`Name`].Value|[0]]" --output table
---------------------------------------------------------------
| vpc-0abc1234def56789 | 10.0.0.0/16  | False | available | Production-VPC |
| vpc-0def5678abc12345 | 172.31.0.0/16 | True  | available | default        |

EC2.desc: Lists all subnets with their ID, VPC, CIDR block, availability zone, available IP count, and whether instances auto-assign public IPs.
EC2.example:
$ aws ec2 describe-subnets --query "Subnets[].[SubnetId,VpcId,CidrBlock,AvailabilityZone,AvailableIpAddressCount,MapPublicIpOnLaunch]" --output table
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
| subnet-0abc12  | vpc-0abc1234 | 10.0.1.0/24 | us-east-1a |  250 | False |
| subnet-0def56  | vpc-0abc1234 | 10.0.2.0/24 | us-east-1b |  248 | False |
| subnet-0ghi90  | vpc-0abc1234 | 10.0.0.0/24 | us-east-1a |  249 | True  |

EC2.desc: Returns all route tables with their routes, subnet associations, and VPC — essential for verifying traffic routing through IGWs, NAT GWs, TGWs, or VPX endpoints.
Lists Elastic Network Interfaces (ENIs) with their attachment, private IP, public IP, security groups, and VPC — useful for finding orphaned ENIs that are accumulating charges.
EC2.example:
$ aws ec2 describe-network-interfaces --query "NetworkInterfaces[].[NetworkInterfaceId,Status,PrivateIpAddress,Association.PublicIp,Description]" --output table
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
| eni-0abc1234 | in-use    | 10.0.1.42 | 54.82.11.200 | Primary network interface     |
| eni-0def5678 | available | 10.0.1.77 | None         | ELB app/my-alb/abc123         |
# Status "available" means detached — check before deleting

EC2.desc: Lists all Elastic IP addresses in the region, their association with instances or ENIs, and allocation IDs — unassociated EIPs incur hourly charges.
EC2.example:
$ aws ec2 describe-addresses --query "Addresses[].[PublicIp,AllocationId,InstanceId,AssociationId]" --output table
--------------------------------------------------------------------
|  54.82.11.200 | eipalloc-0abc1234 | i-0a1b2c3d | eipassoc-0xyz |
| 100.25.44.123 | eipalloc-0def5678 | None       | None          |
# The second EIP is unassociated — $0.005/hr charge applies

EC2.desc: Lists all AMIs owned by your account with their ID, name, creation date, state, and architecture — use to audit custom AMIs and identify old snapshots to clean up.
EC2.example:
$ aws ec2 describe-images --owners self --query "Images[].[ImageId,Name,State,CreationDate,Architecture]" --output table
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
| ami-0abc1234 | WebServer-Base-2026-06 | available | 2026-06-01T10:00:00Z | x86_64 |
| ami-0def5678 | AppServer-v2.1         | available | 2026-03-15T08:30:00Z | x86_64 |

EC2.desc: Returns the system and instance status checks for running instances — checks report reachability at the hypervisor and OS level; failed checks typically require a stop/start.
EC2.example:
$ aws ec2 describe-instance-status --instance-ids i-0a1b2c3d4e5f67890 --query "InstanceStatuses[].[InstanceId,SystemStatus.Status,InstanceStatus.Status]" --output table
-------------------------------------------------------
| i-0a1b2c3d4e5f67890 | ok | ok |

# Failing check example:
| i-0b2c3d4e5f678901 | impaired | ok |
# "impaired" system status = hardware issue; stop + start to migrate to new host

EC2.desc: List all Application and Network load balancers with state and DNS name
List RDS instances with status, class, engine and endpoint hostname

Open an interactive shell to an EC2 instance via SSM Session Manager — no SSH key, open port 22, or bastion host required; instance needs the SSM agent and an IAM role with AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore
EC2.example:
# Start an interactive session (opens a shell directly in your terminal):
$ aws ssm start-session --target i-0abc123def456789a

Starting session with SessionId: user-0abcdef1234567890
$

# Port-forward a remote port to localhost (e.g. RDP or a database):
$ aws ssm start-session \
    --target i-0abc123def456789a \
    --document-name AWS-StartPortForwardingSession \
    --parameters '{"portNumber":["3389"],"localPortNumber":["13389"]}'

# Then connect to localhost:13389 with your RDP client.

# Prerequisites:
# 1. EC2 instance must have SSM Agent running (pre-installed on Amazon Linux 2/2023, Windows 2016+)
# 2. EC2 IAM role must include AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore policy
# 3. aws CLI Session Manager plugin must be installed locally:
#    https://docs.aws.amazon.com/systems-manager/latest/userguide/session-manager-working-with-install-plugin.html

EC2.desc: List RDS database instances with endpoint, engine version, instance class, allocated storage, and availability status
Create a point-in-time EBS snapshot of a volume for backup or AMI creation
EC2.example:
> aws ec2 create-snapshot --volume-id vol-0a1b2c3d4e5f67890 --description "Pre-upgrade backup 2026-06-16"
{
    "SnapshotId": "snap-0a1b2c3d4e5f67890",
    "VolumeId":   "vol-0a1b2c3d4e5f67890",
    "State":      "pending",
    "StartTime":  "2026-06-16T10:00:00.000Z",
    "Progress":   "0%",
    "Description":"Pre-upgrade backup 2026-06-16"
}
# Poll until complete:
aws ec2 describe-snapshots --snapshot-ids snap-0a1b2c3d4e5f67890 --query "Snapshots[0].State"

EC2.desc: List all EBS snapshots owned by the current account with their status and creation time
Shows the physical network hierarchy (network-node layers) for EC2 instances — used to place latency-sensitive HPC/ML workloads for the tightest network proximity within a placement group.
EC2.example:
$ aws ec2 describe-instance-topology --instance-ids i-0a1b2c3d4e5f67890 --output table
-------------------------------------------------------------------
|                     DescribeInstanceTopology                    |
+---------------------+----------------+----------------------------+
|  InstanceId          | InstanceType   | NetworkNodes               |
+---------------------+----------------+----------------------------+
|  i-0a1b2c3d4e5f67890 | p4d.24xlarge   | nn-1a2b, nn-3c4d, nn-5e6f  |
+---------------------+----------------+----------------------------+
# NetworkNodes lists the network layers from top-of-rack down; instances
# sharing all node IDs sit on the same rack for lowest inter-node latency.

S3.desc: Lists every S3 bucket in the account with its creation date — the entry point for the high-level `aws s3` sync/cp/rm commands (add a bucket name to list its objects instead).
Copy files to/from S3 or between buckets; supports local paths and s3:// URIs, with --recursive for directories
S3.example:
# Upload local file to S3:
$ aws s3 cp ./report.pdf s3://acme-data-lake/reports/2026/report.pdf
upload: ./report.pdf to s3://acme-data-lake/reports/2026/report.pdf

# Download from S3 to local:
$ aws s3 cp s3://acme-data-lake/reports/2026/report.pdf ./
download: s3://acme-data-lake/reports/2026/report.pdf to ./report.pdf

# Copy between buckets:
$ aws s3 cp s3://acme-src/data/ s3://acme-dst/data/ --recursive

# Upload with SSE-KMS encryption:
#   aws s3 cp file.txt s3://bucket/file.txt --sse aws:kms --sse-kms-key-id alias/my-key

upload: ./dist/index.html to s3://acme-static-assets/index.html
upload: ./dist/app.js to s3://acme-static-assets/app.js
upload: ./dist/styles.css to s3://acme-static-assets/styles.css

S3.desc: Delete a single object from an S3 bucket; add --recursive to delete a prefix/folder and all objects beneath it
S3.example:
# Delete a single file:
$ aws s3 rm s3://acme-data-lake/reports/old-report-2025.pdf
delete: s3://acme-data-lake/reports/old-report-2025.pdf

# Delete all objects under a prefix (recursive):
$ aws s3 rm s3://acme-data-lake/logs/2025/ --recursive
delete: s3://acme-data-lake/logs/2025/01.log
delete: s3://acme-data-lake/logs/2025/02.log
...

# Dry-run to preview (use ls instead):
#   aws s3 ls s3://acme-data-lake/logs/2025/ --recursive

S3.desc: Create a new S3 bucket in the default region; use --region to specify a different region
S3.example:
$ aws s3 mb s3://acme-app-assets-2026
make_bucket: s3://acme-app-assets-2026

# Create in a specific region:
$ aws s3 mb s3://acme-backups-eu --region eu-west-2
make_bucket: s3://acme-backups-eu

# Bucket names must be globally unique, 3-63 chars, lowercase, no underscores.
# Enable versioning after creation:
#   aws s3api put-bucket-versioning --bucket acme-app-assets-2026 --versioning-configuration Status=Enabled

S3.desc: Remove an empty S3 bucket; use --force to delete a non-empty bucket and all its contents
S3.example:
# Remove an empty bucket:
$ aws s3 rb s3://acme-test-bucket
remove_bucket: s3://acme-test-bucket

# Remove a non-empty bucket (DESTRUCTIVE — deletes all objects):
$ aws s3 rb s3://acme-test-bucket --force
delete: s3://acme-test-bucket/logs/2026-01-01.log
delete: s3://acme-test-bucket/reports/q1.pdf
remove_bucket: s3://acme-test-bucket

# WARNING: --force permanently deletes all objects and the bucket.
# Consider aws s3 ls s3://acme-test-bucket first to review contents.

S3.desc: Shows the legacy S3 access-control list for a bucket — owner and each grantee's permission (READ, WRITE, FULL_CONTROL) — separate from and usually overridden by the bucket policy
List all S3 bucket names in the account as a JSON array, filtered from the full list-buckets output
S3.example:
$ aws s3api list-buckets --query "Buckets[].Name"
[
    "cf-templates-corp-eu-west-2",
    "acme-data-lake",
    "acme-logs-archive",
    "acme-static-assets"
]

# Include creation dates:
#   aws s3api list-buckets --query "Buckets[].{Name:Name,Created:CreationDate}"

# Filter by prefix:
#   aws s3api list-buckets --query "Buckets[?starts_with(Name,'acme')].Name"

# Count total buckets:
#   aws s3api list-buckets --query "length(Buckets)"

S3.desc: Return the AWS region where a bucket resides; returns null for us-east-1 (S3 global default region)

S3.cmd: aws s3api get-bucket-policy --bucket <b> --output text --query Policy | jq .

All object versions incl. delete markers

S3.example:
$ aws s3api list-buckets --query "Buckets[].Name" --output table

|       ListBuckets        |
+---------------------------+

        {"Name": "acme-static-assets", "CreationDate": "2023-08-02T14:03:10+00:00"}

2026-06-12T09:42:17 INFO Starting request processing
2026-06-12T09:42:18 INFO Request completed in 42ms
2026-06-12T09:42:20 WARN High memory usage: 87%

CloudWatch.desc: Searches a CloudWatch Logs group across all (or a time-bounded set of) log streams for entries matching a filter pattern — the CLI equivalent of the Logs Insights "Filter" box, useful for grepping application errors without opening the console
CloudWatch.example:
$ aws logs filter-log-events \
    --log-group-name /aws/lambda/order-processor \
    --filter-pattern "ERROR" \
    --start-time $(date -d "-1 hour" +%s000)

{
    "events": [
        {"logStreamName": "2026/07/02/[$LATEST]abc123", "timestamp": 1749714137000,
         "message": "ERROR Connection refused to db.internal:5432", "eventId": "123"},
        {"logStreamName": "2026/07/02/[$LATEST]abc123", "timestamp": 1749714198000,
         "message": "ERROR Timeout on db query", "eventId": "124"}
    ]
}

# --filter-pattern supports the CloudWatch Logs pattern syntax, e.g. "?ERROR ?WARN"
# for an OR match, or JSON-field matching like "{ $.statusCode = 500 }" for JSON logs.

CloudWatch.desc: Set the CloudWatch Logs retention period for a log group; older log events are automatically deleted after the retention window
CloudWatch.example:
$ aws logs put-retention-policy \
    --log-group-name /app/prod/api \
    --retention-in-days 90

# No output on success.

# Verify:
$ aws logs describe-log-groups --log-group-name-prefix /app/prod/api \
    --query "logGroups[].{Name:logGroupName,Retention:retentionInDays}"
[
    {
        "Name": "/app/prod/api",
        "Retention": 90
    }
]

# Common retention values: 1,3,5,7,14,30,60,90,120,150,180,365,400,545,731,1827,3653

CloudWatch.desc: Permanently delete a CloudWatch log group and all its log streams and events; irreversible
CloudWatch.example:
# Preview what will be deleted:
$ aws logs describe-log-groups --log-group-name-prefix /app/test \
    --query "logGroups[].logGroupName"
[
    "/app/test/api",
    "/app/test/worker"
]

# Delete:
$ aws logs delete-log-group --log-group-name /app/test/api

# No output on success. Verify:
$ aws logs describe-log-groups --log-group-name-prefix /app/test/api
{
    "logGroups": []
}

# WARNING: This is permanent — no recovery without a CloudTrail + S3 export backup.

START RequestId: abc-123 Version: $LATEST
END RequestId: abc-123
REPORT RequestId: abc-123  Duration: 42.12 ms  Billed Duration: 43 ms  Memory Size: 512 MB  Max Memory Used: 98 MB

IAM.desc: Assumes an IAM role and returns temporary credentials for it
Lists the managed (customer or AWS) policies attached to an IAM role
Returns account-wide IAM entity counts and quota usage (users, groups, roles, policies, MFA devices) — a quick way to check how close the account is to IAM service limits
IAM.example:
$ aws iam get-account-summary
{
    "SummaryMap": {
        "Users": 42,
        "UsersQuota": 5000,
        "Groups": 8,
        "GroupsQuota": 300,
        "Roles": 116,
        "RolesQuota": 1000,
        "Policies": 63,
        "PoliciesQuota": 1500,
        "MFADevices": 39,
        "MFADevicesInUse": 39,
        "AccountMFAEnabled": 1,
        "AccessKeysPerUserQuota": 2
    }
}

What it means:
- "*Quota" fields are the account's hard service limits — most IAM quotas
  cannot be raised via a support ticket, so watch Roles/Policies/Users
  approaching quota before automation starts failing.
- AccountMFAEnabled refers to the root user only, not IAM users.

SNS.desc: Publish a message to an SNS topic, triggering delivery to all subscribed endpoints (email, SQS, Lambda, HTTP)
SNS.example:
$ aws sns publish \
    --topic-arn arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:MyAlerts \
    --message "Disk usage on prod-web01 is above 90%" \
    --subject "High Disk Alert"
{
  "MessageId": "da68f62c-0c07-4bee-bf5f-7e856EXAMPLE"
}

SNS.desc: List all subscriptions on an SNS topic — shows protocol, endpoint, and subscription ARN
Lists the ARNs of every SNS topic in the current region — the starting point for auditing notification topics before drilling into subscriptions or publishing test messages.

SQS.desc: Receive up to 10 messages from an SQS queue — messages are not deleted until you send a delete-message request
Show all attributes of an SQS queue: message counts, visibility timeout, ARN, encryption settings
Lists the URLs of every SQS queue in the current region — supports --queue-name-prefix to filter — the usual first step before calling get-queue-attributes or receive-message on a specific queue.

CloudFormation.desc: Show full status and parameters of a CloudFormation stack — use to check deployment health, outputs, and drift

List all CloudFormation stacks matching the specified status filters. Useful for inventorying live stacks. Supports filters: CREATE_COMPLETE, UPDATE_COMPLETE, DELETE_FAILED, ROLLBACK_COMPLETE, and others.
CloudFormation.example:
$ aws cloudformation list-stacks     --stack-status-filter CREATE_COMPLETE UPDATE_COMPLETE     --query 'StackSummaries[*].[StackName,StackStatus,CreationTime]'     --output table

---------------------------------------------------
|                   ListStacks                    |
+---------------------+------------------+--------+
| prod-vpc-stack      | CREATE_COMPLETE  | 2025-01-10T09:22:14Z |
| prod-rds-stack      | UPDATE_COMPLETE  | 2025-03-14T15:33:01Z |
| staging-eks-cluster | CREATE_COMPLETE  | 2025-05-02T11:05:44Z |
+---------------------+------------------+--------+

Note: Deleted stacks are retained in the list for 90 days.
Filter by DELETE_FAILED to find stacks that failed to clean up.

EKS.desc: Lists all EKS cluster names in the current AWS region — use --region to query other regions.
EKS.example:
$ aws eks list-clusters
{
    "clusters": [
        "prod-cluster",
        "staging-cluster",
        "dev-cluster"
    ]
}

# List across all regions:
$ for r in $(aws ec2 describe-regions --query "Regions[].RegionName" --output text); do
  echo "=== $r ===" && aws eks list-clusters --region $r 2>/dev/null
done

EKS.desc: Returns full details for an EKS cluster: Kubernetes version, endpoint URL, RBAC, VPC config, logging settings, status, and add-on versions.
EKS.example:
$ aws eks describe-cluster --name prod-cluster --query "cluster.[name,version,status,endpoint]" --output table
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
| prod-cluster | 1.30 | ACTIVE | https://ABCDEF1234567890.gr7.us-east-1.eks.amazonaws.com |

# Get kubeconfig for kubectl access:
$ aws eks update-kubeconfig --name prod-cluster --region us-east-1
Updated context arn:aws:eks:us-east-1:123456789012:cluster/prod-cluster in ~/.kube/config

EKS.desc: Writes the cluster endpoint, CA data, and an aws-iam-authenticator entry into ~/.kube/config so kubectl can authenticate to the specified EKS cluster.
EKS.example:
$ aws eks update-kubeconfig --name my-cluster --region us-east-1
Added new context arn:aws:eks:us-east-1:813759318741:cluster/my-cluster to /home/user/.kube/config

ACM.desc: Lists all ACM certificates in ISSUED state with their ARN and domain name — use to audit TLS certificates, identify soon-to-expire certs, and find cert ARNs for ALB/CloudFront attachment.
ACM.example:
$ aws acm list-certificates --certificate-statuses ISSUED --query "CertificateSummaryList[].[DomainName,CertificateArn]" --output table
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
| *.example.com     | arn:aws:acm:us-east-1:123456789012:certificate/abc1-def2-ghi3 |
| api.example.com   | arn:aws:acm:us-east-1:123456789012:certificate/jkl4-mno5-pqr6 |

# Check expiry for a specific cert:
$ aws acm describe-certificate --certificate-arn arn:aws:acm:... --query "Certificate.[DomainName,NotAfter,Status]"

ACM.desc: Returns full metadata for a single ACM certificate — domain validation status, issuance/expiry dates, key algorithm, and in-use-by resources (ALB, CloudFront) — the standard follow-up to list-certificates when auditing a specific cert's health.

KMS.desc: Lists all KMS key IDs and ARNs in the current region — use describe-key to get metadata (alias, key state, deletion date) for each key ID.
KMS.example:
$ aws kms list-keys --query "Keys[].[KeyId,KeyArn]" --output table
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
| 12345678-1234-1234-1234-123456789012 | arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789012:key/12345678... |
| abcdef12-abcd-abcd-abcd-abcdef123456 | arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789012:key/abcdef12... |

# Get aliases (friendly names) for all keys:
$ aws kms list-aliases --query "Aliases[].[AliasName,TargetKeyId]" --output table
| alias/prod-secrets   | 12345678-1234-1234-1234-123456789012 |
| alias/dev-rds-backup | abcdef12-abcd-abcd-abcd-abcdef123456 |

KMS.desc: Returns metadata for a single KMS key — key state (Enabled/Disabled/PendingDeletion), origin, rotation-eligible key spec, and creation date — used to confirm a key is usable before referencing it in an encryption policy or IAM condition.

Secrets Manager.desc: Retrieve and decrypt the current value of a stored secret
Secrets Manager.example:
$ aws secretsmanager get-secret-value --secret-id prod/db/password --query SecretString --output text
{"username":"app_user","password":"REDACTED"}

Secrets Manager.desc: List the names of all secrets visible to the current IAM principal

CloudTrail.desc: Searches recent CloudTrail management events by attribute (e.g. EventName, Username)

Lambda.desc: Synchronously invokes a Lambda function with the given JSON payload and writes the function's response body to response.json — the fastest way to test a function from the CLI without going through API Gateway; add --invocation-type Event to fire-and-forget instead.
Lambda.example:
$ aws lambda invoke --function-name my-function \
  --cli-binary-format raw-in-base64-out \
  --payload '{"key": "value"}' response.json
{
    "StatusCode": 200,
    "ExecutedVersion": "$LATEST"
}

$ cat response.json
{"statusCode": 200, "body": "Hello from Lambda!"}

# StatusCode 200 only confirms the invocation succeeded — check the response
# body (or FunctionError in the invoke output) for errors raised inside the function.
# Official docs: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/reference/lambda/invoke.html

ECS.desc: Shows a service's deployment status, task definition, desired/running/pending task counts, and load balancer bindings — the go-to command for confirming a deployment finished rolling out (desiredCount == runningCount, pendingCount == 0) or diagnosing why tasks won't reach steady state.
ECS.example:
$ aws ecs describe-services --cluster prod-cluster --services my-http-service \
  --query "services[0].[serviceName,status,desiredCount,runningCount,pendingCount]" --output table
----------------------------------------------------
|  my-http-service | ACTIVE | 10 | 10 | 0           |

# runningCount < desiredCount for an extended period usually means tasks are
# crash-looping — check 'aws ecs describe-tasks' or CloudWatch Logs next.
# Official docs: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/reference/ecs/describe-services.html

ECR.cmd: aws ecr get-login-password --region <region> | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin <account-id>.dkr.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com
ECR.desc: Fetches a short-lived (12-hour) ECR authorization token and pipes it into 'docker login' so the local Docker CLI can push/pull images from a private ECR registry — the current, non-deprecated replacement for the old 'aws ecr get-login' command which printed a full docker login string.
ECR.example:
$ aws ecr get-login-password --region us-east-1 \
  | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com
Login Succeeded

$ docker push 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/my-app:latest

# Re-run before every push/pull once the 12-hour token expires (Docker itself
# does not refresh it automatically).
# Official docs: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/reference/ecr/get-login-password.html

DynamoDB.desc: Reads every item in a table (or up to 1MB per page) and returns them as Items — useful for small tables or ad-hoc inspection, but an expensive, non-indexed full read; prefer 'aws dynamodb query' with a key condition on production-sized tables since scan consumes read capacity proportional to the whole table.

Auto Scaling.desc: Shows an Auto Scaling group's min/max/desired capacity, availability zones, health-check type, and the current list of member instances with their lifecycle state — the first command to run when instance count looks wrong or a scaling policy doesn't seem to be firing.
Auto Scaling.example:
$ aws autoscaling describe-auto-scaling-groups --auto-scaling-group-names web-asg \
  --query "AutoScalingGroups[0].[AutoScalingGroupName,MinSize,MaxSize,DesiredCapacity]" --output table
----------------------------------------
|  web-asg | 2 | 6 | 3                 |

$ aws autoscaling describe-auto-scaling-groups --auto-scaling-group-names web-asg \
  --query "AutoScalingGroups[0].Instances[].[InstanceId,LifecycleState,HealthStatus]" --output table

# DesiredCapacity stuck below MinSize/above MaxSize, or instances stuck in
# Pending:Wait, points to a launch template or health-check misconfiguration.
# Official docs: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/reference/autoscaling/describe-auto-scaling-groups.html

System.desc: Displays SDX system summary
System.example:
> show system

Hostname: sdx-dc-01
IP:       10.10.10.100  Netmask: 255.255.255.0
Gateway:  10.10.10.1
Version:  SDX 14.1 Build 21.57
Platform: SDX 22000
Uptime:   45 days, 3 hours, 22 minutes
CPUs: 2  Memory: 65536 MB  Disk: 1200 GB

What it means:
- Hostname / IP confirm you are on the right appliance.
- Version and Platform are needed for compatibility checks and SR filing.
- Uptime resets after every reboot — a recent value may indicate an unplanned restart.

System.desc: Shows SDX firmware version
System.example:
> show system version

SDX 14.1: Build 21.57.nc, Date: Mar  5 2026, 14:22:30

System.desc: Lists all available system backup files on the SDX management service with their name, size, creation timestamp, and type (full or incremental).
System.example:
> show system backup

Name                           Size      Created            Type
SDX_full_2026-06-25_02-00.tgz  1.24 GB   Jun 25 02:00:15    Full
SDX_full_2026-06-18_02-00.tgz  1.21 GB   Jun 18 02:00:12    Full
SDX_full_2026-06-11_02-00.tgz  1.19 GB   Jun 11 02:00:09    Full

# Trigger a manual backup:
> create system backup filename SDX_pre-upgrade backup-level full

System.desc: Shows details of a specific backup
System.example:
show system backup backup-20260615

Name:      backup-20260615
Size:      4.2 MB
Created:   Jun 15 2026 02:00:01
Type:      Full
Status:    Complete

System.desc: Lists all local user accounts on the SDX management service with their username, role (superuser/admin/read-only), and last login time.
System.example:
> show system user

nsroot         superuser         Jun 28 09:45:11
admin-ops      admin             Jun 27 16:22:05
monitoring-ro  read-only         Jun 28 08:00:01
backup-svc     admin             Jun 25 02:00:00

# Add a user:
> add system user <username> <password> -externalauth DISABLED

System.example:
> show system user nsroot

Last Login:  Jun 28 09:45:11 from 10.0.0.5

System.example:

  SNMP Community: public

System.desc: Displays disk partition usage on the SDX management hypervisor: total capacity, used space, available space, and mount points for each filesystem.
System.example:
> show system storage

Filesystem         Size    Used    Avail   Use%  Mounted On
/dev/sda1          50 GB   18 GB   32 GB   36%   /
/dev/sda2          400 GB  220 GB  180 GB  55%   /var
/var/mps/datastore  500 GB  380 GB  120 GB  76%   /var/mps/datastore

# /var/mps/datastore at 76% — consider purging old VPX images or backups

System.desc: Reports how long the SDX management service (MPS) and the underlying hypervisor have been running since last boot — useful for correlating uptime with incident timelines.
System.example:

Host (XenServer) uptime   : 45 days, 3 hours, 30 minutes, 22 seconds

System.desc: Shows the NTP synchronisation status on the SDX management service: sync state, stratum, configured NTP servers, and last sync time.
System.example:
> show system ntp

NTP Sync Status : Synchronized
NTP Stratum     : 3
Reference Clock : 10.0.1.50 (ntp-primary.corp.local)

NTP Servers Configured:
  Server           State       Last Used
  10.0.1.50        reachable   Jun 28 09:40:00  (primary)
  10.0.1.51        reachable   Jun 28 09:38:00  (secondary)

# Unsynchronised status causes certificate validity errors and log timestamp skew

System.desc: Lists configured NTP servers
System.example:
> show system ntp servers

NTP Server    Stratum  Offset(ms)  State
-----------   -------  ----------  ------
192.168.1.100    2       +0.423    SYNC
192.168.1.101    2       +1.102    REACH
ntp.example.com  1       +0.088    SYNC

Done

Note: SYNC = actively syncing from this peer. REACH = reachable but not selected as primary source.

System.desc: Shows the DNS configuration on the SDX management service: primary and secondary resolver addresses and the search domain list.
System.example:
> show system dns

DNS Configuration:
  Primary   : 10.0.1.53
  Secondary : 10.0.1.54
  Search Domain:
    corp.local
    corp.example.com

# Verify resolution works from SDX:
> exec nslookup ntp-primary.corp.local
Server:  10.0.1.53
Address: 10.0.1.53

Name:    ntp-primary.corp.local
Address: 10.0.1.50

System.desc: Lists the configured DNS resolver addresses and their operational status on the SDX management service — distinct from "show system dns" in that it focuses on server reachability.
System.example:
> show system dns servers

DNS Servers:
  Primary  : 10.0.1.53  State: Reachable    RTT: 1ms
  Secondary: 10.0.1.54  State: Reachable    RTT: 2ms

# If Primary is unreachable, SDX MPS will use Secondary for all name resolution
# including license server lookups, SMTP relay, and SNMP trap destinations

System.desc: Displays the hostname configured on the SDX management service (SVM) — the identity shown in its own UI/CLI prompt, independent of DNS or the hostnames of any VPX instances it hosts
System.example:
> show system hostname

Hostname: sdx-datacenter-01

What it means:
- This is the SDX Management Service's own hostname, not the hostname
  of any NetScaler VPX instance running on it — check inside each VPX
  (via its own "show ns hostName") for that.
- Change it with: > set system hostname <new-name>
  (takes effect immediately; does not require a reboot).

XenServer CLI.desc: Lists the Virtual Block Devices (disk attachments) for a given VM — each VBD's uuid, the userdevice slot it's attached at, and whether it's bootable — the step before xe vbd-unplug/vbd-destroy when detaching or resizing a VPX instance's virtual disk.

[root@sdx ~]# xe vbd-list vm-name-label=NS-VPX01
uuid ( RO)             : 3f7a9c1e-4b2d-4a11-9c3e-1122334455aa
         vm-uuid ( RO): 8b21e4d0-...
 vm-name-label ( RO): NS-VPX01
    userdevice ( RW): 0
      bootable ( RW): true
          mode ( RW): RW
          type ( RO): Disk

# Find the attached VDI (virtual disk) for that VBD:
[root@sdx ~]# xe vbd-param-get uuid=3f7a9c1e-4b2d-4a11-9c3e-1122334455aa param-name=vdi-uuid

Xen Hypervisor.desc: Immediately powers off (hard-kills) a running Xen domain by ID — equivalent to pulling the power cord; use 'xl shutdown' for a graceful ACPI shutdown instead when the VPX guest OS is still responsive.
Xen Hypervisor.example:
# xl destroy 4
Destroying domain 4
Domain 4 has been destroyed
$ xl list
Name                          ID   Mem VCPUs State    Time(s)
Domain-0                        0  4096     4 r-----   18432.1
# The domain disappears from 'xl list' immediately; any unflushed disk
# writes inside the VPX guest are lost — reserve this for hung/unresponsive VPX.

vif2.0

System.desc: Slot inventory (StackWise stack members, supervisor, line cards, transceivers) with status, hardware revision, serial, uptime.

System.cmd: show platform software status switch active
System.desc: IOS-XE platform status on the active stack switch: CPU/memory by core, process states, dataplane health.
System.example:
sw# show platform software status switch active
Load Average
  Slot  Status  1-Min  5-Min  15-Min
  1-RP0 Healthy   0.45   0.42   0.40

Memory (kB)
  Slot  Status    Total      Used (Pct)       Free (Pct) Committed (Pct)
  1-RP0 Healthy   3987654    1567823 (39%)   2419831 (61%)  2103456 (53%)

CPU Utilization
  Slot  CPU   User System   Nice   Idle    IRQ   SIRQ IOwait
  1-RP0   0  4.20   1.10   0.00  94.00   0.10   0.60   0.00
  1-RP0   1  3.80   0.95   0.00  95.10   0.05   0.10   0.00
  1-RP0   2  3.20   0.85   0.00  95.80   0.05   0.10   0.00
  1-RP0   3  2.90   0.75   0.00  96.20   0.05   0.10   0.00

System.cmd: show platform software status switch standby
System.desc: Same as switch active but for the standby supervisor in HA stack — confirms sync and idle behaviour.
System.example:
sw# show platform software status switch standby
Load Average
  Slot  Status  1-Min  5-Min  15-Min
  2-RP0 Healthy   0.21   0.18   0.16

Memory (kB)
  Slot  Status    Total      Used (Pct)       Free (Pct)
  2-RP0 Healthy   3987654    1234567 (31%)   2753087 (69%)

CPU Utilization
  Slot  CPU   User System   Idle    IRQ   SIRQ
  2-RP0   0  1.50   0.40   97.80   0.10   0.20
  2-RP0   1  1.20   0.30   98.30   0.05   0.15

# Standby idle (~98%) is normal — it only processes sync traffic.

System.desc: All running IOS-XE processes (Linux-kernel level): PID, name, CPU%, memory. Use to find a misbehaving daemon.
System.example:
sw# show platform software process list switch active r0
Name                    Pid    PPid   Status       Size                   uTime    sTime
platform_mgr           12345      1   sleeping     324567 KB              123456    12345
iosd                   12346      1   sleeping     876543 KB              987654   123456
fman_rp                12347      1   sleeping     234567 KB               45678     6789
fed                    12348      1   sleeping     456789 KB              234567    34567
btman                  12349      1   sleeping     123456 KB               12345     1234
snmp                   12350      1   sleeping      98765 KB                3456      345
logger                 12351      1   sleeping      87654 KB                4567      456

System.cmd: show platform software memory switch active
System.desc: Per-process memory pool usage. Compare with `show platform software memory switch standby` for HA divergence.
System.example:
sw# show platform software memory switch active r0
module fed:
  Total Available 0 bytes, Total Used 1234567 bytes
module iosd:
  Total Available 0 bytes, Total Used 8765432 bytes
module fman_rp:
  Total Available 0 bytes, Total Used 2345678 bytes
module platform_mgr:
  Total Available 0 bytes, Total Used 3456789 bytes

# Growth in any one module across hours = leak candidate.

System.cmd: show platform software memory switch standby
System.desc: Standby supervisor memory state. Should track active closely after sync.
System.example:
sw# show platform software memory switch standby r0
module iosd:
  Total Available 0 bytes, Total Used 8765000 bytes
# Within a few hundred KB of active = normal sync.

System.cmd: show platform software fed switch active
System.desc: Forwarding Engine Driver (FED) — the IOS-XE component that programs the ASIC. Sub-commands give per-feature TCAM/queue/punt info.
System.example:
sw# show platform software fed switch active
# This command summarises FED subsystem state; specific feature views:
#   ... fed switch active acl       — ACL TCAM programming
#   ... fed switch active qos       — QoS policers/queues
#   ... fed switch active punt      — packets sent to CPU
#   ... fed switch active resources — hardware resource utilisation
#   ... fed switch active fwd-asic drops/errors/utilization
sw# show platform software fed switch active resources
Resource Information for Module: 1
Hardware Resource Utilisation:
   IPv4 Routes        : 18234/65536    27%
   IPv6 Routes        :  3210/24576    13%
   MAC Addresses      : 12345/32768    37%
   Multicast Routes   :    50/8192      0%
   QoS Policers       :   234/8192      2%
   ACL TCAM (IPv4)    :   876/12288     7%

System.cmd: show platform software fed switch active qos
System.desc: QoS data-plane state from the FED: policer entries, queues, scheduler config, marking maps.
System.example:
sw# show platform software fed switch active qos policy summary
Number of policies allocated: 12
Number of policies in-use: 8

Policy Name                        Type          Total Classes  Policers  Markers
INGRESS-DSCP                       input         5              3         5
WAN-OUT                            output        4              2         4
VOIP-PROTECT                       input         3              1         3

sw# show platform software fed switch active qos interface GigabitEthernet1/0/1 ingress
QoS Subblock Information for Interface: GigabitEthernet1/0/1
  Policy Name: INGRESS-DSCP
  Direction: Ingress
  Total Classes: 5

System.cmd: show platform software fed switch active punt
System.desc: Per-CPU-queue punt path — packets sent from ASIC to CPU. Sustained high rates indicate punted-traffic problems (CDP flood, OSPF storm, ICMP redirects).
System.example:
sw# show platform software fed switch active punt cpuq rates
CPU Punt Path Receive Rates

  CPUQ            Drop_Rcv   Drop_Send   Rcv_Rate    Curr_Rcv_Rate    Avg_Rcv_Rate
                  Packets    Packets     (pps)       (pps)            (pps)
  ----            --------   ---------   ---------   --------------   --------------
   0  CPU_Q_DOT1X_AUTH          0          0          0                0                0
   1  CPU_Q_L2_CONTROL          0          0         12               12               10
   2  CPU_Q_BROADCAST           0          0          2                2                2
   3  CPU_Q_LEARNING_CACHE_OVFL 0          0          0                0                0
   4  CPU_Q_SW_FORWARDING       0          0          1                1                1
   5  CPU_Q_TOPOLOGY_CONTROL    0          0          8                8                7
   6  CPU_Q_PROTO_SNOOPING      0          0          0                0                0
   7  CPU_Q_BFD_LOW_LATENCY     0          0          0                0                0

System.cmd: show platform software fed switch active acl
System.desc: ACL TCAM consumption — which ACLs are programmed in hardware vs CPU-evaluated. Hardware exhaustion punts the ACE to CPU = severe perf hit.
System.example:
sw# show platform software fed switch active acl usage
##### ACL Type Summary ##############
  IFG: 16  IFGS: 14
  Entries: 876 / 12288 free
  Stats: 234 / 4096 free

##### Class Hardware Entry Usage ##############
Feature/Direction          Total  Used   Free
L3 IPv4 Ingress             8192   543   7649
L3 IPv4 Egress              4096   123   3973
L3 IPv6 Ingress             4096    87   4009
L2 Mac Ingress              2048    23   2025

System.cmd: show platform software fed switch active resources
System.desc: Overall hardware resource utilisation: routes (v4/v6), MAC, multicast, policers, TCAM. Use to plan capacity before hitting hardware caps.
System.example:
sw# show platform software fed switch active resources
Resource Information for Module: 1
Hardware Resource Utilisation:
   IPv4 Routes        : 18234/65536    27%
   IPv6 Routes        :  3210/24576    13%
   MAC Addresses      : 12345/32768    37%
   Multicast Routes   :    50/8192      0%
   QoS Policers       :   234/8192      2%
   Bridge Domains     :     5/4096      0%
   NetFlow Entries    :  4567/8192    55%
   Tunnels            :     2/1024      0%
   ACL TCAM (IPv4)    :   876/12288     7%
   ACL TCAM (IPv6)    :    87/6144      1%
   ACL TCAM (MAC)     :    23/2048      1%

System.cmd: show platform software fed switch active ifm mappings
System.desc: Interface Manager mappings — physical to logical interface (IF_ID) mapping the FED uses. Useful when debug output references IF_IDs.
System.example:
sw# show platform software fed switch active ifm mappings
Interface                  IF_ID         Type            State
GigabitEthernet1/0/1       0x9           ether           UP
GigabitEthernet1/0/2       0xa           ether           UP
GigabitEthernet1/0/24      0x20          ether           DOWN
TenGigabitEthernet1/1/1    0x40          ether           UP
Port-channel1              0x80          etherchnl       UP
Vlan1                      0x100         svi             UP
Vlan10                     0x101         svi             UP
Loopback0                  0x200         loopback        UP

System.cmd: show platform software fed switch active fwd-asic drops
System.desc: ASIC-level drop counters per drop-reason (RPF, MTU, ACL, TCAM, QoS tail). The authoritative answer for "where did my packet go".
System.example:
sw# show platform software fed switch active fwd-asic drops
Drop Reason                            Drop Count
-------                                ----------
MaclearningOverflow                            0
MtuViolation                                  12
RpfFailed                                      0
QOSTailDrop                                    8
L3SinkOnly                                     0
NoForwarding                                   0
AclDeny                                     1234
Dot1xDeny                                      0
FlowMaskGenFailure                             0
L2BroadcastSuppress                            0

System.cmd: show platform software fed switch active fwd-asic errors
System.desc: ASIC programming errors (rare but critical). Non-zero = hardware/firmware fault.
System.example:
sw# show platform software fed switch active fwd-asic errors
Error Type                           Error Count
----                                 -----------
ProgramFailures                                0
ReadFailures                                   0
MemoryEccErrors                                0
LinkCrcErrors                                  0
ParityErrors                                   0
IfgErrors                                      0

# Any non-zero value should trigger TAC engagement.

System.cmd: show platform software fed switch active fwd-asic utilization
System.desc: Per-ASIC utilisation: pipeline throughput, lookup engines busy %. Sustained >90% = approaching forwarding capacity.
System.example:
sw# show platform software fed switch active fwd-asic utilization
Forwarding Engine Utilisation:
  ASIC 0:
    Pipeline 0: 23% (1-min avg), 18% (5-min avg)
    Pipeline 1: 22% (1-min avg), 18% (5-min avg)
    Lookup Engine: 31%
    Modify Engine: 28%
  ASIC 1:
    Pipeline 0: 27% (1-min avg), 22% (5-min avg)

System.cmd: show platform software fed switch active punt cause summary
System.desc: Ranks packet-punt reasons hitting the CPU (ARP, DHCP snooping, CoPP-exceeded, L2 control, etc.) — the first place to look when CPU is pegged from data-plane traffic on an IOS-XE switch.
System.example:
switch# show platform software fed switch active punt cause summary

Cause                          Rx Pkts        Rx Rate (pps)
------------------------------ -------------- -------------
ARP_REQ_OR_RES                 1,842,033      412
DHCP_SNOOPING                    284,912       58
L2_CONTROL_PKT                    98,412       12
COPP_EXCEEDED                     12,048        2
# A high Rx Rate on one cause is the fastest way to spot a control-plane
# storm; cross-reference with 'show platform software fed switch active punt cpuq rates'.

  Airflow Direction: Front to Back
  Fan Speed: Auto

  Fans: 3/3 OK

Processes.desc: Per-process CPU on the route processor (5-second / 1-min / 5-min). High `% in interrupts` (the first number) = data-plane punting heavily.
Processes.example:
sw# show processes cpu sorted | exclude 0.00
CPU utilization for five seconds: 8%/2%; one minute: 7%; five minutes: 7%
 PID Runtime(ms) Invoked      uSecs    5Sec   1Min   5Min TTY Process
  73       12345 1234567        10    3.20%  3.15%  3.10%   0 IOSXE-RP Punt Se
 102        9876  876543        11    2.10%  2.05%  2.00%   0 Hulc LED Process
 154        5432  543210        10    0.90%  0.88%  0.85%   0 IP Input
# 8%/2% = 8% total, 2% interrupts. >5% interrupts = high punt traffic.

Processes.desc: CPU view sorted by 5-sec column (descending) — top hogs first.
Processes.example:
sw# show processes cpu sorted 5sec | head -15
CPU utilization for five seconds: 8%/2%; one minute: 7%; five minutes: 7%
 PID Runtime(ms) Invoked      uSecs    5Sec   1Min   5Min TTY Process
  73       12345 1234567        10    3.20%  3.15%  3.10%   0 IOSXE-RP Punt Se
 102        9876  876543        11    2.10%  2.05%  2.00%   0 Hulc LED Process
 154        5432  543210        10    0.90%  0.88%  0.85%   0 IP Input
 211        2345  234567         9    0.40%  0.42%  0.45%   0 SNMP Engine

Processes.desc: ASCII histogram of CPU utilisation over last 60 s, 60 min, 72 hr — spot recent spikes.
Per-process memory allocation. "Holding" = currently allocated, "Allocated/Freed" = lifetime totals.
Processes.example:
sw# show processes memory
Processor Pool Total: 4294967296 Used: 1234567890 Free: 3060399406
      I/O Pool Total: 1073741824 Used:  234567890 Free:  839173934

 PID TTY  Allocated      Freed    Holding    Getbufs    Retbufs Process
   0   0 1234567890 1098765432  135802458          0          0 *Init*
  73   0  234567890  198765432   35802458       1234        543 IOSXE-RP Punt Se
 102   0   45678901   34567890   11111011       1245        890 Hulc LED Process

Processes.desc: Memory view sorted by Holding column (descending) — biggest consumers first.
Processes.example:

Processor Pool Total: 4294967296 Used: 1234567890 Free: 3060399406

 PID TTY  Allocated      Freed    Holding Process

  73   0  234567890  198765432   35802458 IOSXE-RP Punt Se

 154   0   12345678    9876543    2469135 IP Input

Syslog logging: enabled (0 messages dropped, 0 flushes, 0 overruns, xml disabled, filtering disabled)

No Active Message Discriminator.

Monitor logging: level debugging, 0 messages logged
Buffer logging:  level informational, 234567 messages logged
Exception Logging: size (4096 bytes)
File logging: disabled

  Logging to 10.50.50.50 (udp port 514, audit disabled,
        link up), 12345 message lines logged

%LINK-3-UPDOWN: Interface GigabitEthernet1/0/24, changed state to up

%DOT1X-5-RESULT: Authentication result: 'success' from interface Gi1/0/1 for client (aabb.ccdd.eeff)

Logging.desc: On-board failure logging (OBFL) — environmental + crash history stored in non-volatile flash across reloads.
SNMP trap history — last 100 (default) traps generated.

critical(2)    SYS-CRIT        Critical conditions
errors(3)      SYS-ERROR       Error conditions
warnings(4)    SYS-WARN        Warning conditions

Logging.desc: Persistent logs written to flash. Survive reload (unlike buffered logs). Configure with `logging persistent`.

Redundancy.desc: Displays the Cisco IOS XE redundancy state for dual-supervisor or stacked configurations: active/standby supervisor states, sync status, and switchover reason.
Redundancy.example:
sw# show redundancy status
my state = ACTIVE
peer state = STANDBY HOT
Mode = Duplex
Unit = Secondary
Unit ID = 1

Redundancy Protocol (ISSU)
  = Version check passed
Primary Version = 17.09.03a
Secondary Version = 17.09.03a

Commands that take the standby offline:
  'clear' commands
  'debug' commands

Redundancy.desc: Chronological list of every switchover with reason code.
Redundancy.example:
sw# show redundancy switchover history
Index   Previous       Current        Switchover               Switchover
        active         active         reason                   time
-----   --------       --------       ----------               ----------
1       2              1              active unit failed       14:23:45 Apr 19 2026
2       1              2              user forced              09:00:00 Feb 14 2026

Redundancy.desc: Config-sync status — confirms standby is sync'd with active. Mismatch here = stale standby config.
Redundancy.example:
sw# show redundancy config-sync
Redundancy Config Sync:
  Status: Normal
  Last Standby Sync: 14:25:01 Apr 21 2026
  Sync state: in-sync
  Pending sync messages: 0

Configuration Sync Statistics:
  Successful Syncs: 12345
  Failed Syncs: 0
  Avg sync time: 234 ms

Redundancy.desc: Standby peer view — what the active sees about its peer (software version, capabilities).
Redundancy.example:
sw# show redundancy peer
Peer (slot 2):
  Software: cat9k_iosxe.17.12.04.SPA.bin
  Active state: STANDBY HOT
  Capabilities: SSO-enabled
  Last sync: 14:25:01 Apr 21 2026

Redundancy.desc: Operational redundancy state with detailed timers and last-event reason.
Redundancy.example:
sw# show redundancy state
my state = 13 -ACTIVE
peer state = 8  -STANDBY HOT
Mode = Duplex
Unit = Primary
Unit ID = 1

Redundancy Mode (Operational) = sso
Redundancy Mode (Configured) = sso
Redundancy State = sso
Manual Swact = enabled
Communications = Up

Redundancy.desc: HA timer values: keepalive, hold, RPR-to-SSO failover times.
Redundancy.example:
sw# show redundancy timers
Redundancy Timers:
  RF Keepalive Interval: 4000 ms
  RF Keepalive Holdtime: 16000 ms
  RF Notify Timer: 2000 ms
  RF Switchover Delay: 1000 ms
  RF Cold Sync Timer: 600 sec

Redundancy.desc: Full HA subsystem view: NSF (Non-Stop Forwarding), graceful restart, ISSU readiness.
Redundancy.example:
sw# show redundancy ha
Non Stop Forwarding
  IPv4 NSF: Enabled (gen 1)
  IPv6 NSF: Enabled (gen 1)
  OSPF NSF: Enabled, helper mode enabled
  BGP NSF: Enabled, graceful-restart 120s
  ISSU Ready: YES

Redundancy.desc: HA event counters: switchovers, sync attempts/failures, NSF events.
Redundancy.example:
sw# show redundancy ha statistics
Redundancy HA Statistics:
  Switchovers attempted: 2
  Switchovers succeeded: 2
  Standby resets:        0
  Sync messages sent:    98765432
  Sync messages received: 98765400
  Sync failures:         0
  NSF helper events:     8

Redundancy.desc: Per-protocol NSF/GR detail with timers and last-event timestamps.
Redundancy.example:
sw# show redundancy ha detail
Non Stop Forwarding (NSF) details:

  Protocol      Mode      State        Last Event
  --------      ----      -----        ----------
  IPv4          Enabled   Active       14:23:45 Apr 19 2026
  OSPF          Enabled   Helper       14:23:45 Apr 19 2026
  BGP           Enabled   Active       14:23:45 Apr 19 2026, GR timer 120s

   QSFP-100G-FR-S           ALL
   QSFP-100G-LR-S           ALL
   QSFP_100G_SR1.2          ALL

What it means:
- Lists every transceiver this IOS-XE image recognises, so you can
  confirm an optic is supported before inserting it.
- The right-hand column is the minimum Cisco part-number version that
  reports DOM (Digital Optical Monitoring): ALL = every revision
  supports DOM; NONE = DOM not supported (typical for passive DAC
  copper and AOC cables); a CPN/Vxx value = that part revision or newer.
- If an optic is not on the list the platform may reject it as an
  unsupported transceiver; `service unsupported-transceiver` can force
  acceptance (unsupported by TAC, use at your own risk).

Interfaces.desc: L2 switchport state per port: mode, native/access VLAN, trunk allowed VLANs, voice VLAN.
Interfaces.example:
sw# show interfaces GigabitEthernet1/0/1 switchport
Name: Gi1/0/1
Switchport: Enabled
Administrative Mode: static access
Operational Mode: static access
Administrative Trunking Encapsulation: dot1q
Operational Trunking Encapsulation: native
Negotiation of Trunking: Off
Access Mode VLAN: 10 (USER-DATA)
Trunking Native Mode VLAN: 1 (default)
Voice VLAN: 20 (USER-VOICE)
Administrative private-vlan host-association: none
Operational private-vlan: none
Trunking VLANs Enabled: ALL
Pruning VLANs Enabled: 2-1001

Protected: false
Unknown unicast blocked: disabled
Unknown multicast blocked: disabled
Appliance trust: none

Po1       10,20,30,99

Interfaces.desc: Just the ports currently in err-disable state — fastest way to find them.
Interfaces.example:
sw# show interfaces status err-disabled
Port      Name                 Status       Reason             Err-disabled Vlans
Gi1/0/24  ESXi-host-03         err-disabled bpduguard         10
Gi1/0/36  printer-floor3       err-disabled psecure-violation  30
# Recover with `errdisable recovery cause <cause>` or manual `shut`/`no shut`.

Interfaces.desc: Shows which errdisable causes have automatic recovery enabled and the recovery interval (default 300 sec) — verify the right causes are set to auto-recover to avoid manual port re-enabling.
Interfaces.example:
Switch# show errdisable recovery

ErrDisable Reason            Timer Status   Timer Interval
-----------------            -------------- --------------
bpduguard                    Enabled        300
channel-misconfig (STP)      Disabled         -
dhcp-rate-limit              Enabled        300
dot1x-timeout                Enabled        300
link-flap                    Disabled         -
psecure-violation            Enabled        300
uplinkfast                   Disabled         -

# Adjust recovery time: errdisable recovery interval 60

Interfaces.desc: Enable auto-recovery for a specific err-disable cause. The interface re-enables after the configured interval.
Interfaces.example:
sw(config)# errdisable recovery cause bpduguard
sw(config)# errdisable recovery cause psecure-violation
sw(config)# errdisable recovery interval 300   ; recover after 5 min
# Manually clear without waiting:
sw(config)# interface GigabitEthernet1/0/24
sw(config-if)# shutdown
sw(config-if)# no shutdown

Interfaces.desc: Set the recovery timer (default 300 s = 5 min). Applies to all enabled recovery causes.
Interfaces.example:
sw(config)# errdisable recovery interval 300
sw# show errdisable recovery | include Timer
Timer interval: 300 seconds

Interfaces.desc: SFP/SFP+ EEPROM contents (IDPROM) — vendor data, calibration, fault history. Deep diagnostic.
Interfaces.example:
sw# show idprom interface TenGigabitEthernet1/1/1
IDPROM for transceiver TenGigabitEthernet1/1/1:
  Description = SFP-10G-LR
  Transceiver Type:             0x03 (SFP)
  Connector Code:               0x07 (LC)
  Encoding Code:                0x06 (64B66B)
  Nominal Bitrate per Channel:  10300 Mbps
  Cisco PID:                    SFP-10G-LR
  Vendor Name:                  CISCO-FINISAR
  Vendor Part Number:           FTLX1471D3BCL
  Vendor Serial Number:         ABCD25400000
  Vendor Date Code:             250101
  Cisco Extended ID Number:     0x04

Interfaces.cmd: show platform software fed switch active punt cpuq rates
Interfaces.desc: Per-CPU-queue punt rates — high values for any queue indicate punted-traffic problems.
Interfaces.example:
sw# show platform software fed switch active punt cpuq rates
CPU Punt Path Receive Rates
  CPUQ                              Drop_Rcv   Rcv_Rate    Curr_Rcv_Rate    Avg_Rcv_Rate
                                    Packets    (pps)       (pps)            (pps)
   1  CPU_Q_L2_CONTROL                     0         12               12               10
   5  CPU_Q_TOPOLOGY_CONTROL               0          8                8                7
  16  CPU_Q_LOGGING                        0          3                3                3
  20  CPU_Q_CPU_NICE                       0          1                1                1
  21  CPU_Q_SW_FORWARDING                  0          1                1                1
# Sustained high rates on a single queue = investigate the protocol (BPDU
# storm, ARP flood, unresolved L3 next-hop, etc.).

Interfaces.desc: IOS / IOS-XE — transceiver vendor, part number, serial, DOM detail.
Interfaces.example:
Vendor Name: CISCO
Vendor PN: SFP-10G-SR
Serial No: FNS1234567A

Interfaces.desc: Detailed L3 status and configuration for a single interface.
Interfaces.example:
GigabitEthernet0/0 is up, line protocol is up
  Internet address is 192.168.1.1/24
  MTU 1500 bytes, BW 1000000 Kbit/sec
  Encapsulation ARPA

Interfaces.desc: Physical / controller-level interface information (link state, PHY, hardware).
Interfaces.example:
Interface GigabitEthernet0/0
  Hardware is iGbE
  Link is Up - 1Gbps Full Duplex

Interfaces.desc: PoE status for a single port: admin state, oper state, power drawn, device class, max power. Class 4 = 802.3at PoE+ (30W); Class 6+ = 802.3bt PoE++ (needed for Wi-Fi 6E APs at full power).
Interfaces.example:
SWITCH-01# show power inline gigabitethernet 1/0/12
Interface  Admin  Oper       Power   Device              Class  Max
                              (Watts)
---------  -----  ---------- ------- ------------------- -----  ----
Gi1/0/12   auto   on         30.0    CW9166I-ROW         4      30.0

(Class 4 = 802.3at PoE+, 30W ceiling. A 9166 wants Class 6+ / 802.3bt for full
radio power -- on Class 4 it runs in reduced-power mode.)

Interfaces.desc: Look for "Power drawn" and "Power request levels" to confirm what the AP has been granted via CDP PoE negotiation.
Interfaces.example:
SWITCH-01# show cdp neighbors gigabitethernet 1/0/12 detail
Device ID: AP-OFFICE-01
  IP address: 10.16.2.11
Platform: cisco CW9166I-ROW,  Capabilities: Router Trans-Bridge
Interface: GigabitEthernet1/0/12,  Port ID (outgoing port): GigabitEthernet0
Power drawn: 30.000 Watts
Power request levels are:30000 15400 0 0 0

Interfaces.desc: LLDP neighbour detail per port: neighbour name, system description, port, capabilities, mgmt address.
Interfaces.example:
sw# show lldp neighbors GigabitEthernet1/0/12 detail
------------------------------------------------
Local Intf: Gi1/0/12
Chassis id: 70bd.9685.9c80
Port id: GigabitEthernet0
Port Description: GigabitEthernet0
System Name: AP-OFFICE-01
System Description:
Cisco AP Software, [Cupertino] Catalyst 9100 Series Software, Version 17.12.04, RELEASE SOFTWARE (fc1)

Time remaining: 105 seconds
System Capabilities: B,R
Enabled Capabilities: B,R
Management Addresses:
    IP: 10.16.2.11
Auto Negotiation - supported, enabled
Physical media capabilities:
    1000baseT(FD)
Media Attachment Unit type: 30
Vlan ID: -

Interfaces.desc: On an AP-facing trunk, include the management VLAN (as native) plus all wireless data VLANs. The AP needs every WLAN-VLAN tagged through to it.
Interfaces.example:
SWITCH-01(config-if)# switchport mode trunk
SWITCH-01(config-if)# switchport trunk native vlan 10
SWITCH-01(config-if)# switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,1110,1120,1130,1140,1150,1160,1164,1168

Interfaces.desc: Permit the port to negotiate up to N mW for 802.3bt-capable APs (e.g. 60000 = 60 W). On 802.3at-only switch HW the effective max is ~30 W regardless.
Interfaces.example:
sw(config)# interface GigabitEthernet1/0/12
sw(config-if)# power inline auto max 60000
sw# show interfaces GigabitEthernet1/0/12 power inline | include Max|Class
Class: Class 4 (802.3at PoE+)
Max: 60.0 W
# On 802.3at-only switch hardware the effective max remains ~30W.

Interfaces.desc: Critical on AP-facing trunks — without it CAPWAP can time out during STP convergence on link-up. Pair with `spanning-tree bpduguard enable`.
Interfaces.example:
SWITCH-01(config-if)# interface gigabitethernet 1/0/12
SWITCH-01(config-if)# description AP-OFFICE-01
SWITCH-01(config-if)# switchport mode trunk
SWITCH-01(config-if)# switchport trunk native vlan 10
SWITCH-01(config-if)# spanning-tree portfast trunk
SWITCH-01(config-if)# spanning-tree bpduguard enable
SWITCH-01(config-if)# power inline auto max 60000

Interfaces.desc: Shows platform port manager data for an interface — port-type, port-state, port-subblock state, and hardware programming details; useful for diagnosing ports stuck in wrong state
Interfaces.example:
Switch# show platform pm port-data GigabitEthernet1/0/1
IF_ID     : 0x000f
IF Name   : GigabitEthernet1/0/1
Port Type : GB_PHY_COPPER
Port State: PORT_FSM_S_CONNECTED
Speed     : 1G
Duplex    : Full
Admin     : UP
Linestate : UP

VLAN.example:

VLAN Name                             Status    Ports

10   USER-DATA                        active    Gi1/0/1, Gi1/0/11, Po1

1002 fddi-default                     act/unsup
1003 token-ring-default               act/unsup

VLAN Type   SAID    MTU   Parent  RingNo  BridgeNo  Stp BrdgMode Trans1 Trans2
---- ----- ------- ------ ------ ------- --------- --- -------- ------ ------
1    enet  100001   1500  -      -       -         -   -        0      0
10   enet  100010   1500  -      -       -         -   -        0      0

VLAN.desc: Compact VLAN list — id, name, status, member ports. Most common quick check.
VLAN.example:
sw# show vlan brief
VLAN Name                             Status    Ports

99   MGMT                             active    Po1

VLAN.desc: Single-VLAN detail: member ports, name, type, MTU, SAID.

VLAN.example:
sw# show vlan name USER-DATA
VLAN Name                             Status    Ports
---- -------------------------------- --------- -------------------------------
10   USER-DATA                        active    Gi1/0/1, Po1

VLAN.desc: Internal VLAN allocation — VLANs 1006-4094 are auto-assigned to routed ports / SVIs on Catalyst 9k.
VLAN.example:
sw# show vlan internal usage

VLAN Usage
---- --------------------
1006 GigabitEthernet1/1/1
1007 GigabitEthernet1/1/2
1008 TenGigabitEthernet1/0/24
1009 online diag vlan0
1010 online diag vlan1

VLAN.desc: VLAN access-maps (VACL) bound to specific VLANs.
VLAN.example:
sw# show vlan filter
VLAN Map BLOCK-RFC1918 is filtering VLANs: 10,20

VLAN.desc: VACL definitions — match clauses (ACL or MAC ACL) and action (forward/drop/redirect).
VLAN.example:
sw# show vlan access-map
Vlan access-map "BLOCK-RFC1918"  10
  Match clauses:
    ip address: NO-RFC1918
  Action:
    drop
Vlan access-map "BLOCK-RFC1918"  20
  Match clauses:
  Action:
    forward

VLAN.desc: PVLAN promiscuous-port mappings (which primary VLAN gets which secondaries).
VLAN.example:
sw# show vlan private-vlan mapping
Interface Secondary VLAN
--------- --------------
Gi1/0/24  101 102 199
# Gi1/0/24 is the promiscuous port — sees traffic from all secondaries.

VLAN.desc: List each VLAN with its private-VLAN type (primary/community/isolated/normal).
VLAN.example:
sw# show vlan private-vlan type
Vlan       Type
----       ---------------
100        primary
101        community
102        community
199        isolated

VLAN.desc: VTP version, mode, domain, config revision. Best practice: VTP mode transparent or VTPv3 with feature off.
VLAN.example:

--------------

Number of existing VLANs          : 8
Number of existing extended VLANs : 0
Configuration Revision            : 0

VLAN.desc: Confirm a specific VLAN is allowed/active/forwarding on every trunk in one shot.
VLAN.example:
sw# show interfaces trunk | include 10
Gi1/0/2   on           802.1q         trunking      1
Po1       on           802.1q         trunking      99
Gi1/0/2   1,10,20,30,99
Po1       10,20,30,99

VLAN0010             GigabitEthernet1/0/24    Root Inconsistent

Number of inconsistent ports (segments) in the system : 1

STP.example:

BackboneFast is disabled by default

Backbone Fast Statistics
------------------------

Number of inferior BPDUs received (all VLANs)                  : 0
Number of RLQ request PDUs received (all VLANs)                : 0

STP.desc: Per-VLAN root bridge info: who is root, this-switch cost to reach root, root port.
STP.example:
sw# show spanning-tree root
                                        Root    Hello Max Fwd
Vlan                   Root ID          Cost    Time  Age  Dly  Root Port
---------------- -------------------- --------- ----- ---  ---  ------------
VLAN0001         24577 aabb.cc99.0001        4    2   20   15  Po1
VLAN0010         24586 aabb.cc99.0001        4    2   20   15  Po1
VLAN0020         24596 aabb.cc99.0001        4    2   20   15  Po1

STP.desc: Per-VLAN this-switch bridge info: priority, address, configured timers.
STP.example:
sw# show spanning-tree bridge
                                                  Hello  Max  Fwd
Vlan                Bridge ID                       Time  Age  Dly  Protocol
----- ----------------------------------------------- ---- ---- ---- --------
VLAN0001  32769 (32768,   1) aabb.ccdd.0001              2   20   15  rstp
VLAN0010  32778 (32768,  10) aabb.ccdd.0001              2   20   15  rstp
VLAN0020  32788 (32768,  20) aabb.ccdd.0001              2   20   15  rstp

STP.desc: Root-guard on a port — if a superior BPDU arrives, the port goes root-inconsistent (blocking) until BPDUs stop. Protects against rogue switches becoming root.
STP.example:
sw(config)# interface GigabitEthernet1/0/24
sw(config-if)# spanning-tree guard root

# When triggered:
sw# show logging | include SPANTREE
%SPANTREE-2-ROOTGUARD_BLOCK: Root guard blocking port GigabitEthernet1/0/24 on VLAN0010.
sw# show spanning-tree inconsistentports
Name       Interface                Inconsistency
---------- ------------------------ ------------------
VLAN0010   GigabitEthernet1/0/24    Root Inconsistent

STP.desc: BPDU guard on a port — if ANY BPDU arrives, the port goes err-disabled. Pair with `spanning-tree portfast` on edge ports.
STP.example:
sw(config)# interface GigabitEthernet1/0/1
sw(config-if)# spanning-tree portfast
sw(config-if)# spanning-tree bpduguard enable

# When triggered:
sw# show logging | include BPDU
%SPANTREE-2-BLOCK_BPDUGUARD: Received BPDU on port GigabitEthernet1/0/1 with BPDU Guard enabled. Disabling port.
%PM-4-ERR_DISABLE: bpduguard error detected on Gi1/0/1, putting Gi1/0/1 in err-disable state.

STP_RPVST.example:
sw# show spanning-tree rapid-pvst
VLAN0001

  Root ID    Priority    24577
             Address     aabb.cc99.0001
             Cost        4
             Port        Po1 (Port-channel1)

VLAN0010
  Spanning tree enabled protocol rstp
  Root ID    Priority    24586
             Address     aabb.cc99.0001

Gi1/0/48  SA      bndl      32768         0x1       0x1     0x130     0x3D

EtherChannel.desc: PAgP partner info (legacy Cisco-proprietary aggregation — use LACP for new deployments).
EtherChannel.example:
sw# show pagp neighbor

Channel group 2 neighbors
        Partner              Partner          Partner         Partner Group
Port    Name                 Device ID        Port       Age  Flags   Cap.
Gi1/0/49 sw-distro            aabb.cc99.0002   Gi3/0/1    25s   SC      A0001

EtherChannel.desc: Shows internal PAgP (Port Aggregation Protocol) state for EtherChannel members: timers, device ID, port priority, and learn method — use when PAgP channels fail to form.
EtherChannel.example:
sw# show pagp internal
Flags:  S - Device is sending Slow hello.  C - Device is in Consistent state.
        A - Device is in Auto mode.        P - Device learns on physical port.

Channel group 1
                          Hello    Partner  PAgP     Learning  Group
Port      Flags State     Timers   Count    Priority  Method   Ifindex
Gi1/0/23  SC    U6/S7    H   30s  1        128       Any      19
Gi1/0/24  SC    U6/S7    H   30s  1        128       Any      20

sw# show pagp counters
            Information         Flush
Port       Sent   Recv     Sent   Recv
----------------------------------------
Channel group: 2
Gi1/0/49   1234   1230      0      0

EtherChannel.cmd: test etherchannel load-balance interface port-channel<n> ip <src> <dst>
EtherChannel.desc: Predict which member port the hash will pick for a given src/dst IP — confirms ECMP distribution before changing it.
EtherChannel.example:
sw# test etherchannel load-balance interface port-channel 1 ip 10.10.10.20 8.8.8.8
Would select Gi1/0/47 of Po1

sw# test etherchannel load-balance interface port-channel 1 ip 10.10.10.21 8.8.8.8
Would select Gi1/0/48 of Po1

Switching.desc: L2 MAC table — VLAN, MAC, type (DYNAMIC/STATIC), port. Aging time configurable; default 300 s.
Switching.example:
sw# show mac address-table
          Mac Address Table
-------------------------------------------

Vlan    Mac Address       Type        Ports
----    -----------       --------    -----
   1    aabb.ccdd.0001    STATIC      CPU
  10    1122.3344.5566    DYNAMIC     Gi1/0/1
  10    aabb.ccdd.eeff    DYNAMIC     Po1
  20    1122.3344.5577    DYNAMIC     Gi1/0/1
  30    2233.4455.6677    DYNAMIC     Po1
  99    aabb.ccdd.aa01    DYNAMIC     Po1
Total Mac Addresses for this criterion: 6

Switching.desc: Just dynamically-learned MACs (excludes static, CPU, multicast).
Switching.example:
sw# show mac address-table dynamic
Vlan    Mac Address       Type        Ports
----    -----------       --------    -----
  10    1122.3344.5566    DYNAMIC     Gi1/0/1
  10    aabb.ccdd.eeff    DYNAMIC     Po1
  20    1122.3344.5577    DYNAMIC     Gi1/0/1
Total Mac Addresses for this criterion: 3

Switching.desc: Static MAC entries — admin-configured or CPU/system reserved.
Switching.example:
sw# show mac address-table static
Vlan    Mac Address       Type        Ports
----    -----------       --------    -----
   1    aabb.ccdd.0001    STATIC      CPU
  10    1234.5678.9abc    STATIC      Gi1/0/12   ; manually pinned
Total Mac Addresses for this criterion: 2

Switching.desc: MAC aging timer per VLAN. Default 300 s; reduce to ~120 s if FHRP MAC moves are frequent.
Switching.example:
sw# show mac address-table aging-time
Global Aging Time:  300
Vlan    Aging Time
----    ----------
   1       300
  10       300
  20       120   ; voice VLAN — shorter for faster MAC re-learn

Switching.desc: Total MAC count per VLAN — quick view of L2 fan-out per segment.
Switching.example:
sw# show mac address-table count
Mac Entries for Vlan 1:
---------------------------
Dynamic Address Count  :     2
Static  Address Count  :     1
Total Mac Addresses    :     3

Mac Entries for Vlan 10:
---------------------------
Dynamic Address Count  :   245
Static  Address Count  :     1
Total Mac Addresses    :   246

Switching.desc: MACs learned on a specific port — find which host(s) live behind it.
Switching.example:
sw# show mac address-table interface GigabitEthernet1/0/1
Vlan    Mac Address       Type        Ports
----    -----------       --------    -----
  10    1122.3344.5566    DYNAMIC     Gi1/0/1
  20    1122.3344.5577    DYNAMIC     Gi1/0/1
Total Mac Addresses for this criterion: 2

Switching.desc: Filters the MAC address table to show only entries for a specific VLAN — helps locate a device MAC address and confirm which port it is learned on.
Switching.example:
Switch# show mac address-table vlan 10

          Mac Address Table
-------------------------------------------

Vlan    Mac Address       Type        Ports
----    -----------       --------    -----
  10    0050.5601.abcd    DYNAMIC     Gi1/0/1
  10    0050.5602.efgh    DYNAMIC     Gi1/0/2
  10    0050.5603.ijkl    STATIC      Gi1/0/48  (SVI gateway)

Total Mac Addresses for this criterion: 3

Switching.desc: Multicast MAC entries (IGMP-snooping populated). 0100.5e... range = IPv4 multicast.
Switching.example:
sw# show mac address-table multicast
Vlan    Mac Address       Type        Ports
----    -----------       --------    -----
  10    0100.5e00.0001    STATIC      Gi1/0/1, Po1   ; 224.0.0.1 all-hosts
  10    0100.5e00.0002    STATIC      Po1            ; 224.0.0.2 all-routers
  10    0100.5e7f.fffa    IGMP        Gi1/0/2, Gi1/0/3 ; SSDP / mDNS

Switching.desc: Sticky MACs learned by port-security (alternative view to `show port-security address`).
Switching.example:
sw# show mac address-table secure
Vlan    Mac Address       Type        Ports
----    -----------       --------    -----
  10    1122.3344.5566    STATIC      Gi1/0/1   ; sticky
  10    1122.3344.5577    STATIC      Gi1/0/1   ; sticky

Switching.desc: MAC move/learn/age SNMP notification config and counters.

sw# show mac address-table notification
MAC Notification Feature is Enabled on the switch

Global History Size     : 1

PortSecurity.desc: Per-port detail: max MACs, current learned, violations, aging, sticky, last-violation source.

Vlan    Mac Address       Type                Ports

PortSecurity.desc: Clear all sticky-learned MACs. Forces re-learning next time hosts speak.
