Group Address    Interface              Uptime    Expires  Last Reporter   Group Accounted
224.0.1.40       Vlan10                 3w2d      00:02:42 10.10.10.20
239.255.255.250  Vlan10                 1d4h      00:02:38 10.10.10.21
239.10.10.10     Vlan20                 02:34:56  00:02:55 10.20.20.10

Multicast.desc: IGMP snooping state per VLAN — enabled/disabled, querier, mrouter ports.
Multicast.example:
sw# show ip igmp snooping
Global IGMP Snooping configuration:
-------------------------------------------
IGMP snooping              : Enabled
Global PIM Snooping        : Disabled
IGMPv3 snooping (minimal)  : Enabled
Report suppression         : Enabled
TCN solicit query          : Disabled
TCN flood query count      : 2
Last member query interval : 1000

Vlan 10:
--------
IGMP snooping              : Enabled
IGMPv2 immediate leave     : Disabled
Multicast router learning  : pim-dvmrp
CGMP interoperability      : IGMP_ONLY
Vlan 10 has 3 groups

Multicast.desc: Multicast groups learnt by IGMP snooping with port lists.
PIM neighbours: address, interface, uptime, DR priority.
Multicast.example:
sw# show ip pim neighbor
PIM Neighbor Table
Mode: B - Bidir Capable, DR - Designated Router, N - Default DR Priority,
      P - Proxy Capable, S - State Refresh Capable, G - GenID Capable
Neighbor          Interface           Uptime/Expires    Ver   DR
Address                                                       Prio/Mode
10.0.0.2          Vlan99              3w2d/00:01:30     v2    1 / DR S P G
10.0.0.3          Vlan99              3w2d/00:01:35     v2    1 / S P G

Multicast.desc: PIM-enabled interfaces with mode (sparse/dense), DR, hello interval.
Multicast.example:
sw# show ip pim interface
Address         Interface          Ver/  Nbr   Query     DR        DR
                                   Mode  Count Intvl     Prior
10.0.0.1        Vlan99              v2/S  2     30        1         10.0.0.2
1.1.1.1         Loopback0           v2/S  0     30        1         1.1.1.1

Multicast.desc: PIM RP-to-group mappings — static, BSR-learnt, Auto-RP-learnt.
Multicast.example:
sw# show ip pim rp mapping
PIM Group-to-RP Mappings
Group(s) 224.0.0.0/4
  RP 10.99.99.1 (?), v2
    Info source: 10.99.99.1 (?), via Auto-RP
         Uptime: 3w2d, expires: 00:02:43

Multicast.desc: IPv4 multicast routing table (MRIB) — (S,G) and (*,G) entries with OIL (outgoing interface list).
Multicast.example:
sw# show ip mroute
IP Multicast Routing Table
Flags: D - Dense, S - Sparse, B - Bidir Group, s - SSM Group, C - Connected,
       L - Local, P - Pruned, R - RP-bit set, F - Register flag, T - SPT-bit set,
       J - Join SPT, M - MSDP created entry

(*, 239.10.10.10), 02:34:56/stopped, RP 10.99.99.1, flags: SJC
  Incoming interface: Vlan99, RPF nbr 10.0.0.2
  Outgoing interface list:
    Vlan20, Forward/Sparse, 02:34:55/00:02:14

(10.50.50.50, 239.10.10.10), 02:34:55/00:02:14, flags: T
  Incoming interface: Vlan99, RPF nbr 10.0.0.2
  Outgoing interface list:
    Vlan20, Forward/Sparse, 02:34:55/00:02:14

Multicast.desc: Aggregate multicast packet/byte counts per (S,G).
Multicast.example:
sw# show ip mroute count
IP Multicast Statistics
9 routes using 4068 bytes of memory
4 groups, 1.25 average sources per group

Group: 239.10.10.10, Source count: 1, Packets forwarded: 12345, Packets received: 12345
  RP-tree: Forwarding: 0/0/0/0, Other: 0/0/0
  Source: 10.50.50.50/32, Forwarding: 12345/8/512/0, Other: 12345/0/0

Multicast.desc: Multicast routing summary.
Multicast.example:
sw# show ip mroute summary
IP Multicast Routing Table
4 groups, 1.25 average sources per group
Flags: D - Dense, S - Sparse, B - Bidir Group, s - SSM Group, C - Connected,
       L - Local, P - Pruned, R - RP-bit set, F - Register flag, T - SPT-bit set

(*, 239.10.10.10), 02:34:56/stopped, RP 10.99.99.1, OIF count: 1, flags: SJC
(10.50.50.50, 239.10.10.10), 02:34:55/00:02:14, OIF count: 1, flags: T

Multicast.desc: Global multicast routing state.
Multicast.example:
sw# show ip multicast
Multicast Routing: enabled
Multicast Multipath: disabled
Multicast Route limit: No limit
Multicast Triggered Routing: enabled
Multicast Fallback group mode: Sparse
Number of multicast boundaries configured with filter-autorp option: 0
MRTS:
  IP multicast RPF Backoff disabled
  Multicast multicast-routing aggregate: disabled

Multicast.desc: Active multicast streams — sources currently transmitting above the active-rate threshold.
Multicast.example:
sw# show ip mroute active
Active IP Multicast Sources - sending > 4 kbps

Group: 239.10.10.10, (?)
   Source: 10.50.50.50 (?)
     Rate: 1234 pps/567 kbps(1sec), 1100 pps/520 kbps(last 30 secs), 980 pps/470 kbps(life avg)

Security.desc: SSH server config and current cipher/protocol policy.
Security.example:
sw# show ip ssh
SSH Enabled - version 2.0
Authentication methods:publickey,keyboard-interactive,password
Authentication Publickey Algorithms:x509v3-ssh-rsa,ssh-rsa,rsa-sha2-256,rsa-sha2-512
Hostkey Algorithms:rsa-sha2-256,rsa-sha2-512,ssh-rsa
Encryption Algorithms:aes256-ctr,aes192-ctr,aes128-ctr,aes256-cbc,aes192-cbc
MAC Algorithms:hmac-sha2-256,hmac-sha2-512,hmac-sha1

Minimum expected Diffie Hellman key size : 2048 bits

Security.desc: SSH session counters (connections, authentication attempts, failures).
Security.example:
sw# show ip ssh statistics
SSH Statistics:
  Total Connections: 12345
  Active Connections: 3
  Total Authentication Failures: 234
  Total Bytes Sent: 9876543
  Total Bytes Received: 1234567

Security.desc: Currently active SSH sessions with user and source.
Security.example:
sw# show ssh

%No SSHv1 server connections running.

Security.desc: HTTP server state. Best practice: disabled (only HTTPS).
Security.example:

Security.example:

Security.desc: Login failure log — usernames + source IPs that failed authentication. Useful for spotting bruteforce attempts.
Security.example:

Information about login failure's:

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------

root              198.51.100.99       22   12      02:15:33 GMT Apr 21 2026

Security.desc: Login-on-failure logging policy.
Security.example:
sw# show login on-failure log
login on-failure log every 1 attempts

Security.desc: Login-on-success logging policy.
Security.example:
sw# show login on-success log
login on-success log every 1 attempts

Security.desc: RADIUS/TACACS server reachability and request statistics.
Security.example:
sw# show aaa servers
RADIUS: id 1, priority 1, host 10.10.10.50, auth-port 1812, acct-port 1813, hostname ISE-01

Security.desc: Active AAA sessions (typically 802.1X / MAB).
Security.example:

Security.example:

Auth.    : Maximum inQ length: 1

Security.desc: Configured RADIUS server groups.
Security.example:
sw# show radius server-group all
Server group radius
    Server(s) info:
         (Server status not shown)

Server group RAD-GROUP
    Sharecount = 0   sg_unconfigured = FALSE

NTP.desc: Displays the switch's current time as synchronised via NTP, without the peer/association detail that "show ntp status" or "show ntp associations" include
NTP.example:
sw# show ntp clock

14:30:01.234 GMT Tue Apr 21 2026

What it means:
- Confirms wall-clock time only — pair with "show ntp status" to see
  whether that time is actually synchronised (clock state) versus just
  free-running off the local clock after losing all NTP peers.
- Timestamp skew here throws off syslog correlation and can break TLS/
  certificate validation, so check this first when logs "don't line up"
  across devices.

[OK]

Config Management.cmd: show archive config differences nvram:startup-config system:running-config
Config Management.desc: Diffs the startup config against the running config line-by-line, prefixing lines only in the running config with + and lines only in the startup config with - — confirms exactly what unsaved changes exist before a reload discards them or before running write memory.
Config Management.example:
Router# show archive config differences nvram:startup-config system:running-config

!Contextual Config Diffs:
+interface GigabitEthernet0/1
+ description Uplink to Core
+ no shutdown
-interface GigabitEthernet0/1
-shutdown

! Lines prefixed with + exist only in the running config (unsaved changes).
! Lines prefixed with - exist only in the startup config (lost on reload
! if the running config is never saved with "write memory").

Show & Status.desc: Pipe regex — match digit sequences 1-3 chars long; useful for filtering numeric values like VLAN/MTU.
Show & Status.example:
vlan 1
mtu 900
interface GigabitEthernet0/2

Show & Status.desc: Quick filter — only show running-config lines containing digits (IPs, VLAN IDs, timer values).
Show & Status.example:
sw# show running-config | include [0-9]
 ip address 10.0.0.1 255.255.255.252
 switchport access vlan 10
 switchport voice vlan 20
 spanning-tree vlan 1-10 priority 24576
 ntp server 10.10.0.10 prefer

Show & Status.desc: Memory pool summary (Processor, I/O) with size/used/free.

NTP / SNMP / Logging.desc: Filter logging buffer by severity — useful when looking for specific %xxx-N-MSG severity tags.
NTP / SNMP / Logging.example:
sw# show logging | include severity
%SYS-6-LOGGINGHOST_STARTSTOP: Logging to host 10.50.50.50 port 514 started
# Use `show logging | include %.*-3-` for severity 3 (errors)
# Use `show logging | include %.*-2-` for severity 2 (critical)

Success rate is 100 percent (5/5)

System & Status.desc: Reads this node's stored configuration options — ACME account/domains bound to the node, description, and the wake-on-LAN MAC address — the node-level counterpart to 'qm config'/'pct config'.
System & Status.example:
root@pve# pvenode config get
acme: account=default
acmedomain0: domain=pve01.example.com
description: Rack 3, U12 — primary hypervisor
wakeonlan: 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E

Cluster (pvecm).desc: Displays the Proxmox cluster membership status: quorum state, number of nodes, votes, and current cluster leader — run when suspecting a split-brain or quorum loss.
Cluster (pvecm).example:
root@pve# pvecm status

Cluster information
-------------------
Name:             pve-cluster
Config Version:   8
Transport:        knet
Secure auth:      on

Quorum information
------------------
Date:             Sat Jun 28 10:00:00 2026
Quorum provider:  corosync_votequorum
Nodes:            3
Node votes:       1
Expected votes:   3
Total votes:      3
Quorum:           2
Flags:            Quorate

# Flags: Quorate = healthy; WaitForAll = stalled waiting for member

Cluster (pvecm).desc: Lists all nodes in the Proxmox cluster with their node ID, name, IP address, and online status — use to quickly verify all cluster members are reachable.

root@pve# pvecm nodes

Membership information
----------------------

# For full connectivity detail:
root@pve# pvecm status | grep -A5 "Membership"

Cluster (pvecm).example: Setting expected votes to 2 (emergency quorum recovery)

Cluster (pvecm).desc: Re-issue PVE certificates after IP/hostname changes
Add an external Corosync QDevice for two-node cluster quorum
Cluster (pvecm).example:
Configuring Corosync QDevice for 2-node cluster...
QDevice 10.0.0.250 added as vote-device

Cluster (pvecm).desc: Lists the status of every storage replication job configured on this node: source/target guest, last sync time, next scheduled run, and last error — the primary command for confirming ZFS storage replication is healthy across a cluster.
Cluster (pvecm).example:
root@pve# pvesr status
JobID    Enabled    Target                        LastSync             NextSync             Duration    FailCount    State
100-0    Yes        local/pve02                   2026-07-07_10:00:01  2026-07-07_10:15:00  4.201       0
101-0    Yes        local/pve03                   2026-07-07_10:00:03  2026-07-07_10:15:00  2.884       0

# Column 7 (FailCount) is the one to alert on — a non-zero value means the
# last replication run failed; check 'pvesr read <jobid>' or
# 'journalctl -u pvesr' for the underlying zfs send/recv error.
# Official docs: https://pve.proxmox.com/pve-docs/pvesr.1.html

Virtual Machines (qm).desc: List all VMs on this node with VMID, name, status, memory, disk
Virtual Machines (qm).example:
VMID NAME                 STATUS     MEM(MB)    BOOTDISK(GB) PID
       100 web01                running    4096       32.00        18432
       101 db01                 running    8192       128.00       18441
       102 backup-proxy         stopped    2048       16.00        0

Virtual Machines (qm).desc: Show pending and current configuration for a VM
Virtual Machines (qm).example:
balloon: 0
boot: order=scsi0;ide2;net0
cores: 4
ide2: none,media=cdrom
memory: 8192
name: web01
net0: virtio=AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:01,bridge=vmbr0,tag=10
onboot: 1
ostype: l26
scsi0: DS-FAST:vm-100-disk-0,discard=on,iothread=1,size=32G
scsihw: virtio-scsi-single
smbios1: uuid=...
sockets: 1

Virtual Machines (qm).desc: Shows the live, in-effect VM configuration — differs from plain "qm config" when pending changes are queued for next boot (e.g. after "qm set" on an offline-only setting)
Virtual Machines (qm).example:
$ qm set 100 --memory 8192
$ qm config 100
memory: 8192
pending:
  memory: 4096
$ qm config 100 --current
memory: 8192
# 'qm config' without --current shows the pending (not-yet-applied) value
# for offline-only settings; --current shows what the running VM actually
# has right now — 4096 MB until the VM is rebooted to pick up 8192.

Virtual Machines (qm).desc: Starts a stopped or suspended VM by VMID — equivalent to pressing the Power On button in the GUI. Use "qm status <VMID>" to confirm the VM is running.
Virtual Machines (qm).example:
# Start VM with VMID 100:
root@pve# qm start 100

# Verify it is running:
root@pve# qm status 100
status: running

# Check the VM console:
root@pve# qm terminal 100
# Or access via Proxmox web UI > VM 100 > Console

Virtual Machines (qm).desc: Graceful shutdown via ACPI/guest agent
Virtual Machines (qm).example: (Graceful ACPI shutdown requested; wait for VM to stop)

Virtual Machines (qm).desc: Try graceful then force-stop after timeout
Virtual Machines (qm).example: (Graceful shutdown attempted; if not stopped in 60s, force-power-off applied)

Virtual Machines (qm).desc: Hard power off (equivalent to pulling the plug)
Virtual Machines (qm).example: (Hard power-off — immediate. No data flush.)

Virtual Machines (qm).desc: Send an ACPI reboot signal to the VM, triggering a clean OS shutdown and restart
Virtual Machines (qm).example:
root@pve# qm reboot 100

# No output; the VM OS receives an ACPI power-button event and reboots gracefully.

# Monitor state:
$ qm status 100
status: running

# If the VM does not reboot within ~60s (OS hung), use hard reset:
#   qm reset 100

Virtual Machines (qm).desc: Hard-reset a VM (equivalent to pressing the physical reset button); no OS shutdown performed — use only when the guest is unresponsive
Virtual Machines (qm).example:
root@pve# qm reset 100

# Immediately power-cycles the VM — no ACPI signal, no graceful shutdown.
# The guest OS will run fsck or journal recovery on next boot.

# Safer alternatives in order of preference:
# 1. qm shutdown 100       (ACPI graceful shutdown)
# 2. qm reboot 100         (ACPI graceful reboot)
# 3. qm reset 100          (hard reset — last resort)

root@pve# qm status 100
status: running

Virtual Machines (qm).desc: Suspends a running VM to RAM (ACPI S3 sleep state), preserving its memory state — faster than a full shutdown but requires the VM to remain powered on in suspended state.
Virtual Machines (qm).example:
root@pve# qm suspend 100

# Verify state:
root@pve# qm status 100
status: paused

# Resume:
root@pve# qm resume 100

# Note: Suspend to RAM keeps memory contents in host RAM
# For disk-based suspension (saves memory to disk): qm suspend 100 --todisk 1

Virtual Machines (qm).desc: Resume a VM that was suspended to RAM (qm suspend), restoring execution from the saved memory state
Virtual Machines (qm).example:
root@pve# qm status 101
status: suspended

root@pve# qm resume 101

# No output; VM resumes from the exact point of suspension.

root@pve# qm status 101
status: running

# Note: suspend-to-disk (hibernate) is a different mechanism and requires
# guest OS support — see qm template and cloudinit for that workflow.

Virtual Machines (qm).desc: Send a key/key-combo to the VM console — e.g. qm sendkey 100 ctrl-alt-delete to send CAD without VNC
Virtual Machines (qm).example:
# Send Ctrl+Alt+Del to VM 100 (triggers Windows restart dialog or Linux logout):
$ qm sendkey 100 ctrl-alt-delete
(Key sent to VM console — check Proxmox VNC console to confirm)

# Send Enter key:
$ qm sendkey 100 ret

# Common keys: esc, tab, space, ctrl-c, ctrl-d, alt-f2
# Use the Proxmox web console or 'qm terminal 100' to see the result.

Virtual Machines (qm).desc: Switch a Linux guest to TTY1 from the host
Virtual Machines (qm).example: (Ctrl+Alt+F1 sent to VM 100; Linux guest switches to TTY1)

Virtual Machines (qm).desc: Send Enter — useful to dismiss bootloader prompts in headless start-up
Virtual Machines (qm).example: (Enter key sent to VM console)

Virtual Machines (qm).desc: Migrates a VM to another Proxmox cluster node — online migration with --online flag for shared storage; offline migration for local storage (VM is briefly stopped during transfer).
Virtual Machines (qm).example:
# Online migration (shared storage required):
root@pve# qm migrate 100 pve-node-02 --online

# After completion verify VM runs on target:
root@pve# pvesh get /cluster/resources --type vm | grep " 100 "
│ pve-node-02 │ vm │ 100 │ webserver-01 │ running │

# Offline migration (copies local disk):
root@pve# qm migrate 100 pve-node-02
# VM will be stopped, disk transferred via SSH, then started on target

Virtual Machines (qm).example:

Virtual Machines (qm).desc: Take a snapshot including RAM (vmstate) for live VMs
Virtual Machines (qm).example: (Snapshot pre-upgrade created for VM 100 with RAM state captured)

Virtual Machines (qm).desc: Roll back to a snapshot. VM is stopped first.
Virtual Machines (qm).example: (VM 100 rolled back to snapshot pre-upgrade; VM was powered off, then restored)

Virtual Machines (qm).desc: Deletes a named snapshot from a VM, freeing the disk space occupied by the snapshot delta — snapshots should be deleted after confirming the VM is stable following an upgrade or change.
Virtual Machines (qm).example:
root@pve# qm listsnapshot 100
Name              SnapTime           Description
pre-upgrade       2026-06-25 09:00   Before kernel upgrade
post-test         2026-06-26 14:30   After regression testing

# Delete the older snapshot:
root@pve# qm delsnapshot 100 pre-upgrade

# Verify deletion:
root@pve# qm listsnapshot 100
Name              SnapTime
post-test         2026-06-26 14:30

Virtual Machines (qm).desc: Lists all snapshots for a VM with their name, creation time, and optional description — shows the snapshot tree hierarchy including the "current" state pointer.
Virtual Machines (qm).example:
root@pve# qm listsnapshot 100
         -> Current (you are here)
            |-> post-upgrade    (2026-06-26 14:30:00) After regression testing OK
            |-> pre-upgrade     (2026-06-25 09:00:00) Before kernel 6.8 upgrade

# Delete a snapshot:
root@pve# qm delsnapshot 100 pre-upgrade

# Roll back to snapshot:
root@pve# qm rollback 100 pre-upgrade

Virtual Machines (qm).desc: Clear a stuck lock (e.g. backup, migrate) — only after confirming the operation has actually died
Virtual Machines (qm).example:
$ qm unlock 100
(Lock removed from VM 100 — no output on success)

# A VM lock is set during backup, snapshot, or migration to prevent
# concurrent conflicting operations. If the operation crashed, the lock
# may remain and prevent starting/modifying the VM.

# Check if a VM is locked:
$ qm config 100 | grep lock
lock: backup

# Unlock and then start:
$ qm unlock 100 && qm start 100

Virtual Machines (qm).desc: Run a command inside the guest via QEMU Guest Agent (must be installed in guest)
Ping the QEMU guest agent to verify it is running and responsive inside the VM
Virtual Machines (qm).example:
root@pve# qm guest cmd 100 ping
{}

# An empty JSON object {} means the guest agent responded successfully.
# If it times out or errors, the agent is not running inside the guest:
#   - Check: systemctl status qemu-guest-agent (inside the VM)
#   - Install: apt install qemu-guest-agent && systemctl enable --now qemu-guest-agent

LXC Containers (pct).desc: Create a new unprivileged LXC from a template
LXC Containers (pct).example:
creating rootfs from DS-FAST:vztmpl/debian-12-standard_12.7-1_amd64.tar.zst
Generating locales (this might take a while)...
Container 203 created.

LXC Containers (pct).desc: Starts a stopped LXC container by container ID — equivalent to qm start for VMs. Use "pct status <CTID>" to verify the container reached running state.
LXC Containers (pct).example:
root@pve# pct start 201

# Verify running:
root@pve# pct status 201
status: running

# Enter container shell:
root@pve# pct enter 201
root@ct201:~#

# Start all stopped containers on the node:
root@pve# for ct in $(pct list | awk "NR>1 && \$2=="stopped" {print \$1}"); do pct start \$ct; done

LXC Containers (pct).desc: Gracefully shuts down an LXC container by sending SIGTERM to the init process and waiting for a clean shutdown — preferable to "pct stop" which kills the container immediately.
LXC Containers (pct).example:
root@pve# pct shutdown 201 --timeout 30
# Waits up to 30 seconds for graceful shutdown; force-kills if timeout expires

# Verify stopped:
root@pve# pct status 201
status: stopped

# If container is frozen and pct shutdown hangs:
root@pve# pct stop 201   # immediate kill

LXC Containers (pct).desc: Forcefully stops a running LXC container immediately without a graceful shutdown — use "pct shutdown <CTID>" for a clean stop that runs the container init system shutdown sequence.
LXC Containers (pct).example:
root@pve# pct stop 201

# Graceful shutdown (preferred):
root@pve# pct shutdown 201 --timeout 30

# Verify stopped:
root@pve# pct status 201
status: stopped

# Force-stop is appropriate when a container is hung and pct shutdown times out

LXC Containers (pct).desc: Gracefully reboot an LXC container by stopping and restarting its init process
LXC Containers (pct).example:
root@pve# pct reboot 200

# No output; the container stops cleanly and restarts.

# Monitor:
root@pve# pct status 200
status: running

# If the container is frozen and pct reboot hangs, force-stop first:
#   pct stop 200 --skiplock
#   pct start 200

LXC Containers (pct).desc: Open a shell inside the container (no SSH needed)
LXC Containers (pct).example:
$ pct enter 200
root@nginx-proxy:~#

# You are now in a shell inside container 200.
# Type 'exit' or press Ctrl+D to return to the Proxmox host shell.

# Equivalent using lxc-attach (lower level):
$ lxc-attach -n 200

# Run a single command without entering the container:
$ pct exec 200 -- systemctl status nginx

LXC Containers (pct).desc: Run a single command in the container without an interactive shell
LXC Containers (pct).example: (Command output returned; e.g. pct exec 200 -- systemctl status nginx)

LXC Containers (pct).desc: Copy a file from host into the container
LXC Containers (pct).example: (File pushed into container filesystem — no output on success)

LXC Containers (pct).desc: Copy a file from the container to the host
LXC Containers (pct).example: (File pulled from container to local host — no output on success)

LXC Containers (pct).desc: Mount the container rootfs on the host (under /var/lib/lxc/<CTID>/rootfs) for offline edits
LXC Containers (pct).example: mounting container 200 at /var/lib/lxc/200/rootfs

LXC Containers (pct).desc: Unmount a stopped container's filesystem after manual inspection with pct mount; leaves the container stopped
LXC Containers (pct).example:
# Mount for inspection:
root@pve# pct mount 200
mounted CT 200 in '/var/lib/lxc/200/rootfs'

# Inspect files:
root@pve# ls /var/lib/lxc/200/rootfs/etc/

# Unmount when done:
root@pve# pct unmount 200
unmounted /var/lib/lxc/200/rootfs

# Always unmount before starting the container:
#   pct start 200

LXC Containers (pct).desc: Migrate a container to another node (offline; --restart restarts after)
LXC Containers (pct).example:
migrating CT 200 to node pve02: 100%
CT started on pve02.

LXC Containers (pct).desc: Creates a full clone of an LXC container with a new ID and hostname — unlike a linked clone, a full clone is completely independent with its own disk and does not require the original.
LXC Containers (pct).example:
root@pve# pct clone 200 210 --hostname ct-clone-01 --full 1
create full clone of subvolume /var/lib/lxc/200/rootfs

# Set the cloned container IP:
root@pve# pct set 210 --net0 name=eth0,bridge=vmbr0,ip=10.0.1.210/24,gw=10.0.1.1

# Start the clone:
root@pve# pct start 210

LXC Containers (pct).desc: Take a container snapshot (filesystem-level)
LXC Containers (pct).example: (Snapshot pre-update created for CT 200)

LXC Containers (pct).desc: Rolls an LXC container back to a previously taken snapshot, discarding all changes made after that snapshot — the container must be stopped first.
LXC Containers (pct).example:
root@pve# pct stop 201
root@pve# pct rollback 201 pre-config-change
(CT 201 rolled back to snapshot pre-config-change)

# Start the container in its restored state:
root@pve# pct start 201

# Warning: any data written since the snapshot was taken is permanently lost

LXC Containers (pct).desc: Delete container and its disks
LXC Containers (pct).example:
removing disk image DS-FAST:subvol-200-disk-0...
CT 200 (nginx-proxy) removed.

LXC Containers (pct).desc: Remove a stuck lock file from a container; use only when the container is not in an active task (migration, backup, snapshot)
LXC Containers (pct).example:
root@pve# pct start 200
ERROR: CT is locked (backup)

root@pve# pct unlock 200

# No output on success — the lock file is removed.

root@pve# pct start 200

# Only run this if you are certain no vzdump/migration task is actually running.
# Check active tasks first: pveperf or journalctl -u pvestatd

LXC Containers (pct).desc: List all snapshots for an LXC container with name, parent, and description; LXC snapshots never include RAM state
LXC Containers (pct).example:
root@pve# pct snapshot list 200

┌─────────────────┬──────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┐
│ Name            │ Parent       │ Description                      │
╞═════════════════╪══════════════╪══════════════════════════════════╡
│ current         │ baseline     │                                  │
├─────────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────┤
│ baseline        │ no-parent    │ Fresh Debian 12 install          │
└─────────────────┴──────────────┴──────────────────────────────────┘

# Roll back: pct rollback 200 baseline
# Delete:    pct delsnapshot 200 baseline
# Take new:  pct snapshot 200 snap-$(date +%Y%m%d) -description "Pre-update"
# Note: snapshots require ZFS, LVM-thin, or Btrfs storage backend.

LXC Containers (pct).desc: Runs a filesystem check (fsck) against a stopped container's root filesystem (or a specific mount point with --device) — use after an unclean shutdown or when a container fails to start with a filesystem error; add --force to check even if the filesystem reports clean.
LXC Containers (pct).example:
root@pve# pct stop 200
root@pve# pct fsck 200
running fsck on volume /dev/mapper/pve-vm--200--disk--0 [rootfs]
fsck from util-linux 2.38
ext2fs_check_desc: Corrupt group descriptor: bad block for block bitmap
fsck.ext4: Group descriptors look bad... trying backup blocks...
Pass 1: Checking inodes, blocks, and sizes
...
200-rootfs: 12345/655360 files, 456789/2621440 blocks

# Check a specific mount point instead of the rootfs:
root@pve# pct fsck 200 --device mp0 --force
# Official docs: https://pve.proxmox.com/pve-docs/pct.1.html

Storage (pvesm).desc: Discover iSCSI targets on a portal
Discover NFS exports on a server
Scan the local system for importable ZFS pools not yet configured as Proxmox storage
Storage (pvesm).example:

rpool
data

Storage (pvesm).desc: Add an NFS storage with content types

Storage (pvesm).cmd: pvesm add lvmthin <NAME> --vgname pve --thinpool data --content rootdir,images
Storage (pvesm).desc: Adds an LVM-Thin pool storage backend to Proxmox, making it available for VM disk and container root filesystem allocation with thin-provisioning support.
Storage (pvesm).example:
root@pve# pvesm add lvmthin local-lvm-fast --vgname pve --thinpool data --content rootdir,images

Name          Type     Status  Total    Used     Available
local-lvm-fast lvmthin active  400.0G   0.0G     400.0G

Storage (pvesm).desc: Disable a storage without removing it
Storage (pvesm).example:
$ pvesm set DS-SLOW --disable 1
(Storage DS-SLOW disabled — no output on success)

# Confirm:
$ pvesm status
Name       Type   Status     Total         Used    Available   %
local      dir    active     100G          20G     80G          20%
DS-SLOW    nfs    disabled      -            -          -        -

# Re-enable:

Storage (pvesm).desc: Remove a storage definition (does not delete data)
Storage (pvesm).example: (Storage definition removed — data on the server is untouched)

Storage (pvesm).desc: Manually allocate a 32 GB disk for VMID on storage
Storage (pvesm).example:
$ pvesm alloc DS-FAST 999 vm-999-disk-0 32G
DS-FAST:vm-999-disk-0

# A new 32 GB volume named 'vm-999-disk-0' has been allocated on DS-FAST.
# The output is the full Proxmox volume ID (storage:volume-name).

# Attach the volume to a VM:
$ qm set 999 --scsi1 DS-FAST:vm-999-disk-0

# List allocated volumes for a storage:
$ pvesm list DS-FAST

Storage (pvesm).desc: Frees (deletes) a specific storage volume from Proxmox-managed storage — use to clean up detached or orphaned VM disks and recover storage space.
Storage (pvesm).example:
root@pve# pvesm free local-lvm:vm-999-disk-0
(Volume local-lvm:vm-999-disk-0 freed successfully)

# List orphaned volumes (not attached to any VM):
root@pve# pvesm list local-lvm | grep "^local-lvm" | awk "{print \$1}" | while read vol; do
  vmid=$(echo \$vol | grep -o "vm-[0-9]*" | cut -d- -f2)
  qm status \$vmid 2>/dev/null || echo "ORPHANED: \$vol"
done

Networking.desc: Apply changes from /etc/network/interfaces without rebooting
Networking.example: (Network interfaces reloaded from /etc/network/interfaces without reboot)

Networking.desc: API view of node network config
Creates a new Linux bridge on the Proxmox host via the REST API with a physical NIC as its uplink port — activate with "pvesh set ... --reload 1".
Networking.example:
root@pve# pvesh create /nodes/pve-node-01/network --iface vmbr2 --type bridge --bridge_ports eno2 --autostart 1
(Bridge vmbr2 created with eno2 as uplink)

# Apply immediately:
root@pve# pvesh set /nodes/pve-node-01/network --reload 1

# Verify:
root@pve# ip link show vmbr2
6: vmbr2: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 1500 state UP

Networking.desc: Applies pending network configuration changes on the Proxmox node without a reboot — equivalent to "ifreload -a" on Debian; use after adding or modifying bridges, bonds, or VLANs.
Networking.example:
root@pve# pvesh set /nodes/pve-node-01/network --reload 1
(Pending network changes applied successfully)

# Verify new bridge is up:
root@pve# ip link show vmbr2
6: vmbr2: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 1500 qdisc noqueue state UP

# If reload fails, check /etc/network/interfaces syntax:
root@pve# ifupdown2 --syntax-check -a

ZFS.desc: Lists every ZFS snapshot across all pools/datasets with space used and referenced size — USED reflects only data unique to that snapshot, not the full dataset size.
ZFS.example:

ZFS.cmd: zfs send -R <POOL>/<DS>@<SNAP> | ssh dr-host 'zfs recv -F dr-pool/<DS>'
ZFS.desc: Replicate dataset (and children) to a remote pool
ZFS.example:
sending from rpool/data/vm-100-disk-0
receiving at dr-host:dr-pool/vm-100-disk-0
(32.0 GB transferred in 00:04:22)

ZFS.desc: Starts a background scrub that reads every block in the pool and verifies its checksum against the redundant copy/parity, repairing silent corruption on redundant (mirror/RAIDZ) pools
ZFS.example:
$ zpool scrub tank
$ zpool status tank
  pool: tank
 state: ONLINE
  scan: scrub in progress since Sun Jul  6 02:00:03 2026
        1.20T scanned at 512M/s, 340G issued at 145M/s, 3.50T total
        0B repaired, 9.71% done, 0 days 06:15:22 to go

What it means:
- Runs continuously in the background at low priority; check progress
  any time with "zpool status" — it will show "scrub repaired N in
  HH:MM:SS with 0 errors" when finished.
- Non-redundant (single-disk) pools can detect corruption via checksum
  mismatch but cannot repair it — a scrub there only reports errors.
- Proxmox schedules a monthly scrub by default (via a systemd timer);
  running it manually here is for on-demand verification after a
  suspected disk issue or unclean shutdown.
- Cancel an in-progress scrub with: zpool scrub -s tank
(Resilver started; monitor with zpool status — may take hours on large pools)

Users & Auth (pveum).desc: Auth realms (pve, pam, AD, LDAP, OIDC)
List all API tokens for a Proxmox user account: token ID, comment, expiry, and privilege-separation flag
Users & Auth (pveum).example:
root@pve# pveum user token list admin@pam

┌─────────────────────────┬──────────────────┬────────────────────┬─────────┐
│ full tokenid            │ comment          │ expire             │ privsep │
╞═════════════════════════╪══════════════════╪════════════════════╪═════════╡
│ admin@pam!terraform     │ Terraform runner │ 2027-01-01 00:00   │ 0       │
├─────────────────────────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────┼─────────┤
│ admin@pam!monitoring    │ Zabbix read-only │ never              │ 1       │
└─────────────────────────┴──────────────────┴────────────────────┴─────────┘

# privsep=0: token inherits ALL user permissions.
# privsep=1: token limited to roles explicitly assigned to the token (safer).
# Create: pveum user token add admin@pam monitoring --comment "Zabbix" --privsep 1
# Delete: pveum user token remove admin@pam terraform

API & Automation (pvesh).desc: Queries the Proxmox VE REST API version endpoint — a quick, low-privilege connectivity/health check for the pvedaemon API before running scripted automation against a node.
API & Automation (pvesh).example:
$ pvesh get /version
{
  "release": "8.2",
  "repoid": "abcd1234",
  "version": "8.2.4"
}
# Same data is reachable over HTTPS at https://<node>:8006/api2/json/version
# once authenticated — 'pvesh' just wraps the local API call for CLI/scripts.

Updates & Maintenance.desc: Download a template into the named storage

Removes packages that were pulled in as dependencies but are no longer required by anything installed (typically old kernel/pve-kernel versions after an upgrade), and --purge also deletes their configuration files — run after "apt dist-upgrade" to reclaim /boot and /var space.
Updates & Maintenance.example:
# apt autoremove --purge -y
Reading package lists... Done
Building dependency tree... Done
The following packages will be REMOVED:
  pve-kernel-6.8.4-2-pve* pve-kernel-6.8.4-3-pve*
0 upgraded, 0 newly installed, 2 to remove
After this operation, 612 MB disk space will be freed.
(Reading database ...)
Removing pve-kernel-6.8.4-2-pve (6.8.4-2) ...

Interfaces.desc: Lists all trunk interfaces, their native VLAN, allowed VLAN list, and the VLANs currently forwarding on each trunk — essential for verifying VLAN propagation.
Just the per-interface config block. Pair with `show interface Ethernet1/1` (operational) to compare intent vs reality.
Interfaces.example:
switch# show running-config interface Ethernet1/1
!Command: show running-config interface Ethernet1/1
!Time: Tue Apr 21 14:30:12 2026

version 10.3(4a)

interface Ethernet1/1
  description ACCESS-PORT host01
  switchport
  switchport access vlan 10
  spanning-tree port type edge
  spanning-tree bpduguard enable
  no shutdown

Interfaces.cmd:
interface Ethernet1/1
 description ACCESS
 switchport
 switchport mode access
 switchport access vlan 10
 no shutdown
Interfaces.desc: Access-port baseline. On Nexus, `switchport` flips the interface from default routed-mode (L3) to L2 — required before `switchport mode access/trunk`.
Interfaces.example:
switch(config)# interface Ethernet1/1
switch(config-if)# description ACCESS-PORT host01
switch(config-if)# switchport
switch(config-if)# switchport mode access
switch(config-if)# switchport access vlan 10
switch(config-if)# spanning-tree port type edge
switch(config-if)# spanning-tree bpduguard enable
switch(config-if)# no shutdown
switch(config-if)# end
switch# show interface Ethernet1/1 status
Port      Name               Status      Vlan      Duplex  Speed   Type
Eth1/1    ACCESS-PORT host01 connected   10        full    10G     10Gbase-CR

Interfaces.cmd:
interface Ethernet1/1
 switchport mode trunk
 switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30
 switchport trunk native vlan 99
Interfaces.desc: Trunk-port baseline. `switchport` is required first; default allowed list is "all" — restrict explicitly.
Interfaces.example:
switch(config)# interface Ethernet1/1
switch(config-if)# switchport
switch(config-if)# switchport mode trunk
switch(config-if)# switchport trunk native vlan 99
switch(config-if)# switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30
switch(config-if)# no shutdown
switch(config-if)# end
switch# show interface Ethernet1/1 trunk
Port         Native  Status        Port-Channel
Eth1/1       99      trunking      --
Port        Vlans Allowed on Trunk
Eth1/1      10,20,30

Interfaces.cmd:
interface port-channel1
 switchport
 switchport mode trunk
Interfaces.desc: Logical port-channel for LACP bundles. Member ports inherit settings from the port-channel; configure switchport mode here, not on members.
Interfaces.example:
switch(config)# feature lacp
switch(config)# interface port-channel 1
switch(config-if)# description LACP bundle to leaf02
switch(config-if)# switchport
switch(config-if)# switchport mode trunk
switch(config-if)# switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30,99
switch(config-if)# switchport trunk native vlan 99

Interfaces.desc: Bind a physical interface to a port-channel. `mode active` = LACP active (sends LACPDUs); `mode passive` = LACP passive; `mode on` = static no-LACP.
Interfaces.example:
switch(config)# interface Ethernet1/1-2
switch(config-if-range)# channel-group 1 mode active
switch(config-if-range)# no shutdown
switch# show port-channel summary
Flags:  D - Down        P - Up in port-channel (members)
Group Port-       Type     Protocol  Member Ports
      Channel
------------------------------------------------------------
1     Po1(SU)     Eth      LACP      Eth1/1(P)    Eth1/2(P)

Interfaces.cmd:
interface Ethernet1/49
 no switchport
 mtu 9216
 ip address 10.0.0.1/31
 no shutdown
Interfaces.desc: Routed (L3) point-to-point uplink — typical spine-leaf fabric link. `no switchport` flips back to L3 mode. MTU 9216 (jumbo) is standard for VXLAN underlay.
Interfaces.example:
switch(config)# interface Ethernet1/49
switch(config-if)# description SPINE-01
switch(config-if)# no switchport
switch(config-if)# mtu 9216
switch(config-if)# ip address 10.0.0.1/31
switch(config-if)# ip router ospf UNDERLAY area 0.0.0.0
switch(config-if)# ip ospf network point-to-point
switch(config-if)# ip ospf bfd
switch(config-if)# no shutdown
switch# show interface Ethernet1/49 | include MTU|address|protocol
  MTU 9216 bytes, BW 100000000 Kbit, DLY 10 usec
  Internet Address is 10.0.0.1/31

Interfaces.desc: NX-OS — transceiver vendor / part / type / serial.
Interfaces.example:
type is 10Gbase-SR
name is CISCO
part number is SFP-10G-SR

Interfaces.desc: Displays cumulative RX/TX packet and byte counters for every Layer-2 interface in one table — the fast way to spot which port is passing traffic without drilling into each interface individually.

VLANs / VPC.desc: VLAN database with member-port lists. Status "active" = operational; "act/unsup" = configured but not supported on a given module.
VLANs / VPC.example:
switch# show vlan
VLAN Name                             Status    Ports
---- -------------------------------- --------- -------------------------------
1    default                          active    Eth1/1, Eth1/3, Eth1/5
10   USER-DATA                        active    Eth1/1, Po1
20   USER-VOICE                       active    Po1
30   SERVERS                          active    Eth1/2, Po1
99   MGMT                             active    Po1

VLAN Type         Vlan-mode
---- -----        ----------
1    enet         CE
10   enet         CE
20   enet         CE
30   enet         CE
99   enet         CE

VLANs / VPC.desc: Displays a compact VLAN table showing VLAN ID, name, status (active/act/lshut), and associated trunk/access ports — the fastest way to verify VLAN provisioning on a Nexus switch.
VLANs / VPC.example:
switch# show vlan brief

VLAN Name                             Status    Ports
---- -------------------------------- --------- -------------------------------
1    default                          active    Eth1/3, Eth1/4
10   VLAN_PROD_SERVERS                active    Eth1/1, Eth1/2, Po1
20   VLAN_MGMT                        active    Eth1/5, Eth1/6
100  VLAN_STORAGE                     active    Eth1/7, Eth1/8, Po2
999  VLAN_NATIVE                      active

Legend:
                (*) - local vPC is down, forwarding via vPC peer-link
vPC domain id                     : 100
Peer status                       : peer adjacency formed ok
vPC keep-alive status             : peer is alive

VLANs / VPC.desc: Enable vPC (virtual Port-Channel) — required before any `vpc domain` or vPC member-port config.
VLANs / VPC.example:
switch(config)# feature vpc
switch(config)# feature lacp
switch(config)# show feature | include vpc|lacp
lacp                  1         enabled
vpc                   1         enabled

VLANs / VPC.desc: Enable LACP (802.3ad) — required before any `channel-group ... mode active|passive` interface command.
VLANs / VPC.example:
switch(config)# feature lacp
switch(config)# show feature | include lacp
lacp                  1         enabled

VLANs / VPC.cmd:
vpc domain 1
 peer-switch
 peer-keepalive destination 10.99.99.2 source 10.99.99.1
 peer-gateway
 delay restore 150
 auto-recovery
VLANs / VPC.desc: vPC domain baseline. peer-switch lets both peers share STP root; peer-gateway lets either peer reply to packets destined for the other's MAC; delay-restore pauses vPC bring-up after a reload so routes converge first.
VLANs / VPC.example:
switch(config)# vpc domain 1
switch(config-vpc-domain)# peer-switch
switch(config-vpc-domain)# peer-keepalive destination 10.99.99.2 source 10.99.99.1 vrf management
switch(config-vpc-domain)# peer-gateway
switch(config-vpc-domain)# delay restore 150
switch(config-vpc-domain)# auto-recovery
switch(config-vpc-domain)# ip arp synchronize
switch(config-vpc-domain)# end

switch# show vpc brief
Legend:

Per-vlan consistency status       : success
Type-2 consistency status         : success

Delay-restore status              : Timer is off.(timeout = 150s)

VLANs / VPC.example:
vPC domain id                     : 1
vPC Peer Status               : peer adjacency formed ok

10    Po10   up     success     success   1,10,20,100-102
20    Po20   up     success     success   1,10,20

VLANs / VPC.desc: Display the Spanning Tree topology for all VLANs, including the bridge role (Root/Designated), port states, port costs, and the root bridge MAC address for each VLAN instance.
VLANs / VPC.example:
N9K# show spanning-tree

VLAN0001
  Spanning tree enabled protocol rstp
  Root ID    Priority    4097
             Address     0023.eb9e.4680
             Cost        4
             Port        Po1 (Port-channel1)
             Hello Time   2 sec  Max Age 20 sec  Forward Delay 15 sec

  Bridge ID  Priority    32769  (priority 32768 sys-id-ext 1)
             Address     001e.1432.5f00
             Hello Time   2 sec  Max Age 20 sec  Forward Delay 15 sec
             Aging Time  300 sec

Interface           Role Sts Cost      Prio.Nbr Type
------------------- ---- --- --------- -------- --------------------------------
Po1                 Root FWD 4         128.4097 P2p
Eth1/3              Desg FWD 4         128.3    P2p
Eth1/4              Desg FWD 4         128.4    P2p

What it means:
- Role Root  : this port is the root port (path to root bridge)
- Role Desg  : designated port (forwarding for the segment)
- Sts FWD    : forwarding — carrying traffic
- Sts BLK    : blocking — preventing loop

VLANs / VPC.desc: Detailed per-VLAN STP output including topology change counters, port cost, hello/forward-delay timers, and BPDUs sent/received — useful for diagnosing TCN storms or slow convergence.
VLANs / VPC.example:
N9K# show spanning-tree detail

VLAN0010 is executing the rstp compatible Spanning Tree protocol
  Bridge Identifier has priority 32768, sysid 10, address 001e.1432.5f00
  Configured hello time 2, max age 20, forward delay 15
  Current root has priority 4106, address 0023.eb9e.4680
  Root port is Po1 (Port-channel1), cost of root path is 4
  Topology change flag not set, detected flag not set
  Number of topology changes 12, last change occurred 4d02h ago
    from Ethernet1/5
  Times:  hold 1, topology change 35, notification 2
          hello 2, max age 20, forward delay 15
  Timers: hello 0, topology change 0, notification 0

Port 4097 (Port-channel1) of VLAN0010 is Root Forwarding
   Port path cost 4, Port priority 128, Port Identifier 128.4097
   Designated root has priority 4106, address 0023.eb9e.4680
   BPDUs: sent 43210, received 287654

What it means:
- topology changes 12 in 4d02h — low churn, healthy
- last change from Eth1/5 — investigate that port if changes spike

Routing.desc: IPv4 routing table (default VRF). NX-OS includes the source protocol, admin-distance/metric, next-hop, age, and outgoing interface.
Routing.example:
Codes: L - local, C - connected, S - static, R - RIP, M - mobile, B - BGP
       D - EIGRP, EX - EIGRP external, O - OSPF, IA - OSPF inter area
       N1 - OSPF NSSA external type 1, N2 - OSPF NSSA external type 2
       E1 - OSPF external type 1, E2 - OSPF external type 2
       i - IS-IS, su - IS-IS summary, L1 - IS-IS level-1, L2 - IS-IS level-2

Gateway of last resort is 10.0.0.254 to network 0.0.0.0

S*    0.0.0.0/0 [1/0] via 10.0.0.254
      10.0.0.0/8 is variably subnetted, 4 subnets, 2 masks
C        10.0.0.0/24 is directly connected, GigabitEthernet0/1
L        10.0.0.1/32 is directly connected, GigabitEthernet0/1
O        10.20.0.0/24 [110/2] via 10.0.0.2, 00:14:23, GigabitEthernet0/1
B        172.16.0.0/16 [200/0] via 1.1.1.1, 1d04h

Routing.desc: IPv4 routing table for a specific VRF. Without VRF specification only the default VRF is shown — VRF-aware platforms must always pass the VRF.
Routing.example:
switch# show ip route vrf TENANT_A
IP Route Table for VRF "TENANT_A"

10.50.0.0/24, ubest/mbest: 1/0, attached

10.50.0.1/32, ubest/mbest: 1/0, attached
    *via 10.50.0.1, Vlan150, [0/0], 4w2d, local
10.50.10.0/24, ubest/mbest: 1/0
    *via 10.0.0.5%default, [200/0], 03:21:55, bgp-65001, internal, tag 65001
        (evpn) segid: 50001 tunnelid: 0xa000005 encap: VXLAN

  am                     1            0              1
Total                 12346           42         12388

Routing.desc: PIM neighbours per VRF: address, interface, uptime, expiration timer, version, DR-priority.
Routing.example:
switch# show ip pim neighbor

Neighbor       Interface            Uptime    Expires   DR        Bidir-  BFD    ECMP Redirect
                                                        Priority  Capable State        Capable

Routing.desc: PIM-enabled interfaces with mode (sparse/dense/sparse-dense), DR election, hello interval, JP holdtime.
Routing.example:
switch# show ip pim interface
PIM Interfaces for VRF "default"
Interface       IP Address      PIM       DR         DR        Hello  JP         Border
                                Mode      Address   Priority   Intvl  Intvl      Intf

Ethernet1/50    10.0.0.3        sparse    10.0.0.3  1          30     60         no

Routing.desc: Enable PIM multicast routing. Required before any `ip pim sparse-mode` interface command.
Routing.example:
switch(config)# feature pim
switch(config)# ip pim rp-address 1.1.1.1 group-list 224.0.0.0/4
switch(config)# interface Ethernet1/49
switch(config-if)# ip pim sparse-mode

Routing.cmd:
vrf context TENANT_A
 description Tenant A
 rd auto
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  route-target both auto
Routing.desc: New VRF with route-distinguisher and route-target. `auto` derives the RD as <BGP-AS>:<VNI-or-VLAN>; cleaner than hard-coding.
Routing.example:
switch(config)# vrf context TENANT_A
switch(config-vrf)# description Tenant A — production
switch(config-vrf)# vni 50001
switch(config-vrf)# rd auto
switch(config-vrf)# address-family ipv4 unicast
switch(config-vrf-af-ipv4)#   route-target both auto
switch(config-vrf-af-ipv4)#   route-target both auto evpn
switch# show vrf TENANT_A detail
VRF-Name: TENANT_A, VRF-ID: 3, State: Up
    VPNID: unknown
    RD: 1.1.1.1:50001
    Max Routes: 0  Mid-Threshold: 0
    Table-ID: 0x80000003, AF: IPv6, Fwd-ID: 0x80000003, State: Up
    Table-ID: 0x00000003, AF: IPv4, Fwd-ID: 0x00000003, State: Up

Routing.desc: Shows the hardware-programmed FIB (forwarding table) as installed in the ASIC/TCAM — compare against 'show ip route' (software RIB) to catch RIB/FIB programming mismatches.
Routing.example:
switch# show forwarding ipv4 route

IPv4 routes for table default/base
-------------------+----------------+----------------+-----------
Prefix              Next-Hop          Interface        Partial-flag
0.0.0.0/0            10.0.0.254        Vlan10           false
10.0.0.0/24          attached          Vlan10           false
172.16.0.0/16        1.1.1.1           Eth1/1           false
# 'Partial-flag: true' means the FIB entry is incomplete (e.g. unresolved
# adjacency) — traffic to that prefix punts to software or drops.

Routing › OSPF.desc: Enable the OSPFv2 feature on Nexus. Required before any `router ospf` command. Without it the command will return a parser error.
Routing › OSPF.example:
switch(config)# feature ospf
switch(config)# show feature | include ospf
ospf                  1         enabled
switch(config)# router ospf UNDERLAY  ; now this works
switch(config-router)# router-id 1.1.1.1

Routing › OSPF.desc: Nexus uses tagged process names (string), not numeric process IDs like classic IOS. Multiple processes can coexist as long as their tags differ.
Routing › OSPF.example:
switch(config)# router ospf UNDERLAY
switch(config-router)# router-id 1.1.1.1
switch(config-router)# log-adjacency-changes detail
switch(config-router)# bfd
switch(config-router)# auto-cost reference-bandwidth 400 Gbps
switch(config-router)# end
switch# show ip ospf | include process
Routing Process UNDERLAY with ID 1.1.1.1 VRF default

Routing › OSPF.desc: Verbose adjacency-change syslog. Without `detail` only up/down is logged; with `detail` every state transition (Init, 2-way, ExStart, Exchange, Loading, Full) is logged.
Routing › OSPF.example:
switch(config-router)# log-adjacency-changes detail
switch# show logging last 10 | include OSPF
%OSPF-5-ADJCHG: ospf-UNDERLAY [1234] Process UNDERLAY, Nbr 10.0.0.2 on Ethernet1/49 from LOADING to FULL, Loading Done
%OSPF-5-ADJCHG: ospf-UNDERLAY [1234] Process UNDERLAY, Nbr 10.0.0.2 on Ethernet1/49 from EXSTART to EXCHANGE, Negotiation Done
%OSPF-5-ADJCHG: ospf-UNDERLAY [1234] Process UNDERLAY, Nbr 10.0.0.2 on Ethernet1/49 from EXCHANGE to LOADING, Exchange Done

Routing › OSPF.desc: Enable BFD across all OSPF-enabled interfaces (process-wide). Per-interface BFD with `ip ospf bfd disable` lets you opt out.
Routing › OSPF.example:
switch(config)# feature bfd
switch(config)# router ospf UNDERLAY
switch(config-router)# bfd
switch(config)# interface Ethernet1/49
switch(config-if)# bfd interval 250 min_rx 250 multiplier 3
switch# show bfd neighbors
OurAddr         NeighAddr       LD/RD                 RH/RS           Holdown(mult) State     Int     Vrf
10.0.0.1        10.0.0.2        1090519041/1090519041 Up              750(3)        Up        Eth1/49 default

Routing › OSPF.desc: Set the reference bandwidth used to compute OSPF cost = ref-bw / interface-bw. Default 40 Gbps makes all 40G+ links cost 1 (no preference). Use 400 Gbps in 400G/800G fabrics.
Routing › OSPF.example:
switch(config-router)# auto-cost reference-bandwidth 400 Gbps

switch# show ip ospf interface brief
 Interface           PID     Area            Cost      State    Neighbors  Status
 Eth1/49             UNDERLAY 0.0.0.0          4         BDR      1          up
 Eth1/50             UNDERLAY 0.0.0.0          4         BDR      1          up

# 100G link cost = 400 / 100 = 4 (rounded). With default 40 Gbps both 100G and
# 40G links would cost 1 — same — defeating SPF preference.

Routing › OSPF.desc: OSPF process summary: process ID, router-ID, area count, LSA counts, SPF stats, BFD status.
Routing › OSPF.example:

Routing › OSPF.desc: Lists OSPF neighbor adjacencies with state, dead-timer, and DR/BDR role — "FULL/DR" or "FULL/BDR" is healthy; other states indicate an adjacency problem.
Routing › OSPF.example:
switch# show ip ospf neighbors

OSPF Process ID 1 VRF default
Total number of neighbors: 3

Neighbor ID     Pri State            Dead Time  Address         Interface
10.0.0.2         1  FULL/DR          00:00:35   10.10.12.2      Eth1/1
10.0.0.3         1  FULL/BDR         00:00:39   10.10.13.3      Eth1/2
10.0.0.4         1  2WAY/DROTHER     00:00:37   10.10.14.4      Eth1/3
# 2WAY/DROTHER is normal for non-DR/BDR routers on a multi-access segment

Gi0/2        1     0               10.0.1.1/30        1     P2P   1/1

Routing › OSPF.desc: Displays the OSPF link-state database (LSDB) summary for all areas, listing LSA types (Router, Network, Summary, External) with their link-state ID, ADV router, age, and sequence number.
Routing › OSPF.example:

        OSPF Router with ID (10.0.0.1) (Process ID 1 VRF default)

Link ID         ADV Router      Age  Seq#       Checksum Link count

10.0.0.2        10.0.0.2        408  0x80000010 0x00D1B3 3
10.0.0.3        10.0.0.3        415  0x8000000F 0x00A4C1 2

Routing › OSPF.desc: ABRs and ASBRs reachable through this process. Path to each border router with metric and next-hop.
Routing › OSPF.example:
switch# show ip ospf border-routers
 OSPF Process ID UNDERLAY VRF default, Internal Routing Table

Codes: i - Intra-area route, I - Inter-area route

Dest     Type Cost  Type    Next Hop                Interface       Area
i 5.5.5.5  ABR    4      intra   10.0.0.2                Eth1/49        0.0.0.0
I 6.6.6.6  ASBR   8      inter   10.0.0.2                Eth1/49        0.0.0.0

Routing › OSPF.desc: SPF execution counts and timings — useful when chasing churn (frequent SPF = unstable topology).
Routing › OSPF.example:
switch# show ip ospf statistics
 Area 0.0.0.0: SPF algorithm executed 18 times
 Summary:
  Full SPF runs:               18
  External SPF runs:           0
  Time since last Full SPF:    01:23:45
  Avg full SPF runtime:        2 ms
  Max full SPF runtime:        8 ms
  Last full SPF triggered by:  Router LSA 1.1.1.1
  Throttling:
    SPF delay timer:            200 ms
    SPF hold timer:             1000 ms
    SPF max wait:               5000 ms

Routing › OSPF.cmd:
interface Ethernet1/49
 no switchport
 ip address 10.0.0.1/31
 ip router ospf UNDERLAY area 0.0.0.0
 ip ospf network point-to-point
 ip ospf bfd
Routing › OSPF.desc: Per-interface OSPF binding. Nexus does NOT use the IOS `network` statement under the process — interfaces are enabled directly with `ip router ospf <tag> area <area>`.
Routing › OSPF.example:
switch(config)# interface Ethernet1/49
switch(config-if)# no switchport
switch(config-if)# mtu 9216
switch(config-if)# ip address 10.0.0.1/31
switch(config-if)# ip router ospf UNDERLAY area 0.0.0.0
switch(config-if)# ip ospf network point-to-point
switch(config-if)# ip ospf bfd
switch(config-if)# no shutdown

switch# show ip ospf interface Ethernet1/49
 Ethernet1/49 is up, line protocol is up
    IP address 10.0.0.1/31, Process ID UNDERLAY VRF default, area 0.0.0.0
    Enabled by interface configuration
    State P2P, Network type P2P, cost 4
    Hello interval 10 sec, Dead interval 40 sec

Routing › OSPF.cmd:
interface loopback0
 ip address 1.1.1.1/32
 ip router ospf UNDERLAY area 0.0.0.0
Routing › OSPF.desc: Loopback advertised into OSPF area 0 — used as router-ID and as BGP/EVPN VTEP source. Always /32.
Routing › OSPF.example:
switch(config)# interface loopback0
switch(config-if)# ip address 1.1.1.1/32
switch(config-if)# ip router ospf UNDERLAY area 0.0.0.0
switch# show ip route 1.1.1.1
1.1.1.1/32, ubest/mbest: 1/0, attached
    *via 1.1.1.1, Lo0, [0/0], 12w3d, local

Routing › OSPF.desc: Configure area 1 as a totally-stubby area — blocks external (type 5) and summary (type 3) LSAs. The ABR injects only a default route. Reduces LSDB size at the cost of optimal routing.
Routing › OSPF.example:
switch(config-router)# area 1 stub no-summary

switch# show ip ospf | include Area
 Number of areas is 2, 1 normal, 1 stub, 0 nssa
   Area (0.0.0.0)
     Area has existed for 12w3d
   Area (0.0.0.1)
     This area is a STUB area (No summary LSAs)
     Area has existed for 04:23:45

Routing › OSPF.desc: Configure area 1 as a Not-So-Stubby Area (NSSA) and have the ABR generate a default route into it. NSSA permits external routes (type-7) while still blocking type-5 from the backbone.
Routing › OSPF.example:
switch(config-router)# area 1 nssa default-information-originate
switch# show ip ospf | section Area
   Area (0.0.0.0)
   Area (0.0.0.1)
     This area is a NSSA area
     Perform type-7/type-5 LSA translation
     Default information originated

Routing › OSPF.desc: Inter-area summary at the ABR — collapse all area-1 type-1/2 LSAs into a single type-3 summary advertised to other areas.
Routing › OSPF.example:
switch(config-router)# area 1 range 10.1.0.0/16
switch# show ip ospf summary-address
   Area 0.0.0.1: 10.1.0.0/16 - Summary - Advertise

Routing › OSPF.desc: External summary at the ASBR (applied to redistributed routes before they become type-5 LSAs).
Routing › OSPF.example:
switch(config-router)# summary-address 192.168.0.0/16
switch# show ip ospf summary-address
   External: 192.168.0.0/16 - Advertise
   Total: 1 summary addresses
     1 with metric 20

Routing › OSPF.desc: Area-wide MD5 authentication. Both sides of every adjacency in the area must agree on the keychain — otherwise no neighbours form.
Routing › OSPF.example:
switch(config)# key chain OSPF-KC
switch(config-keychain)#   key 1
switch(config-keychain-key)#     key-string 7 <encrypted>
switch(config)# router ospf UNDERLAY
switch(config-router)#   area 0 authentication message-digest
switch(config)# interface Ethernet1/49
switch(config-if)#   ip ospf authentication key-chain OSPF-KC

Routing › OSPF.desc: SPF throttling timers: start (50 ms wait after first trigger), hold (200 ms minimum gap), max (5000 ms ceiling). Tighter values converge faster but burn more CPU during churn.
Routing › OSPF.example:
switch(config-router)# timers throttle spf 50 200 5000
switch# show ip ospf | include throttling
 SPF throttling delay time of 50.000 msecs,
   SPF throttling hold time of 200.000 msecs,
   SPF throttling maximum wait time of 5000.000 msecs

Routing › OSPF.desc: Enable OSPFv3 (IPv6) feature.
Routing › OSPF.example:
switch(config)# feature ospfv3
switch(config)# show feature | include ospfv3
ospfv3                1         enabled

Routing › OSPF.desc: OSPFv3 process (IPv6). Tagged with string just like OSPFv2.
Routing › OSPF.example:
switch(config)# router ospfv3 V6
switch(config-router)# router-id 1.1.1.1
switch(config-router)# log-adjacency-changes detail

Routing › OSPF.cmd:
interface Ethernet1/49
 ipv6 address 2001:db8::1/64
 ipv6 router ospfv3 V6 area 0.0.0.0
Routing › OSPF.desc: Per-interface OSPFv3 (IPv6) binding.
Routing › OSPF.example:
switch(config)# interface Ethernet1/49
switch(config-if)# ipv6 address 2001:db8::1/64
switch(config-if)# ipv6 router ospfv3 V6 area 0.0.0.0
switch(config-if)# ipv6 ospfv3 network point-to-point

Routing › OSPF.desc: OSPFv3 process summary (mirror of show ip ospf for IPv6).
Routing › OSPF.example:
switch# show ipv6 ospfv3
 Routing Process V6 with ID 1.1.1.1 VRF default
 Reference Bandwidth is 400000 Mbps
 Number of areas is 1, 1 normal, 0 stub, 0 nssa

Routing › OSPF.desc: Displays OSPFv3 IPv6 adjacencies, their state (FULL/2-WAY/EXSTART), dead-timer countdown, and the interface the neighbour was discovered on.
Routing › OSPF.example:
switch# show ipv6 ospfv3 neighbor

OSPFv3 Process ID 1 VRF default
Total number of neighbors: 2

Neighbor ID     Pri  State         Dead T  Interface         Addr
10.0.0.2         1   FULL/ -       00:00:35  Eth1/1          fe80::2
10.0.0.3         1   FULL/ -       00:00:38  Eth1/2          fe80::3

Routing › OSPF.cmd:
router ospf UNDERLAY
 router-id 1.1.1.1
 log-adjacency-changes detail
 bfd
Routing › OSPF.desc: OSPF process baseline — pin router-ID to a loopback IP, enable detailed adjacency logging, turn on BFD process-wide.
Routing › OSPF.example:
switch(config)# router ospf UNDERLAY
switch(config-router)#  router-id 1.1.1.1
switch(config-router)#  log-adjacency-changes detail
switch(config-router)#  bfd
switch(config-router)#  auto-cost reference-bandwidth 400 Gbps
switch(config-router)#  timers throttle spf 50 200 5000
switch(config-router)# end
switch# show ip ospf | head -3
 Routing Process UNDERLAY with ID 1.1.1.1 VRF default
 Routing Process Instance Number 1
 Stateful High Availability enabled

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Enable EIGRP feature on Nexus. Required before any `router eigrp` command.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
switch(config)# feature eigrp
switch(config)# show feature | include eigrp
eigrp                 1         enabled

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Enter EIGRP process for AS 100. Nexus uses tagged names; AS specified separately via `autonomous-system`.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
switch(config)# router eigrp 100
switch(config-router)# autonomous-system 100
switch(config-router)# router-id 1.1.1.1

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Explicit AS under named-process style on Nexus. The `router eigrp 100` "100" is the tag — the AS number is set here.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
switch(config-router)# autonomous-system 100
switch# show ip eigrp
IP-EIGRP AS 100 ID(1.1.1.1) VRF default

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Bring the EIGRP instance up — Nexus EIGRP is shutdown by default. Easy to miss; symptom is no neighbours and no adjacencies even though config "looks right".
Routing › EIGRP.example:
switch(config-router)# no shutdown
switch# show ip eigrp | include shutdown|state
  EIGRP NSF-aware route hold timer is 240
  EIGRP shutdown is FALSE

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Per-process EIGRP summary: AS, router-ID, K-values, NSF state, number of neighbours.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
switch# show ip eigrp
IP-EIGRP AS 100 ID(1.1.1.1) VRF default
  Process-tag: 100
  Instance Number: 1
  Status: running
  Authentication mode: none
  Metric weights: K1=1 K2=0 K3=1 K4=0 K5=0
  IP proto: 88  Multicast group: 224.0.0.10
  Router-ID: 1.1.1.1
  Topology-ids: 0
  Number of EIGRP interfaces: 2 (0 loopbacks)
  Number of EIGRP passive interfaces: 0
  Number of EIGRP peers: 2
  Redistributing:
  Graceful-Restart: Enabled

Routing › EIGRP.desc: EIGRP adjacency table.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
switch# show ip eigrp neighbors
IP-EIGRP AS 100 neighbors for process 100 VRF default
H   Address          Interface  Hold  Uptime  SRTT  RTO  Q   Seq
                                (sec)          (ms)        Cnt Num
0   10.0.0.2         Eth1/49    14    3w2d    12    200  0   124
1   10.0.0.3         Eth1/50    13    3w2d    10    200  0   118

Routing › EIGRP.desc: EIGRP topology table (all feasible successors). P = passive (stable), A = active (re-converging).
Routing › EIGRP.example:
switch# show ip eigrp topology
IP-EIGRP Topology Table for AS(100)/ID(1.1.1.1) VRF default
Codes: P - Passive, A - Active, U - Update, Q - Query, R - Reply
       r - reply Status, s - sia Status

P 10.0.0.0/30, 1 successors, FD is 256
        via Connected, Eth1/49
P 172.16.0.0/16, 2 successors, FD is 2816
        via 10.0.0.2 (2816/256), Eth1/49
        via 10.0.0.3 (3328/768), Eth1/50  ; feasible successor

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Per-interface EIGRP state: peers on the link, packet counters, hold/hello timers.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
switch# show ip eigrp interfaces
IP-EIGRP interfaces for process 100 VRF default
                        Xmit Queue   Mean   Pacing Time   Multicast    Pending
Interface        Peers  Un/Reliable  SRTT   Un/Reliable   Flow Timer   Routes
Eth1/49          1      0/0          12     0/1           50           0
Eth1/50          1      0/0          10     0/1           50           0

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Per-process EIGRP packet counters (hellos, updates, queries, replies, retransmissions).
Routing › EIGRP.example:
switch# show ip eigrp traffic
IP-EIGRP Traffic Statistics for AS 100 VRF default
  Hellos sent/received: 12345/12340
  Updates sent/received: 234/231
  Queries sent/received: 12/10
  Replies sent/received: 8/8
  Acks sent/received: 245/241
  SIA-Queries sent/received: 0/0
  SIA-Replies sent/received: 0/0
  Hello Process ID: 12345

Routing › EIGRP.cmd:
interface Ethernet1/1
 no switchport
 ip address 10.1.1.1/24
 ip router eigrp 100
Routing › EIGRP.desc: Per-interface EIGRP enable on Nexus. Like OSPF, Nexus EIGRP uses `ip router eigrp <tag>` per-interface rather than a `network` statement.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
switch(config)# interface Ethernet1/1
switch(config-if)# no switchport
switch(config-if)# ip address 10.1.1.1/24
switch(config-if)# ip router eigrp 100
switch(config-if)# no shutdown
switch# show ip eigrp interfaces Ethernet1/1
IP-EIGRP interfaces for process 100 VRF default
                        Xmit Queue   Mean
Interface        Peers  Un/Reliable  SRTT
Eth1/1           0      0/0          0

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Sub-second hello (2-second interval here). Reduces failure-detection time but raises CPU.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
switch(config-if)# ip eigrp 100 hello-interval 2
switch(config-if)# ip eigrp 100 hold-time 6
switch# show ip eigrp interfaces detail Ethernet1/1 | include hello
  Hello Interval: 2

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Hold timer — must be ≥ 3 × hello-interval. Default is 15 s with 5 s hello.
Routing › EIGRP.example: switch(config-if)# ip eigrp 100 hold-time 6

Routing › EIGRP.cmd:
ip authentication mode eigrp 100 md5
ip authentication key-chain eigrp 100 EIGRP-KC
Routing › EIGRP.desc: MD5 authentication via key-chain. Both sides must reference the same keychain string.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
switch(config)# key chain EIGRP-KC
switch(config-keychain)#   key 1
switch(config-keychain-key)#     key-string 7 <encrypted>
switch(config)# interface Ethernet1/1
switch(config-if)#   ip authentication mode eigrp 100 md5
switch(config-if)#   ip authentication key-chain eigrp 100 EIGRP-KC

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Unequal-cost load-balance up to 2× best metric — EIGRP will install feasible successors whose FD is within variance × best-FD.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
switch(config-router)# variance 2
switch# show ip route 172.16.0.0/16
172.16.0.0/16, ubest/mbest: 2/0
    *via 10.0.0.2, [90/2816], 03:21:55, eigrp-100, internal
    *via 10.0.0.3, [90/3072], 03:21:55, eigrp-100, internal  ; unequal-cost installed

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Stub router — reduces query scope. Advertises only connected + summary routes; neighbours skip this router in active query.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
switch(config-router)# eigrp stub connected summary
switch# show ip eigrp | include Stub
  EIGRP-IPv4 stub-router: connected, summary

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Redistribute connected (Nexus calls connected "direct") into OSPF/EIGRP. The route-map is MANDATORY on Nexus — without it the command is rejected.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
switch(config)# route-map ALLOW permit 10
switch(config-route-map)#   match interface loopback0
switch(config)# router ospf UNDERLAY
switch(config-router)#   redistribute direct route-map ALLOW
switch# show ip ospf database external
   OSPF Router with ID (1.1.1.1) (Process ID UNDERLAY VRF default)
   Type-5 AS External Link States
   LS age: 245
   Options: 0x22 (No TOS-capability, DC, E)
   LS Type: AS External Link
   Link State ID: 1.1.1.1
   Advertising Router: 1.1.1.1
   LS Seq Number: 0x80000001
   Network Mask: /32
     Metric Type: 2 (Larger than any link state path)
     TOS: 0
     Metric: 20

Routing › EIGRP.desc: Redistribute static routes. Route-map is REQUIRED on Nexus — without it the command is rejected.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
switch(config)# route-map ALLOW permit 10
switch(config-route-map)# match interface Null0
switch(config)# router eigrp 100
switch(config-router)# redistribute static route-map ALLOW

Routing › EIGRP.cmd:
interface Ethernet1/1
 ipv6 address 2001:db8::1/64
 ipv6 router eigrp 100
Routing › EIGRP.desc: Per-interface EIGRPv6 enable. The IPv6 address-family must be enabled under the process first.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
switch(config)# interface Ethernet1/1
switch(config-if)# ipv6 address 2001:db8::1/64
switch(config-if)# ipv6 router eigrp 100
switch(config-if)# no shutdown

Routing › EIGRP.desc: EIGRPv6 adjacency table (mirror of show ip eigrp neighbors for IPv6).
Routing › EIGRP.example:
switch# show ipv6 eigrp neighbors
IPv6-EIGRP AS 100 neighbors for process 100 VRF default
H   Address                Interface  Hold  Uptime  SRTT  RTO  Q   Seq
                                       (sec)        (ms)       Cnt Num
0   FE80::AABB:CCFF:FEDD:1 Eth1/1     14    3w2d    12    200  0   124

Routing › EIGRP.desc: EIGRPv6 topology table.
Routing › EIGRP.example:
switch# show ipv6 eigrp topology
IPv6-EIGRP Topology Table for AS(100)/ID(1.1.1.1) VRF default
P 2001:db8::/64, 1 successors, FD is 256
        via Connected, Eth1/1
P 2001:db8:1::/64, 1 successors, FD is 2816
        via FE80::2 (2816/256), Eth1/1

Routing › BGP.desc: Enable BGP feature on Nexus. Required before any `router bgp` command.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch(config)# feature bgp
switch(config)# show feature | include bgp
bgp                   1         enabled

Routing › BGP.desc: Enter BGP process for local AS. Only one BGP process per VRF on Nexus.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch(config)# router bgp 65001
switch(config-router)# router-id 1.1.1.1
switch(config-router)# log-neighbor-changes

Routing › BGP.desc: Pin BGP/OSPF/EIGRP router-id under the process. Without this the router-id is auto-derived (loopback0 IP preferred). Pinning ensures predictability across reloads.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch(config-router)# router-id 1.1.1.1
switch# show ip bgp summary | include router
BGP router identifier 1.1.1.1, local AS number 65001

Routing › BGP.desc: Log BGP peer state transitions (Established/Idle/Active) to syslog. Cheap visibility worth enabling everywhere.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch(config-router)# log-neighbor-changes
switch# show logging last 5 | include BGP
%BGP-5-ADJCHANGE: neighbor 10.0.0.5 Up
%BGP-5-ADJCHANGE: neighbor 10.0.0.6 Down - User reset

Routing › BGP.desc: Enter the IPv4 unicast AF — required to advertise networks under BGP and to activate peers for IPv4. Nexus separates per-AF configuration cleanly.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch(config-router)# address-family ipv4 unicast
switch(config-router-af)# network 10.0.0.0/24
switch(config-router-af)# maximum-paths 8
switch(config-router-af)# bestpath as-path multipath-relax

Routing › BGP.desc: Originate a prefix into BGP (Nexus uses prefix/length form, not the IOS `network ... mask ...` style). The prefix must exist in the RIB to be advertised.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch(config-router-af)# network 10.0.0.0/24
switch# show ip bgp 10.0.0.0/24
BGP routing table information for VRF default, address family IPv4 Unicast
BGP routing table entry for 10.0.0.0/24, version 12345
Paths: (1 available, best #1)
  Advertised to update-groups:  1
  Local
    0.0.0.0 (metric 0) from 0.0.0.0 (1.1.1.1)
      Origin IGP, MED 0, localpref 100, weight 32768, valid, sourced, local, best

Routing › BGP.cmd:
neighbor 1.2.3.4
 remote-as 65002
 update-source loopback0
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  send-community both
Routing › BGP.desc: eBGP peer block — Nexus puts AF-specific policy (route-maps, prefix-lists, community) inside the AF stanza nested under the peer.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch(config-router)# neighbor 1.2.3.4
switch(config-router-neighbor)#   remote-as 65002
  update-source loopback0
  ebgp-multihop 3
  password 7 <encrypted>
  address-family ipv4 unicast
    send-community both
    route-map ISP-IN in
    route-map ISP-OUT out
    maximum-prefix 50000 80 restart 5

Routing › BGP.cmd:
neighbor 1.2.3.4
 remote-as 65001
 update-source loopback0
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  next-hop-self
  route-reflector-client
Routing › BGP.desc: iBGP peer + RR role. `next-hop-self` rewrites NH on advertise (essential for iBGP); `route-reflector-client` reflects routes from this peer to other RR clients.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch(config-router)# neighbor 1.2.3.4
switch(config-router-neighbor)#   remote-as 65001
  update-source loopback0
  address-family ipv4 unicast
    next-hop-self
    route-reflector-client
    send-community both

Routing › BGP.cmd:
template peer SPINE
 remote-as external
 bfd
 update-source Ethernet1/49
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  send-community both
  allowas-in 1
Routing › BGP.desc: Peer template — Nexus equivalent of IOS peer-groups. Cleaner separation between control-plane policy and per-peer adjacency.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch(config-router)# template peer SPINE
switch(config-router-neighbor-template)#   remote-as external
  bfd
  address-family ipv4 unicast
    send-community both
    allowas-in 1
  address-family l2vpn evpn
    send-community both
switch(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5
switch(config-router-neighbor)#   inherit peer SPINE

Routing › BGP.desc: Inherit a peer template — neighbour reuses all template settings (encryption, AFs, policies). Override at the neighbour level still works.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5
switch(config-router-neighbor)#   inherit peer SPINE
  description SPINE-01

Routing › BGP.desc: IPv4 BGP peer summary: state, prefix-rcvd, msg-counters, uptime.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch# show bgp ipv4 unicast summary
BGP summary information for VRF default, address family IPv4 Unicast
BGP router identifier 1.1.1.1, local AS number 65001
BGP table version is 12345, IPv4 Unicast config peers 2, capable peers 2
4234 network entries and 8472 paths using 1234567 bytes of memory
BGP attribute entries [1234/123456], BGP AS path entries [56/345]
BGP community entries [12/456], BGP clusterlist entries [0/0]

Neighbor        V    AS  MsgRcvd  MsgSent   TblVer  InQ OutQ Up/Down  State/PfxRcd
10.0.0.5        4 65002    98421    98412    12345    0    0 3w2d         4234
10.0.0.6        4 65003     1234     1230    12345    0    0 00:00:14    Idle

Routing › BGP.desc: IPv4 BGP RIB — every prefix with all known paths.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch# show bgp ipv4 unicast
BGP routing table information for VRF default, address family IPv4 Unicast
BGP table version is 12345, local router ID is 1.1.1.1

*>e8.8.8.0/24       10.0.0.5              0           0     65002 15169 i

*  192.0.2.0/24     10.0.0.6              0           0     65003 65002 i

Routing › BGP.example:
switch# show bgp ipv4 unicast neighbors 10.0.0.5
BGP neighbor is 10.0.0.5, remote AS 65002, ebgp link, Peer index 1

Routing › BGP.desc: Per-prefix path detail: all paths, best-path selection reasoning, communities, AS_PATH.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch# show bgp ipv4 unicast 8.8.8.0/24
BGP routing table entry for 8.8.8.0/24, version 9876
Paths: (2 available, best #1)
