Routing › BGP.desc: Peer summary across all configured AFs (IPv4, IPv6, VPNv4, L2VPN EVPN). Useful first-look on RR / DC fabric switches.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch# show bgp all summary
[truncated per-AF blocks for: IPv4 Unicast, IPv6 Unicast, VPNv4 Unicast, L2VPN EVPN]

Neighbor        V    AS  MsgRcvd  MsgSent   TblVer  InQ OutQ Up/Down  State/PfxRcd
For IPv4 Unicast:
10.0.0.5        4 65002    98421    98412    12345    0    0  3w2d           4234
For L2VPN EVPN:
10.0.0.5        4 65002    98421    98412    12345    0    0  3w2d            512

Routing › BGP.cmd: clear bgp ipv4 unicast 1.2.3.4 soft in
Routing › BGP.desc: Inbound soft-refresh on a single peer — re-process Route-Refresh without dropping the session. Use after editing an inbound route-map.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch# clear bgp ipv4 unicast 10.0.0.5 soft in
switch# show bgp ipv4 unicast neighbors 10.0.0.5 | include Refresh
  Route refresh request: received 0, sent 1

Routing › BGP.desc: Soft-refreshes inbound and outbound policy on every IPv4 unicast BGP peer without tearing down sessions — use after an in/out route-map or prefix-list change
Routing › BGP.example:
switch# clear bgp ipv4 unicast * soft
switch# show bgp ipv4 unicast summary
Neighbor        V    AS MsgRcvd MsgSent   TblVer  InQ OutQ Up/Down  State/PfxRcd
10.0.0.1        4 65001   18234   18201    48212    0    0 3d02h          212
10.0.0.2        4 65002   15021   14998    48212    0    0 5d11h          140
# Session Up/Down time is unaffected — soft refresh re-applies policy
# in-place using stored (soft-reconfig or route-refresh capable) state.

Routing › BGP.cmd: ip prefix-list PEER-IN seq 10 permit 0.0.0.0/0 le 24
Routing › BGP.desc: Prefix-list matching default route through to /24 — common inbound filter. Nexus prefix-list syntax matches IOS exactly.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch(config)# ip prefix-list PEER-IN seq 10 permit 0.0.0.0/0 le 24
switch(config)# ip prefix-list PEER-IN seq 20 deny 0.0.0.0/0 le 32
switch# show ip prefix-list PEER-IN
ip prefix-list PEER-IN: 2 entries
   seq 10 permit 0.0.0.0/0 le 24
   seq 20 deny 0.0.0.0/0 le 32

Routing › BGP.desc: AS-path filter — match prefixes whose AS_PATH starts with 65002. Nexus uses quoted regex (IOS does not require quotes).
Routing › BGP.example:
switch(config)# ip as-path access-list 100 permit "^65002_"
switch(config)# route-map FILTER permit 10
switch(config-route-map)#   match as-path 100
switch# show route-map FILTER
route-map FILTER, permit, sequence 10
  Match clauses:
    as-path (as-path filter): 100

Routing › BGP.cmd:
route-map TO-ISP permit 10
 match ip address prefix-list MY-NETS
 set as-path prepend 65001 65001
Routing › BGP.desc: Outbound prepend route-map. Prepending lengthens AS_PATH and makes the path less preferred — commonly used for traffic engineering away from a backup link.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch(config)# route-map TO-ISP permit 10
switch(config-route-map)#   match ip address prefix-list MY-NETS
  set as-path prepend 65001 65001
switch(config-router-neighbor-af)# route-map TO-ISP out
switch# show ip bgp neighbors 10.0.0.5 advertised-routes | head -2
   Network          Next Hop         Metric  LocPrf  Weight Path
*> 10.0.0.0/24      0.0.0.0               0       100 32768 65001 65001 i

Routing › BGP.desc: eBGP ECMP cap (under address-family). Without this BGP installs only one path even if multiple equal-cost paths exist.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch(config-router-af)# maximum-paths 8
switch# show bgp ipv4 unicast 8.8.8.0/24 | include path
Paths: (8 available, best #1) ; all 8 installed

Routing › BGP.desc: iBGP ECMP cap. Separate from eBGP because the path-selection criteria differ.
Routing › BGP.example: switch(config-router-af)# maximum-paths ibgp 8

Routing › BGP.desc: Allow multipath even when AS-paths differ but length matches. Critical for VXLAN-EVPN fabric where leafs receive same prefix from multiple spines with different AS paths.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch(config-router-af)# bestpath as-path multipath-relax
switch# show bgp ipv4 unicast 10.50.10.0/24 | include path
Paths: (4 available, best #1)
  Advertised to update-groups: 1
  Path type: external, path is valid, is best path, eligible for multipath
  ...

Routing › BGP.desc: IPv6 AF inside EIGRP — Nexus uses MTR/AF style. Same process tag, separate address-family.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch(config)# router eigrp 100
switch(config-router)# address-family ipv6 unicast
switch(config-router-af)#   no shutdown
switch(config-router-af)#   router-id 1.1.1.1

Routing › BGP.desc: Enter BGP EVPN AF — needed for VXLAN-EVPN fabric. Carries Type-1 through Type-5 EVPN routes.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch(config-router)# address-family l2vpn evpn
switch(config-router-af)#   retain route-target all
switch(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5
switch(config-router-neighbor)#   address-family l2vpn evpn
    send-community both

Routing › BGP.desc: VPNv4 (MPLS L3VPN) AF — for traditional MPLS provider-edge boxes that run BGP/MPLS VPNs (not VXLAN-EVPN).
Routing › BGP.example:
switch(config-router)# address-family vpnv4 unicast
switch(config-router-af)# retain route-target all

Routing › BGP.desc: IPv6 BGP peer summary (mirror of `show bgp ipv4 unicast summary`).
Routing › BGP.example:
switch# show bgp ipv6 unicast summary
BGP summary information for VRF default, address family IPv6 Unicast
BGP router identifier 1.1.1.1, local AS number 65001
Neighbor        V    AS  MsgRcvd  MsgSent   TblVer  InQ OutQ Up/Down  State/PfxRcd
2001:db8::5     4 65002    12345    12340     1234    0    0  3w2d           512

Routing › BGP.desc: Bind BFD to a BGP peer. Sub-second peer-failure detection — beats waiting for hold-timer.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch(config-router)# neighbor 10.0.0.5
switch(config-router-neighbor)#   bfd
switch# show bfd neighbors application bgp
OurAddr         NeighAddr       LD/RD                 RH/RS  Holdown(mult) State    Int     Vrf
10.0.0.1        10.0.0.5        1090519041/1090519041 Up     750(3)        Up       N/A     default

Routing › BGP.desc: Enable BGP graceful-restart — peers preserve forwarding state across a control-plane restart of this router, avoiding a full RIB churn.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch(config-router)# graceful-restart
switch# show bgp ipv4 unicast neighbors 10.0.0.5 | include restart
  Graceful-Restart is enabled, restart-time 120 seconds, stalepath-time 300 seconds

Routing › BGP.desc: Defer best-path computation under churn (NX-OS 9.x+). Coalesces multiple updates within the deferral window into a single SPF/best-path pass.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch(config-router)# timers bestpath-defer 100 maximum 300
switch# show bgp ipv4 unicast | include defer
  Best-path defer timer: 100 ms, max 300 ms

Routing › BGP.desc: Displays the BGP summary table: neighbor address, AS, message queues, uptime, prefixes received, and state (Established/Active/Idle) — the first command to run when troubleshooting BGP.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch# show ip bgp summary

BGP summary information for VRF default, address family IPv4 Unicast
BGP router identifier 10.0.0.1, local AS number 65001

Neighbor        V    AS MsgRcvd MsgSent   TblVer  InQ OutQ Up/Down  State/PfxRcd
203.0.113.1     4 65002    1234    1230    45678    0    0 2d03h    8
10.0.0.2        4 65001      89      91    45678    0    0 1d12h    14

Routing › BGP.desc: BGP RIB (default VRF, IPv4 Unicast AF — Nexus shorthand for `show bgp ipv4 unicast`).
Routing › BGP.example:
BGP table version is 2143, local router ID is 1.1.1.1
Status codes: s suppressed, d damped, h history, * valid, > best, i - internal,
              r RIB-failure, S Stale, m multipath, b backup-path, x best-external

   Network          Next Hop            Metric LocPrf Weight Path
*> 0.0.0.0          1.2.3.4                              0 65002 i
*> 8.8.8.0/24       1.2.3.4                  0           0 65002 15169 i
*> 10.0.0.0/8       0.0.0.0                  0         32768 i
* i 172.16.0.0/16   2.2.2.2                  0    100      0 65003 i
*>                  5.6.7.8                              0 65003 i

Routing › BGP.desc: Per-peer detail for a single IPv4 BGP neighbour.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch# show ip bgp neighbors 10.0.0.5
BGP neighbor is 10.0.0.5, remote AS 65002
  BGP state = Established, up for 3w2d
  Last read 00:00:23, hold time is 180, keepalive interval is 60
  Received 98421 messages, Sent 98412 messages
  Connections established 1, dropped 0

Routing › BGP.cmd:
router bgp 65001
 router-id 1.1.1.1
 log-neighbor-changes
 address-family ipv4 unicast
 neighbor 1.2.3.4
  remote-as 65002
  address-family ipv4 unicast
   send-community both
Routing › BGP.desc: eBGP peer baseline — process config + AF activation + per-peer AF activation.
Routing › BGP.example:
switch(config)# router bgp 65001
switch(config-router)#   router-id 1.1.1.1
  log-neighbor-changes
  address-family ipv4 unicast
    network 10.0.0.0/24
    maximum-paths 8
  neighbor 10.0.0.5
    remote-as 65002
    update-source loopback0
    address-family ipv4 unicast
      send-community both

VXLAN / EVPN.desc: NVE (Network Virtualization Edge) interface state: source loopback, host-reachability protocol, member VNIs, VTEP encap.
VXLAN / EVPN.example:
switch# show nve interface nve1 detail
Interface: nve1, State: Up, encapsulation: VXLAN
 VPC Capability: VPC-VIP-Only [notified]
 Local Router MAC: aabb.cc00.0001
 Host Learning Mode: Control-Plane
 Source-Interface: loopback0 (primary: 1.1.1.1, secondary: 0.0.0.0)

Interface: nve1, NVE Mcast-group: 0.0.0.0, VNI Range: 10100,50001
 NVE Flags:
 NVE Adjacency Learning: Disabled
 NVE Authentication: Disabled

VXLAN / EVPN.desc: VTEP peers learned via BGP-EVPN: peer-IP, learning method, uptime, state.
VXLAN / EVPN.example:
switch# show nve peers
Interface  Peer-IP           State LearnType Uptime    Router-Mac
---------  ----------------  ----- --------- --------  -----------------
nve1       2.2.2.2           Up    CP        3w2d      aabb.cc00.0002
nve1       3.3.3.3           Up    CP        3w2d      aabb.cc00.0003
nve1       4.4.4.4           Up    CP        1d:04:22  aabb.cc00.0004

# LearnType CP = control-plane (BGP-EVPN); DP = data-plane (flood-and-learn).

VXLAN / EVPN.desc: Per-VNI state with mode (L2/L3), VLAN/VRF binding, ingress replication peers.
VXLAN / EVPN.example:
switch# show nve vni
Codes: CP - Control Plane     DP - Data Plane
       UC - Unconfigured      SA - Suppress ARP
       S-ND - Suppress ND

Interface VNI      Multicast-group   State Mode Type [BD/VRF]      Flags
--------- -------- ----------------- ----- ---- ------------------ -----
nve1      10100    UnicastBGP        Up    CP   L2 [100]           SA
nve1      10200    UnicastBGP        Up    CP   L2 [200]
nve1      50001    n/a               Up    CP   L3 [TENANT_A]

VXLAN / EVPN.desc: NVE per-VNI binding (lighter than `show nve vni`).
VXLAN / EVPN.example:
switch# show nve vni interface
Interface VNI      Multicast-group   Mode Type [BD/VRF]      State
nve1      10100    UnicastBGP        CP   L2 [100]           Up
nve1      10200    UnicastBGP        CP   L2 [200]           Up
nve1      50001    n/a               CP   L3 [TENANT_A]      Up

VXLAN / EVPN.desc: EVPN BGP peer summary. Use in VXLAN-EVPN fabric to confirm all spines are established as EVPN peers.
VXLAN / EVPN.example:
switch# show bgp l2vpn evpn summary
BGP summary information for VRF default, address family L2VPN EVPN
BGP router identifier 1.1.1.1, local AS number 65001
BGP table version is 9876

Neighbor        V    AS  MsgRcvd  MsgSent   TblVer  InQ OutQ Up/Down  State/PfxRcd
10.0.0.5        4 65002    98421    98412     9876    0    0  3w2d            512
10.0.0.6        4 65002    98412    98421     9876    0    0  3w2d            512

VXLAN / EVPN.desc: EVPN BGP table — every Type-1/2/3/5 route the fabric has advertised. Look for the VNI label in brackets.
VXLAN / EVPN.example:
switch# show bgp l2vpn evpn
BGP routing table information for VRF default, address family L2VPN EVPN
BGP table version is 9876, local router ID is 1.1.1.1

   Network            Next Hop         Metric    LocPrf     Weight Path
Route Distinguisher: 1.1.1.1:32867    (L2VNI 10100)
*>l[2]:[0]:[0]:[48]:[aabb.ccdd.eeff]:[0]:[0.0.0.0]/216
                      1.1.1.1                  100       32768 i
*>l[3]:[0]:[32]:[1.1.1.1]/88
                      1.1.1.1                  100       32768 i

Route Distinguisher: 2.2.2.2:32867    (L2VNI 10100)
*>i[2]:[0]:[0]:[48]:[1122.3344.5566]:[0]:[0.0.0.0]/216
                      2.2.2.2                  100           0 i

VXLAN / EVPN.desc: Type-2 (MAC/IP) EVPN routes only. Each entry binds a MAC address (and optionally IP) to a remote VTEP — the foundation of VXLAN L2 forwarding.
VXLAN / EVPN.example:
switch# show bgp l2vpn evpn route-type 2
BGP routing table information for VRF default, address family L2VPN EVPN

Route Distinguisher: 2.2.2.2:32867    (L2VNI 10100)
BGP routing table entry for [2]:[0]:[0]:[48]:[1122.3344.5566]:[0]:[0.0.0.0]/216, version 9876
Paths: (1 available, best #1)
  Path type: internal, path is valid, is best path
  AS-Path: NONE, path sourced internal to AS
    2.2.2.2 (metric 4) from 10.0.0.5 (1.2.3.5)
      Origin IGP, MED not set, localpref 100, weight 0
      Received label 10100
      Extcommunity: RT:65001:10100 ENCAP:8 Router MAC:aabb.cc00.0002

VXLAN / EVPN.desc: Type-5 IP-prefix EVPN routes — used to advertise full IP prefixes (rather than per-host /32 in Type-2) for inter-VRF routing in the fabric.
VXLAN / EVPN.example:
switch# show bgp l2vpn evpn route-type 5
BGP routing table information for VRF default, address family L2VPN EVPN

Route Distinguisher: 2.2.2.2:3
BGP routing table entry for [5]:[0]:[0]:[24]:[10.50.10.0]/224
Paths: (1 available, best #1)
  Path type: internal, path is valid, is best path
  AS-Path: NONE
    2.2.2.2 (metric 4) from 10.0.0.5 (1.2.3.5)
      Origin IGP, MED not set, localpref 100, weight 0
      Received label 50001
      Extcommunity: RT:65001:50001 ENCAP:8 Router MAC:aabb.cc00.0002

VXLAN / EVPN.desc: Locally-built EVPN MAC table — both local (learned via data plane) and remote (learned via BGP-EVPN Type-2).
VXLAN / EVPN.example:
switch# show l2route evpn mac all
Flags -(Rmac):Router MAC (Stt):Static (L):Local (R):Remote (V):vPC link
      (Dup):Duplicate (Spl):Split (Rcv):Recv (D):Del Pending (S):Stale

Topology    Mac Address    Prod   Flags         Seq No     Next-Hops
----------- -------------- ------ ------------- ---------- ----------------------
100         aabb.ccdd.eeff Local  L,            0          Eth1/3
100         1122.3344.5566 BGP    SplRcv        0          2.2.2.2 (Label: 10100)
200         2233.4455.6677 BGP    SplRcv        0          3.3.3.3 (Label: 10200)

VXLAN / EVPN.desc: EVPN MAC + IP bindings (Type-2 routes). The IP column is populated when ARP is learned/suppressed.
VXLAN / EVPN.example:
switch# show l2route evpn mac-ip all
Flags -(Rmac):Router MAC (Stt):Static (L):Local (R):Remote
      (V):vPC link (Dup):Duplicate (Spl):Split (Rcv):Recv

Topology    Mac Address    Host IP                                 Prod   Flags         Next-Hops
----------- -------------- --------------------------------------- ------ ------------- ----------
100         aabb.ccdd.eeff 10.100.0.10                             Local  L,            Local
100         1122.3344.5566 10.100.0.20                             BGP    SplRcv        2.2.2.2
200         2233.4455.6677 10.200.0.10                             BGP    SplRcv        3.3.3.3

VXLAN / EVPN.desc: ARP-suppression cache — entries learned via BGP-EVPN that the local VTEP can answer ARP for without flooding.
VXLAN / EVPN.example:
switch# show ip arp suppression-cache detail
Flags: + - Adjacencies synced via CFSoE
       L - Local Adjacency, R - Remote Adjacency, L2 - Learnt over L2 interface

Ip Address      Age          Mac Address    Vlan   Physical-ifc  Flags
10.100.0.10     00:23:45    aabb.ccdd.eeff 100    Ethernet1/3   L
10.100.0.20     00:18:34    1122.3344.5566 100    (null)        R
10.100.0.30     00:12:11    2233.4455.6677 100    (null)        R

VXLAN / EVPN.desc: Enable VXLAN overlay feature — prerequisite for any `interface nve`.
VXLAN / EVPN.example:
switch(config)# feature nv overlay
switch(config)# feature vn-segment-vlan-based
switch(config)# show feature | include nv|vn-segment
nv overlay            1         enabled
vn-segment-vlan-based 1         enabled

VXLAN / EVPN.desc: Enable VLAN→VNI mapping. Required before `vn-segment <vni>` under VLAN context.
VXLAN / EVPN.example:
switch(config)# feature vn-segment-vlan-based
switch(config)# vlan 100
switch(config-vlan)# vn-segment 10100
switch# show vlan id 100 vn-segment
Vlan   Segment-id
----   ----------
100    10100

VXLAN / EVPN.desc: Set the global anycast gateway MAC — every leaf uses this MAC as the SVI MAC for distributed gateways. Must be identical across the entire fabric.
VXLAN / EVPN.example:
switch(config)# fabric forwarding anycast-gateway-mac 0001.0001.0001
switch(config)# interface Vlan100
switch(config-if)#   ip address 10.100.0.1/24
  fabric forwarding mode anycast-gateway
switch# show running-config | include anycast
fabric forwarding anycast-gateway-mac 0001.0001.0001
  fabric forwarding mode anycast-gateway

VXLAN / EVPN.cmd:
interface nve1
 no shutdown
 host-reachability protocol bgp
 source-interface loopback0
 member vni 10100
  suppress-arp
  ingress-replication protocol bgp
 member vni 50001 associate-vrf
VXLAN / EVPN.desc: NVE interface baseline for VXLAN-EVPN: source-interface = VTEP loopback; host-reachability protocol bgp = control-plane learning via EVPN; per-VNI ingress-replication uses unicast (no multicast needed); L3 VNI is associated with the tenant VRF.
VXLAN / EVPN.example:
switch(config)# interface nve1
switch(config-if-nve)#   no shutdown
  host-reachability protocol bgp
  source-interface loopback0
  member vni 10100
    suppress-arp
    ingress-replication protocol bgp
  member vni 10200
    suppress-arp
    ingress-replication protocol bgp
  member vni 50001 associate-vrf

switch# show nve interface nve1
Interface: nve1, State: Up, encapsulation: VXLAN
 Source-Interface: loopback0 (primary: 1.1.1.1)
 Host Learning Mode: Control-Plane

VXLAN / EVPN.desc: Displays VXLAN logical interface (NVE) configuration including source address, VNIs, and encapsulation state
VXLAN / EVPN.example:
switch# show vxlan interface
Nve Interface    State  Mode        Source-Intf      Primary IP
----------------  -----  ----------  ---------------  ---------------
nve1             Up     CP-Mode     loopback0        10.0.0.1
  VNI : 10010  MCAST : 239.1.1.1  Status: Up
  VNI : 10020  MCAST : 239.1.1.2  Status: Up

VXLAN / EVPN.desc: Displays the FabricPath IS-IS topology database including switch IDs, addresses, and link states
VXLAN / EVPN.example:
switch# show fabricpath topology
FabricPath IS-IS Topology table
Switch-id  System-id        Hostname         State
---------  ---------------  ---------------  -----
100        d8b1.9004.0000   N5K-1            UP
101        d8b1.9004.0001   N5K-2            UP
102        d8b1.9004.0002   N5K-3            UP

Security & AAA.desc: All ACLs with per-ACE hit counters.
Security & AAA.example:
switch# show access-lists
IPv4 access list EXAMPLE
        10 permit ip 10.0.0.0/24 any [match=12345]
        20 permit tcp any any eq 443 [match=98765]
        30 deny ip any any log [match=42]

IPv4 access list MGMT
        10 permit tcp 10.99.99.0/24 any eq 22 [match=234]
        20 deny ip any any [match=12]

Security & AAA.desc: Single ACL with hit-counts. Use to verify which ACEs are actually matching.
Security & AAA.example:
switch# show ip access-lists EXAMPLE
IP access list EXAMPLE
        10 permit ip 10.0.0.0/24 any [match=12345]
        20 permit tcp any any eq 443 [match=98765]
        30 deny ip any any log [match=42]

# An ACE with [match=0] for weeks is suspicious — either no relevant traffic
# is hitting the interface, or NAT/route has changed and the packet bypasses
# this ACL entirely.

Security & AAA.desc: AAA authentication methods configured for login, console, etc.
Security & AAA.example:
switch# show aaa authentication
         default: group tacacs+ local
         console: local
         dot1x: group radius
         eou: group radius

Security & AAA.desc: TACACS+/RADIUS server groups with member servers, deadtime, VRF.
Security & AAA.example:
switch# show aaa server-groups
total number of groups: 2

following TACACS+ server groups are configured:
     group TAC:
            Server  10.10.10.50
            VRF: management
            deadtime: 0

following RADIUS server groups are configured:
     group RAD:
            Server  10.10.10.60
            VRF: default

Security & AAA.desc: NX-OS RBAC roles: network-admin (full), network-operator (read-only), and any custom roles with rule lists.
Security & AAA.example:
switch# show role
Role: network-admin
  Description: Predefined network admin role
  Vlan policy: permit (default)
  Interface policy: permit (default)
  Vrf policy: permit (default)
  -------------------------------------------------------------------
  Rule    Perm    Type        Scope               Entity
  -------------------------------------------------------------------
  1       permit  read-write

Role: network-operator
  Description: Predefined network operator role
  Vlan policy: permit (default)
  Interface policy: permit (default)
  Vrf policy: permit (default)
  -------------------------------------------------------------------
  Rule    Perm    Type        Scope               Entity
  -------------------------------------------------------------------
  1       permit  read

Security & AAA.desc: Local user accounts with assigned roles, password expiry, SSH-key presence.
Security & AAA.example:
switch# show user-account
user:admin
        this user account has no expiry date
        roles: network-admin
        no public key configured
user:netops
        this user account has no expiry date
        roles: network-operator
        public key: SHA256:abcd1234... (ssh-rsa)
user:monitor
        password expires in 30 days
        roles: network-operator

Security & AAA.desc: Enable TACACS+ feature.
Security & AAA.example:
switch(config)# feature tacacs+
switch(config)# show feature | include tacacs
tacacs                1         enabled

Security & AAA.cmd:
tacacs-server host 10.10.10.50 key <shared-key>
aaa group server tacacs+ TAC
 server 10.10.10.50
 use-vrf management
Security & AAA.desc: TACACS+ server group — define server (with shared key), then add it to a group that AAA methods reference. use-vrf management = source from the mgmt0 interface VRF.
Security & AAA.example:
switch(config)# tacacs-server host 10.10.10.50 key 0 <shared-key>
switch(config)# aaa group server tacacs+ TAC
switch(config-tacacs+)#   server 10.10.10.50
  use-vrf management
switch(config)# aaa authentication login default group TAC local

Security & AAA.cmd: aaa authentication login default group TAC local
Security & AAA.desc: Default login authentication via TACACS+ first, then fall back to local. Always include a local fallback or you can lock yourself out when the AAA server is unreachable.
Security & AAA.example:
switch(config)# aaa authentication login default group TAC local
switch(config)# aaa authentication login console local  ; protect console from AAA failures
switch# show aaa authentication
         default: group TAC local
         console: local

Security & AAA.desc: Display per-server TACACS+ authentication/authorisation/accounting request counts, success/fail totals, and round-trip time statistics
Security & AAA.example:
Server: 10.10.10.50
  Authentication requests: 1248   successes: 1240   failures: 8
  Authorization  requests:  623   successes:  623   failures: 0
  Accounting     requests:  623   successes:  623   failures: 0
  Average RTT: 4 ms   Max RTT: 18 ms

Security & AAA.desc: AAA accounting log of executed commands and sessions: who ran what, from where, and when
Security & AAA.example:
switch# show accounting log
Tue Jun 30 09:14:02 2026:type=update:id=10.10.10.50:user=admin:cmd=show running-config (SUCCESS)
Tue Jun 30 09:15:47 2026:type=start:id=10.10.10.50:user=admin:cmd=configure terminal (SUCCESS)
Tue Jun 30 09:16:12 2026:type=update:id=10.10.10.50:user=admin:cmd=interface Ethernet1/1 ; no shutdown (SUCCESS)
Tue Jun 30 09:16:40 2026:type=stop:id=10.10.10.50:user=admin (SUCCESS)

Logging / Monitoring.desc: Persistent local log file contents (stored in logflash partition). Survives reload.
Logging / Monitoring.example:
switch# show logging logfile
2026 Apr 21 14:23:45 switch01 %ETHPORT-5-IF_UP: Interface Ethernet1/1 is up in mode access
2026 Apr 21 14:24:01 switch01 %BGP-5-ADJCHANGE: neighbor 10.0.0.5 Up
2026 Apr 21 14:24:02 switch01 %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Process UNDERLAY, Nbr 2.2.2.2 from LOADING to FULL, Loading Done

Logging / Monitoring.desc: Last N lines from the in-memory log buffer. Newest at bottom.
Logging / Monitoring.example:
switch# show logging last 5
2026 Apr 21 14:30:01 switch01 %BFD-1-BFD_SESSION_REMOVED: BFD session to neighbor 10.0.0.6 on interface Eth1/50 has been removed.
2026 Apr 21 14:30:02 switch01 %BGP-5-ADJCHANGE: neighbor 10.0.0.6 Down
2026 Apr 21 14:30:14 switch01 %BGP-5-ADJCHANGE: neighbor 10.0.0.6 Up
2026 Apr 21 14:30:14 switch01 %BFD-5-BFD_SESSION_CREATED: BFD session to neighbor 10.0.0.6 on interface Eth1/50 has been created.
2026 Apr 21 14:30:15 switch01 %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Process UNDERLAY, Nbr 3.3.3.3 from LOADING to FULL

Logging / Monitoring.desc: Configured syslog destinations with severity and VRF used to source.
Logging / Monitoring.example:
switch# show logging server
Logging destination(s) Severity:                informational
Logging server(s):
      10.50.50.50         informational  facility:local7  vrf:management
      10.50.50.51         warnings       facility:local7  vrf:management

switch# show snmp community
Community            Group / Access      context        acl_filter
---------            --------------      -------        ----------
public               network-operator    n/a            n/a
corp-snmp            network-admin       n/a            SNMP-ACL

Logging / Monitoring.desc: Configured SNMP trap/inform destinations with version, security, VRF.
Send syslog at severity ≤ 6 (informational) to a server, sourced from mgmt0 VRF.
Logging / Monitoring.example:
switch(config)# logging server 10.50.50.50 6 use-vrf management
switch(config)# logging server 10.50.50.51 4 use-vrf management   ; lower severity to second server
switch# show logging server
Logging server(s):
      10.50.50.50         informational  facility:local7  vrf:management
      10.50.50.51         warnings       facility:local7  vrf:management

Logging / Monitoring.desc: SNMPv3 trap host with auth+priv. The user must be defined separately via `snmp-server user`.
Logging / Monitoring.example:
switch(config)# snmp-server user snmpuser01 network-admin auth sha <auth-pass> priv aes-128 <priv-pass>
switch(config)# snmp-server host 10.50.50.50 traps version 3 priv snmpuser01
switch(config)# snmp-server enable traps

Logging / Monitoring.cmd:
feature telemetry
telemetry
 destination-group DG1
  ip address 10.50.50.50 port 57500 protocol gRPC encoding GPB
 sensor-group SG1
  data-source DME
  path "Cisco-NX-OS-device-yang:sys/intf"
 subscription 1
  dst-grp DG1
  snsr-grp SG1 sample-interval 30000
Logging / Monitoring.desc: Streaming telemetry baseline — gRPC dial-out to a collector. sample-interval is in ms (30000 = 30 s). DME data-source covers most operational state.
Logging / Monitoring.example:
switch(config)# feature telemetry
switch(config)# telemetry
switch(config-telemetry)#   destination-group DG1
switch(config-tm-dest)#     ip address 10.50.50.50 port 57500 protocol gRPC encoding GPB
switch(config-telemetry)#   sensor-group SG1
switch(config-tm-sensor)#     data-source DME
    path "Cisco-NX-OS-device-yang:sys/intf" depth unbounded
  subscription 1
switch(config-tm-subscription)#     dst-grp DG1
    snsr-grp SG1 sample-interval 30000
switch# show telemetry transport
Session Id: 1, Type: gRPC, Status: Connected, Destination: 10.50.50.50:57500

Logging / Monitoring.desc: Displays On-Board Failure Logging (OBFL) data stored on the supervisor/line card itself — boot history, environmental temperature history, interface statistics history, and hardware error events — survives reloads and is invaluable for diagnosing intermittent hardware faults after the fact.
Logging / Monitoring.example:
switch# show logging onboard boot-uptime

----------------  -------------------------  ------------------
Switch, Rev. 0     Reset Reason               Uptime
----------------  -------------------------  ------------------
1                  Reload Command             45 days, 3 hrs
1                  Power Loss                 210 days, 11 hrs

What it means:
- Use "show logging onboard ?" for sub-options (error-stats, temperature,
  boot-uptime, etc.) — OBFL is stored in NVRAM/flash independent of the
  running log buffer.
- Cross-reference an unexpected "Reset Reason" against the timeline of
  a reported outage.

Diagnostics.desc: Ping sourced from the management VRF. Without `vrf management` ping uses the default VRF and probably fails on an OOB-managed device.
Diagnostics.example:
switch# ping 1.1.1.1 vrf management
PING 1.1.1.1 (1.1.1.1): 56 data bytes
64 bytes from 1.1.1.1: icmp_seq=0 ttl=64 time=0.241 ms
64 bytes from 1.1.1.1: icmp_seq=1 ttl=64 time=0.196 ms
64 bytes from 1.1.1.1: icmp_seq=2 ttl=64 time=0.187 ms
--- 1.1.1.1 ping statistics ---
5 packets transmitted, 5 packets received, 0.00% packet loss
round-trip min/avg/max = 0.187/0.215/0.241 ms

Diagnostics.desc: Ping sourced from a specific local IP — confirms a particular interface's reachability, not just any path.
Diagnostics.example:
switch# ping 1.1.1.1 source 10.0.0.1
PING 1.1.1.1 (1.1.1.1) from 10.0.0.1: 56 data bytes
64 bytes from 1.1.1.1: icmp_seq=0 ttl=64 time=0.241 ms

Diagnostics.desc: Hop-by-hop path discovery in the management VRF.
Diagnostics.example:
switch# traceroute 1.1.1.1 vrf management
traceroute to 1.1.1.1 (1.1.1.1), 30 hops max, 40 byte packets
 1  10.99.99.254 (10.99.99.254)  0.421 ms  0.398 ms  0.412 ms
 2  1.1.1.1 (1.1.1.1)  0.823 ms  0.798 ms  0.812 ms

Diagnostics.desc: ARP cache in the default VRF.
Diagnostics.example:
switch# show ip arp
Flags: * - Adjacencies learnt on non-active FHRP router
       + - Adjacencies synced via CFSoE
       # - Adjacencies Throttled for Glean
       CP - Added via L2RIB, Control plane Adjacencies
       PS - Added via L2RIB, Peer Sync
       RO - Re-Originated Peer Sync Entry
       D - Static Adjacencies attached to down interface

IP ARP Table for context default
Total number of entries: 3
Address         Age       MAC Address     Interface
10.0.0.2        00:01:23  aabb.ccdd.0002  Ethernet1/49
10.0.0.3        00:02:45  aabb.ccdd.0003  Ethernet1/50
10.100.0.10     00:00:14  aabb.ccdd.eeff  Vlan100

Diagnostics.desc: Displays the Layer 2 MAC address table: MAC, VLAN, port, and age — used to locate a device by MAC address, verify port-to-MAC mappings, and troubleshoot flooding.
Diagnostics.example:
switch# show mac address-table

Legend:
        * - primary entry, G - Gateway MAC, (R) - Routed MAC
        O - Overlay MAC, S - Static MAC, B - BlackHole MAC
        H - Hidden MAC

   VLAN     MAC Address      Type      age     Secure NTFY Ports/SWID.SSID.LID
---------+-----------------+----------+-------+------+----+------------------
 *   10     0050.5612.3456   dynamic   00:01:33   F    F   Eth1/1
 *   10     0050.5678.abcd   dynamic   00:00:12   F    F   Po1
 *   20     000c.29aa.bbcc   dynamic   00:00:48   F    F   Eth1/5

Diagnostics.desc: Find a specific MAC and which port it is learnt on — fast way to locate a host.
Diagnostics.example:
switch# show mac address-table address 0050.5612.3456
Legend:
        * - primary entry, G - Gateway MAC, (R) - Routed MAC, O - Overlay MAC

   VLAN     MAC Address      Type      age     Secure NTFY Ports
---------+-----------------+--------+---------+------+----+------------------
*  100    0050.5612.3456   dynamic  12        F      F    Eth1/5

Diagnostics.desc: Full per-neighbour CDP detail (platform, IOS/NX-OS version, all management addresses, native VLAN, duplex, holdtime) rather than the one-line-per-neighbour summary from "show cdp neighbors" — use when the terse table doesn't show enough to confirm the far end is what you expect, or when LLDP is disabled and CDP is the only neighbour-discovery source
Diagnostics.example:
-------------------------
Device ID: Distribution1.corp.example.com
Entry address(es):
  IP address: 10.0.0.2
Platform: cisco WS-C9500-48Y4C,  Capabilities: Router Switch IGMP
Interface: GigabitEthernet1/0/24,  Port ID (outgoing port): GigabitEthernet1/0/12
Holdtime : 132 sec

Version :
Cisco IOS XE Software, Version 17.12.04

advertisement version: 2
Duplex: full
Management address(es):
  IP address: 10.0.0.2

Diagnostics.desc: Shows full LLDP neighbor detail per local interface — chassis/port IDs, system name/description, capabilities, and management IP — the fastest way to confirm physical cabling matches the intended topology.
Diagnostics.example:
------------------------------------------------
Local Intf: Gi1/0/24
Chassis id: 0c4b.f0d2.c000
Port id: Gi1/0/12
Port Description: UPLINK-TO-ACCESS1
System Name: Distribution1

System Description:
Cisco IOS Software [Cupertino], Catalyst L3 Switch Software (CAT9K_IOSXE), Version 17.12.04

Time remaining: 105 seconds
System Capabilities: B,R
Enabled Capabilities: B,R
Management Addresses:
    IP: 10.0.0.2

Diagnostics.desc: BFD sessions with state (Up = sub-second failure detection live), timers, owning application.
Diagnostics.example:
switch# show bfd neighbors
OurAddr         NeighAddr       LD/RD                 RH/RS    Holdown(mult) State    Int      Vrf      Type
10.0.0.1        10.0.0.2        1090519041/1090519041 Up       750(3)        Up       Eth1/49  default  SH
10.0.0.3        10.0.0.4        1090519042/1090519042 Up       750(3)        Up       Eth1/50  default  SH

# Apps using BFD shown with `show bfd neighbors application`.

Diagnostics.desc: Hardware adjacency table — what the ASIC actually uses to rewrite L2 headers per next-hop. Differs from `show ip route` (RIB) and is the authoritative forwarding source.
Diagnostics.example:
switch# show forwarding adjacency
IPv4 adjacency information

 next-hop          rewrite info         interface
 -------------     ---------------      ------------
 10.0.0.2          aabb.ccdd.0002       Ethernet1/49
 10.0.0.3          aabb.ccdd.0003       Ethernet1/50
 10.0.0.254        aabb.ccdd.00fe       Ethernet1/1
 attached         (--)                  Vlan100

Diagnostics.desc: On-box packet capture of CPU-bound traffic (control plane). Use sparingly — high traffic floods the supervisor. Combine with capture-filter to scope.
Diagnostics.example:
switch# ethanalyzer local interface inband decode-internal limit-captured-frames 50 display-filter "bgp"
Capturing on inband
2026-04-21 14:30:01.234567 10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.1 BGP 145 KEEPALIVE Message
2026-04-21 14:30:01.245678 10.0.0.1 → 10.0.0.5 BGP 145 KEEPALIVE Message
2026-04-21 14:30:23.456789 10.0.0.2 → 10.0.0.1 BGP 89 UPDATE Message

3 packets captured

# Common filters:
#   capture-filter "host 10.0.0.5"
#   display-filter "ospf"
#   display-filter "lldp"

Diagnostics.desc: Full diagnostic dump for TAC. Always redirect — interactive scrollback will time out long before the command completes.
Diagnostics.example:
switch# show tech-support detail | redirect bootflash:tech.txt
# Takes 1–5 minutes; ~50–200 MB output.
switch# dir bootflash: | include tech
    143847362    Apr 21 14:35:12 2026  tech.txt

switch# copy bootflash:tech.txt sftp://admin@10.99.99.50/tech-switch01.txt vrf management

Diagnostics.cmd: show system internal sysmgr service all
Diagnostics.desc: NX-OS service-manager state — every system process with status, restart count, last exit code. Use when troubleshooting a crashed feature.
Diagnostics.example:
switch# show system internal sysmgr service all
Name                          UUID   PID    SAP   state           Start count    Tag     PluginID
----                          ----   ---    ---   -----           -----------    ---     --------
bgp                       0x000003E8 12345 0x0001 SRV_STATE_HANDSHAKED   1            N/A   1
ospf                      0x000003E9 12346 0x0002 SRV_STATE_HANDSHAKED   1            N/A   1
vpc                       0x000003EA 12347 0x0003 SRV_STATE_HANDSHAKED   1            N/A   1
eltm                      0x000003EB 12348 0x0004 SRV_STATE_HANDSHAKED   3            N/A   1   ; restarted 3 times — investigate

Diagnostics.desc: Show TCAM and forwarding hardware resource utilization across all modules — critical for diagnosing TCAM exhaustion causing ACL/route drops
Diagnostics.example:
> show hardware capacity
Module: 1
  FIB (IPv4 unicast):  Used: 12,045 / Total: 32,768  (36%)
  FIB (IPv6 unicast):  Used: 3,211  / Total: 8,192   (39%)
  ARP/ND:              Used: 5,100  / Total: 8,000   (63%)
  ACL:                 Used: 18,432 / Total: 32,768  (56%)
  VLAN:                Used: 248    / Total: 4,094   (6%)
  Multicast:           Used: 512    / Total: 2,048   (25%)

Note: ACL capacity above 80% may cause silent drops.

Diagnostics.desc: Lists processes that have generated core dump files on the switch, with the owning module, instance, PID, and timestamp — the starting point when investigating an unexpected process restart.
Diagnostics.example:
switch# show cores
Module  Instance  Process-name    PID     Date(Year-Month-Day Time)
------  --------  --------------  ------  -------------------------
28      1         bgp-64551       5179    2026-05-08 23:51:26

Diagnostics.desc: Lists only the interfaces currently in the err-disabled state along with the triggering reason (BPDU Guard, UDLD, link-flap, etc.) — the fastest way to find which ports a protection feature has shut down without scanning the full show interface status table.
Diagnostics.example:
switch# show interface status err-disabled

Port          Name               Status       Reason
--------------------------------------------------------------
Eth1/12       --                 err-disabled BPDUGuard errDisable
Eth1/27       --                 err-disabled UDLD errDisable

# Recover a port once the underlying cause is fixed:
switch(config)# interface eth1/12
switch(config-if)# shutdown
switch(config-if)# no shutdown
# Or configure automatic recovery: errdisable recovery cause bpduguard

Config Management.desc: Save running-config to non-volatile startup-config. Equivalent to `write memory` on IOS. NX-OS prompts for confirmation by default.
Config Management.example:
switch# copy running-config startup-config
[########################################] 100%
Copy complete, now saving to disk (please wait)...
Copy complete.

Config Management.desc: Save running-config to a named file in bootflash. Useful for taking a "before" snapshot prior to a change.
Config Management.example:
switch# copy running-config bootflash:running-2026-04-21.cfg
[########################################] 100%
Copy complete.
switch# dir bootflash: | include running
      45678   Apr 21 14:30:12  running-2026-04-21.cfg

Config Management.desc: Backup running-config to TFTP over the management VRF. Use scp/sftp for any non-trusted network.
Config Management.example:
switch# copy running-config tftp://10.50.50.50/switch01-2026-04-21.cfg vrf management
Trying to connect to tftp server......
Connection to server Established.
[########################################] 100%
TFTP put operation was successful.

Config Management.desc: Show what changed in running-config since the last `copy running startup` (or last commit in two-stage mode).
Config Management.example:
switch# show running-config diff
--- startup-config
+++ running-config
@@ -45,6 +45,9 @@
 interface Ethernet1/5
   description ESXi-host-03
   switchport
+  switchport mode trunk
+  switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30,99
+  switchport trunk native vlan 99
   no shutdown

Config Management.cmd:
configure dual-stage
!! enter changes
show configuration
commit
Config Management.desc: Two-stage commit — make changes in a candidate buffer, preview with `show configuration`, then `commit` atomically. Safer than direct `configure terminal` for multi-line changes.
Config Management.example:
switch# configure dual-stage
Enter configuration commands, one per line. End with CNTL/Z.
switch(config-s)# interface Ethernet1/6
switch(config-s-if)# description new server
switch(config-s-if)# switchport access vlan 30
switch(config-s-if)# show configuration
interface Ethernet1/6
  description new server
  switchport access vlan 30
switch(config-s-if)# commit
Commit Successful

Config Management.desc: Roll back to the previous saved commit. NX-OS keeps multiple commit checkpoints automatically.
Config Management.example:
switch# rollback running-config last 1
Note: Applying config parallelly may fail Rollback verification
Collecting Running-Config

#Generating Rollback Patch
Rollback Patch is Empty
Rollback Successful.

# Older revisions:
switch# show running-config diff checkpoint last-2

Config Management.desc: Reboot the device. NX-OS prompts to save config first if dirty. Use `reload force` to skip the prompt (dangerous in production).
Config Management.example:
switch# reload
This command will reboot the system. (y/n)?  [n] y
2026 Apr 21 14:45:00 switch01 %SYSMGR-2-SYSTEM_RELOAD: System will be reloaded due to user request

System & Status.desc: Reports fan tray status, current RPM, and operational state for each fan module — a fan in Warning or Failed state requires immediate attention to prevent thermal shutdown.
System & Status.example:
switch# show environment fan

Fan   Speed      Status    Location
----- ---------- --------- --------
FAN0  4800 RPM   Normal    Slot 1
FAN1  4750 RPM   Normal    Slot 2
FAN2  4820 RPM   Normal    Slot 3
FAN3  4790 RPM   Normal    Slot 4

Fan direction: Front-to-back
Airflow direction: Front-to-Back (port side to non-port side)

System & Status.desc: Power-supply status, current draw, capacity
System & Status.example:
PSU   Status  Input     Output
PSU0  OK      AC 240V   12V/25A
PSU1  OK      AC 240V   12V/22A

System & Status.desc: Displays the active configuration currently running in memory — this is what the switch is using right now, which may differ from the saved startup-config if uncommitted changes exist.
System & Status.example:
switch# show running-config
!Version ArubaOS-CX GL.10.13.0060
hostname aruba-core-01
!
vlan 1,10,20,100
interface 1/1/1
    description "UPLINK-TO-ROUTER"
    no shutdown
    no routing
    vlan trunk native 1
    vlan trunk allowed 10,20,100
interface 1/1/2
    description "ACCESS-WORKSTATION"
    no shutdown
    no routing
    vlan access 10

System & Status.desc: Per-interface running-config
System & Status.example:
interface 1/1/1
    description Access-PC1
    no shutdown
    no routing
    vlan access 10

System & Status.desc: Shows the saved configuration that will be applied on next reboot — compare against "show running-config" to identify uncommitted changes before a maintenance window.
System & Status.example:
switch# show startup-config
!Version ArubaOS-CX GL.10.13.0060
hostname aruba-core-01
!
# (same structure as running-config)
# If these differ, save with: write memory
# To compare:
switch# show running-config | diff - <(show startup-config 2>/dev/null) 
# Or check:
switch# show config-diff running startup

System & Status.desc: System event log (info / warn / err)
System & Status.example:
Jun 12 09:42:15  Interface 1/1/12 changed state to down
Jun 12 09:41:22  OSPF neighbor 10.0.0.2 changed to Full
Jun 12 09:30:00  LLDP neighbor on 1/1/49 updated

System & Status.desc: Filter event log by minimum severity
System & Status.example:
Jun 12 09:42:15  WARNING  Interface 1/1/12 changed state to down
Jun 12 09:30:12  WARNING  PSU1 input voltage low
Jun 12 08:14:22  WARNING  NTP sync lost — retrying

Timezone: UTC+0
NTP: synchronized (10.0.0.51)

Interfaces.desc: Lists all LLDP-discovered neighbors with the local port, remote chassis ID (MAC), remote port ID, and system name — use to quickly map physical connectivity.
Interfaces.example:
switch# show lldp neighbors

LOCAL-PORT  CHASSIS-ID           PORT-ID         SYSTEM-NAME
1/1/49      00:50:56:ab:cd:ef    Gi0/0           core-router-01.corp.local
1/1/50      48:0f:cf:12:34:56    1/1/49          aruba-dist-02
1/1/51      00:1a:2b:3c:4d:5e    Eth1/1          nexus-dc-01

Interfaces.desc: Shows the full LLDP neighbor record for all ports including capabilities, management IP, port description, VLAN memberships, and TTL — more complete than the summary view.
Interfaces.example:
switch# show lldp neighbors detail

Interface 1/1/49, via: LLDP, RID: 1, Time: 0 day, 04:12:33
  Chassis id: 00:50:56:ab:cd:ef
  Port id: GigabitEthernet0/0
  System Name: core-router-01.corp.local
  System Description: Cisco IOS XE Software, Version 17.09.03a
  System Capabilities: B, R
  Enabled Capabilities: R
  Management Address: 10.0.0.1
  Port Description: UPLINK-TO-ARUBA-CORE

Interfaces.desc: Shows LLDP transmit and receive status, packet counts, and error counters per interface — use to verify LLDP is enabled on a specific port and diagnose missing neighbour entries.
Interfaces.example:
switch# show lldp interface

Interface  Tx       Rx       Tx-Pkts  Rx-Pkts  Tx-Discard  Rx-Discard
1/1/1      enabled  enabled  4,231    4,229    0           0
1/1/2      enabled  enabled  4,230    0        0           4
1/1/49     enabled  enabled  4,231    4,231    0           0

# Rx-Pkts=0 on 1/1/2 means no LLDP neighbor sending on that port

Interfaces.desc: Shows PoE power budget summary: total available watts, currently allocated watts, and remaining capacity — critical before adding PoE devices to ensure sufficient power.
Interfaces.example:
switch# show poe-power

PoE Power Status:
  Total Power (W)   : 1440
  Used Power (W)    :  382
  Remaining (W)     : 1058
  Power Source      : Internal
  Guard Band (W)    :   10

# Per-port breakdown:
switch# show poe-power interface all | include "1/1"

Interfaces.desc: Shows PoE status, negotiated wattage, class, device type, and fault reason for a single port — essential when a PoE device fails to power on.
Interfaces.example:
switch# show poe-power interface 1/1/1

Interface 1/1/1 PoE Status:
  Admin     : enabled
  State     : delivering
  Power (W) : 7.2
  Max (W)   : 30.0
  Class     : 2
  Priority  : low
  Device    : IP Phone
  Fault     : none

# State "fault" with reason:
  Fault: power-denied   ← budget exhausted; increase priority or reduce other ports

Interfaces.cmd:
interface 1/1/1
 description ACCESS
 vlan access 10
 no shutdown
Interfaces.desc: Configures a port as an untagged access port in VLAN 10 with description — the standard template for workstation and IP phone ports on ArubaOS-CX switches.
Interfaces.example:
switch(config)# interface 1/1/1
switch(config-if)# description "DESK-SALES-101"
switch(config-if)# no routing
switch(config-if)# vlan access 10
switch(config-if)# no shutdown
switch(config-if)# exit
switch(config)# write memory

# Verify:
switch# show interface 1/1/1
  Enabled, Link is Up, Mode: access, VLAN: 10

Interfaces.cmd:
interface 1/1/24
 description TRUNK
 no shutdown
 no routing
 vlan trunk native 99
 vlan trunk allowed 10,20,30
Interfaces.desc: Trunk port baseline
Interfaces.example: (Interface 1/1/24 configured as trunk: native VLAN 99, allowed VLANs 10,20,30)

Interfaces.cmd:
interface 1/1/49
 no shutdown
 routing
 ip address 10.0.0.1/31
 mtu 9216
Interfaces.desc: L3 routed point-to-point uplink
Interfaces.example: (Interface 1/1/49 configured as L3 /31 link with jumbo frames enabled)

Interfaces.cmd:
interface lag 1
 no shutdown
 no routing
 vlan trunk allowed 10,20,30
 lacp mode active
Interfaces.desc: LACP LAG (port-channel)
Interfaces.example: (LAG 1 configured as LACP active trunk with VLANs 10,20,30)

Interfaces.desc: Adds physical interface 1/1/49 as a member port of LAG 1 — issued from interface config context; all member ports must share matching speed/duplex before the LAG comes up.
Interfaces.example:
switch(config)# interface 1/1/49
switch(config-if)# lag 1
switch(config-if)# exit
switch# show lag 1
LAG 1 is up
 Interfaces: 1/1/49, 1/1/50
 Aggregate-mode: active (LACP)
# 'show lag <n>' confirms the port joined and LACP negotiated; a port stuck
# in 'individual' state indicates an LACP mismatch on the connected peer.

Interfaces.desc: Administratively disable an interface; traffic is blocked, the link goes down, and STP removes the port from the topology
Interfaces.example:
switch(config)# interface 1/1/5
switch(config-if)# shutdown

# Verify:
switch# show interface 1/1/5
 Interface 1/1/5 is down
  Admin state is down
  Physical link is down

# The interface will show "Admin state is down" even if a cable is plugged in.
# To re-enable:
#   interface 1/1/5
#   no shutdown

Interfaces.desc: Administratively enable an interface that was previously shut down; issued from interface config context
Interfaces.example:
switch(config)# interface 1/1/5
switch(config-if)# no shutdown

# Verify state after a few seconds:
switch# show interface 1/1/5
 Interface 1/1/5 is up
  Admin state is up
  Link state is up, Link speed is 1 Gbps, Link duplex is Full
  ...

# If link stays down after "no shutdown":
#   Check cable: show interface 1/1/5 transceiver
#   Check the far end is also not shut: show cdp neighbors

Interfaces.desc: Shows per-interface LACP actor/partner state for all interfaces bundled into LAGs — port ID, priority, key, and aggregation flags
Interfaces.example:
switch# show lacp interfaces

State abbreviations :
A - Active        P - Passive      F - Forced
S - Short-timeout  L - Long-timeout ...

------------------------------------------------------------------
                    Actor                   Partner
------------------------------------------------------------------
Interface   Key    Port   Prio  State  |  Key   Port   Prio  State
------------------------------------------------------------------
1/1/1       1      1      1     ALFPC  |  1     1      1     ALFPC
1/1/2       1      2      1     ALFPC  |  1     2      1     ALFPC

What it means:
- Both sides must show the "C" (Collecting/Distributing) flag for the
  member to actually pass traffic — if only "A" and "S" show, LACP is
  negotiating but the port isn't forwarding yet.
- Mismatched Key values between members means they won't join the same
  aggregate — check "show lacp aggregates" for the resulting LAG state.

VLANs.desc: All VLANs + member ports
Single-VLAN detail
VLAN counts + active range
VLANs.example:
Number of existing VLANs: 5
VLAN IDs: 1,10,20,30,99

VLANs.desc: Create VLAN 10 and assign it a name in one config sequence; enter VLAN context then name it
VLANs.example:
switch(config)# vlan 10
switch(config-vlan)# name DATA
switch(config-vlan)# exit

# Verify:
switch# show vlan 10
 VLAN  Name        Status    Reason         Interfaces
 ----  ----------  --------  -------------  ----------
   10  DATA        up        ok             1/1/1-1/1/4

# Assign an access port to this VLAN:
#   interface 1/1/5
#   vlan access 10

VLANs.desc: Create a contiguous VLAN range (10 through 20) in a single command; names are not set and default to the VLAN number
VLANs.example:
switch(config)# vlan 10-20

# No output on success.

# Verify:
switch# show vlan
 VLAN  Name        Status
 ----  ----------  ------
   10  VLAN10      up
   11  VLAN11      up
   ...
   20  VLAN20      up

# Name each VLAN individually after creation:
#   vlan 10
#   name DATA
# Or delete the whole range:
#   no vlan 10-20

VLANs.desc: Delete VLAN 10 from the switch; all ports still assigned to this VLAN revert to default VLAN behavior
VLANs.example:
switch(config)# no vlan 10

# Verify deletion:
switch(config)# show vlan 10
VLAN 10 is not found.

# Ports that were access ports on VLAN 10 will retain their PVID configuration
# but the VLAN itself no longer exists — reassign ports to a valid VLAN:
#   interface 1/1/5
#   no shutdown
#   vlan access 20

VLANs.desc: Shows EVPN Ethernet Virtual Instance (EVI) configuration, import/export route targets, and associated VXLAN VNI for BGP EVPN fabrics

Spanning Tree.desc: Switch the global spanning-tree mode to MSTP; existing RSTP topology is preserved and CIST maps to MST instance 0
Spanning Tree.example:
switch(config)# spanning-tree mode mstp

# Configure MST region (required for MSTP to work across switches):
switch(config)# spanning-tree config-name CORP_MST
switch(config)# spanning-tree config-revision 1
switch(config)# spanning-tree instance 1 vlan 10,20,30
switch(config)# spanning-tree instance 2 vlan 40,50

# Verify:
switch# show spanning-tree

Topology Change : 0        Active: 0s

Spanning Tree.cmd:
spanning-tree config-name CORP
spanning-tree config-revision 1
spanning-tree instance 1 vlan 10,20
Spanning Tree.example: (MST region configured: name CORP, revision 1, instance 1 with VLANs 10,20)

Spanning Tree.cmd:
interface 1/1/1
 spanning-tree port-type admin-edge
 spanning-tree bpdu-guard
Spanning Tree.example: (Interface 1/1/1 set as edge port with BPDU-guard enabled — will err-disable on BPDU receipt)

router ospf 1
 router-id 1.1.1.1
 area 0.0.0.0
!
interface 1/1/49

L3 Routing.cmd:
router bgp 65001
 bgp router-id 1.1.1.1
 neighbor 1.2.3.4 remote-as 65002
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  neighbor 1.2.3.4 activate
L3 Routing.example: (BGP AS 65001 configured; eBGP session to AS 65002 neighbor 1.2.3.4 activated for IPv4)

L3 Routing.example:

L3 Routing.example:

VRF : default
BGP Summary
-----------
  Local AS               : 65000      BGP router-id        : 10.0.0.1
  Peers Total            : 2          Established Count    : 2

Neighbor         AS       MsgRcvd  MsgSent  Up/Down       State  PfxRcd
10.1.1.1         65001    1250     875      5d00h12m      Estab  125
10.1.1.2         65002    875      654      2d14h05m      Estab  89

L3 Routing.desc: Display detailed OSPF neighbor information including state, dead timer, and DR/BDR roles
L3 Routing.example:
switch# show ip ospf neighbor detail

Neighbor 2.2.2.2 on interface 1/1/49
  In the area 0.0.0.0 via interface 1/1/49
  Neighbor priority is 1, state is Full, 6 state changes
  DR is 10.0.12.2 (2.2.2.2), BDR is 10.0.12.1 (1.1.1.1)
  Options is E (external routing)
  Dead timer due in 00:00:35
  Neighbor is up for 3d 14h 22m
  Database summary list: 0
  Link state request list: 0
  Link state retransmission list: 0

What it means:
- "Full" = adjacency is complete; LSDBs are synchronised.
- Dead timer < 10 s while state is Full indicates a flapping link.
- DR/BDR roles show which router controls the broadcast segment.

VRF Configuration:
Name          State  L3 Ports  Description
default       up     14
MGMT          up      1        Out-of-band management

  State         : up

VRF.desc: Create a named VRF (or enter its config context if it already exists); used for routing table separation
VRF.example:
switch(config)# vrf MGMT
switch(config-vrf)# description "Out-of-band management VRF"
switch(config-vrf)# exit

# Assign an interface to the VRF:
switch(config)# interface 1/1/48
switch(config-if)# vrf attach MGMT
switch(config-if)# ip address 10.99.99.1/24

# Verify:
switch# show vrf
Name         Description                     Interfaces
-----------  ------------------------------  ----------
default      Default VRF                     1/1/1-47
MGMT         Out-of-band management VRF      1/1/48
(Interface 1/1/1 attached to VRF MGMT — note: removes any existing L3 config on interface)

VRF.desc: Extended VRF listing including route distinguisher, import/export route targets, associated interfaces, and per-VRF unicast/multicast route counts
VRF.example:
VRF: default
  Route Distinguisher: none
  Interfaces: 1/1/1, 1/1/2, loopback0
  Routes: 24 unicast

VRF: MGMT
  Route Distinguisher: none
  Interfaces: mgmt
  Routes: 2 unicast

VRF: Customer-A
  Route Distinguisher: 65001:100
  Import RT: 65001:100
  Export RT: 65001:100
  Interfaces: 1/1/3, 1/1/4
  Routes: 18 unicast

VSX / VSF.desc: VSX peer status, ISL state, role
VSX / VSF.example:
VSX Status
  Role            : Primary
  Config Sync     : Complete
  ISL State       : Up
  Keepalive State : Alive
  Peer            : vsx-secondary (10.99.99.2)
  Last Sync       : Jun 12 09:42:10

Configuration Consistency Check
  Result: Consistent
  Last Check: Jun 12 09:42:10
  Differences: None

Active Forwarding VLANs:
  VLAN 10: Primary
  VLAN 20: Primary
  VLAN 30: Secondary

VSX Brief
  Role: Primary  Peer: vsx-secondary
  ISL: Up (lag 256)  Keepalive: Alive
  Config Sync: Complete

VSF Status: Not configured
(VSF is the stacking feature; VSX is the active-active chassis)

VSX / VSF.desc: Detailed VSF (Virtual Switching Framework) stack member state: role (conductor/standby/member), member ID, MAC, priority, and split-detection status — the primary command when a stack won't form or a member drops out.
VSX / VSF.example:
switch# show vsf detail

VSF Status                    : Enabled
VSF Split Detection            : Enabled, LACP MAD
Member  Role       MAC Address        Priority  Status
1       Conductor  aa:bb:cc:00:01:01  128       In-domain
2       Standby    aa:bb:cc:00:01:02  64        In-domain
3       Member     aa:bb:cc:00:01:03  0         In-domain
# A member showing 'Split' or missing entirely from the table indicates a
# broken VSF link — check the dedicated VSF/stacking ports and cabling first.
VSF topology: not applicable — VSF not configured

VSX / VSF.cmd:
vsx
 inter-switch-link lag 256
 role primary
 keepalive peer 10.99.99.2 source 10.99.99.1
VSX / VSF.desc: VSX baseline on the primary peer
VSX / VSF.example: (VSX configured: ISL on LAG 256, this switch is primary, keepalive to 10.99.99.2)

ACLs.example:
Access List Summary
Name            Type   Entries  Refs
ALLOW_WEB       ip     3        2
DENY_RFC1918    ip     12       0
INTERNAL_ONLY   ip     4        5

Access List ALLOW_WEB (IPv4):
  10 permit tcp any any eq 443
  20 permit tcp any any eq 80
  30 deny any any any log

Access List ALLOW_WEB hit counts:
  10 permit tcp any any eq 443    : 1,824,441 matches
  20 permit tcp any any eq 80     :   124,221 matches
  30 deny any any any log          :        82 matches

ACLs.cmd:
access-list ip ALLOW_WEB
 10 permit tcp any any eq 443
 20 permit tcp any any eq 80
 30 deny any any any log
ACLs.desc: Creates a named IPv4 ACL permitting HTTPS/HTTP and logging everything else denied — must be applied to an interface to take effect (see "apply access-list")
ACLs.example:
switch(config)# access-list ip ALLOW_WEB
switch(config-acl-ip)# 10 permit tcp any any eq 443
switch(config-acl-ip)# 20 permit tcp any any eq 80
switch(config-acl-ip)# 30 deny any any any log
switch(config-acl-ip)# exit
switch# show access-list ip ALLOW_WEB
ACL ALLOW_WEB (IPv4)
 10 permit tcp any any eq 443
 20 permit tcp any any eq 80
 30 deny any any any log
# Logged denies show up in 'show access-list log'; unattached ACLs have
# no effect on traffic until applied to an interface with 'apply access-list ... in'.

ACLs.cmd:
interface 1/1/1
 apply access-list ip ALLOW_WEB in

Security & AAA.desc: Configure a TACACS+ server for CLI authentication; specify the shared secret as plaintext (stored encrypted in running-config)

switch(config)# tacacs-server host 10.10.10.50 key plaintext MySecret123

switch(config)# aaa authentication login default group tacacs local

# Test reachability:
switch# test aaa authentication tacacs-server 10.10.10.50 admin mypassword
Authentication Successful

# Verify server config:
switch# show tacacs-server detail
  Host:     10.10.10.50
  Port:     49
  Key:      ****
  Timeout:  5
  Retries:  1

aaa group server tacacs+ TAC
 server 10.10.10.50
Security & AAA.desc: Server group
Security & AAA.example: (TACACS+ server group TAC created with server 10.10.10.50)

Security & AAA.cmd: aaa authentication login default group TAC local
Security & AAA.desc: Login auth: TACACS first, local fallback
Security & AAA.example: (Login auth order: TACACS group TAC, then local fallback)

Security & AAA.cmd: user admin password plaintext <pwd> role administrators
Security & AAA.desc: Set the local admin user password and assign the built-in administrators role for full CLI access
Security & AAA.example:
switch(config)# user admin password plaintext Str0ng!Pass role administrators

# Verify the user exists and role is correct:
switch# show users
Username     Role             Status
-----------  ---------------  ------
admin        administrators   active
monitor      operators        active

# NOTE: Use a strong password — this account has full configuration access.
# For production, enforce TACACS+/RADIUS and keep the local user as a fallback only.

  Queue 1: strict
  Queue 2: WRR weight 40
  Queue 3: WRR weight 60

Queue Profile "default-profile"
  Queue 0: dscp 0-7   (Best Effort)

  Queue 4: dscp 32-39 (CS4)
  Queue 6: dscp 46,47 (EF/Voice)

QoS.desc: List all defined QoS class maps including match criteria such as DSCP values or ACLs
QoS.example:
switch# show class
Class map VOICE (IPv4)
  Match: dscp ef  (46)
Class map VIDEO (IPv4)
  Match: dscp af41  (34)
Class map SCAVENGER (IPv4)
  Match: dscp cs1  (8)
Class map DATA (IPv4)
  Match: dscp default  (0)

# Class maps are used in policy maps applied to interfaces:
#   policy-map QOS_POLICY
#   class VOICE
#     priority
#   class VIDEO
#     bandwidth percent 20

QoS.desc: Defined policies
QoS.example:
Policy MARK
  Class VOICE
    Action: mark dscp 46

QoS.desc: Policy hit counts
QoS.example:
Policy MARK (applied on 1/1/1 ingress)
  Class VOICE: 284,441 packets matched

QoS.desc: Globally trust DSCP
QoS.example: (Globally trust DSCP markings from connected devices)

QoS.cmd:
class ip VOICE
 10 match dscp 46
!
policy MARK
 class VOICE
  action mark dscp 46
QoS.desc: Class + policy baseline
QoS.example: (Class VOICE matching EF DSCP 46; policy MARK applies it)

Diagnostics.desc: Display Control Plane Policing (CoPP) statistics: packets passed and dropped per traffic class (ARP, BGP, OSPF, ICMP, SSH, etc.). High drop counters on a class indicate control-plane rate limiting is active and may affect adjacency stability.
Diagnostics.example:
aruba-cx# show copp statistics

Traffic Class               Passed Pkts   Dropped Pkts   Passed Bytes  Dropped Bytes
-----------                 -----------   ------------   ------------  -------------
ARP                           123456789              0    9,876,543,210             0
BGP                             3456789              0      345,678,900             0
OSPF                              45678              0        4,567,800             0
ICMP                           1234567             45      123,456,700         4,500
SSH/Telnet                       23456              0        2,345,600             0
LACP                             12345              0        1,234,500             0
STP                               4567              0          456,700             0
UNCLASSIFIED                    234567           1234       23,456,700       123,400

What it means:
- ICMP has 45 dropped packets — switch is rate-limiting ICMP toward CPU (normal)
- UNCLASSIFIED drops indicate unrecognised traffic hitting the CPU — investigate
- BGP/OSPF drops=0 is healthy; non-zero may cause neighbour flaps
- Run 'clear copp statistics' to reset counters after baselining

Diagnostics.desc: Collects a scoped 'basic' diagnostic bundle (vs the full 'diag-dump') — faster to generate when only core system, interface, and log state are needed for a quick triage.
Diagnostics.example:
switch# diag-dump basic
Collecting basic diagnostic information...
Basic diag-dump collected to /var/diagnostic/diag-dump-basic-20260703-101532.tar.gz
# Levels available: basic, l2, l3, all. 'basic' skips the larger L2/L3 state
# tables, keeping the bundle small enough to email directly to TAC.

Config Management.desc: Persists the active running-config to startup-config (NVRAM) so it survives a reboot — AOS-CX does not auto-save, so any unsaved change is lost on the next power cycle.
Config Management.example:
switch# copy running-config startup-config
Copying running-config to startup-config
#
switch# show running-config diff startup-config
(no differences)
# Confirming with 'show running-config diff startup-config' is the standard
# post-save check — an empty diff means startup-config is fully in sync.

Config Management.desc: Alias for copy running-config startup-config

Logging & Diagnostics.desc: Bundles controller/AP log files plus tech-support data into logs.tar on flash — copy it off with `copy flash: logs.tar tftp: <server-ip>` before opening a support case.
Logging & Diagnostics.example:
(host) [mynode] #tar logs tech-support
Creating logs.tar ... done

(host) [mynode] #copy flash: logs.tar tftp: 10.0.0.50
Transfer complete

Config Management.desc: Save the running configuration to flash memory; must be run after any configuration change to survive a controller reload
Config Management.example:
(ArubaOS-controller) # write memory
Saving Configuration...
Saved.

# Always run write memory before:
#   - Scheduling a reload
#   - Planned maintenance
#   - After major policy changes

# Create a named backup before overwriting:
#   copy running-config flash:pre-change-backup.cfg
# Verify config was saved:
#   dir flash: | include cfg

Config Management.desc: Saves the current running configuration to a named file in onboard flash — use before risky changes so you can restore with "copy flash:bkp.cfg running-config"
Config Management.example:
(ArubaOS) #copy running-config flash:bkp.cfg
Copying running-config...
Configuration saved to bkp.cfg
(ArubaOS) #dir flash:
-rw-  148213  Jun 30 2026  bkp.cfg
-rw-  147890  Jun 12 2026  default.cfg
# Distinct from 'write memory', which saves to the active startup-config —
# named checkpoints like this let you keep multiple restore points.

Config Management.desc: Push backup config to TFTP
Config Management.example:
Copying flash:bkp.cfg to tftp://10.50.50.50/bkp.cfg...
.........
Transfer complete.

Config Management.desc: Installed image partitions + which is active
Config Management.example:
Partition 0: ArubaOS 8.11.2.2 (active)
Partition 1: ArubaOS 8.10.0.8
Booted from: Partition 0

Config Management.cmd: copy ftp 10.50.50.50 admin <pwd> arubaos.ari system: partition 1
Config Management.desc: Stage ArubaOS image into partition 1
Config Management.example:

[####################################] 100%
Image installed in partition 1

System will reload now...

Reload scheduled at 02:00
Use "no reload at" to cancel.

System & Status.desc: Shows the Instant AP software version, model, build number, serial number, and uptime — essential first step when troubleshooting or verifying a firmware upgrade.
System & Status.example:
(instant-AP) # show version
Aruba AP-615 (Aruba Networks)
  ArubaOS-Instant Version:
  Image version    : 8.10.0.4-8.10.0.4
  Image build time : 2026-04-10 09:00:00 UTC
  Serial number    : CNF1234ABCD
  Board revision   : R2
  CPU              : ARMv8 (Cortex-A72, 1.8GHz)
  Memory           : 1024 MB
  Uptime           : 3 days 04:22:11

System & Status.desc: Shows a concise cluster summary for an Aruba Instant deployment: AP name, model, IP, number of clients, channel assignments, and Virtual Controller information.
System & Status.example:
(instant-AP) # show summary
Name         : ap-floor3
Model        : AP-615
IP Address   : 10.0.5.130
VC IP        : 10.0.5.100
VC Role      : Member
Clients      : 12 (8 on 5GHz, 4 on 2.4GHz)
Channel 2.4G : 1
Channel 5G   : 48
Uptime       : 3d 04:22:11

System & Status.desc: Displays the active configuration on the Aruba Instant AP, including SSID profiles, radio settings, security configuration, and uplink settings.
System & Status.example:
(instant-AP) # show running-config
hostname AP-FLOOR1-01
country US

wlan access-rule "default_instant_access_rule"
  index 0
  rule any any match any any any permit

wlan ssid-profile "Corp-WiFi"
  essid Corporate
  type employee
  opmode wpa3-aes-ccm-128
  wpa-passphrase ****
  rf-band all
  ...

System & Status.desc: Displays AP environment variables including provisioning parameters set via AirWave/Central, cluster role, VC (Virtual Controller) IP, and country code.
System & Status.example:
(instant-AP) # show ap-env
AP Environment:
  Hostname          : AP-FLOOR1-01
  AP Group          : default
  Virtual Controller: 10.0.5.100
  AirWave IP        : 10.0.1.200
  Country Code      : US
  VC Role           : Member
  Organization      : Corp-HQ
  AP License        : RW-AP-S1 (valid)

System & Status.desc: Display the current system date and time on the AP; verify against controller and NTP for log correlation accuracy
System & Status.example:
AP-OFFICE-01# show clock
Current Time: Thu Jun 26 09:42:17 GMT 2026

# If the time is wrong, check NTP config on the Mobility Controller:
#   From controller: show ntp servers
# AP time is synced from the controller during association.
# Time skew > 5 minutes can cause certificate authentication failures (802.1X).

System & Status.desc: Displays the regulatory country code configured on the Instant AP, which determines allowed channels, maximum Tx power, and DFS requirements.
System & Status.example:
(instant-AP) # show country
Country Code: GB
Country Name: United Kingdom
RegulatoryDomain: ETSI

# Available channels for this domain:
# 2.4GHz: 1-13
# 5GHz: 36-48, 52-64 (DFS), 100-140 (DFS), 149-173
# Country code is set at provisioning and cannot be changed without factory reset

System & Status.desc: Displays hardware inventory for the Aruba Instant AP: model, part number, serial number, and memory — matches information on the physical label for RMA and support cases.
System & Status.example:
(instant-AP) # show inventory
NAME: AP-615
DESCR: Aruba AP-615
PID: APIN0615
VID: 01
SN: CNF1234ABCD

Hardware Version: 2.0
CPU: ARMv8 Cortex-A72 @ 1.8 GHz
Flash: 1024 MB
DRAM: 512 MB

System & Status.desc: Display total, used, and free memory on the AP; high memory usage may indicate a memory leak requiring a reboot
System & Status.example:
AP-OFFICE-01# show memory
Total Memory: 512 MB
Used Memory : 268 MB (52%)
Free Memory : 244 MB (48%)

# Memory usage above 85% may degrade AP performance.
# Processes consuming memory:
#   show process memory sorted | head -20

# If memory has been steadily climbing for days without correlation to client count,
# schedule a maintenance reboot or upgrade the firmware.

System & Status.desc: Displays AP-level traffic statistics including bytes transmitted and received per radio band, error counters, and total client throughput — use to assess overall AP load and health.
System & Status.example:
(instant-AP) # show stats
Radio 0 (2.4 GHz):
  Tx bytes   : 1,234,567,890
  Rx bytes   : 987,654,321
  Tx packets : 8,345,678
  Rx packets : 7,234,567
  Tx errors  : 0
  Retry rate : 4.2%

Radio 1 (5 GHz):
  Tx bytes   : 8,901,234,567
  Rx bytes   : 3,456,789,012
  Retry rate : 1.8%

System & Status.desc: Generates a comprehensive diagnostic bundle including system info, RF stats, client table, event log, CPU/memory usage, and configuration — attach this output when opening a TAC support case.
System & Status.example:
(instant-AP) # show tech-support
<Compiling tech-support data...>

--- System Info ---
Hostname: AP-FLOOR1-01
Model: AP-505
Firmware: 8.10.0.4-8.10.0.4
Uptime: 3 days 04:22:11

--- Radio Summary ---
Radio 0: Channel 6, Tx Power 17 dBm, Clients 8
Radio 1: Channel 36, Tx Power 20 dBm, Clients 14

# Output is long (~10,000 lines) — redirect to file: show tech-support > flash:tech-support.txt

System & Status.desc: Shows the ARM (Adaptive Radio Management) channel and power-change history per radio, with a reason code for each change (interference, radar, noise, rogue containment) — the first place to look when a radio has been hopping channels.
System & Status.example:
(instant-AP) # show ap arm history

Radio 0 (2.4GHz) ARM History
-----------------------------
Time                 Channel  Power  Reason
2026-06-20 09:14:02   1        18     I (Interference)
2026-06-20 14:02:11   6        18     N (Noise exceeded)
2026-06-21 02:00:00   11       18     AIRMATCH

Legend: I=Interference R=Radar N=Noise Q=Bad Quality G=Rogue Containment

Network.desc: Lists all IP addresses assigned to AP interfaces (wired uplink, management, VLAN bridges) with their protocol state — the first check when an AP loses connectivity.

lo         127.0.0.1       255.0.0.0       UP

Network.desc: Lists VLANs configured on the Aruba Instant AP with their type (native/access/trunk) and associated interface — use to verify VLAN tagging for multi-SSID deployments.
Network.example:
(instant-AP) # show vlan
VLAN  Type     Interface
----  -------  ----------
1     native   eth0
10    trunk    eth0  (mapped to Corp-WiFi SSID)
50    trunk    eth0  (mapped to Guest SSID)

# Verify VLAN tagging with packet capture:
(instant-AP) # ap-debug pkgcapture uplink

Port  Type      Speed    Duplex  Link  POE

e1    n/a       n/a      n/a     Down  n/a

Network.desc: Shows a summary of all AP physical and virtual interfaces with their operational state, speed, and MAC address — useful for verifying uplink bonding and radio interface status.
Network.example:
(instant-AP) # show interface brief
Interface  Type      Status  Speed    MAC
---------  --------  ------  ------   -----------------
eth0       Ethernet  Up      1Gbps    00:1a:1e:ab:cd:ef
wlan0      Radio     Up      802.11ax 00:1a:1e:ab:cd:f0  (2.4GHz)
wlan1      Radio     Up      802.11ax 00:1a:1e:ab:cd:f1  (5GHz)
wlan2      Radio     Up      802.11ax 00:1a:1e:ab:cd:f2  (6GHz)

Network.desc: Sends ICMP echo requests from the AP to verify IP reachability and measure latency and packet loss to a target — run this from the AP CLI to test connectivity independently of clients.
Network.example:
(instant-AP) # ping 1.1.1.1
PING 1.1.1.1 (1.1.1.1): 56 data bytes
64 bytes from 1.1.1.1: icmp_seq=0 ttl=57 time=8.2 ms
64 bytes from 1.1.1.1: icmp_seq=1 ttl=57 time=8.0 ms
64 bytes from 1.1.1.1: icmp_seq=2 ttl=57 time=8.3 ms

--- 1.1.1.1 ping statistics ---
3 packets transmitted, 3 packets received, 0.0% packet loss
round-trip min/avg/max = 8.0/8.2/8.3 ms

Network.desc: Performs a traceroute from the AP to identify each network hop and latency between the AP and destination — useful for diagnosing routing or path issues from the AP perspective.
Network.example:
(instant-AP) # traceroute 1.1.1.1
traceroute to 1.1.1.1, 30 hops max, 38 byte packets
 1  10.0.5.1     0.6 ms   0.5 ms   0.5 ms
 2  192.168.1.1  1.2 ms   1.1 ms   1.2 ms
 3  10.10.10.1   3.4 ms   3.3 ms   3.5 ms
 4  1.1.1.1      8.2 ms   8.0 ms   8.1 ms
# Latency spike between hops 2 and 3 — WAN link boundary

Wireless / SSID.desc: Lists every BSSID this Instant AP is broadcasting, one row per radio per SSID, with channel, power, and the associated client count
Wireless / SSID.example:
(instant-AP) # show ap bss-table

BSS                Ess          Ph  Ch  EIRP  Cl
---                ---          --  --  ----  --
00:1a:1e:ab:cd:ef  Corporate    AC  36  18    14
00:1a:1e:ab:cd:f0  Corporate    AN  6   15    9
00:1a:1e:ab:cd:f1  Guest        AC  36  18    3

Num BSS:3

What it means:
- One row per radio/band the SSID is broadcast on — a single "Ess"
  (SSID) usually appears twice (2.4GHz + 5GHz) unless band-steering or
  band-only config restricts it.
- "Cl" is live associated client count per BSSID — use it to spot an
  overloaded radio before clients start reporting slow Wi-Fi.

Clients.desc: Lists all clients currently associated to this Instant AP with their MAC address, IP, SSID, band, RSSI, data rate, and session duration.
Single-client detail

Diagnostics.desc: Makes the physical LEDs on the named AP blink so you can visually locate it in a rack or ceiling — restore normal status LEDs with 'ap-leds ap-name <name> normal'.
Diagnostics.example:
(instant-AP) # ap-leds ap-name ap-floor3 blink
LEDs on ap-floor3 set to blink

# Once located, restore normal status LEDs:
(instant-AP) # ap-leds ap-name ap-floor3 normal
LEDs on ap-floor3 set to normal

Provisioning & Updates.desc: AP-side variables incl. master IP, AP group
Provisioning & Updates.example:
Aruba Part No.    : APIN0615
Serial No.        : CNXXXXXXX
Model             : AP-615
Master IP         : 10.0.50.23
AP Group          : floor3-grp

Partition 0: ArubaOS 10.6.0.2 (active)
Partition 1: ArubaOS 10.5.0.3 (backup)
Booted from: Partition 0

Provisioning & Updates.desc: Provision the AP to a master controller / VC
Provisioning & Updates.example: (AP provisioned to connect to VC/controller at <ip>; reboots to apply)

Provisioning & Updates.desc: Reboot the AP immediately; the AP will disconnect all clients, shut down radio interfaces, and restart
Provisioning & Updates.example:
AP-OFFICE-01# apboot

System is going down for reboot NOW!
Stopping radios...
Flushing filesystem...
Rebooting...

# All associated clients are deauthenticated and must reassociate after reboot.
# Use "reload" in ArubaOS for a softer variant that waits for clients to disconnect.
# Check firmware version after reboot:
#   show ap image version

Provisioning & Updates.desc: Initiate a graceful AP reload; sends deauthentication frames to associated clients before restarting
Provisioning & Updates.example:
AP-OFFICE-01# reload
Do you really want to reset the system? (yes/no): yes

Sending deauth to associated clients...
Rebooting...

# More graceful than "apboot" — clients receive deauth frames and can roam
# to another AP before the reload completes.
# Monitor the AP coming back online:
#   From controller: show ap database  (wait for Up state to return)

             (bps)    98756432     97223567     95113456     93866543

# Load is utilisation of the forwarding ASIC — distinct from CPU.
